> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Cleaning and Preprocessing: Develop a pipeline for cleaning and preprocessing a large messy dataset like UCI Machine Learning Repository - Adult Data Set. Tasks: Handle missing values, outliers, and noisy data. Apply dimensionality reduction techniques to simplify the dataset. Implement data transformation and normalization processes.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Data Cleaning and Preprocessing?

**Data Cleaning and Preprocessing** refers to the systematic process of detecting, correcting, and transforming raw, "messy" data into a clean, structured, and analysis-ready format. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Algorithms for Data Science*, this is the foundational step of any data science pipeline — often quoted as consuming **70–80%** of a data scientist's time (a Harvard Business Review estimate frequently cited in KTU board evaluations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Data preprocessing is the iterative process of converting raw data into a clean, consistent, and structured form using techniques such as missing value imputation, outlier treatment, noise reduction, dimensionality reduction, and feature scaling, to enable reliable downstream analytics and machine learning modeling.*

### 1.2 The UCI Adult Data Set — A KTU "Model Messy Dataset"

The **UCI Machine Learning Repository – Adult Data Set** (also known as the *Census Income Dataset*) is a canonical benchmark dataset used in KTU lab examinations and KTU model question papers. It contains approximately **48,842 instances** with **14 attributes** plus a binary target variable (`<=50K` or `>50K`).

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Number of Instances | **48,842** |
| Number of Attributes | **14** (6 continuous, 8 categorical) |
| Target Variable | `income` (binary) |
| Missing Values | Present as `'?'` in workclass, occupation, native-country |
| Class Distribution | Imbalanced (~76% `<=50K`, ~24% `>50K`) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Raw Vegetable" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you receive a basket of vegetables from a farm (your raw dataset). Before cooking (training an ML model), you must:
> 1. **Wash** the vegetables (remove noise, fix typos like `?`),
> 2. **Cut off rotten parts** (handle outliers and missing values),
> 3. **Peel and slice uniformly** (normalize and scale),
> 4. **Discard the parts you don't need** (dimensionality reduction).
>
> Data preprocessing is exactly this — a culinary preparation before the real cooking (modeling) begins.

### 1.4 The Preprocessing Pipeline (Top-Level View)

A *pipeline* is a sequence of transformations chained together so that raw input flows through and emerges as clean, model-ready data. The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates demonstrating this pipeline on the Adult dataset.

```
Raw Adult Data → [Missing Value Handler] → [Outlier Detector] → 
[Noise Smoother] → [Encoder] → [Scaler/Normalizer] → 
[Dimensionality Reducer] → Clean Model-Ready Data
```

### 1.5 Taxonomy of Data Quality Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus Point:** Examiners frequently ask students to *classify* data quality issues. Memorize these four categories:
> - **Missing Data** — absent values (NaN, None, `'?'`)
> - **Noisy Data** — random error or variance in measured variables
> - **Outliers** — values that deviate drastically from the distribution
> - **Inconsistent Data** — contradictions (e.g., age = -5, salary = "negative")

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Handling Missing Values

Missingness is classified by **Rubin (1976)** into three mathematically distinct mechanisms, and KTU board questions frequently test this classification.

### 2.1.1 Missingness Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Symbol | Meaning | Example in Adult Dataset |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Missing Completely At Random** | MCAR | Probability of missingness is independent of any data | A row's `workclass` got corrupted during CSV export |
| **Missing At Random** | MAR | Missingness depends on *observed* features | Women (observed `sex = Female`) less likely to report `occupation` |
| **Missing Not At Random** | MNAR | Missingness depends on the *missing value itself* | High earners refusing to disclose `income` |

### 2.1.2 Missing Value Treatment Strategies

1. **Deletion Methods**
   - *Listwise Deletion:* Drop any row containing a missing value.
   - *Pairwise Deletion:* Use available values for each specific computation.
2. **Simple Imputation**
   - Mean / Median / Mode replacement.
   - Constant value imputation (e.g., `'Unknown'` for categorical).
3. **Advanced Imputation**
   - **KNN Imputer:** Replace missing value with the average of the $k$ nearest neighbors.
   - **Iterative Imputer (MICE):** Model each feature with missing values as a function of all other features iteratively.
   - **Regression Imputation:** Predict the missing value using a regression model.

### 2.1.3 Statistical Test for MCAR — Little's Test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Little's chi-square test checks if missingness is MCAR. The test statistic is:
>
> $$\chi^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{p} n_j (\bar{y}_{j,\text{obs}} - \bar{y})^T \Sigma_{j,\text{obs}}^{-1} (\bar{y}_{j,\text{obs}} - \bar{y})$$
>
> where $n_j$ is the number of complete cases under pattern $j$, $\bar{y}_{j,\text{obs}}$ is the mean of observed variables, and $\bar{y}$ is the overall mean.

## 2.2 Outlier Detection

### 2.2.1 Statistical Methods

**A. Z-Score Method** — For Gaussian-distributed data:

$$Z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$$

A data point is flagged as an outlier if $\vert Z_i \vert > 3$ (the 3-sigma rule, covering **99.7%** of data).

**B. Modified Z-Score (using Median Absolute Deviation):**

$$M_i = \frac{0.6745 \cdot (x_i - \tilde{x})}{\text{MAD}}$$

where $\tilde{x}$ is the median and MAD is the Median Absolute Deviation. Robust to outliers themselves.

**C. Interquartile Range (IQR) Method:**

$$\text{Lower Fence} = Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$$

$$\text{Upper Fence} = Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$$

Any $x_i$ outside $[\text{Lower Fence}, \text{Upper Fence}]$ is an outlier.

### 2.2.2 Distance and Density-Based Methods

- **Local Outlier Factor (LOF):** Measures the local density deviation of a data point with respect to its neighbors. A score $> 1$ indicates an outlier.
- **Isolation Forest:** Isolates observations by randomly selecting features and split values. Outliers are isolated faster (shorter path lengths).
- **DBSCAN:** Points not reachable from any core point are labeled as noise (outliers).

## 2.3 Noise Reduction

> [!NOTE]
> **Noise** is *random error* in a measured variable — distinct from outliers, which can be legitimate extreme values.

Common smoothing techniques:

- **Binning:** Sort data, partition into bins, replace each bin with its mean, median, or boundary.
- **Regression Smoothing:** Fit a regression line and replace values with predicted values.
- **Clustering:** Detect and remove values lying in sparse clusters.
- **Moving Average Filter:** Apply a sliding window of size $k$:
$$
\hat{x}_t = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=t-k+1}^{t} x_i
$$

## 2.4 Dimensionality Reduction

### 2.4.1 Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

PCA is the **workhorse** of linear dimensionality reduction and the most-tested topic in KTU board exams for this module.

**Objective:** Project data onto orthogonal axes (principal components) that maximize variance.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

Given a centered data matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ with covariance matrix $\Sigma$:

$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X^T X$$

Find the eigenvectors $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_k$ of $\Sigma$ corresponding to the $k$ largest eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_k$.

The projected (reduced) data is:

$$Z = X W_k$$

where $W_k = [w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_k]$.

**Variance Explained Ratio:**

$$\text{Variance Explained}_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{p} \lambda_j}$$

### 2.4.2 Other Dimensionality Reduction Techniques

| Method | Type | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **LDA** | Supervised Linear | Classification, class separability |
| **t-SNE** | Non-linear | Visualization (2D/3D) |
| **UMAP** | Non-linear | Visualization + preserves global structure |
| **Autoencoders** | Non-linear | Large feature sets, deep learning |
| **Feature Selection (Filter/Wrapper/Embedded)** | Subset selection | Interpretability preservation |

## 2.5 Data Transformation and Normalization

### 2.5.1 Min-Max Normalization (Rescaling)

$$x_{\text{norm}} = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

Resulting range: $[0, 1]$. Sensitive to outliers.

### 2.5.2 Z-Score Standardization

$$x_{\text{std}} = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Resulting distribution: $\mu = 0$, $\sigma = 1$. Preferred for PCA, SVM, neural networks.

### 2.5.3 Robust Scaler

$$x_{\text{robust}} = \frac{x - \tilde{x}}{\text{IQR}}$$

Uses median and IQR — robust to outliers.

### 2.5.4 Log Transformation

$$x_{\text{log}} = \log(x + 1)$$

Used for right-skewed data (e.g., `capital-gain` in the Adult dataset is heavily right-skewed).

### 2.5.5 Box-Cox Transformation

$$x^{(\lambda)} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{x^\lambda - 1}{\lambda} & \text{if } \lambda \neq 0 \\ \ln(x) & \text{if } \lambda = 0 \end{cases}$$

Requires $x > 0$. Optimal $\lambda$ is found via maximum likelihood.

### 2.5.6 Categorical Encoding

- **Label Encoding:** Map categories to integers (introduces ordinality — avoid for nominal data).
- **One-Hot Encoding:** Create $k$ binary columns for $k$ categories.
- **Target Encoding:** Replace category with the mean of the target variable for that category.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas appear in 80% of KTU board questions on this module.**

| Concept | Formula | Range / Output |
|---|---|---|
| Z-Score | $Z = (x - \mu) / \sigma$ | $\vert Z \vert > 3 \Rightarrow$ outlier |
| IQR Bounds | $[Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR,\; Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR]$ | Fence for outliers |
| Min-Max Scaling | $(x - x_{\min}) / (x_{\max} - x_{\min})$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Z-Score Standardization | $(x - \mu) / \sigma$ | $\mu = 0,\; \sigma = 1$ |
| Robust Scaler | $(x - \tilde{x}) / IQR$ | Robust to outliers |
| Log Transform | $\log(x + 1)$ | Reduces right skew |
| PCA Reconstruction | $Z = X W_k$ | $Z \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ |
| Variance Explained | $\lambda_i / \sum \lambda_j$ | Cumulative sum threshold $\geq 0.95$ |
| LOF (k-distance) | $\text{lrd}_k(A) / \text{lrd}_k(B)$ | $\text{LOF} > 1 \Rightarrow$ outlier |
| Box-Cox | $(x^\lambda - 1)/\lambda$ | $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$, $x > 0$ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** Cleaning EHR (Electronic Health Record) data before diagnosis models.
- **Finance:** Outlier detection in credit-card fraud detection.
- **IoT:** Smoothing sensor noise in industrial monitoring.
- **NLP:** Normalizing text embeddings before transformer fine-tuning.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 End-to-End Pipeline for the Adult Dataset — Mathematical Walkthrough

We now derive, step by step, the mathematical operations that govern a complete preprocessing pipeline on the Adult dataset. Each step is shown in full algebraic detail — no skipping.

### Step 1 — Load and Inspect the Dataset

Suppose we load the Adult dataset and obtain a matrix $D \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ where $n = 48{,}842$ and $p = 14$. We first count missing entries (denoted by `'?'`):

$$N_{\text{miss}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mathbb{1}\{D_{ij} = {`?'}\}$$

For the Adult dataset, it is known that $N_{\text{miss}} = 4{,}265$ missing entries distributed as:
- `workclass` $\rightarrow$ 1,836
- `occupation` $\rightarrow$ 1,843
- `native-country` $\rightarrow$ 583

### Step 2 — Replace '?' with NaN and Decide Imputation

The numerical column subset (after dropping `income` and the string columns) is:

$$X = [\, \text{age},\; \text{fnlwgt},\; \text{education-num},\; \text{capital-gain},\; \text{capital-loss},\; \text{hours-per-week} \,]$$

For a continuous column $X_j$ with $m$ missing values, the **mean imputation** replaces them with:

$$
\hat{X}_{j,\text{imputed}} = \frac{1}{n - m} \sum_{i: X_{ij} \neq \text{NaN}} X_{ij}
$$

For the categorical column `workclass`, **mode imputation** is used:

$$
\hat{X}_{\text{mode}} = \arg\max_{c \in \text{workclass}} \text{count}(X = c)
$$

In Adult, $\text{mode} = \text{"Private"}$ with frequency $\approx 33{,}156$.

### Step 3 — Outlier Detection via IQR (worked example for `capital-gain`)

The `capital-gain` column is heavily right-skewed (most values are 0, a few are > 99,999).

**Step 3a:** Compute $Q_1$ and $Q_3$:

$$
Q_1 = \text{percentile}(X_{\text{cg}},\; 25), \quad Q_3 = \text{percentile}(X_{\text{cg}},\; 75)
$$

For the Adult dataset, $Q_1 = 0$ and $Q_3 = 0$ (since the 25th and 75th percentiles of `capital-gain` are both 0). Thus:

$$
\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 0
$$

**Step 3b:** Compute fences:

$$
\text{Lower} = Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} = 0, \quad \text{Upper} = Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} = 0
$$

**Step 3c:** Every $X_{ij} > 0$ is flagged as an outlier — i.e., essentially all non-zero capital-gain values. This motivates a **log transformation** instead of deletion.

### Step 4 — Log Transformation to Reduce Skew

Apply $x' = \log(x + 1)$ to `capital-gain`:

$$
X'_{\text{cg}, i} = \log(X_{\text{cg}, i} + 1)
$$

This compresses extreme values and brings the distribution closer to Gaussian. The skewness drops from $\approx 11.95$ to $\approx 3.0$ (closer to 0).

### Step 5 — One-Hot Encode Categorical Variables

For a categorical column with $k$ distinct values, one-hot encoding produces $k$ binary columns. For the `workclass` column with $k = 8$ values $\{$Private, Self-emp-not-inc, Self-emp-inc, Federal-gov, Local-gov, State-gov, Without-pay, Never-worked$\}$:

$$
\text{OneHot}(X_i) = [\,\mathbb{1}\{X_i = c_1\},\; \mathbb{1}\{X_i = c_2\},\; \ldots,\; \mathbb{1}\{X_i = c_k\}\,]
$$

Total new columns added: $8 + 14 + 41 + 6 + 5 + 2 = 76$ binary columns (typical for Adult).

### Step 6 — Standardization via Z-Score

For each numerical column $X_j$, compute:

$$
\mu_j = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_{ij}, \quad \sigma_j = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_{ij} - \mu_j)^2}
$$

Then standardize:

$$
Z_{ij} = \frac{X_{ij} - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}
$$

Result: each standardized column has $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma = 1$.

### Step 7 — PCA Dimensionality Reduction

After standardization, compute the covariance matrix:

$$
\Sigma = \frac{1}{n - 1} Z^T Z \in \mathbb{R}^{p' \times p'}
$$

where $p'$ is the post-encoding feature count (~82). Compute the eigendecomposition:

$$
\Sigma = V \Lambda V^T
$$

where $V$ contains the eigenvectors (principal components) and $\Lambda$ is a diagonal matrix of eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_{p'}$.

Select $k$ components such that:

$$
\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{p'} \lambda_i} \geq 0.95
$$

For Adult, this typically yields $k \approx 50$ components retaining 95% of variance.

Project data:

$$
Z_{\text{pca}} = Z V_k \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}
$$

### Step 8 — Final Model-Ready Tensor

$$
D_{\text{final}} = Z_{\text{pca}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}
$$

This is the input to any downstream classifier (Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, etc.).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Technique Selection Guide

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This decision matrix is a high-yield KTU exam artifact.** Examiners often ask: "Given dataset X, which technique would you apply and why?"

| Issue Detected | Recommended Technique | Why |
|---|---|---|
| $< 5\%$ missing, MCAR | Listwise deletion | Safe; minimal info loss |
| $5\text{–}20\%$ missing, MAR | KNN or Iterative Imputer | Preserves relationships |
| $> 20\%$ missing | Mode for categorical, median for numerical | Avoids overfitting the imputer |
| Gaussian numeric, few outliers | Z-score (3-sigma) | Simple, classical |
| Heavy outliers present | Robust Scaler / MAD | Resistant to outliers |
| Skewed numeric (e.g., capital-gain) | Log or Box-Cox transform | Restores Gaussianity |
| High cardinality categorical | Target encoding | Avoids curse of dimensionality |
| Many correlated features | PCA | Removes redundancy |
| Need visualization | t-SNE or UMAP | Non-linear structure preservation |
| Small dataset, many features | LDA (supervised) | Maximizes class separability |

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Data Preprocessing Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Adult Dataset n=48842, p=14] --> B[Missing Value Detection symbol question mark]
    B --> C{Numerical or Categorical}
    C -->|Numerical| D[Median Imputation]
    C -->|Categorical| E[Mode Imputation]
    D --> F[Outlier Detection using IQR and Z-Score]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Severe Skew Detected}
    G -->|Yes| H[Log or Box-Cox Transform]
    G -->|No| I[Keep Original Distribution]
    H --> J[One-Hot Encoding of Categoricals]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Standardization using Z-Score]
    K --> L[Correlation Analysis]
    L --> M{High Multicollinearity}
    M -->|Yes| N[Apply PCA with k components variance ge 0.95]
    M -->|No| O[Retain All Features]
    N --> P[Final Model-Ready Tensor]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Downstream ML Model LR, RF, XGBoost]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — PCA Internals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Standardized Matrix Z] --> covStep[Compute Covariance Matrix sigma equals 1 over n minus 1 times Z transpose Z]
    covStep --> eigenStep[Eigendecomposition sigma equals V Lambda V transpose]
    eigenStep --> sortStep[Sort Eigenvalues descending lambda 1 to lambda p]
    sortStep --> chooseStep[Select top k components variance explained ge 0.95]
    chooseStep --> projectStep[Project Z reduced equals Z times V sub k]
    projectStep --> outputA[Reduced Matrix Z reduced in real n by k]
```

## 4.3 Outlier Detection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startX[Input Numeric Column X] --> distCheck{Is X Gaussian}
    distCheck -->|Yes| zPath[Apply Z-Score threshold 3 sigma]
    distCheck -->|No| iqrPath[Apply IQR Method fences Q1 minus 1.5 IQR and Q3 plus 1.5 IQR]
    zPath --> zDecision{abs Z greater than 3}
    zDecision -->|Yes| out1[Flag as Outlier]
    zDecision -->|No| keep1[Keep as Normal]
    iqrPath --> iqrDecision{X outside fences}
    iqrDecision -->|Yes| out2[Flag as Outlier]
    iqrDecision -->|No| keep2[Keep as Normal]
    out1 --> treat[Apply Treatment: Cap, Remove, or Transform]
    out2 --> treat
```

## 4.4 Data Quality Diagnostic Sub-System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawInput[Raw Adult Dataset] --> m1[Missing Value Module]
    rawInput --> o1[Outlier Detection Module]
    rawInput --> n1[Noise Detection Module]
    rawInput --> c1[Consistency Check Module]
    m1 --> report[Unified Quality Report]
    o1 --> report
    n1 --> report
    c1 --> report
    report --> pipeline[Preprocessing Pipeline Dispatcher]
    pipeline --> finalData[Cleaned Output]
```

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Define data preprocessing. List and briefly explain any three techniques used in data preprocessing.*

**Model Answer:**

Data preprocessing is the process of transforming raw data into a clean, structured, and analysis-ready format suitable for machine learning algorithms. It addresses data quality issues such as missing values, noise, outliers, and inconsistent formats.

**Three key techniques:**

1. **Missing Value Imputation:** Replacing missing entries with statistical estimates (mean, median, mode) or model-based predictions (KNN, MICE). For example, in the Adult dataset, `workclass` missing values are filled with `"Private"` (the mode).

2. **Normalization / Standardization:** Scaling features to a common range. Min-Max scaling compresses values to $[0, 1]$, while Z-score standardization yields $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma = 1$. Critical for distance-based algorithms (KNN, SVM, PCA).

3. **Dimensionality Reduction:** Reducing the number of features while preserving information. PCA projects data onto orthogonal axes (principal components) that capture maximum variance, e.g., reducing the Adult dataset from ~82 features to ~50 components while retaining 95% variance.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
*Differentiate between Z-score normalization and Min-Max normalization. State one scenario where each is preferred.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Z-Score Standardization | Min-Max Normalization |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $(x - \mu) / \sigma$ | $(x - x_{\min}) / (x_{\max} - x_{\min})$ |
| Output Range | Unbounded (typically $[-3, 3]$) | Bounded $[0, 1]$ |
| Sensitivity to Outliers | Lower (uses mean and std) | Higher (uses min and max) |
| Distribution Assumed | Approximately Gaussian | None |

**Preferred scenarios:**
- **Z-score preferred** for PCA, logistic regression, SVM, neural networks — when features follow an approximately Gaussian distribution and downstream algorithms assume standardized input.
- **Min-Max preferred** for image processing (pixel values to $[0, 1]$), neural network activation inputs, and when a strict bounded range is required.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 1 Full Question]**
*Consider the UCI Adult Data Set with 14 attributes and the target variable `income`. Design a complete data preprocessing pipeline for this dataset. Your answer should cover:*
*(a) Identification and handling of missing values, outliers, and noisy data. (7 marks)*
*(b) Application of dimensionality reduction and data transformation/normalization techniques. (7 marks)*

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Handling Missing Values, Outliers, and Noise (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify Missing Values:**
The Adult dataset contains 4,265 missing entries encoded as `'?'` in three columns:
- `workclass` (1,836 missing)
- `occupation` (1,843 missing)
- `native-country` (583 missing)

**Step 2 — Imputation Strategy:**

$$
\hat{x}_{\text{numerical}} = \text{median}(X_{\text{obs}}), \quad \hat{x}_{\text{categorical}} = \text{mode}(X_{\text{obs}})
$$

For numerical columns, median imputation is used (robust to skew). For categorical columns, mode imputation is used — `"Private"` for `workclass`, `"Prof-specialty"` for `occupation`, `"United-States"` for `native-country`.

**Step 3 — Outlier Detection:**
Apply IQR method to numerical columns. For `capital-gain`, IQR = 0 (since 91.7% of values are 0). All non-zero values are flagged. Apply IQR for `hours-per-week`:
- $Q_1 = 40, Q_3 = 45, \text{IQR} = 5$
- Upper fence $= 45 + 1.5 \cdot 5 = 52.5$
- Lower fence $= 40 - 1.5 \cdot 5 = 32.5$

Values outside $[32.5, 52.5]$ are outliers (approximately 35% of the column).

**Step 4 — Outlier Treatment:**
Apply **Winsorization** — cap values at the 5th and 95th percentiles:

$$
x'_i = \begin{cases} P_{95} & \text{if } x_i > P_{95} \\ P_5 & \text{if } x_i < P_5 \\ x_i & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Noise Reduction:**
Apply **binning** to `age` (discretize into 5 bins: Young, Adult, Middle-aged, Senior, Old). This smooths out micro-variations in age data.

---

#### Part (b) — Dimensionality Reduction and Transformation (7 Marks)

**Step 6 — Categorical Encoding:**
Apply **One-Hot Encoding** to all 8 categorical columns, producing ~76 binary features. Total feature count becomes ~82 (76 binary + 6 numerical).

**Step 7 — Standardization:**
Apply Z-score to all numerical columns:

$$
Z_j = \frac{X_j - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}
$$

This ensures all features have $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma = 1$, a prerequisite for PCA.

**Step 8 — PCA Application:**
Compute covariance matrix $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} Z^T Z$, then eigendecompose. Sort eigenvalues and select $k$ components where:

$$
\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{p'} \lambda_i} \geq 0.95
$$

For Adult, this typically gives $k \approx 50$ components.

**Step 9 — Log Transformation for Skewed Features:**
Apply $\log(x+1)$ to `capital-gain` and `capital-loss` *before* standardization. This restores approximate normality and reduces the influence of extreme high values.

**Step 10 — Final Model-Ready Output:**
The clean dataset $D_{\text{final}} \in \mathbb{R}^{48{,}842 \times 50}$ is now ready for classifiers (Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost).

**Expected Performance:** With proper preprocessing, Logistic Regression achieves ~85% accuracy on the Adult income prediction task; XGBoost reaches ~87%.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative Long Answer)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
*(a) Explain the three mechanisms of missing data (MCAR, MAR, MNAR) with one example each from the Adult dataset. Discuss the appropriate imputation strategy for each. (7 marks)*
*(b) Compare PCA, LDA, and t-SNE as dimensionality reduction techniques. Apply PCA mathematically to a 3×3 correlation matrix of the Adult dataset features `age`, `hours-per-week`, and `education-num` and show that at least one principal component is sufficient. (7 marks)*

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Missing Data Mechanisms (7 Marks)

**MCAR (Missing Completely At Random):**
The probability of a value being missing is independent of all observed and unobserved data.
*Adult Example:* A random hardware failure during data archival corrupts 100 random `workclass` entries. No pattern with age, gender, or income.
*Strategy:* Listwise deletion is unbiased.

**MAR (Missing At Random):**
The probability of missingness depends only on *observed* data.
*Adult Example:* Among respondents with `education = Preschool`, `occupation` is more frequently missing (perhaps due to early-career retirees who never worked). Missingness depends on observed `education`.
*Strategy:* Use regression imputation or MICE, conditioning on `education`.

**MNAR (Missing Not At Random):**
The probability of missingness depends on the missing value itself.
*Adult Example:* High-income individuals (`income > 100K`) deliberately skip `capital-gain` reporting. The missingness mechanism is the value being withheld.
*Strategy:* Requires domain modeling, sensitivity analysis, or pattern-mixture models.

**Summary Decision Matrix:**

| Mechanism | Test | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| MCAR | Little's test (p > 0.05) | Deletion or simple imputation |
| MAR | Compare missing vs. observed groups | MICE, regression imputation |
| MNAR | Domain reasoning | Pattern-mixture models |

---

#### Part (b) — Dimensionality Reduction Comparison and PCA Computation (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | PCA | LDA | t-SNE |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Unsupervised | Supervised | Unsupervised |
| Linearity | Linear | Linear | Non-linear |
| Objective | Max variance | Max class separability | Preserve local neighborhoods |
| Output | $k$ components | $C - 1$ components | 2D/3D for visualization |
| Use Case | Pre-ML, denoising | Classification pipeline | Visualization only |

**Mathematical PCA Walkthrough on 3×3 Correlation Matrix:**

Given the correlation matrix $\Sigma$ of `age`, `hours-per-week`, `education-num`:

$$
\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 1.000 & 0.0686 & 0.0655 \\ 0.0686 & 1.000 & 0.1481 \\ 0.0655 & 0.1481 & 1.000 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Solve the characteristic equation $\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = 0$:

$$
\det \begin{pmatrix} 1 - \lambda & 0.0686 & 0.0655 \\ 0.0686 & 1 - \lambda & 0.1481 \\ 0.0655 & 0.1481 & 1 - \lambda \end{pmatrix} = 0
$$

Expanding yields a cubic with approximate roots:

$$
\lambda_1 \approx 1.170, \quad \lambda_2 \approx 0.918, \quad \lambda_3 \approx 0.912
$$

Total variance $= 1.170 + 0.918 + 0.912 = 3.000$.

**Variance explained by PC1:**

$$
\frac{\lambda_1}{\sum \lambda_i} = \frac{1.170}{3.000} \approx 0.390
$$

Since the three features are weakly correlated, no single component dominates — we need at least 2 components (cumulative $\approx 0.696$) and likely all 3 to capture $\geq 95\%$ variance. Therefore, in this specific submatrix, **PCA does not reduce dimensionality effectively** — an excellent KTU board exam observation that demonstrates deep understanding.

**Conclusion:** PCA's reduction power depends on feature correlation. Strongly correlated features yield greater compression.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Pitfalls (read carefully!):**
> 1. **Confusing "noise" with "outliers":** Noise is *random measurement error*; outliers may be *legitimate extremes*. Examiners specifically check this distinction — losing 2 marks for conflating the two.
> 2. **Forgetting to standardize before PCA:** If you skip Z-score standardization, features with larger scales (like `fnlwgt` ≈ 100,000s) will dominate the principal components. This is a 3-mark deduction in most valuation schemes.
> 3. **Using mean imputation on skewed data:** The `capital-gain` column is heavily right-skewed (skewness ≈ 11.95). Use **median** or **log-transform first**. Examiners mark 2 marks for this.
> 4. **Mixing MCAR with MAR:** MCAR means missingness is independent of *all* data; MAR means it depends on *observed* data. The distinction is a 3-mark question by itself.
> 5. **Applying PCA on non-standardized data:** Always center (subtract mean) and ideally scale (divide by std) before PCA. This single step is worth 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- ✅ **Preprocessing consumes 70–80% of project time** — emphasize this in 14-mark questions.
- ✅ **Adult dataset has 48,842 rows and 14 features** — these are KTU-favorite numbers.
- ✅ **Missing values are encoded as `'?'`** — must replace with NaN before processing.
- ✅ **Three missingness mechanisms:** MCAR, MAR, MNAR (Rubin's classification).
- ✅ **IQR formula:** $[Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR,\; Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR]$.
- ✅ **Z-score outlier rule:** $\vert Z \vert > 3$ (or $\vert Z \vert > 2.5$ in stricter domains).
- ✅ **Min-Max scaling formula:** $(x - x_{\min}) / (x_{\max} - x_{\min})$ — output range $[0, 1]$.
- ✅ **Z-score standardization formula:** $(x - \mu) / \sigma$ — output $\mu = 0,\; \sigma = 1$.
- ✅ **PCA steps:** center → covariance → eigendecompose → sort eigenvalues → select $k$ → project.
- ✅ **Variance explained threshold:** typically $\geq 0.95$ for retaining components.
- ✅ **Log transform for right-skewed data:** $x' = \log(x + 1)$.
- ✅ **One-hot encoding creates $k$ binary columns** for $k$ categories — beware of curse of dimensionality.
- ✅ **Always standardize before PCA and distance-based algorithms** — frequent 3-mark trap.
- ✅ **Box-Cox requires $x > 0$** — apply only to positive numerical features.
- ✅ **Outliers ≠ Noise** — outliers can be valid extremes; noise is random measurement error.
- ✅ **PCA is unsupervised, LDA is supervised** — common 2-mark conceptual question.
- ✅ **t-SNE is for visualization only** — never use as preprocessing for downstream ML models.
- ✅ **Robust Scaler uses median and IQR** — preferred when outliers are present.
- ✅ **Winsorization caps at percentiles** — alternative to deletion for outliers.
- ✅ **KNN Imputer** uses $k$ nearest neighbors to estimate missing values.
- ✅ **Isolation Forest** uses random splits — outliers have shorter path lengths.
- ✅ **Cumulative variance formula:** $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i / \sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algorithms for Data Summarization and Visualization: Data Summarization Techniques - Central tendency measures (mean, median, mode)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST785 - ALGORITHMS FOR DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Algorithms for Data Summarization and Visualization: Data Summarization Techniques - Central tendency measures (mean, median, mode)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Data Summarization?

**Data Summarization** is the computational process of condensing a large, complex, and often high-dimensional raw dataset into a concise, meaningful, and interpretable representation that retains the essential statistical and structural characteristics of the original data. It is a foundational preprocessing step in the **Data Science Lifecycle**, sitting between raw data ingestion and downstream tasks like visualization, modeling, and inference.

In the context of **PECST785 – Algorithms for Data Science**, data summarization techniques are classified into two broad families:

1. **Descriptive Statistical Summaries** — central tendency (mean, median, mode), dispersion (variance, standard deviation, IQR), skewness, and kurtosis.
2. **Structural/Algorithmic Summaries** — histograms, frequency tables, data cubes, clustering prototypes, sampling, and dimensionality-reduction embeddings.

This note focuses exclusively on **Central Tendency Measures**, which aim to identify a *single representative value* that best describes the center of a distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Central tendency measures form the first evaluated sub-topic under "Data Summarization Techniques". Students must master all three measures, their mathematical derivations, and their applicability to ungrouped versus grouped data.

### 1.2 Central Tendency — Formal Definition

**Central Tendency** is defined as the statistical measure that identifies a **single value as the representative of an entire distribution of data**. It attempts to provide an accurate description of the *center* of the data, around which most observations cluster. The three principal measures, in order of mathematical simplicity and historical precedence, are:

| Measure | Symbol | Intuitive Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Arithmetic Mean** | $\bar{x}$ or $\mu$ | The *balance point* of the data |
| **Median** | $\tilde{x}$ or $M$ | The *positional middle* of the data |
| **Mode** | $\hat{x}$ or $Mo$ | The *most frequent* observation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three measures?** No single central tendency measure is universally optimal. The *mean* is sensitive to outliers, the *median* ignores magnitude, and the *mode* may not exist or be unique. Choosing the right measure is a core data-science decision.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Class Photograph" Intuition

Imagine you are the class teacher and asked to describe the **"typical height"** of your 60 students to a parent on Parent-Teacher Day.

- If you say **"the average height is 165 cm"** — you are using the **MEAN** (you added all heights and divided by 60).
- If you sort every student in a line, shortest to tallest, and point to the **30th or 31st student** (the exact middle) and say *"this is the typical height"* — you are using the **MEDIAN**.
- If you notice that **170 cm is the most common height** (15 students share it) and announce *"most students are around 170 cm"* — you are using the **MODE**.

Each statement is valid. Each describes the *center* differently. The mean is mathematically elegant but dominated by extremes (one basketball player can shift it). The median is robust to outliers but ignores magnitude (a 6-foot student and a 5-foot student both shift it minimally). The mode captures popularity but may not be unique.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a question says *"the data has extreme values / outliers / is skewed"*, your answer must justify the **median** as the better measure. When data is symmetric and outlier-free, **mean** is preferred. When data is categorical or nominal, **mode** is the only valid choice.

### 1.4 Classification of Data for Central Tendency Computation

Before computing any measure, the data must be classified:

1. **Ungrouped (Raw) Data** — Individual observations listed directly: $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$.
2. **Grouped Data** — Observations organized into class intervals with frequencies (Frequency Distribution Table).
3. **Discrete Data** — Finite or countable values; mode is well-defined.
4. **Continuous Data** — Values form a continuum; mode is defined via modal class.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Applying raw-data formulas (e.g., $\bar{x} = \sum x_i / n$) to a frequency distribution yields incorrect results. Always check whether the data is grouped before choosing the formula.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Measures — Step-by-Step Logic

#### A. Arithmetic Mean (AM)

**Operational Definition:** The arithmetic mean of $n$ observations is the sum of all observations divided by the number of observations.

**Logical Steps:**

1. **Collect** all $n$ observations: $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$.
2. **Aggregate** the values using summation: $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$.
3. **Normalize** by dividing by the count: $\bar{x} = S / n$.
4. For **frequency-distributed data**, weight each value by its frequency: $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i}$ where $k$ is the number of distinct classes and $f_i$ is the frequency of class $i$.
5. For **grouped data with class intervals**, use the **class midpoint** $m_i = \frac{L_i + U_i}{2}$ as a proxy for $x_i$, where $L_i$ and $U_i$ are the lower and upper class limits.

**Properties of Arithmetic Mean:**
- **Algebraic Properties:**
  * $\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$ (deviations sum to zero).
  * $\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ is **minimized** at the mean (Least Squares property).
  * If every observation is shifted by a constant $c$, the mean shifts by $c$: $\bar{x}_{new} = \bar{x} + c$.
  * If every observation is scaled by a constant $k$, the mean scales by $k$: $\bar{x}_{new} = k \cdot \bar{x}$.
- **Mathematical Properties:**
  * $AM \geq GM \geq HM$ (the AM-GM-HM Inequality).
  * For two positive numbers $a$ and $b$: $AM \cdot HM = GM^2$.
- **Limitation:** Highly sensitive to **outliers** and **extreme values**. A single anomalous observation can substantially distort the mean.

#### B. Median

**Operational Definition:** The median is the **middle value** of an ordered dataset, or the average of the two middle values when the dataset size is even.

**Logical Steps (Ungrouped Data):**

1. **Sort** the $n$ observations in ascending order.
2. **Compute** the position of the median:
   * If $n$ is **odd**, the median position is $\frac{n+1}{2}$. The median is the value at this position.
   * If $n$ is **even**, the median is the **arithmetic mean** of the values at positions $\frac{n}{2}$ and $\frac{n}{2} + 1$.

**Logical Steps (Grouped Data):**

1. Compute **cumulative frequencies** $cf_i$.
2. Identify the **median class** — the first class whose cumulative frequency is $\geq n/2$.
3. Apply the **interpolation formula** using the lower limit, cumulative frequency before the median class, frequency of the median class, and class width.

**Properties of Median:**
- **Robustness:** Insensitive to outliers (only rank-order matters, not magnitudes).
- **Uniqueness:** Always unique for any dataset.
- **Algebraic Property:** The sum of absolute deviations $\sum \vert x_i - M \vert$ is **minimized** at the median.
- **Limitation:** Ignores the actual magnitudes of extreme observations; computationally more expensive due to the sorting requirement.

#### C. Mode

**Operational Definition:** The mode is the value (or values) that occurs with the **highest frequency** in the dataset.

**Logical Steps (Ungrouped Data):**

1. Tally the frequency of each distinct observation.
2. Identify the value(s) with the maximum frequency.
3. **Unimodal** — exactly one mode; **Bimodal** — two modes; **Multimodal** — more than two modes; **Amodal** — no mode (when all values appear with equal frequency).

**Logical Steps (Grouped Data):**

1. Identify the **modal class** — the class with the highest frequency.
2. Apply the **King's Formula** (also called the empirical mode formula) using the frequencies of the modal class and its adjacent classes.

**Properties of Mode:**
- **Applicability:** The only central tendency measure valid for **nominal (categorical)** data.
- **Limitation:** May not exist, may not be unique, and is algebraically unstable (small data changes can dramatically shift the mode).
- **Positional Property:** In a moderately skewed unimodal distribution, $Mode \approx 3 \cdot Median - 2 \cdot Mean$ (empirical relationship).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU Board Examination essentials**. Memorize the grouped-data formulas precisely — they appear almost every semester in Part B questions.

| # | Measure | Data Type | Formula | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean | Ungrouped | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ | $n$ = total count |
| 2 | Weighted Mean | Weighted | $\bar{x}_w = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$ | $w_i$ = weight |
| 3 | Arithmetic Mean | Grouped (Frequency) | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i}$ | $f_i$ = frequency |
| 4 | Arithmetic Mean | Grouped (Class Interval) | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f_i m_i}{\sum f_i}$ | $m_i$ = class midpoint |
| 5 | Geometric Mean | Positive values | $GM = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{n} x_i \right)^{1/n}$ | useful for ratios |
| 6 | Harmonic Mean | Positive values | $HM = \dfrac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{x_i}}$ | useful for rates |
| 7 | Median (Odd $n$) | Ungrouped | $\tilde{x} = x_{\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)}$ | sorted data |
| 8 | Median (Even $n$) | Ungrouped | $\tilde{x} = \dfrac{x_{\left(\frac{n}{2}\right)} + x_{\left(\frac{n}{2}+1\right)}}{2}$ | sorted data |
| 9 | Median | Grouped | $\tilde{x} = L + \left( \dfrac{\dfrac{n}{2} - F}{f} \right) \cdot h$ | $L$=lower limit, $F$=cf of prev class, $f$=median class freq, $h$=class width |
| 10 | Mode | Grouped (King's Formula) | $\hat{x} = L + \left( \dfrac{f_1 - f_0}{2 f_1 - f_0 - f_2} \right) \cdot h$ | $f_1$=modal freq, $f_0$=prev freq, $f_2$=next freq, $L$=lower limit of modal class |
| 11 | Empirical Relation | Skewed data | $Mode \approx 3 \cdot Median - 2 \cdot Mean$ | approximation |
| 12 | AM-GM-HM Inequality | Any positive data | $AM \geq GM \geq HM$ | holds universally |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Substitution/Shortcut Formula for Mean (Grouped Data):** When class midpoints are large, use $\bar{x} = A + \left( \dfrac{\sum f_i d_i}{\sum f_i} \right) \cdot i$, where $A$ is the assumed mean, $d_i = (m_i - A)/i$, and $i$ is the class interval width. This is a **frequently-tested KTU shortcut**.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Data Science & Engineering

- **Mean** is the foundation of **ANOVA, linear regression, PCA, neural-network loss functions (MSE)**, and virtually every parametric statistical test.
- **Median** powers **robust regression, median filters in image processing, L1-loss neural networks**, and outlier-resistant KPIs like *median household income*.
- **Mode** is essential for **categorical encoding decisions, recommendation systems (most-purchased item), natural language processing (most common token)**, and image-classification majority voting.
- In **data-streaming systems**, computing an exact mean over unbounded data requires **online algorithms** (e.g., Welford's method), while median requires **order-statistic trees** or **approximate sketches** (e.g., t-digest, Q-digest).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Arithmetic Mean Minimizes the Sum of Squared Deviations

**Claim:** The arithmetic mean $\bar{x}$ is the unique value $c$ that minimizes the function $S(c) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - c)^2$.

**Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation:**

Starting from the objective function:

$$
S(c) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - c)^2
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the squared terms.**

Expanding each $(x_i - c)^2 = x_i^2 - 2 c x_i + c^2$:

$$
S(c) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 - 2 c \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + n c^2
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $S(c)$ with respect to $c$.**

Using the power rule term-by-term (the first term is constant in $c$):

$$
\frac{dS}{dc} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + 2 n c
$$

**Step 3 — Set the derivative to zero to find the critical point.**

$$
-2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + 2 n c = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $c$.**

$$
2 n c = 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n} = \bar{x}
$$

**Step 5 — Confirm it is a minimum via the second derivative.**

$$
\frac{d^2 S}{dc^2} = 2n > 0
$$

Since the second derivative is positive for all $n \geq 1$, the critical point is a **global minimum**. Thus, the arithmetic mean uniquely minimizes the sum of squared deviations. $\blacksquare$

**Interpretation:** This is precisely why **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression** uses the mean of $y$ as the prediction when no features are available — it is the *least-squares best estimate* under squared-error loss.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Median Minimizes the Sum of Absolute Deviations

**Claim:** The median $M$ minimizes the function $T(c) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - c \vert$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Differentiate $T(c)$.** Since $\frac{d}{dc}\vert x_i - c \vert = -\text{sign}(x_i - c)$ for $x_i \neq c$:

$$
\frac{dT}{dc} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sign}(x_i - c)
$$

**Step 2 — Set the derivative to zero.**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sign}(x_i - c) = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the sign function.** The sign equals $+1$ when $x_i > c$, $-1$ when $x_i < c$, and $0$ when $x_i = c$. Therefore:

$$
(\text{Number of observations} > c) - (\text{Number of observations} < c) = 0
$$

This is satisfied exactly when $c$ has **equal counts** of observations on both sides — which is precisely the **definition of the median**. $\blacksquare$

**Interpretation:** This justifies **L1-loss regression (LAD regression)** and explains why the median is robust to outliers — a single large deviation contributes only linearly to $T(c)$, whereas in squared loss it contributes quadratically.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Grouped Data (Full KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** The following table shows the marks obtained by 60 students in a Data Science examination. Compute the mean, median, and mode using the appropriate grouped-data formulas.

| Marks | Frequency ($f_i$) | Cumulative Frequency ($cf_i$) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 – 10 | 5 | 5 |
| 10 – 20 | 8 | 13 |
| 20 – 30 | 12 | 25 |
| 30 – 40 | 18 | 43 |
| 40 – 50 | 10 | 53 |
| 50 – 60 | 7 | 60 |

#### Step 1 — Construct the Computation Table

First, compute the **class midpoints** $m_i = (L_i + U_i)/2$ and the products $f_i m_i$:

| Marks | $f_i$ | $cf_i$ | $m_i$ | $f_i m_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 – 10 | 5 | 5 | 5 | 25 |
| 10 – 20 | 8 | 13 | 15 | 120 |
| 20 – 30 | 12 | 25 | 25 | 300 |
| 30 – 40 | 18 | 43 | 35 | 630 |
| 40 – 50 | 10 | 53 | 45 | 450 |
| 50 – 60 | 7 | 60 | 55 | 385 |
| **Total** | **60** | — | — | **1910** |

#### Step 2 — Compute the Arithmetic Mean

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i m_i}{\sum f_i} = \frac{1910}{60} = 31.8333\ldots \approx 31.83 \text{ marks}
$$

**Interpretation:** The class average is approximately 31.83 marks out of 60.

#### Step 3 — Compute the Median

- Total $n = 60$, so $n/2 = 30$.
- Identify the median class: the first class with $cf_i \geq 30$. From the table, $cf = 25$ for class 20–30 and $cf = 43$ for class 30–40. So the **median class is 30 – 40**.
- Parameters: $L = 30$, $F = 25$ (cf of previous class), $f = 18$, $h = 10$.

$$
\tilde{x} = L + \left( \frac{\frac{n}{2} - F}{f} \right) \cdot h
$$

$$
\tilde{x} = 30 + \left( \frac{30 - 25}{18} \right) \cdot 10
$$

$$
\tilde{x} = 30 + \left( \frac{5}{18} \right) \cdot 10 = 30 + 2.777\ldots \approx 32.78 \text{ marks}
$$

**Interpretation:** Half the students scored below 32.78 marks and half scored above.

#### Step 4 — Compute the Mode

- Identify the modal class: the class with the **highest frequency**. From the table, $f = 18$ is the maximum, occurring in class **30 – 40**.
- Parameters: $L = 30$, $f_1 = 18$ (modal frequency), $f_0 = 12$ (preceding class), $f_2 = 10$ (succeeding class), $h = 10$.

$$
\hat{x} = L + \left( \frac{f_1 - f_0}{2 f_1 - f_0 - f_2} \right) \cdot h
$$

$$
\hat{x} = 30 + \left( \frac{18 - 12}{2(18) - 12 - 10} \right) \cdot 10
$$

$$
\hat{x} = 30 + \left( \frac{6}{36 - 22} \right) \cdot 10
$$

$$
\hat{x} = 30 + \left( \frac{6}{14} \right) \cdot 10 = 30 + 4.2857\ldots \approx 34.29 \text{ marks}
$$

**Interpretation:** The most typical score is approximately 34.29 marks.

#### Step 5 — Cross-Validation via Empirical Relationship

$$
\text{Predicted Mode} \approx 3 \cdot Median - 2 \cdot Mean = 3(32.78) - 2(31.83) = 98.34 - 63.66 = 34.68
$$

The computed mode (34.29) is reasonably close to the empirical estimate (34.68), confirming the calculation. The small discrepancy is normal because the empirical formula is an approximation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Measure

| Criterion | Mean | Median | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Type** | Numerical (interval/ratio) | Numerical (ordinal+) | Any (incl. nominal) |
| **Uses Every Value?** | Yes | Indirectly (via rank) | No (only frequency) |
| **Affected by Outliers?** | Strongly | Minimally | Minimally |
| **Uniqueness** | Always unique | Always unique | May be multimodal |
| **Algebraic Stability** | Stable under transformations | Robust | Unstable |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ with hashing |
| **Use Case** | Symmetric, no outliers | Skewed, outlier-prone | Categorical, popularity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of Central Tendency Measures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Central Tendency Measures"]:::root --> B["Mean"]:::branch
    A --> C["Median"]:::branch
    A --> D["Mode"]:::branch

    B --> B1["Arithmetic Mean"]:::leaf
    B --> B2["Weighted Mean"]:::leaf
    B --> B3["Geometric Mean"]:::leaf
    B --> B4["Harmonic Mean"]:::leaf

    C --> C1["Ungrouped Median"]:::leaf
    C --> C2["Grouped Median via Interpolation"]:::leaf

    D --> D1["Ungrouped Mode"]:::leaf
    D --> D2["Grouped Mode via King Formula"]:::leaf
    D --> D3["Multimodal Cases"]:::leaf

    B1 --> E1["Balanced Center"]:::note
    C1 --> E2["Positional Center"]:::note
    D1 --> E3["Frequency Center"]:::note

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0d1b2a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#3d6a9c,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#a8c4e0,stroke:#3d6a9c,color:#0d1b2a,stroke-width:1px
    classDef note fill:#f4e285,stroke:#b89730,color:#0d1b2a,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.2 Algorithm Flow — Computing Grouped Mean

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start: Input frequency table"]:::start --> S2["Compute class midpoints mi"]:::proc
    S2 --> S3["Compute fi * mi for each class"]:::proc
    S3 --> S4["Sum all fi mi to get numerator"]:::proc
    S4 --> S5["Sum all fi to get denominator N"]:::proc
    S5 --> S6["Divide numerator by N"]:::proc
    S6 --> S7["Output: Arithmetic Mean"]:::end

    classDef start fill:#2e8b57,stroke:#1a5235,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#2e8b57,color:#0d1b2a,stroke-width:1px
    classDef end fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#a13838,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Which Measure to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["What is the data type?"]:::q --> Q2{"Categorical Nominal?"}:::d
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1["Use MODE"]:::ans
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Is data skewed or has outliers?"}:::d
    Q3 -- Yes --> R2["Use MEDIAN"]:::ans
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Need to use every numerical value algebraically?"}:::d
    Q4 -- Yes --> R3["Use MEAN"]:::ans
    Q4 -- No --> R4["Use MEDIAN as default robust choice"]:::ans

    classDef q fill:#ffd166,stroke:#b8860b,color:#0d1b2a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef d fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#666666,color:#0d1b2a,stroke-width:1px
    classDef ans fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#048a64,color:#0d1b2a,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Data Summarization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RAW["Raw Data Stream"]:::in --> CLEAN["Data Cleaning and Validation"]:::proc
    CLEAN --> CLASS["Classify Data Type: Ungrouped or Grouped"]:::proc
    CLASS --> CT["Central Tendency Engine"]:::engine
    CT --> CT1["Mean Module"]:::mod
    CT --> CT2["Median Module"]:::mod
    CT --> CT3["Mode Module"]:::mod
    CT1 --> OUT["Summary Output Dashboard"]:::out
    CT2 --> OUT
    CT3 --> OUT
    OUT --> VIZ["Visualization Layer: Bar Chart, Histogram, Boxplot"]:::out

    classDef in fill:#118ab2,stroke:#073b4c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#073b4c,stroke:#001219,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px

    classDef mod fill:#ffd166,stroke:#ef476f,color:#0d1b2a,stroke-width:1px
```
