# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pearson Correlation Coefficient

### Step 1 — Start with the Concept of Covariance

For two centered variables $\tilde{x}_i = x_i - \bar{x}$ and $\tilde{y}_i = y_i - \bar{y}$, we wish to measure *how much they co-vary*. The natural inner product is:

$$\text{Cov}(X, Y) = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \tilde{x}_i \, \tilde{y}_i$$

**Why divide by $n-1$, not $n$?** Because after computing $\bar{x}$ from the same sample, we lose 1 degree of freedom. The $n-1$ divisor is the **Bessel correction** that makes $s^2$ an unbiased estimator of population variance.

### Step 2 — Normalize to Eliminate Units

$\text{Cov}$ has units of $(\text{unit}_X \cdot \text{unit}_Y)$, which makes cross-comparison across feature pairs meaningless. We divide by the product of standard deviations:

$$r_{XY} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\sigma_X \, \sigma_Y} = \frac{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum \tilde{x}_i \tilde{y}_i}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum \tilde{x}_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum \tilde{y}_i^2}}$$

The $\frac{1}{n-1}$ factors cancel, leaving:

$$r_{XY} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$$

### Step 3 — Prove the Range $[-1, +1]$ via Cauchy-Schwarz

By the **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality** for real vectors $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}$:

$$\left( \sum a_i b_i \right)^2 \le \left( \sum a_i^2 \right) \left( \sum b_i^2 \right)$$

Setting $a_i = \tilde{x}_i$ and $b_i = \tilde{y}_i$:

$$\left( \sum \tilde{x}_i \tilde{y}_i \right)^2 \le \left( \sum \tilde{x}_i^2 \right) \left( \sum \tilde{y}_i^2 \right)$$

Taking square roots:

$$\left\vert \sum \tilde{x}_i \tilde{y}_i \right\vert \le \sqrt{\sum \tilde{x}_i^2 \cdot \sum \tilde{y}_i^2}$$

Therefore:

$$-1 \le r_{XY} \le +1$$

**Equality condition:** Cauchy-Schwarz becomes equality iff $\mathbf{a} = k \mathbf{b}$, i.e., $\tilde{x}_i = k \tilde{y}_i$ for some scalar $k$. This means $X$ and $Y$ are **perfectly linearly related** ($k>0 \Rightarrow r=+1$, $k<0 \Rightarrow r=-1$).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Building a Correlation Matrix

Consider a small dataset with $n = 5$ samples and 3 features:

| Sample | $X_1$ (Height cm) | $X_2$ (Weight kg) | $X_3$ (Age yr) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 160 | 55 | 25 |
| 2 | 170 | 65 | 30 |
| 3 | 165 | 60 | 28 |
| 4 | 180 | 75 | 35 |
| 5 | 175 | 70 | 40 |

### Compute Means

$$\bar{x}_1 = \frac{160+170+165+180+175}{5} = \frac{850}{5} = 170$$

$$\bar{x}_2 = \frac{55+65+60+75+70}{5} = \frac{325}{5} = 65$$

$$\bar{x}_3 = \frac{25+30+28+35+40}{5} = \frac{158}{5} = 31.6$$

### Compute Centered Values $(x_i - \bar{x})$

| Sample | $x_1 - 170$ | $x_2 - 65$ | $x_3 - 31.6$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-10$ | $-10$ | $-6.6$ |
| 2 | $0$ | $0$ | $-1.6$ |
| 3 | $-5$ | $-5$ | $-3.6$ |
| 4 | $+10$ | $+10$ | $+3.4$ |
| 5 | $+5$ | $+5$ | $+8.4$ |

### Compute Sum of Squares

$$\sum(x_1 - \bar{x}_1)^2 = (-10)^2 + 0^2 + (-5)^2 + 10^2 + 5^2 = 100+0+25+100+25 = 250$$

$$\sum(x_2 - \bar{x}_2)^2 = (-10)^2 + 0^2 + (-5)^2 + 10^2 + 5^2 = 250$$

$$\sum(x_3 - \bar{x}_3)^2 = (-6.6)^2 + (-1.6)^2 + (-3.6)^2 + (3.4)^2 + (8.4)^2$$
$$= 43.56 + 2.56 + 12.96 + 11.56 + 70.56 = 141.2$$

### Compute Cross-Products

$$\sum(x_1 - \bar{x}_1)(x_2 - \bar{x}_2) = (-10)(-10) + (0)(0) + (-5)(-5) + (10)(10) + (5)(5)$$
$$= 100 + 0 + 25 + 100 + 25 = 250$$

$$\sum(x_1 - \bar{x}_1)(x_3 - \bar{x}_3) = (-10)(-6.6) + (0)(-1.6) + (-5)(-3.6) + (10)(3.4) + (5)(8.4)$$
$$= 66 + 0 + 18 + 34 + 42 = 160$$

$$\sum(x_2 - \bar{x}_2)(x_3 - \bar{x}_3) = (-10)(-6.6) + (0)(-1.6) + (-5)(-3.6) + (10)(3.4) + (5)(8.4)$$
$$= 66 + 0 + 18 + 34 + 42 = 160$$

### Compute $r_{12}$

$$r_{12} = \frac{250}{\sqrt{250 \cdot 250}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1.000$$

**Interpretation:** Height and weight are **perfectly linearly correlated** in this sample (which is unusual but happens with this tiny dataset because height and weight move in lockstep).

### Compute $r_{13}$

$$r_{13} = \frac{160}{\sqrt{250 \cdot 141.2}} = \frac{160}{\sqrt{35300}} = \frac{160}{187.88} \approx 0.852$$

### Compute $r_{23}$

$$r_{23} = \frac{160}{\sqrt{250 \cdot 141.2}} \approx 0.852$$

### Final Correlation Matrix

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 1.000 & 1.000 & 0.852 \\ 1.000 & 1.000 & 0.852 \\ 0.852 & 0.852 & 1.000 \end{bmatrix}$$

When this matrix is rendered as a heatmap with a diverging colormap centered at 0, the off-diagonal cells appear in **strong red** (high positive correlation), warning the data scientist that **multicollinearity** is present — Height and Weight are essentially redundant features for any downstream regression model.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks *"Given a scenario, which visualization would you choose and why?"* Use this decision matrix in your answers.

| Scenario | Best Choice | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| 100+ features, need quick redundancy check | **Correlation Matrix Heatmap** | Compact, scannable, surfaces multicollinearity |
| 4–8 features, want shape of relationships | **Pair Plot** | Scatter reveals linear/non-linear/cluster patterns |
| Time × Category matrix (e.g., sales by region by month) | **Heatmap (sequential colormap)** | Reveals seasonal and regional patterns |
| Confusion matrix of 10-class classifier | **Heatmap (diverging)** | Highlights misclassification pathways |
| Detecting outliers in bivariate data | **Pair Plot with hex-bin density** | Hex-bin shows density even with 1M points |
| Gene expression across 1000 genes × 50 samples | **Clustered Heatmap** | Hierarchical clustering groups similar genes |
| Comparing 3 numeric features' distributions | **Pair Plot with KDE diagonal** | Diagonal shows shape; off-diagonal shows co-variation |
| Verifying independence assumption (ANOVA/regression) | **Pair Plot of residuals** | Residuals should be feature-independent & homoscedastic |

---

## 3.4 Statistical Significance of Correlation (Bonus — for 14-mark questions)

When asked to comment on *significance*, the test statistic is:

$$t = \frac{r \sqrt{n-2}}{\sqrt{1 - r^2}}$$

which follows a **Student's $t$-distribution** with $n-2$ degrees of freedom. The two-tailed $p$-value tests $H_0: \rho = 0$ vs. $H_1: \rho \ne 0$. For our example with $n=5, r=0.852$:

$$t = \frac{0.852 \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{1 - 0.726}} = \frac{0.852 \times 1.732}{\sqrt{0.274}} = \frac{1.476}{0.523} \approx 2.82$$

With $df = 3$, the critical $t$ at $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) is $\pm 3.182$. Since $2.82 < 3.182$, the correlation is **not statistically significant** at the 5% level for this tiny sample — a classic reminder that **small samples demand large $r$ values to achieve significance**.

## 4.4 Heatmap Color Encoding Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    low[Low Value]
    mid[Midpoint Zero for Diverging]
    high[High Value]
    cBlue[Cool Color e.g. Blue]
    cWhite[Neutral Color e.g. White]
    cRed[Warm Color e.g. Red]

    low --> cBlue
    mid --> cWhite
    high --> cRed
    cBlue --> out[Perceptual Color Encoded Cell]
    cWhite --> out
    cRed --> out
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **sequential** heatmap (e.g., gene expression intensity), use a single-hue gradient (e.g., white → dark red). For a **diverging** heatmap (e.g., correlation matrix), use a two-hue gradient with a meaningful midpoint (e.g., blue → white → red, centered at 0). The KTU examiner specifically tests whether you can justify the colormap choice.

```mermaid
    q1 -->|Yes| div
    q2 -->|Categorical| cat
    q2 -->|Numeric| num

```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define correlation matrix. Explain how it differs from a covariance matrix.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **correlation matrix** is a square symmetric matrix $R \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p}$ whose $(i, j)$-th element is the **Pearson correlation coefficient** $r_{ij}$ between features $X_i$ and $X_j$. Each diagonal element $r_{ii} = 1$, and the matrix is symmetric: $r_{ij} = r_{ji}$. **(1 mark)**

The **covariance matrix** $\Sigma$, by contrast, has elements $\text{Cov}(X_i, X_j)$ on the off-diagonal and $\text{Var}(X_i)$ on the diagonal. **(1 mark)**

**Key differences:**
- Correlation matrix is **unitless** and bounded in $[-1, +1]$; covariance matrix has units of $(\text{unit}_X \cdot \text{unit}_Y)$ and is unbounded.
- Correlation is **scale-invariant**; covariance changes with linear scaling of features.
- Correlation is preferable for **comparing relationships across heterogeneous features**; covariance is preferred in **multivariate Gaussian modeling**. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a pair plot? What information is shown on the diagonal versus the off-diagonal cells?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **pair plot** (also called a *scatter plot matrix* or *SPLOM*) is a $p \times p$ grid of subplots that visualizes the pairwise relationships among $p$ numeric features in a dataset. **(1 mark)**

- **Diagonal cells $(i, i)$** display the **univariate (marginal) distribution** of feature $X_i$ — typically a histogram, KDE curve, or density plot. They answer *"What is the shape of $X_i$ alone?"* **(1 mark)**
- **Off-diagonal cells $(i, j)$** display the **bivariate relationship** between $X_i$ (y-axis) and $X_j$ (x-axis) as a scatter plot, often with a categorical hue overlay. They answer *"How do $X_i$ and $X_j$ co-vary in shape?"* — revealing linear, non-linear, cluster, or outlier patterns that correlation coefficients alone cannot expose. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option (i) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the construction of a **correlation matrix heatmap**. Discuss the choice of colormap and the role of hierarchical clustering in its enhancement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the Pearson correlation coefficient matrix for the following dataset and interpret the result. Identify which feature pairs exhibit multicollinearity. **(7 marks)**

| Sample | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $X_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 4 | 5 |
| 2 | 4 | 6 | 7 |
| 3 | 6 | 8 | 9 |
| 4 | 8 | 10 | 11 |
| 5 | 10 | 12 | 13 |

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Correlation Matrix Heatmap Construction (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
A correlation matrix heatmap is a 2D color-encoded rendering of the matrix $R$ whose entries are pairwise Pearson correlation coefficients $r_{ij} \in [-1, +1]$.

**Step 2 — Construction Pipeline (3 marks):**
1. Standardize each feature: $Z_j = (X_j - \mu_j)/\sigma_j$.
2. For each pair $(i, j)$, compute $r_{ij}$ using the formula derived in Section 3.1.
3. Enforce symmetry: set $r_{ji} = r_{ij}$.
4. Render the matrix as a heatmap with a **diverging colormap** (e.g., *RdBu*) anchored at 0 (white), bounded at $-1$ (deep blue) and $+1$ (deep red).

**Step 3 — Colormap Justification (1 mark):**
A **diverging colormap** is essential because correlations have a meaningful midpoint (0 = no linear relationship). Two-hue gradients (blue ↔ red) emphasize the *direction* of correlation, not just its magnitude. Sequential colormaps like *viridis* are inappropriate because they would treat $r = -0.9$ (strong negative) and $r = +0.9$ (strong positive) as if they were the same "high" color.

**Step 4 — Hierarchical Clustering Enhancement (2 marks):**
Before plotting, apply **agglomerative hierarchical clustering** on the rows and columns using a distance metric $d(R_i, R_j) = 1 - r_{ij}$ (so that strongly correlated features are treated as "close"). Reorder the matrix so that similar features are grouped contiguously. This produces **clustered heatmaps** that visually group blocks of strongly correlated features — invaluable for genomics (co-expressed gene modules) and feature selection.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute means (1 mark):**
$$\bar{x}_1 = \frac{2+4+6+8+10}{5} = 6, \quad \bar{x}_2 = \frac{4+6+8+10+12}{5} = 8, \quad \bar{x}_3 = \frac{5+7+9+11+13}{5} = 9$$

**Step 2 — Compute centered values (1 mark):**

| Sample | $x_1 - 6$ | $x_2 - 8$ | $x_3 - 9$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-4$ | $-4$ | $-4$ |
| 2 | $-2$ | $-2$ | $-2$ |
| 3 | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 4 | $+2$ | $+2$ | $+2$ |
| 5 | $+4$ | $+4$ | $+4$ |

**Step 3 — Compute sums of squares and cross-products (2 marks):**
$$\sum(x_1-6)^2 = 16+4+0+4+16 = 40$$
$$\sum(x_2-8)^2 = 40 \quad (\text{identical pattern})$$
$$\sum(x_3-9)^2 = 40 \quad (\text{identical pattern})$$
$$\sum(x_1-6)(x_2-8) = 16+4+0+4+16 = 40$$
$$\sum(x_1-6)(x_3-9) = 16+4+0+4+16 = 40$$
$$\sum(x_2-8)(x_3-9) = 16+4+0+4+16 = 40$$

**Step 4 — Compute $r_{ij}$ (2 marks):**
$$r_{12} = \frac{40}{\sqrt{40 \cdot 40}} = \frac{40}{40} = 1.00$$
$$r_{13} = \frac{40}{\sqrt{40 \cdot 40}} = 1.00$$
$$r_{23} = \frac{40}{\sqrt{40 \cdot 40}} = 1.00$$

**Step 5 — Final correlation matrix and interpretation (1 mark):**

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 1.00 & 1.00 & 1.00 \\ 1.00 & 1.00 & 1.00 \\ 1.00 & 1.00 & 1.00 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Interpretation:** All three features are **perfectly linearly correlated** ($r = 1.00$). This is the textbook signature of **perfect multicollinearity** — the three columns are exact affine transformations of each other (e.g., $X_2 = X_1 + 2$, $X_3 = X_1 + 3$). In regression, this causes the design matrix $X^T X$ to be **singular** (determinant zero), and the OLE (Ordinary Least Squares) coefficients become non-unique. The data scientist must drop redundant features or use **regularization (Ridge/Lasso)** or **PCA** to proceed.

---

### Question B — Option (ii) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **heatmap** with a real-world analogy. Discuss the difference between sequential and diverging colormaps, citing an appropriate example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a suitable block diagram, describe the **pair plot** visualization technique. How does it complement the correlation matrix? Explain with a hypothetical example where the correlation matrix gives $r = 0$ but the pair plot reveals a strong non-linear relationship. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Heatmap Concept and Colormap Taxonomy (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition and Analogy (2 marks):**
A heatmap is a 2D matrix visualization where each cell $a_{ij}$ is encoded by a color drawn from a perceptual colormap. **Real-world analogy:** A meteorologist's daily weather map where each grid cell (latitude × longitude) is shaded by temperature — deep blue for cold, deep red for hot. Just as the meteorologist can instantly spot the "hot zone" in central India, the data scientist can spot hotspots in any rectangular data structure (e.g., a confusion matrix, a sales-by-region table, a gene expression matrix).

**Step 2 — Sequential Colormap (2 marks):**
A **sequential colormap** is a single-hue (or perceptually uniform) gradient that varies in lightness from low to high values, suitable for data with a natural ordering but no meaningful midpoint. **Example:** A heatmap of *page-view counts* across hours of the day × days of the week. Values range from 0 (no traffic) to 10,000 (peak), and there is no "zero means nothing special" anchor. Use *viridis* (perceptually uniform, colorblind-safe) or *YlOrRd* (yellow → orange → red, intuitive "heat").

**Step 3 — Diverging Colormap (2 marks):**
A **diverging colormap** uses two distinct hues converging at a meaningful midpoint, suitable for data that has both positive and negative values, or deviations from a baseline. **Example:** A **correlation matrix heatmap** with *RdBu* (red ↔ white ↔ blue) anchored at 0. Red cells = strong positive correlation; blue cells = strong negative correlation; white cells = no linear relationship. Another example: a heatmap of *profit-and-loss deviations* from a baseline expectation, where red = above target and blue = below target.

**Step 4 — Selection Heuristic (1 mark):**
Choose **sequential** when the data is one-sided (counts, magnitudes, intensities). Choose **diverging** when the data has a meaningful central reference (correlations, z-scores, signed errors).

---

#### Part (b) — Pair Plot and Its Complementarity to Correlation Matrix (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Pair Plot Definition with Block Diagram (3 marks):**
A pair plot is a $p \times p$ grid where:
- **Off-diagonal cell $(i, j)$** is a scatter plot of $X_j$ (x-axis) vs. $X_i$ (y-axis).
- **Diagonal cell $(i, i)$** is the marginal distribution of $X_i$ (histogram / KDE).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GRID["Pair Plot for 3 Features"]
        A11["Diag: Hist of X1"]
        A12["Scatter X1 vs X2"]
        A13["Scatter X1 vs X3"]
        A21["Scatter X2 vs X1"]
        A22["Diag: Hist of X2"]
        A23["Scatter X2 vs X3"]
        A31["Scatter X3 vs X1"]
        A32["Scatter X3 vs X2"]
        A33["Diag: Hist of X3"]
    end
```

**Step 2 — Complementarity (2 marks):**
The correlation matrix gives a **single scalar summary** $r_{ij}$ per pair — fast to scan across many features but lossy. The pair plot gives the **full joint distribution** of every pair — richer but space-inefficient. The correlation matrix is ideal as a **first-pass redundancy screen**; the pair plot is the **second-pass shape inspection** for any flagged pairs.

**Step 3 — Hypothetical Example where $r = 0$ but pair plot reveals a strong non-linear relationship (2 marks):**
Consider $X = (-3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3)$ and $Y = (9, 4, 1, 0, 1, 4, 9)$ — i.e., $Y = X^2$.

**Compute $r$:**
$$\bar{x} = 0, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{9+4+1+0+1+4+9}{7} = 4$$
$$\sum(x_i - 0)(y_i - 4) = (-3)(5) + (-2)(0) + (-1)(-3) + 0(-4) + 1(-3) + 2(0) + 3(5)$$
$$= -15 + 0 + 3 + 0 - 3 + 0 + 15 = 0$$

Therefore $r = 0$. The correlation matrix would misleadingly suggest *"no relationship"*. However, the pair plot's scatter of $(X, Y)$ reveals a **perfect U-shaped parabola** — a textbook demonstration that **Pearson $r$ only measures linear association**. The pair plot would have immediately revealed the quadratic structure. This is why **pair plots are indispensable** for non-linear EDA.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting symmetry.** The correlation matrix MUST satisfy $r_{ij} = r_{ji}$. If your computed values don't, you have an arithmetic error — recheck centered values.
> 2. **Misinterpreting $r = 0$.** A zero correlation does NOT mean "no relationship" — it means "no *linear* relationship." The pair plot catches what correlation misses. Examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for stating "$r=0$ implies independence."
> 3. **Wrong colormap for correlation.** Using *viridis* (sequential) for a correlation matrix is a half-mark deduction. Always use **diverging** with midpoint at 0.
> 4. **Skipping the formula derivation.** KTU's 14-mark question on correlation often asks for the full derivation from covariance + Cauchy-Schwarz. A bare formula without derivation caps you at 4/7 marks.
> 5. **Pair plot diagonal confusion.** Students often draw histograms on the off-diagonal. The **diagonal** is for univariate distribution; the **off-diagonal** is for bivariate scatter.
> 6. **Confusing covariance and correlation units.** Covariance has units; correlation is dimensionless. Examiners pounce on this.
> 7. **Not stating assumptions.** Pearson $r$ assumes linearity, no major outliers, and approximate bivariate normality. For non-normal data, use Spearman's $\rho$. Examiners reward awareness of assumptions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Advanced Visualization Techniques**

### Heatmap Essentials
- Heatmap = matrix → color encoding via a colormap function $C(\cdot)$
- Always **normalize** values before color mapping (min-max or z-score)
- **Sequential colormap** for one-sided data; **diverging colormap** for signed data
- Add **hierarchical clustering** for clustered heatmaps in high-dimensional contexts
- Use perceptually uniform colormaps (*viridis*) when colorblind accessibility matters

### Correlation Matrix Essentials
- $R$ is **square, symmetric, unit-diagonal** with $r_{ij} \in [-1, +1]$
- **Pearson** $r$ formula: $\dfrac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2 \sum(y_i-\bar{y})^2}}$
- **Spearman** $\rho$: rank-based, robust to outliers, captures monotonic non-linear
- **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality** is the formal proof that $\lvert r \rvert \le 1$
- **Multicollinearity** is detected when $\lvert r_{ij} \rvert > 0.8$ between feature pairs
- Correlation ≠ Causation; correlation = 0 does NOT imply independence
- Always render with a **diverging colormap** centered at 0

### Pair Plot Essentials
- $p \times p$ grid for $p$ numeric features
- **Diagonal** = marginal distribution (histogram / KDE)
- **Off-diagonal** = bivariate scatter plot
- Add **categorical hue** to reveal cluster separability across classes
- For large $N$, use **hex-bin** or **2D KDE** to avoid over-plotting
- Cap at **5–8 features** to keep the grid readable
- Pair plot reveals **shape** (linear, quadratic, cluster, outlier) that $r$ cannot

### Decision Heuristic (Memorize)
- **First pass** with many features → **Correlation heatmap** (compact, scannable)
- **Second pass** on flagged pairs → **Pair plot** (rich, shape-revealing)
- **Standalone matrix** (time × category, confusion) → **Generic heatmap**

### Key Numerical Benchmarks
- $\lvert r \rvert \ge 0.8$ → strong linear relationship
- $0.5 \le \lvert r \rvert < 0.8$ → moderate
- $0.3 \le \lvert r \rvert < 0.5$ → weak
- $\lvert r \rvert < 0.3$ → negligible linear association (but check pair plot!)

### Formulae You Must Write From Memory
1. Pearson $r$
2. Sample covariance (Bessel-corrected, $n-1$ divisor)
3. Spearman's $\rho$ (with tie correction awareness)
4. Coefficient of determination $R^2 = r^2$
5. Cauchy-Schwarz inequality statement
6. Heatmap normalization formula
7. Test statistic $t = r\sqrt{n-2}/\sqrt{1-r^2}$

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Advanced Visualization Techniques: Heatmaps, Correlation Matrices, and Pair Plots**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visualization tools and libraries: Matplotlib, Seaborn, Plotly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST785 - ALGORITHMS FOR DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Visualization tools and libraries: Matplotlib, Seaborn, Plotly

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Visualization Libraries** are software frameworks that provide pre-built functions, methods, and rendering engines to transform raw, multidimensional datasets into graphical primitives (points, lines, bars, surfaces) for **exploratory data analysis (EDA)**, **explanatory analytics**, and **interactive reporting**.

In the context of the KTU **PECST785 – Algorithms for Data Science** syllabus, the three primary libraries are:

| Library | Paradigm | Abstraction Level | Primary Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Matplotlib** | Imperative + Object-Oriented | **Low-Level** | Foundational 2D plotting engine |
| **Seaborn** | Declarative (on top of Matplotlib) | **High-Level** | Statistical / aesthetic visualization |
| **Plotly** | Declarative + Reactive | **High-Level** | Interactive, web-based dashboards |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept — "Stack of Visualization":** Seaborn is *not* an independent engine; it is a **statistical wrapper** built on top of Matplotlib. Plotly, on the other hand, uses an entirely independent JavaScript-rendered (D3.js + WebGL) backend. Understanding this architecture is critical for KTU viva and exam questions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a house:

- **Matplotlib** = the **raw bricks, cement, and scaffolding**. You can build *anything*, but you must lay every brick yourself. Total control, total effort.
- **Seaborn** = a **pre-designed interior designer's catalog** sitting on top of those bricks. It calls Matplotlib under the hood, but gives you beautiful, pre-themed rooms (statistical plots) instantly.
- **Plotly** = a **smart-home system with a touchscreen remote**. You don't even touch the bricks; you swipe a panel and the lights, AC, and 3D camera turn on interactively in the browser.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** In industry, ~80% of static reports use **Matplotlib + Seaborn**, while interactive BI dashboards (Tableau-style) prefer **Plotly / Dash**.

---

## 1.3 Key Technical Metrics & Constants

- **Matplotlib Backend Renderers:** `Agg`, `TkAgg`, `MacOSX`, `WebAgg`
- **Seaborn Themes:** `darkgrid`, `whitegrid`, `dark`, `white`, `ticks`
- **Plotly Chart Count:** **40+** chart types (per official documentation)
- **Default DPI for Matplotlib:** **100 DPI** (`figure.dpi = 100`)
- **Seaborn Color Palettes:** `deep`, `muted`, `pastel`, `bright`, `dark`, `colorblind` (6 default qualitative palettes)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — How the Three Libraries Connect

The three libraries exist on a **layered visualization stack**:

- **Layer 0 (Hardware/Render):** Browser Canvas, OpenGL, Tkinter Canvas
- **Layer 1 (Core Engine):** Matplotlib's `FigureCanvas`, `Renderer`, `Artist` classes
- **Layer 2 (Statistical Abstraction):** Seaborn's `AxesSubplot` wrappers, `DataFrame` integration via Pandas
- **Layer 3 (Web/Interactive):** Plotly's `Figure` object, `graph_objs`, JSON-based `plotly.js`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy Summary:** `Pandas DataFrame` → `Seaborn` (statistical layer) → `Matplotlib` (render layer). Plotly bypasses Matplotlib entirely and renders via **D3.js (SVG) + WebGL** in the browser.

---

## 2.2 Matplotlib — The Foundational Engine

Matplotlib follows the **"Figure → Axes → Artist"** architecture:

- **Figure:** The top-level container (the entire image/window). A Figure can contain multiple Axes.
- **Axes:** What you *think* of as a "plot" — the actual Cartesian region with data points, ticks, and labels. Each Axes has an `xaxis`, `yaxis`, and child Artists.
- **Artist:** Everything visible on the figure — `Line2D`, `Text`, `Patch`, `Rectangle`, `Polygon`.

Matplotlib exposes **two interfaces**:

1. **Pyplot (Stateful) Interface** — MATLAB-style. Internally manages a hidden "current" Figure/Axes.
2. **Object-Oriented (OO) Interface** — Explicit, recommended for complex multi-panel figures.

---

## 2.3 Seaborn — The Statistical Layer

Seaborn is purpose-built for **statistical visualization**. Its core design goals are:

- **DataFrame-Native:** Functions accept long-form (`tidy`) DataFrames using `x`, `y`, `hue`, `size`, `style` parameters.
- **Automatic Estimation:** Functions like `sns.histplot`, `sns.kdeplot`, `sns.regplot` perform **kernel density estimation (KDE)** and **linear regression fits automatically**.
- **Aesthetic Defaults:** Beautiful palettes and themes out-of-the-box.

The two main function families in Seaborn:

- **Relational plots:** `relplot()`, `scatterplot()`, `lineplot()`
- **Distribution plots:** `displot()`, `histplot()`, `kdeplot()`, `ecdfplot()`

---

## 2.4 Plotly — The Interactive Web Layer

Plotly is built on **Plotly.js**, which uses **D3.js + StackGL (WebGL)**. It is fundamentally **declarative**: you describe *what* you want, and the engine handles rendering.

- **Graph Objects (`go`):** Low-level class-based API — full control.
- **Plotly Express (`px`):** High-level "one-liner" API — fast EDA.
- **Output Formats:** HTML (`fig.write_html()`), PNG/SVG/PDF (`fig.write_image()`), JSON (`fig.to_json()`).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since visualization is largely a *computer science application* of statistical math, the following foundational formulas are *high-yield* for numerical questions in KTU exams.

| Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Use in Visualization |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mean (μ)** | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | $n$ = data points, $x_i$ = values | Reference line in `axhline` |
| **Variance (σ²)** | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \mu)^2$ | Spread of data | Boxplot whiskers reference |
| **Standard Deviation (σ)** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Avg. deviation from mean | Error bar length |
| **Z-Score Normalization** | $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Standardized value | Color-mapping (`vmin`, `vmax`) |
| **Linear Regression Slope** | $m = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Best-fit slope | `sns.regplot` overlay line |
| **Pearson Correlation (r)** | $r = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | $-1 \leq r \leq 1$ | Heatmap (`sns.heatmap`) values |
| **Gaussian KDE** | $\hat{f}(x) = \dfrac{1}{nh\sqrt{2\pi}} \sum e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x - x_i}{h}\right)^2}$ | $h$ = bandwidth | `sns.kdeplot` smoothing |
| **Min-Max Normalization** | $x_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$ | Scales to $[0, 1]$ | Colorbar range |

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Matplotlib** is the standard for **publication-quality static figures** in IEEE / Springer / Elsevier research papers.
- **Seaborn** is the go-to for **exploratory data analysis (EDA)** in machine learning pipelines — quick statistical insights.
- **Plotly** powers **interactive dashboards** in financial analytics, geospatial analytics, and is the engine behind **Dash by Plotly** for full-stack analytical web apps.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Data to a Boxplot — Step-by-Step Logical Flow

The boxplot is a **high-yield topic** in KTU exams. Let's derive *exactly* how Matplotlib / Seaborn compute the "five-number summary" used to render a boxplot.

**Given:** A dataset $D = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$

### Step 1: Sort the Data
Sort $D$ in ascending order to obtain the **order statistics**:

$$D_{\text{sorted}} = \{x_{(1)} \leq x_{(2)} \leq \cdots \leq x_{(n)}\}$$

This sorting is mandatory because quartiles depend on rank, not value.

### Step 2: Compute the Median (Q2)
The median is the central value that splits the data into two halves.

$$
Q_2 = \text{Median}(D) =
\begin{cases}
x_{\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)} & \text{if } n \text{ is odd} \\
\dfrac{x_{\left(\frac{n}{2}\right)} + x_{\left(\frac{n}{2}+1\right)}}{2} & \text{if } n \text{ is even}
\end{cases}
$$

This corresponds to the **center line of the box** in the boxplot.

### Step 3: Compute the First Quartile (Q1)
The first quartile is the median of the **lower half** of the data (values below $Q_2$).

$$Q_1 = \text{Median}\left(\{x_{(1)}, \ldots, x_{(\lfloor n/2 \rfloor)}\}\right)$$

This corresponds to the **lower edge of the box**.

### Step 4: Compute the Third Quartile (Q3)
The third quartile is the median of the **upper half** of the data.

$$Q_3 = \text{Median}\left(\{x_{(\lceil n/2 \rceil + 1)}, \ldots, x_{(n)}\}\right)$$

This corresponds to the **upper edge of the box**.

### Step 5: Compute the Interquartile Range (IQR)
The IQR measures the spread of the middle 50% of the data.

$$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$$

The **height of the box** in a boxplot is exactly equal to IQR.

### Step 6: Compute the Whiskers
The whiskers extend to the smallest and largest data points within the fences:

$$
\text{Lower Fence} = Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}
$$

$$
\text{Upper Fence} = Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}
$$

Any point outside these fences is plotted as an **outlier** (a separate dot beyond the whisker).

### Step 7: Final Mapping to Plot Elements

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Box Bottom} &= Q_1 \\
\text{Box Center Line} &= Q_2 \\
\text{Box Top} &= Q_3 \\
\text{Lower Whisker Tip} &= \max(x_{(1)}, \ Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}) \\
\text{Upper Whisker Tip} &= \min(x_{(n)}, \ Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}) \\
\text{Outliers} &= \{x_i \mid x_i < Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} \ \text{or} \ x_i > Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

This exact mathematical mapping is what `sns.boxplot()` and `plt.boxplot()` implement internally. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can derive this on paper.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Given $D = \{2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14\}$, derive the boxplot's five-number summary.

### Step 1: Sort
Data is already sorted: $D_{\text{sorted}} = \{2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14\}$. Here $n = 7$ (odd).

### Step 2: Median
Since $n = 7$, the median is the 4th value:

$$Q_2 = x_{(4)} = 8$$

### Step 3: Q1
Lower half: $\{2, 4, 6\}$. Median is 4:

$$Q_1 = 4$$

### Step 4: Q3
Upper half: $\{10, 12, 14\}$. Median is 12:

$$Q_3 = 12$$

### Step 5: IQR
$$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 12 - 4 = 8$$

### Step 6: Fences
$$\text{Lower Fence} = 4 - 1.5 \cdot 8 = 4 - 12 = -8$$
$$\text{Upper Fence} = 12 + 1.5 \cdot 8 = 12 + 12 = 24$$

### Step 7: Final Summary
All data points lie within $[-8, 24]$, so **no outliers exist**. The whisker tips are at the min ($2$) and max ($14$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Five-Number Summary} &= \{Q_1, \ Q_2, \ Q_3, \ x_{\min}, \ x_{\max}\} \\
&= \{4, \ 8, \ 12, \ 2, \ 14\}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Code Implementation Matrix (CS-Topic Aligned)

> [!TIP]
> Python code is **permitted** in this CS course (PECST785 – Algorithms for Data Science). The following matrix maps each KTU theory concept to its implementation.

| Concept | Matplotlib Code | Seaborn Code | Plotly Code |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Line Plot** | `plt.plot(x, y)` | `sns.lineplot(x=x, y=y, data=df)` | `px.line(df, x='x', y='y')` |
| **Scatter Plot** | `plt.scatter(x, y)` | `sns.scatterplot(x=x, y=y, hue='c', data=df)` | `px.scatter(df, x='x', y='y', color='c')` |
| **Bar Chart** | `plt.bar(cats, vals)` | `sns.barplot(x='cat', y='val', data=df)` | `px.bar(df, x='cat', y='val')` |
| **Histogram** | `plt.hist(data, bins=30)` | `sns.histplot(data, kde=True, bins=30)` | `px.histogram(df, x='col', nbins=30)` |
| **Boxplot** | `plt.boxplot(data)` | `sns.boxplot(x='cat', y='val', data=df)` | `px.box(df, x='cat', y='val')` |
| **Heatmap** | `plt.imshow(M, cmap='viridis')` | `sns.heatmap(M, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm')` | `px.imshow(M, color_continuous_scale='Viridis')` |
| **3D Scatter** | `ax.plot3D(x, y, z)` | ❌ Not supported natively | `px.scatter_3d(df, x, y, z)` |
| **Save to File** | `plt.savefig('out.png')` | Inherits Matplotlib | `fig.write_html('out.html')` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Visualization Library Architecture Stack

This diagram shows the *layered relationship* between the three libraries, a frequently asked concept in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER"]:::topLayer
    userData["USER DATAFRAME"]:::dataLayer
    plotlyTop["PLOTLY EXPRESS px"]:::plotlyHigh
    plotlyGo["PLOTLY GRAPH OBJECTS go"]:::plotlyLow
    seabornLib["SEABORN sns"]:::seaborn
    matplotlibCore["MATPLOTLIB pyplot"]:::mpl
    matplotlibOo["MATPLOTLIB OO API"]:::mpl
    renderers["RENDER BACKENDS Agg TkAgg WebAgg"]:::render
    browserEngine["D3 JS WebGL Browser"]:::render
    canvasTk["Tkinter Qt Cairo Canvas"]:::render
    outputFiles["OUTPUT png pdf html svg json"]:::output

    appLayer --> userData
    userData --> plotlyTop
    userData --> seabornLib
    userData --> matplotlibCore
    userData --> matplotlibOo
    plotlyTop --> plotlyGo
    plotlyGo --> browserEngine
    seabornLib --> matplotlibCore
    matplotlibCore --> renderers
    matplotlibOo --> renderers
    renderers --> canvasTk
    browserEngine --> outputFiles
    canvasTk --> outputFiles

    classDef topLayer fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dataLayer fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Typical Plot Creation Workflow (OO-API Pattern)

This is the **KTU-recommended** pattern for creating multi-panel figures, as it gives explicit control over every subplot.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Step 1 Import Libraries import matplotlib pyplot as plt import numpy as np"]:::step
    stepB["Step 2 Create Figure Object fig plt figure figsize 10 6 dpi 100"]:::step
    stepC["Step 3 Add Axes to Figure ax fig add subplot 1 1 1"]:::step
    stepD["Step 4 Plot Data ax scatter x y color blue label Series A"]:::step
    stepE["Step 5 Add Annotations ax set title label xlabel ylabel legend grid"]:::step
    stepF["Step 6 Save or Show fig savefig output png dpi 300 plt show"]:::step

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF

    classDef step fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Plotly Interactive Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userInput["USER HOVER CLICK SELECT"]:::user
    dataLayer["PLOTLY FIGURE OBJECT go Figure data layout"]:::core
    dataTrace["DATA TRACES Scatter Bar Heatmap"]:::trace
    layoutObj["LAYOUT OBJECT title xaxis yaxis colorway"]:::layout
    serializer["JSON SERIALIZER fig to json"]:::serialize
    jsEngine["PLOTLY JS D3 JS WebGL"]:::engine
    browser["BROWSER RENDERER SVG Canvas"]:::render
    feedback["INTERACTIVE FEEDBACK Zoom Pan Tooltip Legend Toggle"]:::feedback

    userInput --> dataLayer
    dataLayer --> dataTrace
    dataLayer --> layoutObj
    dataLayer --> serializer
    serializer --> jsEngine
    jsEngine --> browser
    browser --> feedback
    feedback --> userInput

    classDef user fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef trace fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef layout fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef serialize fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef engine fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef render fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef feedback fill:#FFC0CB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Library

| If Your Requirement Is... | Best Library | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Publication-quality static PDF for IEEE paper | **Matplotlib** | Vector output, LaTeX support, fine control |
| Quick statistical EDA on a DataFrame | **Seaborn** | One-line `sns.pairplot`, `sns.heatmap` |
| Interactive web dashboard for stakeholders | **Plotly** | Hover, zoom, pan built-in |
| 3D surface / scatter / mesh visualization | **Plotly** | WebGL acceleration, no extra config |
| Animated time-series plots | **Plotly** | Native `animation_frame` parameter |
| Tight integration with NumPy arrays | **Matplotlib** | Native NumPy support |
| Color-coded correlation matrices | **Seaborn** | `sns.heatmap(annot=True, cmap='coolwarm')` |
| Geographical maps (choropleth) | **Plotly** | `px.choropleth` built-in |
| Reproducible scientific figure with error bars | **Matplotlib** | `errorbar()` parameter, full control |
| Quick KPI dashboard for non-technical users | **Plotly Dash** | Full-stack web app framework |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**
**Differentiate between Matplotlib's `pyplot` interface and its Object-Oriented interface. Which is preferred for complex multi-panel figures and why?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> The `pyplot` interface is a **state-based (stateful)** interface where a hidden current Figure and Axes are automatically tracked, similar to MATLAB. Functions like `plt.plot()`, `plt.title()`, `plt.xlabel()` implicitly operate on the "current" axes.
>
> The **Object-Oriented (OO) interface** is **state-independent**. The user explicitly creates a `Figure` object (`fig`) and one or more `Axes` objects (`ax1`, `ax2`, ...), then calls methods on these objects directly (e.g., `ax1.plot()`, `ax1.set_title()`).
>
> For complex multi-panel figures (e.g., subplots), the OO interface is **preferred** because:
> 1. It provides **explicit, unambiguous control** over which subplot receives which command.
> 2. It avoids **cross-contamination** of styling across subplots.
> 3. It is **scalable and reproducible** for production code.
>
> *(Keyword coverage: stateful, OO, Figure, Axes — full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the relationship between Seaborn and Matplotlib. Why is Seaborn said to be a "high-level" wrapper?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> Seaborn is a **statistical visualization library built on top of Matplotlib**. Internally, every Seaborn function (e.g., `sns.boxplot()`) creates a Matplotlib `Axes` object and uses Matplotlib's `Artist` rendering pipeline to draw the final image. Without Matplotlib installed, Seaborn cannot function.
>
> It is called a "high-level" wrapper because:
> 1. **Concise syntax** — `sns.pairplot(df)` replaces dozens of lines of Matplotlib code.
> 2. **DataFrame-native** — Accepts tidy data with semantic mappings (`x`, `y`, `hue`, `size`, `style`).
> 3. **Automatic statistical estimation** — KDE, regression lines, confidence intervals are computed automatically.
> 4. **Aesthetic defaults** — Beautiful themes and color palettes are applied out-of-the-box.
>
> *(Keyword coverage: built on top, Axes, high-level, tidy, KDE — full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Exam) Module 2 guidelines**, you must answer **ONE full 14-mark question**. Two independent choices (A and B) are provided below for practice. Each question has two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**
**With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of Matplotlib's Figure–Axes–Artist hierarchy. Write Python code to create a 2×2 grid of subplots showing (i) a sine wave, (ii) a cosine wave, (iii) a scatter plot of random points, and (iv) a bar chart of categorical data using the Object-Oriented API.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution (7 Marks):**
>
> **The Figure–Axes–Artist Hierarchy:**
>
> - **Figure (`fig`):** The top-level container — the entire window or image. A Figure can have multiple Axes.
> - **Axes (`ax`):** The region where data is plotted (with x and y axes, ticks, labels). One Figure can contain many Axes (subplots).
> - **Artist:** The "primitive" drawing objects inside Axes — `Line2D`, `Rectangle`, `Text`, `Patch`. Everything visible is an Artist.
>
> ```python
> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
> import numpy as np
>
> # Step 1: Create a Figure and 2x2 grid of Axes
> fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(10, 8))
>
> # Subplot 1: Sine wave
> x = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 100)
> axes[0, 0].plot(x, np.sin(x), color='blue')
> axes[0, 0].set_title('Sine Wave')
> axes[0, 0].set_xlabel('x')
> axes[0, 0].set_ylabel('sin(x)')
>
> # Subplot 2: Cosine wave
> axes[0, 1].plot(x, np.cos(x), color='red')
> axes[0, 1].set_title('Cosine Wave')
> axes[0, 1].set_xlabel('x')
> axes[0, 1].set_ylabel('cos(x)')
>
> # Subplot 3: Scatter plot
> rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
> x_rand = rng.normal(0, 1, 100)
> y_rand = rng.normal(0, 1, 100)
> axes[1, 0].scatter(x_rand, y_rand, color='green', alpha=0.6)
> axes[1, 0].set_title('Random Scatter')
> axes[1, 0].set_xlabel('X')
> axes[1, 0].set_ylabel('Y')
>
> # Subplot 4: Bar chart
> categories = ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D']
> values = [23, 45, 56, 33]
> axes[1, 1].bar(categories, values, color='orange')
> axes[1, 1].set_title('Bar Chart')
> axes[1, 1].set_xlabel('Category')
> axes[1, 1].set_ylabel('Value')
>
> # Global title and layout
> fig.suptitle('2x2 Subplot Grid - Matplotlib OO API', fontsize=14)
> plt.tight_layout()
> plt.savefig('subplots_demo.png', dpi=100)
> plt.show()
> ```
>
> **Mark Distribution:**
> - Hierarchy diagram: 2 marks
> - Correct subplot creation: 2 marks
> - Four correct plots: 2 marks
> - Proper labels and title: 1 mark

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**
**Compare and contrast Matplotlib, Seaborn, and Plotly across the following dimensions: (i) abstraction level, (ii) interactivity, (iii) statistical support, (iv) typical use case. Justify which library is most suitable for a real-time interactive financial dashboard.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution (7 Marks):**
>
> | Dimension | Matplotlib | Seaborn | Plotly |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Abstraction Level** | Low (imperative + OO) | High (declarative) | High (declarative) |
> | **Interactivity** | Static (limited via `mplcursors`) | Static (inherits Matplotlib) | Fully interactive (zoom, pan, hover) |
> | **Statistical Support** | Manual (must compute KDE, regression) | Automatic (KDE, regression, CI built-in) | Manual (relies on NumPy/SciPy) |
> | **Typical Use Case** | Publication-quality static figures | Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) | Interactive BI dashboards, web apps |
> | **Output Format** | PNG, PDF, SVG, EPS | Inherits Matplotlib | HTML, JSON, PNG, SVG |
> | **Learning Curve** | Steep (must learn OO API) | Gentle (one-liners) | Gentle (Plotly Express) |
> | **3D Support** | Limited (`mpl_toolkits.mplot3d`) | None | Excellent (WebGL-based) |
>
> **Justification for Real-Time Financial Dashboard:**
>
> **Plotly** is the most suitable choice for a real-time interactive financial dashboard because:
> 1. **Native interactivity** — Built-in zoom, pan, hover tooltips, legend toggling, and range-slider selectors allow stakeholders to explore stock prices, candlestick patterns, and volume data without writing extra code.
> 2. **Web-native rendering** — Uses D3.js + WebGL, so the dashboard can be embedded directly into a web browser or a Flask/Django backend.
> 3. **Real-time data streaming** — Plotly Dash supports `dcc.Interval` components for live-updating charts (e.g., tick-by-tick stock prices).
> 4. **Rich financial chart types** — `go.Candlestick`, `go.Ohlc`, `go.Waterfall` are purpose-built for financial analytics.
> 5. **Cross-platform deployment** — Output is portable HTML/JSON, making it easy to share via email or cloud dashboards.
>
> **Mark Distribution:**
> - Comparison table: 3 marks
> - Financial dashboard justification: 4 marks

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**
**What is Kernel Density Estimation (KDE)? Derive the mathematical formula and explain how Seaborn's `kdeplot` function uses it. Write a code snippet to plot KDEs for two distributions (Normal and Exponential) on the same axis with a legend.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution (7 Marks):**
>
> **KDE Definition:** Kernel Density Estimation is a **non-parametric method** to estimate the Probability Density Function (PDF) of a random variable. It does *not* assume any underlying distribution (Normal, Exponential, etc.), making it more flexible than histograms.
>
> **Mathematical Formula:**
>
> Given $n$ data points $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ and a kernel function $K$ with bandwidth $h$:
>
> $$\hat{f}(x) = \dfrac{1}{nh} \sum_{i=1}^{n} K\left(\dfrac{x - x_i}{h}\right)$$
>
> For the **Gaussian kernel**:
>
> $$K(u) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{u^2}{2}}$$
>
> Substituting:
>
> $$\hat{f}(x) = \dfrac{1}{nh\sqrt{2\pi}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{x - x_i}{h}\right)^2}$$
>
> **Role of the Bandwidth $h$:**
> - Small $h$ → overfitting (bumpy, noisy)
> - Large $h$ → oversmoothing (loss of detail)
> - Seaborn uses **Scott's Rule** or **Silverman's Rule** by default.
>
> **Python Code:**
>
> ```python
> import seaborn as sns
> import numpy as np
> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
>
> # Generate two distributions
> rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
> normal_data = rng.normal(loc=0, scale=1, size=1000)
> exp_data = rng.exponential(scale=1, size=1000)
>
> # Plot KDEs on same axis
> fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
> sns.kdeplot(x=normal_data, ax=ax, color='blue',
>             fill=True, alpha=0.4, label='Normal (0,1)')
> sns.kdeplot(x=exp_data, ax=ax, color='red',
>             fill=True, alpha=0.4, label='Exponential (1)')
>
> ax.set_title('Kernel Density Estimation Comparison')
> ax.set_xlabel('Value')
> ax.set_ylabel('Density')
> ax.legend()
> plt.show()
> ```
>
> **Mark Distribution:**
> - KDE definition + formula: 3 marks
> - Explanation of bandwidth: 1 mark
> - Correct code: 3 marks

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**
**Explain the architecture of Plotly's Figure Object. How does Plotly achieve interactivity using D3.js and WebGL? Write a code snippet using Plotly Express to create an interactive scatter plot with hover tooltips showing custom DataFrame columns.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution (7 Marks):**
>
> **Plotly Figure Architecture:**
>
> A Plotly `Figure` is a **JSON-serializable Python dictionary** with two main keys:
>
> - **`data`:** A list of **traces** (e.g., `Scatter`, `Bar`, `Heatmap`). Each trace is a `graph_objects` instance that describes the data and visual encoding (x, y, color, size, etc.).
> - **`layout`:** A `graph_objects.Layout` instance that describes non-data visual elements (title, axes, legends, annotations, colorbars, fonts).
>
> When the user calls `fig.show()`, Plotly:
> 1. Serializes the Figure to **JSON**.
> 2. Loads `plotly.js` (a JavaScript library built on **D3.js** for SVG and **StackGL** for WebGL).
> 3. Renders the chart in the browser as **SVG** for small datasets or **WebGL** for large datasets (millions of points).
> 4. Attaches event listeners for **hover, click, zoom, pan, select**.
>
> **Why WebGL Matters:**
> - WebGL leverages the **GPU** for parallel rendering, allowing smooth interaction with up to **~10 million data points**.
> - 3D plots (e.g., `scatter3d`) are essentially impossible without WebGL acceleration.
>
> **Python Code (Plotly Express Interactive Scatter):**
>
> ```python
> import plotly.express as px
> import pandas as pd
>
> # Sample DataFrame
> df = pd.DataFrame({
>     'height': [165, 170, 175, 180, 185, 160, 172],
>     'weight': [55, 68, 72, 80, 90, 50, 65],
>     'age': [25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 22, 28],
>     'name': ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E', 'F', 'G'],
>     'gender': ['F', 'M', 'M', 'M', 'M', 'F', 'F']
> })
>
> # Create interactive scatter plot
> fig = px.scatter(
>     df,
>     x='height',
>     y='weight',
>     color='gender',          # Color-code by gender
>     size='age',              # Bubble size proportional to age
>     hover_data=['name', 'age', 'gender'],  # Custom tooltips
>     title='Height vs Weight (Interactive)'
> )
>
> fig.update_layout(
>     xaxis_title='Height (cm)',
>     yaxis_title='Weight (kg)'
> )
>
> fig.write_html('interactive_scatter.html')
> fig.show()
> ```
>
> **Mark Distribution:**
> - Figure object architecture (data + layout): 2 marks
> - D3.js + WebGL explanation: 2 marks
> - Correct Plotly Express code with hover: 3 marks

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `plt` and `fig` references** — Many students call `plt.title()` after creating a Figure with `fig, ax = plt.subplots()`. This creates a *duplicate* title on the hidden pyplot figure. Always use `ax.set_title()` or `fig.suptitle()` in the OO API. *(−1 to 2 marks penalty.)*
>
> 2. **Forgetting `plt.tight_layout()`** — When subplot labels overlap, examiners mark down for poor presentation. Always include `plt.tight_layout()` or `fig.tight_layout()`. *(−0.5 to 1 mark.)*
>
> 3. **Claiming Seaborn is "independent" of Matplotlib** — This is a **factual error**. Seaborn *requires* Matplotlib. Examiners test this conceptual relationship explicitly. *(−1 to 2 marks.)*
>
> 4. **Writing `px.scatter` for 3D plots** — The correct function is `px.scatter_3d`. Many students lose marks for the wrong function name. *(−1 mark.)*
>
> 5. **Forgetting to call `fig.show()` or `plt.show()`** — Code that "compiles" but produces no output gets partial credit only. Always end with a show/save call.
>
> 6. **Using the wrong hue/size parameter in Seaborn** — `hue` requires a *categorical* column; using a continuous variable reduces the plot's interpretability. Examiners notice. *(−0.5 to 1 mark.)*
>
> 7. **Not mentioning `dpi` in `savefig`** — When asked to save a "publication-quality" figure, omitting `dpi=300` is a KTU-unfriendly mistake.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2:**
>
> - **Matplotlib** is the **foundational, low-level** 2D plotting library. It uses the **Figure → Axes → Artist** architecture.
> - Matplotlib exposes **two interfaces**: **Pyplot (stateful)** and **Object-Oriented (explicit)**. **OO is preferred** for production.
> - Default DPI is **100**; publication figures use **DPI ≥ 300**.
> - **`plt.subplots()`** is the most common way to create a Figure with one or more Axes.
> - **Seaborn** is a **statistical wrapper** built on top of Matplotlib — it is **NOT independent**.
> - Seaborn accepts **tidy DataFrames** with semantic parameters: `x`, `y`, `hue`, `size`, `style`, `col`, `row`.
> - Seaborn supports **automatic KDE, regression lines, and confidence intervals** — Matplotlib does not.
> - Five default Seaborn themes: `darkgrid`, `whitegrid`, `dark`, `white`, `ticks`.
> - Six default qualitative palettes: `deep`, `muted`, `pastel`, `bright`, `dark`, `colorblind`.
> - **Plotly** is built on **D3.js (SVG) + StackGL (WebGL)** for interactive web-based visualization.
> - Plotly has **two APIs**: **Plotly Express (`px`)** for one-liners and **Graph Objects (`go`)** for fine control.
> - Plotly outputs **HTML, JSON, PNG, SVG, PDF**.
> - Plotly supports **3D plots, geographical maps, animations** — Matplotlib's 3D is limited.
> - **KDE formula:** $\hat{f}(x) = \dfrac{1}{nh\sqrt{2\pi}} \sum e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-x_i}{h}\right)^2}$. Bandwidth $h$ controls smoothness.
> - **Boxplot five-number summary:** $Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, x_{\min}, x_{\max}$. Whiskers extend to $Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ and $Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$.
> - **Use Matplotlib** for publications, **Seaborn** for EDA, **Plotly** for interactive dashboards.
> - Always end plot code with **`plt.show()`** or **`fig.show()`** (or save with `savefig` / `write_html`).
> - **Pearson correlation** $r$ ranges in $[-1, +1]$ and is what `sns.heatmap` visualizes.
> - For KTU 14-mark questions, always draw a **neat labeled diagram** — examiners allocate **2 marks** purely for diagrams.
