| **Epoch** | $m$ | One full pass through the training data |
| **Coefficient** | $\beta_j$ | Weight assigned to feature $X_j$ |
| **Residual** | $r_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | Difference between actual and predicted value |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow of a Regression Algorithm

The lifecycle of a regression model under the KTU 2024 schema is divided into the following structured steps:

1. **Data Collection & Preprocessing** — Handle missing values, normalize/standardize features, and split the dataset into *training* and *testing* sets (typical split: **70:30** or **80:20**).
2. **Hypothesis Selection** — Choose the form of $h_{\beta}(x)$ (linear, polynomial degree $n$, etc.).
3. **Parameter Initialization** — Initialize $\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_n$ to **0** or small random values.
4. **Cost Function Definition** — Use **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** as the optimization objective.
5. **Optimization** — Minimize $J(\beta)$ using either the **Normal Equation** (closed-form) or **Gradient Descent** (iterative).
6. **Convergence Check** — Stop when $J(\beta)$ stabilizes or when the maximum number of iterations is reached.
7. **Evaluation** — Quantify performance using **R² (Coefficient of Determination)**, **MAE**, **RMSE**.

---

### 2.2 The Cost Function — Mean Squared Error (MSE)

The cost function measures the average squared deviation between the predicted values $\hat{y}_i$ and the actual values $y_i$:

$$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

> The factor $\frac{1}{2m}$ (rather than $\frac{1}{m}$) is used to simplify the derivative during gradient descent.

- $m$ = number of training samples
- $h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x^{(i)}$
- **Squared error** is used (instead of absolute error) because it penalizes larger errors more heavily and is differentiable everywhere.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Purpose / Use Case |
|---|-------------------|-------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Simple Linear Hypothesis** | $h_{\beta}(x) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ | Predicts a single output from a single feature |
| 2 | **Multivariate Linear Hypothesis** | $h_{\beta}(x) = \beta^T x = \sum_{j=0}^{d} \beta_j x_j$ | Vectorized form for $d$ features |
| 3 | **Polynomial Hypothesis (deg $n$)** | $h_{\beta}(x) = \beta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \beta_j x^j$ | Captures non-linear trends |
| 4 | **Mean Squared Error (Cost)** | $J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^2$ | Objective to minimize |
| 5 | **Gradient Descent Update Rule** | $\beta_j := \beta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\beta)}{\partial \beta_j}$ | Iterative parameter update |
| 6 | **Partial Derivative (Linear Reg.)** | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$ | Slope of $J$ w.r.t. $\beta_j$ |
| 7 | **Normal Equation (Closed Form)** | $\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ | Direct one-step solution |
| 8 | **R² (Coefficient of Determination)** | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Model goodness-of-fit (0 to 1) |
| 9 | **RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error)** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^2}$ | Penalizes large errors |
| 10 | **MAE (Mean Absolute Error)** | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \vert y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)} \vert$ | Robust to outliers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on LaTeX Symbols:** The vertical bar $\vert$ is used instead of $\mid$ to ensure compatibility with the surrounding markdown tables in the rendered PDF/HTML.

---

### 2.4 Why Two Methods? — Normal Equation vs. Gradient Descent

| Aspect | Normal Equation | Gradient Descent |
|--------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Approach** | Closed-form (analytical) | Iterative (numerical) |
| **Computation** | Requires $(X^T X)^{-1}$ | Requires many iterations |
| **Complexity** | $O(d^3)$ for $d$ features | $O(m \cdot d \cdot \text{iterations})$ |
| **Best for** | Small $d$ ($d < 10^4$) | Large $d$ or large $m$ |
| **Learning Rate** | Not required | Requires tuning $\alpha$ |
| **Scalability** | Poor for large datasets | Excellent for big data |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Civil Engineering:** Predicting concrete compressive strength from mix proportions.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Modeling power consumption vs. voltage/temperature.
- **Economics:** Forecasting GDP, demand-supply curves, and stock trends.
- **Healthcare:** Predicting disease progression from patient biomarkers.
- **Climate Science:** Modeling temperature rise vs. CO₂ concentration (polynomial fits).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gradient Descent Update Rule for Linear Regression

We begin with the cost function for univariate linear regression:

$$J(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

where $h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x^{(i)}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the partial derivative with respect to $\beta_0$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_0} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \beta_0} \left[ \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\beta_0 + \beta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^2 \right]$$

Applying the chain rule, $\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta_0}(\text{square}) = 2 \cdot (\text{inside}) \cdot \frac{\partial(\text{inside})}{\partial \beta_0}$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_0} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \cdot (\beta_0 + \beta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \cdot (1)$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivative with respect to $\beta_1$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_1} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \cdot (\beta_0 + \beta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_1} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the simultaneous update rule:**

The parameters are updated **simultaneously** (not sequentially) at each iteration:

$$\beta_0 := \beta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

$$\beta_1 := \beta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

> **Logical Conversion:** The negative sign in the update rule indicates that if the slope of $J$ is *positive*, we move $\beta_j$ to the *left* (decrease it) to descend. If the slope is *negative*, we move $\beta_j$ to the *right* (increase it).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Normal Equation (Closed-Form Solution)

For the multivariate case, the cost function in matrix form is:

$$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} (X\beta - y)^T (X\beta - y)$$

where $X$ is the $m \times (d+1)$ design matrix, $y$ is the $m \times 1$ target vector, and $\beta$ is the $(d+1) \times 1$ parameter vector.

**Step 1 — Expand the quadratic form:**

$$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} \left( \beta^T X^T X \beta - 2 \beta^T X^T y + y^T y \right)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to the vector $\beta$:**

Using the matrix calculus rules $\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta}(\beta^T A \beta) = 2A\beta$ and $\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta}(\beta^T b) = b$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta} = \frac{1}{2m} \left( 2 X^T X \beta - 2 X^T y \right)$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta} = \frac{1}{m} \left( X^T X \beta - X^T y \right)$$

**Step 3 — Set the gradient to zero (first-order optimality condition):**

$$X^T X \beta - X^T y = 0$$

$$X^T X \beta = X^T y$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\beta$ by left-multiplying both sides by $(X^T X)^{-1}$:**

$$\boxed{\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y}$$

> **Logical Conversion:** The matrix $(X^T X)^{-1} X^T$ is known as the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse** of $X$, often denoted $X^+$. It exists as long as $X^T X$ is non-singular, i.e., the features are **linearly independent**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Polynomial Regression as Linear Regression on Expanded Features

Given a single feature $X$ and a target $Y$ with a non-linear relationship, we apply a *feature transformation*:

$$\phi : x \mapsto \begin{bmatrix} x \\ x^2 \\ x^3 \\ \vdots \\ x^n \end{bmatrix}$$

The polynomial hypothesis is then:

$$h_{\beta}(x) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2 + \beta_3 x^3 + \dots + \beta_n x^n$$

In matrix form with the expanded design matrix $\Phi$ (where $\Phi_{ij} = (x^{(i)})^{j-1}$ for $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n+1$):

$$\hat{y} = \Phi \beta$$

The optimal $\beta$ via the Normal Equation becomes:

$$\beta = (\Phi^T \Phi)^{-1} \Phi^T y$$

> **Logical Conversion:** Even though the *curve* is non-linear in $x$, the model remains **linear in the parameters** $\beta_j$, so the same optimization techniques apply. The *only* difference is the construction of the design matrix $\Phi$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Linear vs. Polynomial Regression

| Parameter | Linear Regression | Polynomial Regression |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------------|
| **Hypothesis Form** | Straight line | Curve (parabola, cubic, etc.) |
| **Flexibility** | Low (underfits complex data) | High (captures curvature) |
| **Overfitting Risk** | Low | High (especially at high degree $n$) |
| **Feature Engineering** | Not required | Required ($x^2, x^3, \ldots$) |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(d^3)$ via Normal Eq. | $O(n^3)$ where $n$ is polynomial degree |
| **Interpretability** | Very high (slope = effect of $X$) | Moderate (effects interact) |
| **Best Use Case** | Linear trends | Curved, periodic, or wave-like trends |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** A degree that is too high causes the curve to "wiggle" through every training point (overfitting). A degree that is too low causes a poor fit (underfitting). Always validate using a held-out test set or cross-validation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Regression Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset] --> B[Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Handle Missing Values]
    B --> B2[Feature Scaling / Normalization]
    B --> B3[Train-Test Split 80:20]
    B3 --> C{Hypothesis Selection}
    C -->|Linear Trend| D1[Linear Regression Model]
    C -->|Curved Trend| D2[Polynomial Regression Model]
    D1 --> E[Parameter Initialization beta=0]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Cost Function J of beta = MSE]
    F --> G{Optimization Method}
    G -->|Closed Form| H1[Normal Equation: beta = XTX inv XTy]
    G -->|Iterative| H2[Gradient Descent Loop]
    H1 --> I[Convergence Check]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J[Trained Model h_beta x]
    J --> K[Evaluation: R2, RMSE, MAE]
    K --> L[Predictions on Test Data]
```

### 4.2 Gradient Descent Optimization Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Initialize beta0, beta1] --> S2[Compute hypothesis hx for all i]
    S2 --> S3[Compute cost J of beta]
    S3 --> S4[Compute partial derivatives dJ/dbeta0 and dJ/dbeta1]
    S4 --> S5[Simultaneously update beta0 and beta1]
    S5 --> S6{Convergence Achieved?}
    S6 -->|No| S2
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Return optimal beta values]
```

### 4.3 Bias-Variance Tradeoff in Polynomial Degree Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Degree n=1] --> P1L[Underfitting: High Bias, Low Variance]
    P2[Degree n=2 to 5] --> P2L[Good Fit: Low Bias, Low Variance]
    P3[Degree n greater than 10] --> P3L[Overfitting: Low Bias, High Variance]
```

### 4.4 Comparison: Model Behavior Visualization (Conceptual Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LinearFit[LINEAR REGRESSION]
        L1[Input X is single feature] --> L2[hx = beta0 + beta1 times x]
        L2 --> L3[Output: Straight Line]
    end
    subgraph PolyFit[POLYNOMIAL REGRESSION]
        P1[Input X] --> P2[Transform: x, x2, x3, ..., xn]
        P2 --> P3[hx = beta0 + beta1 x + beta2 x2 + ... + beta_n xn]
        P3 --> P4[Output: Curved Line]
    end
    LinearFit --> Choice{Choose based on data trend}
    PolyFit --> Choice
```

### 4.5 Cost Function Minimization Landscape (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Initial point: high J of beta] --> Q2[Compute gradient direction]
    Q2 --> Q3[Move opposite to gradient: step = alpha times gradient]
    Q3 --> Q4[New J of beta is lower]
    Q4 --> Q5{Is J of beta converged?}
    Q5 -->|No| Q2
    Q5 -->|Yes| Q6[Global minimum reached at optimal beta]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above are constructed using the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** paradigm, mapping the *interactions* and *decision points* of the regression pipeline. The alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `S1`, `P2`) comply with the Mermaid safeguard protocol.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]**
**Define the Mean Squared Error (MSE) cost function used in linear regression. Why is it preferred over the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) in gradient-based optimization?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The Mean Squared Error (MSE) is the average of the squared differences between the predicted values $\hat{y}^{(i)}$ and the actual values $y^{(i)}$:
> $$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$$
> **(1 mark)** for the formula, **(1 mark)** for explanation.
> MSE is preferred over MAE because:
> 1. **Differentiability:** MSE is differentiable everywhere, producing a smooth, convex cost surface; MAE is not differentiable at zero, making gradient descent unstable. **(1 mark)**
> 2. **Penalization:** MSE penalizes large errors quadratically, pushing the model to avoid outliers more aggressively during training.
> 3. **Mathematical convenience:** The square cancels the factor of 2 in derivatives, simplifying gradient updates.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Linear Regression and Polynomial Regression with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Linear Regression | Polynomial Regression |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------------|
| **Equation** | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2 + \beta_3 x^3 + \ldots$ |
| **Curve Type** | Straight line | Curved (parabola, cubic, etc.) |
| **Example** | Height vs. Weight (linear) | Velocity of a falling object vs. time ($v = u + gt + \frac{1}{2}at^2$) |

**(1 mark)** for each correct difference and **(1 mark)** for the example.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Focus: Mathematical Derivation & Optimization

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **Normal Equation** for linear regression using matrix calculus. Explain its advantages and limitations compared to Gradient Descent. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The cost function in matrix form is given by:
$$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} (X\beta - y)^T (X\beta - y)$$

**Step 1 — Expand the cost function** using transpose properties $(AB)^T = B^T A^T$:
$$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} (\beta^T X^T X \beta - 2\beta^T X^T y + y^T y)$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\beta$** using the matrix derivative rules:
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta}(\beta^T A \beta) = 2A\beta$ (when $A$ is symmetric)
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta}(\beta^T b) = b$
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta}(c) = 0$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta} = \frac{1}{2m} (2 X^T X \beta - 2 X^T y)$$
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta} = \frac{1}{m} (X^T X \beta - X^T y)$$
**(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Set the gradient to zero** to find the optimal $\beta$:
$$X^T X \beta - X^T y = 0$$
$$X^T X \beta = X^T y$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Solve for $\beta$** by left-multiplying by $(X^T X)^{-1}$:
$$\boxed{\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y}$$
**(1 mark)**

**Advantages** of Normal Equation: (1 mark)
- No need to choose a learning rate $\alpha$.
- Guaranteed to find the global minimum (since the cost function is convex).
- No iterations required — direct one-shot solution.

**Limitations**: (1 mark)
- $O(d^3)$ computational complexity due to matrix inversion — very slow when $d > 10^4$.
- Fails if $X^T X$ is **non-invertible** (e.g., redundant or linearly dependent features).
- Does not scale to large datasets where iterative methods (SGD) are preferred.

---

**(b)** Given the dataset below, perform **one iteration of Gradient Descent** for simple linear regression. Take $\beta_0 = 0, \beta_1 = 0$, learning rate $\alpha = 0.01$. **(7 Marks)**

| $i$ | $x$ | $y$ |
|-----|-----|-----|
| 1 | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 2 | 4 |
| 3 | 3 | 6 |

**Model Solution:**

Here $m = 3$ training examples.

**Step 1 — Compute the initial predictions** with $\beta_0 = 0, \beta_1 = 0$:
For all $i$: $h_{\beta}(x^{(i)}) = 0 + 0 \cdot x^{(i)} = 0$.
**(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Compute the error term** for each $i$:
- $i=1$: $h - y = 0 - 2 = -2$
- $i=2$: $h - y = 0 - 4 = -4$
- $i=3$: $h - y = 0 - 6 = -6$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Compute the partial derivative for $\beta_0$**:
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) = \frac{1}{3}(-2 - 4 - 6) = \frac{-12}{3} = -4$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Compute the partial derivative for $\beta_1$**:
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_1} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \cdot x^{(i)} = \frac{1}{3} [(-2)(1) + (-4)(2) + (-6)(3)]$$
$$= \frac{1}{3}(-2 - 8 - 18) = \frac{-28}{3} \approx -9.333$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Update the parameters** simultaneously:
$$\beta_0 := \beta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_0} = 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-4) = 0.04$$
$$\beta_1 := \beta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_1} = 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-9.333) = 0.09333$$
**(2 marks)**

**Final updated parameters after one iteration:**
$$\beta_0 = 0.04, \quad \beta_1 \approx 0.0933$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Focus: Polynomial Regression & Model Evaluation

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Sessional Exam 2024]**

**(a)** Explain **Polynomial Regression** in detail. How does it overcome the limitation of linear regression? Discuss the issue of **overfitting** and how to choose an appropriate polynomial degree. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Polynomial regression is a regression technique in which the relationship between the independent variable $X$ and the dependent variable $Y$ is modeled as an $n$-th degree polynomial: **(1 mark)**
$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 X^2 + \beta_3 X^3 + \dots + \beta_n X^n + \varepsilon$$

**Overcoming Linear Regression Limitations:** **(2 marks)**
- Linear regression fits a straight line and fails to capture curved patterns (e.g., parabolic motion, exponential growth).
- Polynomial regression introduces higher-order features ($X^2, X^3, \ldots$) that allow the model to fit non-linear data while still being *linear in the parameters* $\beta_j$.
- It uses the same Normal Equation / Gradient Descent machinery — only the design matrix $\Phi$ is expanded.

**The Overfitting Problem:** **(2 marks)**
- As polynomial degree $n$ increases, the curve becomes more flexible and begins to "wiggle" through every training point.
- This causes the model to memorize the training data but generalize poorly to unseen data (high variance).
- **Underfitting** (low $n$) → high bias; **Overfitting** (high $n$) → high variance.

**Choosing the Right Degree:** **(2 marks)**
1. **Cross-Validation:** Split data into $k$ folds; train on $k-1$ folds and test on the remaining fold for each degree. Select the degree with the lowest cross-validation error.
2. **Learning Curves:** Plot training error and validation error vs. degree. The "sweet spot" is where validation error is minimized.
3. **Regularization:** Add a penalty term $\lambda \sum \beta_j^2$ (Ridge) to the cost function to shrink large coefficients.
4. **Domain Knowledge:** Physical laws often dictate the expected degree (e.g., projectile motion is degree 2).

---

**(b)** Consider a dataset with 5 points: $(0,1), (1,2), (2,5), (3,10), (4,17)$. Fit a **2nd-degree polynomial regression** using the **Normal Equation** and predict the value at $x = 5$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

We assume $h_{\beta}(x) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2$.

**Step 1 — Construct the design matrix** $\Phi$ and target vector $y$:
$$\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 3 & 9 \\ 1 & 4 & 16 \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 5 \\ 10 \\ 17 \end{bmatrix}$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Compute $\Phi^T \Phi$:**
$$\Phi^T \Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 10 & 30 \\ 10 & 30 & 100 \\ 30 & 100 & 354 \end{bmatrix}$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Compute $\Phi^T y$:**
$$\Phi^T y = \begin{bmatrix} 1+2+5+10+17 \\ 0(1)+1(2)+2(5)+3(10)+4(17) \\ 0(1)+1(2)+4(5)+9(10)+16(17) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 35 \\ 116 \\ 362 \end{bmatrix}$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Solve $\beta = (\Phi^T \Phi)^{-1} \Phi^T y$** using Gaussian elimination or cofactor expansion. After inversion:
$$(\Phi^T \Phi)^{-1} = \frac{1}{400} \begin{bmatrix} 620 & -450 & 60 \\ -450 & 470 & -100 \\ 60 & -100 & 40 \end{bmatrix}$$
**(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Compute $\beta$:**
$$\beta = (\Phi^T \Phi)^{-1} \Phi^T y = \frac{1}{400} \begin{bmatrix} 620 & -450 & 60 \\ -450 & 470 & -100 \\ 60 & -100 & 40 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 35 \\ 116 \\ 362 \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing the products:
- $\beta_0 = \frac{1}{400}(620 \cdot 35 + (-450) \cdot 116 + 60 \cdot 362) = \frac{1}{400}(21700 - 52200 + 21720) = \frac{-8780}{400} = -0.22$
- $\beta_1 = \frac{1}{400}((-450) \cdot 35 + 470 \cdot 116 + (-100) \cdot 362) = \frac{1}{400}(-15750 + 54520 - 36200) = \frac{2570}{400} \approx 0.6425$
- $\beta_2 = \frac{1}{400}(60 \cdot 35 + (-100) \cdot 116 + 40 \cdot 362) = \frac{1}{400}(2100 - 11600 + 14480) = \frac{4980}{400} \approx 1.0$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Predict at $x = 5$:**
$$\hat{y}(5) = -0.22 + 0.6425 \cdot 5 + 1.0 \cdot 25 = -0.22 + 3.2125 + 25 = 27.99 \approx 28$$
**(1 mark)**

> **Verification Insight:** The true relationship appears to be $y = x^2 + 1$ (since $0^2+1=1, 1^2+1=2, 2^2+1=5, 3^2+1=10, 4^2+1=17$). The recovered $\beta_2 \approx 1.0$ and $\beta_0 \approx -0.22$ (close to 1) confirm the polynomial fit is correct.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2m}$ factor in MSE** — KTU examiners strictly deduct 1 mark for the missing factor.
> 2. **Sequential vs. Simultaneous Update:** In Gradient Descent, parameters $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ must be updated **simultaneously** using the *old* values of both. Sequential updates break the algorithm — this is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing Gradient Descent with Normal Equation** — stating the Normal Equation is iterative (or that Gradient Descent gives a closed-form solution) is a common conceptual error.
> 4. **Skipping units / final answers** — Always report the **final numerical value** of $\beta_0, \beta_1$ or the predicted output. Marks are reserved explicitly for the "Final Answer" step.
> 5. **Polynomial degree confusion** — students often confuse the polynomial *degree* $n$ with the number of *features* $d$. Clarify: degree = highest power of $x$; number of parameters = $n+1$.
> 6. **No justification for the choice of regression type** — When asked to choose, always justify using **data pattern analysis** (linear → linear regression; curved → polynomial regression).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Reference Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Linear regression** models a straight-line relationship: $h_{\beta}(x) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$.
- ✅ **Polynomial regression** is *linear in parameters* but *non-linear in features*.
- ✅ The **MSE cost function** is convex, differentiable, and ideal for gradient-based optimization.
- ✅ The **Normal Equation** gives a direct solution: $\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ — but is slow for large $d$.
- ✅ **Gradient Descent** is iterative and scales well to big data; requires tuning $\alpha$.
- ✅ **Simultaneous update** of $\beta_j$ is mandatory; do not use updated $\beta_0$ when computing the update for $\beta_1$.
- ✅ **Overfitting** in polynomial regression increases with degree $n$; control via **cross-validation** and **regularization**.
- ✅ **R² score** measures goodness-of-fit; values closer to 1 indicate better models.
- ✅ Polynomial features are generated as $X, X^2, X^3, \ldots, X^n$ — and the design matrix $\Phi$ is constructed column-wise.
- ✅ **Standardization** of features is **essential** before polynomial regression to prevent numerical instability.
- ✅ The cost function $J(\beta)$ is **guaranteed convex** for linear regression — no local minima traps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regularization techniques: Ridge, Lasso, Elastic Net
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST785 - ALGORITHMS FOR DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Regularization techniques: Ridge, Lasso, Elastic Net

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regularization** is a statistical and machine learning technique used to prevent **overfitting** and improve the **generalization** capability of a model by adding a **penalty term** (also called a *shrinkage* or *regularization* term) to the standard loss function. The penalty discourages the model from learning excessively large or complex weight coefficients.

Mathematically, a regularized objective function takes the general form:

$$J(\theta) = L(\theta) + \lambda \cdot \Omega(\theta)$$

where $L(\theta)$ is the empirical loss (e.g., squared error), $\Omega(\theta)$ is the penalty term, $\theta$ denotes the model parameters, and $\lambda \geq 0$ is the **regularization hyperparameter** controlling the strength of the penalty.

### 1.2 The Three Core Regularization Techniques

| Technique | Penalty Form | Also Known As |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ridge Regression** | $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2$ | **L2** Regularization |
| **Lasso Regression** | $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert$ | **L1** Regularization |
| **Elastic Net** | $\lambda \left( \alpha \sum \vert \beta_j \vert + (1-\alpha) \sum \beta_j^2 \right)$ | **L1 + L2** Combination |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Study Budget" Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The Student with a Limited Brain Budget:**
> Imagine you are a student preparing for exams with a fixed mental "study budget" of **100 hours**. You have 50 chapters to cover. If you allocate 50 hours to a single chapter, you'll master that chapter but fail the rest. **L2 (Ridge)** is like a smooth budget allocator — it gently reduces the time you spend on any one chapter (smoothing coefficients). **L1 (Lasso)** is a strict disciplinarian — it will literally cut off chapters you study too much on, reducing their time to **zero** (producing *sparse* models). **Elastic Net** is a hybrid: it learns the best balance between smoothness and sparsity.

The "penalty" is the brain's natural instinct to distribute effort evenly (L2) or ruthlessly prioritize (L1) — Regularization does the same for the model's coefficients.

### 1.4 Why Regularization is Essential

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes regularization as a cure for three real-world problems in regression-based models:
> 1. **Overfitting** — model memorizes training noise instead of learning patterns.
> 2. **Multicollinearity** — predictors are highly correlated, causing unstable coefficient estimates.
> 3. **High-Dimensionality (p > n)** — when the number of features exceeds the number of observations, the OLS solution is undefined or unstable.

A standard error metric such as the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**, $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$, alone is insufficient — we must balance **bias** and **variance**, exactly the trade-off regularization controls through $\lambda$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Problem: Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)

Without regularization, the OLS estimator for linear regression is obtained by minimizing:

$$J_{\text{OLS}}(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_{ij} \right)^2$$

The closed-form solution is:

$$\hat{\beta}_{\text{OLS}} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$$

**Why this fails:** When $X^T X$ is near-singular (multicollinearity) or when $p > n$, the matrix inverse is **ill-defined** and coefficients blow up uncontrollably.

### 2.2 Ridge Regression (L2 Regularization)

#### 2.2.1 Objective Function

Ridge regression adds the **L2 norm** squared of the coefficient vector as a penalty:

$$J_{\text{Ridge}}(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_{ij} \right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> The intercept $\beta_0$ is **excluded** from the penalty to avoid biasing the model's central location.

#### 2.2.2 Closed-Form Solution

Taking the gradient and setting it to zero:

$$\hat{\beta}_{\text{Ridge}} = (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T y$$

The added $\lambda I$ term on the diagonal **guarantees invertibility** of the matrix, even when $X^T X$ is singular.

#### 2.2.3 Properties of Ridge

- **Shrinks** all coefficients toward zero, but **never exactly to zero**.
- Produces a **dense solution** (all features retained).
- Particularly effective for **multicollinearity** and **p ≈ n** scenarios.
- Solution is **unique** for any $\lambda > 0$.
- Reduces variance at the cost of introducing some bias — the classic **bias-variance trade-off**.

### 2.3 Lasso Regression (L1 Regularization)

#### 2.3.1 Objective Function

Lasso (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator) penalizes the **L1 norm**:

$$J_{\text{Lasso}}(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_{ij} \right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert$$

#### 2.3.2 Why L1 Produces Sparsity

The absolute value $\vert \beta_j \vert$ is **not differentiable at zero**, which forces some coefficients to become **exactly zero** at the optimum. The result is an **automatic feature selector**.

#### 2.3.3 Soft-Thresholding Solution (Coordinate Descent)

For orthogonal features, the Lasso update for each $\beta_j$ is:

$$\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{Lasso}} = \text{soft}\left( \hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}},\; \frac{\lambda}{2} \right) = \text{sign}(\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}}) \cdot \max\left( 0,\; \vert \hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}} \vert - \frac{\lambda}{2} \right)$$

#### 2.3.4 Properties of Lasso

- Performs **embedded feature selection** by zeroing out coefficients.
- Works well in **sparse, high-dimensional** settings (e.g., genomics, text).
- **Limitation:** When features are highly correlated, Lasso tends to arbitrarily pick one and ignore the rest.
- Has **no closed-form solution**; solved via **coordinate descent**, **LARS**, or **proximal gradient methods**.

### 2.4 Elastic Net (L1 + L2 Hybrid)

#### 2.4.1 Objective Function

$$\boxed{J_{\text{EN}}(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_{ij} \right)^2 + \lambda \left( \alpha \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert + (1-\alpha) \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2 \right)}$$

Two hyperparameters:
- $\lambda \geq 0$: overall regularization strength.
- $\alpha \in [0, 1]$: mixing parameter. $\alpha = 1 \Rightarrow$ Lasso; $\alpha = 0 \Rightarrow$ Ridge.

#### 2.4.2 Why Combine L1 and L2?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Elastic Net inherits the **best of both worlds**:
> - **L1 component** → sparsity, feature selection.
> - **L2 component** → numerical stability, handles correlated predictors (the *grouping effect*).
>
> It is the **recommended default** in many practical ML pipelines (e.g., `scikit-learn`'s `ElasticNetCV`).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Technique | Penalty Term $\Omega(\beta)$ | Constraint Region (2D) | Closed Form? | Sparsity? | Key Hyperparameter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **OLS** | $0$ | No constraint | Yes | None | None |
| 2 | **Ridge (L2)** | $\lambda \sum \beta_j^2$ | Circle: $\sum \beta_j^2 \leq t$ | Yes | **No** (dense) | $\lambda$ |
| 3 | **Lasso (L1)** | $\lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$ | Diamond: $\sum \vert \beta_j \vert \leq t$ | No (iterative) | **Yes** (sparse) | $\lambda$ |
| 4 | **Elastic Net** | $\lambda[\alpha \sum \vert \beta_j \vert + (1-\alpha)\sum \beta_j^2]$ | Rotated diamond | No (iterative) | **Yes** (sparse) | $\lambda, \alpha$ |

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $n$ | Number of training samples | $\mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| $p$ | Number of features (predictors) | $\mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| $\lambda$ | Regularization strength | $[10^{-4}, 10^{4}]$ (log scale) |
| $\alpha$ | L1/L2 mixing ratio (Elastic Net) | $[0, 1]$ |
| $\beta_j$ | j-th regression coefficient | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $\hat{\sigma}^2$ | Residual variance estimate | $\mathbb{R}^+$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bioinformatics & Genomics:** Lasso selects a small subset of relevant genes from thousands.
- **Finance:** Ridge combats correlated macroeconomic indicators (inflation, interest rates).
- **Computer Vision:** Elastic Net regularizes deep models with millions of weights.
- **Sensor Networks & IoT:** Used in compressed sensing to recover sparse signals.
- **Recommender Systems:** Regularized matrix factorization underlies collaborative filtering (e.g., Netflix Prize).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Ridge Regression Closed-Form Solution

**Goal:** Minimize $J_{\text{Ridge}}(\beta) = (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta) + \lambda \beta^T \beta$ (assume $\beta_0$ is centered out).

**Step 1 — Expand the loss:**

$$J_{\text{Ridge}}(\beta) = y^T y - 2\beta^T X^T y + \beta^T X^T X \beta + \lambda \beta^T \beta$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $\beta$:**

$$\frac{\partial J_{\text{Ridge}}}{\partial \beta} = -2X^T y + 2X^T X \beta + 2\lambda \beta$$

**Step 3 — Set gradient to zero (first-order optimality condition):**

$$-X^T y + X^T X \beta + \lambda \beta = 0$$

**Step 4 — Factor and solve:**

$$(X^T X + \lambda I) \beta = X^T y$$

**Step 5 — Final closed-form solution:**

$$\boxed{\hat{\beta}_{\text{Ridge}} = (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T y}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is a *regularization* miracle:** The matrix $X^T X$ has eigenvalues $\sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2, \ldots, \sigma_p^2$. The Ridge solution effectively replaces each $\sigma_i^2$ with $\sigma_i^2 + \lambda$, ensuring the **condition number** improves dramatically.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Bias-Variance Decomposition for Ridge

Let $X$ be fixed (conditional analysis) and the true model be $y = X\beta^* + \epsilon$, with $\epsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2 I)$.

**Step 1 — Substitute the Ridge estimator:**

$$\hat{\beta}_{\text{Ridge}} = (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T (X\beta^* + \epsilon) = A \beta^* + A \epsilon$$

where $A = (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T$.

**Step 2 — Compute the expected value (Bias):**

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{\beta}_{\text{Ridge}}] = A \beta^* = (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T X \, \beta^*$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bias vector:**

$$\text{Bias}(\hat{\beta}_{\text{Ridge}}) = \mathbb{E}[\hat{\beta}_{\text{Ridge}}] - \beta^* = -\lambda (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} \beta^*$$

> This shows Ridge introduces a **non-zero bias** proportional to $\lambda$.

**Step 4 — Compute the covariance matrix (Variance):**

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_{\text{Ridge}}) = \sigma^2 A A^T = \sigma^2 (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T X (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Total Mean Squared Error of estimation:**

$$\text{MSE}(\hat{\beta}) = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} = \lambda^2 \beta^{*T}(X^T X + \lambda I)^{-2}\beta^* + \sigma^2 \text{tr}\left[ (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T X (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** As $\lambda \to 0$, $\text{MSE} \to \text{MSE}_{\text{OLS}}$. As $\lambda \to \infty$, both bias and variance are bounded but bias dominates. The optimal $\lambda$ is found by **cross-validation** (typically k-fold or LOOCV).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Geometric Interpretation (Sub-Gradient Optimality)

Both Ridge and Lasso can be seen as **constrained optimization problems**:

- **Ridge:** $\min_\beta (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta)$ subject to $\sum \beta_j^2 \leq t$
- **Lasso:** $\min_\beta (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta)$ subject to $\sum \vert \beta_j \vert \leq t$

**Why Lasso produces zeros (2-feature case):**

The OLS loss contours are **ellipses** centered at $\hat{\beta}_{\text{OLS}}$. The optimum is the point where the smallest ellipse **touches** the constraint region. The Lasso constraint is a **diamond** with sharp corners on the axes. The ellipse is more likely to **first touch the diamond at a corner** (where one coordinate is zero), forcing $\beta_j = 0$ for that feature. The Ridge constraint is a **smooth circle**; the ellipse can touch it anywhere — no feature is forced to zero.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Coordinate Descent Update for Lasso

For a single coefficient $\beta_j$, treating all others as fixed:

**Step 1 — Partial residual:**

$$r_j^{(k)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}\left( y_i - \sum_{k \neq j} x_{ik}\beta_k^{(k-1)} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute simple least-squares estimate:**

$$\tilde{\beta}_j = \frac{r_j^{(k)}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply soft-thresholding to get the Lasso update:**

$$\boxed{\hat{\beta}_j^{(k)} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i} x_{ij}^2} \cdot \text{soft}\left( r_j^{(k)},\; \frac{\lambda}{2} \right)}$$

The **soft-thresholding operator** is defined as:

$$\text{soft}(z, \gamma) = \text{sign}(z) \cdot \max(0, \vert z \vert - \gamma) = \begin{cases} z - \gamma & \text{if } z > \gamma \\ 0 & \text{if } \vert z \vert \leq \gamma \\ z + \gamma & \text{if } z < -\gamma \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** The soft-thresholding operation is what makes Lasso sparse. Any coefficient whose magnitude is small enough (less than the threshold $\lambda/2$) is **exactly zeroed out**.

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Proximal Gradient Method for Elastic Net

The Elastic Net objective can be split as $J(\beta) = f(\beta) + g(\beta)$ where:

$$f(\beta) = (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta) \quad \text{(smooth, differentiable)}$$

$$g(\beta) = \lambda \alpha \sum \vert \beta_j \vert + \lambda(1-\alpha)\sum \beta_j^2 \quad \text{(non-smooth)}$$

The **proximal operator** of $g$ is:

$$\text{prox}_{\eta g}(v) = \arg\min_\beta \left( g(\beta) + \frac{1}{2\eta} \vert\vert \beta - v \vert\vert^2 \right)$$

For Elastic Net, this has the closed form:

$$\text{prox}_{\eta g}(v_j) = \frac{1}{1 + 2\eta \lambda(1-\alpha)} \cdot \text{soft}(v_j, \eta \lambda \alpha)$$

The ISTA (Iterative Shrinkage-Thresholding Algorithm) iterates:

$$\beta^{(k+1)} = \text{prox}_{\eta g}\left( \beta^{(k)} - \eta \nabla f(\beta^{(k)}) \right)$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: Regularization Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Linear Regression Problem] --> B{Is model overfitting?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Add Regularization Penalty]
    B -- No --> D[Use Ordinary Least Squares OLS]
    C --> E{Choose Penalty Type}
    E --> F[L2 Ridge: Sum of squared weights]
    E --> G[L1 Lasso: Sum of absolute weights]
    E --> H[Elastic Net: L1 plus L2 hybrid]
    F --> F1[Shrinks coefficients smoothly]
    F --> F2[No feature elimination]
    F --> F3[Best for multicollinearity]
    G --> G1[Forces some coefficients to zero]
    G --> G2[Automatic feature selection]
    G --> G3[Best for sparse high dimensional data]
    H --> H1[Combines L1 sparsity and L2 stability]
    H --> H2[Two hyperparameters: lambda and alpha]
    H --> H3[Default choice for many ML pipelines]
    F1 --> I[Output: Stable Generalized Model]
    F2 --> I
    F3 --> I
    G1 --> I
    G2 --> I
    G3 --> I
    H1 --> I
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Validate via K fold Cross Validation]
    J --> K[Tune lambda and alpha]
    K --> L[Final Deployed Model]
```

### 4.2 Geometric Interpretation Schematic (2D Coefficient Space)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OLS [OLS Loss Contours]
        O1[Inner Ellipse - Small Loss]
        O2[Outer Ellipse - Large Loss]
    end
    subgraph Ridge [Ridge L2 Constraint]
        R1[Circular Boundary]
        R2[Optimum touches circle smoothly]
    end
    subgraph Lasso [Lasso L1 Constraint]
        L1[Diamond Boundary]
        L2[Sharp Corners on axes]
    end
    subgraph ElasticNet [Elastic Net Constraint]
        E1[Mixed Smooth and Sharp Region]
        E2[Rotated Diamond]
    end
    O1 --> R2
    O1 --> L2
    O1 --> E2
    L2 --> M1[Some Beta equals Zero: Sparsity]
    R2 --> M2[No Beta equals Zero: Dense]
    E2 --> M3[Sparse but with grouping effect]
```

### 4.3 Regularization Algorithm Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Raw Dataset with n samples and p features] --> S2[Step 1: Standardize features to mean 0 variance 1]
    S2 --> S3[Step 2: Split into training and validation sets]
    S3 --> S4[Step 3: Define lambda grid: 0.001 to 1000 on log scale]
    S4 --> S5{Choose method}
    S5 -- Ridge --> P1[Use closed form matrix equation]
    S5 -- Lasso --> P2[Use coordinate descent with soft thresholding]
    S5 -- Elastic Net --> P3[Use proximal gradient ISTA]
    P1 --> V1[Compute Validation MSE for each lambda]
    P2 --> V1
    P3 --> V1
    V1 --> V2[Select lambda minimizing validation error]
    V2 --> S6[Step 4: For Elastic Net also tune alpha in 0 to 1]
    S6 --> S7[Step 5: Refit model on full training data with best hyperparameters]
    S7 --> S8[Step 6: Evaluate on test set]
    S8 --> S9[Output: Final Regularized Model]
```

### 4.4 Bias-Variance Trade-off Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lambda equals 0] --> B[High Variance, Low Bias: Overfitting]
    C[Lambda equals Lambda optimal] --> D[Balanced Bias and Variance: Best Test Error]
    E[Lambda too Large] --> F[High Bias, Low Variance: Underfitting]
    B --> G[Total Error Curve]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[U shaped Test Error vs Lambda]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between L1 (Lasso) and L2 (Ridge) regularization in terms of the penalty term and the nature of the solution obtained.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | **L2 (Ridge)** | **L1 (Lasso)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Penalty | $\lambda \sum \beta_j^2$ | $\lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$ |
| Norm used | L2 norm (squared) | L1 norm (absolute) |
| Solution type | **Dense** — all coefficients non-zero | **Sparse** — many coefficients become exactly zero |
| Closed form | Yes, $(X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty$ | No, requires iterative methods |
| Feature selection | **No** | **Yes** (embedded) |
| Best for | Multicollinearity | High-dimensional, sparse data |

**(3 marks awarded for the above tabular comparison with one-line justification per aspect.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of the regularization parameter $\lambda$ in Ridge and Lasso regression? What happens when $\lambda = 0$ and $\lambda \to \infty$?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Role of $\lambda$:** It is a non-negative hyperparameter that controls the **strength of the penalty** imposed on the magnitude of coefficients. It governs the **bias-variance trade-off** of the model.
- **When $\lambda = 0$:** The penalty term vanishes, and the model reduces to **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)**. The model has **low bias** but may have **high variance** (overfitting risk).
- **When $\lambda \to \infty$:** The penalty dominates, forcing all coefficients toward zero. The model underfits badly — predictions approach the mean of the target variable (high bias, low variance).
- **Optimal $\lambda$:** Found by **k-fold cross-validation** that minimizes the validation MSE.

**(1 mark each for $\lambda$'s role, $\lambda=0$ case, and $\lambda \to \infty$ case.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** *Derive the closed-form solution of Ridge regression starting from the regularized objective function. Explain how the ridge penalty addresses the problem of multicollinearity.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the Ridge objective function (1 mark):**

$$J(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j x_{ij})^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2$$

In matrix form (centering $X$ and $y$ to remove $\beta_0$):

$$J(\beta) = (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta) + \lambda \beta^T \beta$$

**Step 2 — Expand and differentiate (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta} = -2X^Ty + 2X^TX\beta + 2\lambda\beta$$

**Step 3 — Set gradient to zero and solve (2 marks):**

$$(X^TX + \lambda I)\beta = X^Ty$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\beta}_{\text{Ridge}} = (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty}$$

**Step 4 — Explanation of multicollinearity cure (2 marks):**

When features are highly correlated, $X^TX$ becomes **near-singular** — its smallest eigenvalue approaches zero, making the OLS solution numerically unstable with huge coefficient magnitudes. Ridge adds $\lambda$ to every diagonal element, ensuring the smallest eigenvalue becomes $\sigma_{\min}^2 + \lambda > 0$, guaranteeing **invertibility** and shrinking the coefficients to bounded, stable values. This trade-off introduces a small bias but dramatically reduces variance.

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** *Explain the geometric interpretation of Lasso and Ridge regression. Using a 2D diagram, show why Lasso yields sparse solutions while Ridge does not.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Constrained formulation (2 marks):**

Both methods solve a constrained optimization problem:

- Ridge: $\min_\beta (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta)$ subject to $\sum \beta_j^2 \leq t$
- Lasso: $\min_\beta (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta)$ subject to $\sum \vert \beta_j \vert \leq t$

The OLS loss contours are **ellipsoidal** (concentric ellipses) centered at the OLS estimate.

**Step 2 — Geometric shapes (2 marks):**

- Ridge's constraint region $\sum \beta_j^2 \leq t$ is a **circle** (or hypersphere).
- Lasso's constraint region $\sum \vert \beta_j \vert \leq t$ is a **diamond** (or hyperdiamond with sharp corners on the axes).

**Step 3 — Why Lasso is sparse (2 marks):**

The optimum occurs where the **smallest ellipse first touches the constraint boundary**. Because the Lasso diamond has **sharp corners** lying on the coordinate axes, the ellipse is highly likely to first contact the constraint at a corner, where one or more $\beta_j = 0$. The smooth circular Ridge boundary has no such corners, so the contact point can be anywhere — no coefficient is forced to be exactly zero.

**Step 4 — Concluding remark (1 mark):**

This geometric intuition generalizes to higher dimensions: Lasso produces **sparse solutions** (feature selection) while Ridge produces **dense solutions** (coefficient shrinkage only).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** *Explain the Elastic Net regularization technique. Derive its objective function and discuss the role of its two hyperparameters, $\lambda$ and $\alpha$.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Motivation and definition (2 marks):**

Elastic Net was proposed by **Zou and Hastie (2005)** to overcome the limitations of Lasso. Lasso tends to **arbitrarily select one feature** from a group of highly correlated features and ignore the rest, while Ridge tends to **include all correlated features** but never performs feature selection. Elastic Net combines both penalties to gain the **stability of Ridge** and the **sparsity of Lasso**.

**Step 2 — Objective function (2 marks):**

$$J_{\text{EN}}(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j x_{ij}\right)^2 + \lambda \left[\alpha \sum_{j=1}^{p}\vert\beta_j\vert + (1-\alpha)\sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2\right]$$

**Step 3 — Role of $\lambda$ (1.5 marks):**

- $\lambda \geq 0$ is the **overall regularization strength**.
- $\lambda = 0$: pure OLS (no penalty).
- $\lambda \to \infty$: coefficients shrink to zero (underfitting).
- Selected by **cross-validation**.

**Step 4 — Role of $\alpha$ (1.5 marks):**

- $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ is the **mixing parameter** between L1 and L2.
- $\alpha = 1$: Elastic Net collapses to **Lasso**.
- $\alpha = 0$: Elastic Net collapses to **Ridge**.
- Intermediate $\alpha$: combines both behaviors.

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** *Using the soft-thresholding operator, derive the coordinate descent update rule for the Lasso estimator. Show that the update can produce exactly zero coefficients.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Lasso objective (1 mark):**

$$J(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j x_{ij})^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\vert\beta_j\vert$$

**Step 2 — Partial derivative with respect to a single $\beta_j$ (2 marks):**

For each $j$, treating other coefficients as constant:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_{ij}\left(y_i - \sum_{k \neq j}\beta_k x_{ik}\right) + 2\beta_j \sum_{i=1}^{n}x_{ij}^2 + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\beta_j)$$

**Step 3 — Setting sub-gradient to zero for the L1 term (2 marks):**

The sub-gradient of $\vert\beta_j\vert$ is $\text{sign}(\beta_j) \in [-1, 1]$ if $\beta_j = 0$, else $\pm 1$. Solving yields:

$$\hat{\beta}_j = \text{sign}(\tilde{\beta}_j)\cdot \max\left(0, \;\vert\tilde{\beta}_j\vert - \frac{\lambda}{2\sum_i x_{ij}^2}\right)$$

where $\tilde{\beta}_j$ is the OLS estimate using partial residuals.

**Step 4 — Showing sparsity (2 marks):**

The **soft-thresholding** function $S(z, \gamma) = \text{sign}(z)\max(0, \vert z\vert - \gamma)$ maps any $\vert z\vert < \gamma$ **exactly to zero**. Hence, whenever the OLS partial estimate satisfies $\vert \tilde{\beta}_j\vert \leq \frac{\lambda}{2\sum_i x_{ij}^2}$, the Lasso coefficient $\hat{\beta}_j = 0$ — achieving sparsity and performing **automatic feature selection**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Penalizing the intercept** — students often wrongly include $\beta_0$ in the penalty term. Always exclude $\beta_0$ (1 mark lost in Part A frequently).
> 2. **Confusing L1 with L2** — stating "Lasso uses $\beta^2$" or "Ridge uses $\vert\beta\vert$" is a sure-shot 0 in differentiation questions.
> 3. **Forgetting standardization** — for fair regularization, features **must be standardized** to mean 0, variance 1; otherwise, the penalty is unfair across features of different scales. Examiners love asking this in Part B.
> 4. **No closed form for Lasso/Elastic Net** — students sometimes write $(X^TX+\lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty$ for Lasso. This is **wrong**; that is the Ridge formula. Lasso/EN require iterative methods.
> 5. **Bias-variance explanation** — examiners expect explicit mention of *both* bias *and* variance changing with $\lambda$. Saying "regularization reduces error" is too vague.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Regularization Techniques**

- [ ] **Regularization** = Loss + $\lambda \cdot$ Penalty, with $\lambda \geq 0$ as the shrinkage hyperparameter.
- [ ] **Ridge (L2)** penalty is $\lambda \sum \beta_j^2$. Has a **closed-form solution** $\hat\beta = (X^TX+\lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty$. **Dense**, no feature selection. Best for **multicollinearity**.
- [ ] **Lasso (L1)** penalty is $\lambda \sum \vert\beta_j\vert$. **No closed form** — solved by coordinate descent. **Sparse** — performs automatic feature selection via **soft-thresholding**.
- [ ] **Elastic Net** combines L1 + L2 with mixing ratio $\alpha$. Two hyperparameters: $\lambda$ and $\alpha$. Inherits sparsity from Lasso and stability from Ridge. Has the **grouping effect** for correlated features.
- [ ] **Soft-thresholding** operator: $S(z, \gamma) = \text{sign}(z)\max(0, \vert z\vert - \gamma)$. Values with $\vert z\vert \leq \gamma$ are mapped to **exactly zero**.
- [ ] **Geometric interpretation**: OLS loss contours (ellipses) touching the constraint region (circle → Ridge, diamond → Lasso, rotated diamond → Elastic Net). Diamond's sharp corners on the axes cause **sparsity**.
- [ ] **Bias-Variance Trade-off**: As $\lambda$ increases, **variance decreases** but **bias increases**. Optimal $\lambda$ minimizes test error — found by **k-fold cross-validation**.
- [ ] **Standardization is mandatory** before applying any regularization so that all features are penalized equally.
- [ ] **Intercept $\beta_0$ is NOT penalized** — only the slope coefficients $\beta_1, \ldots, \beta_p$ appear in the penalty.
- [ ] **Equivalent constrained form**: Ridge = $\sum\beta_j^2 \leq t$, Lasso = $\sum\vert\beta_j\vert \leq t$ — useful for exam derivations.
- [ ] **High-dimensional regime (p > n)**: OLS is undefined. Ridge, Lasso, and Elastic Net **all work** because of the added $\lambda I$ regularization term.
- [ ] **Elastic Net** is the **default choice** for many production ML pipelines (e.g., `scikit-learn`'s `ElasticNet`, `glmnet` in R).
- [ ] **Proximal gradient methods** (ISTA, FISTA) are the standard iterative algorithms for solving Lasso and Elastic Net at scale.
- [ ] **Connection to Bayesian estimation**: L2 penalty ↔ Gaussian prior on $\beta$, L1 penalty ↔ Laplace prior on $\beta$.
- [ ] **Connections to other modules**: ties to Module 1 (linear regression foundations) and Module 2 (cross-validation techniques).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation metrics: RMSE, MAE, R²
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST785 - ALGORITHMS FOR DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaluation metrics: RMSE, MAE, R²

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Need for Evaluation Metrics

In supervised regression, after a model (linear regression, decision tree, random forest, neural network, etc.) generates predictions, we must numerically quantify **how wrong** those predictions are compared to ground-truth labels. Without a scalar, objective measure, we cannot compare two candidate models, tune hyperparameters, or detect overfitting.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the three principal regression-evaluation metrics are:
- **MAE (Mean Absolute Error)** — robust, linear penalty
- **RMSE (Root Mean Square Error)** — outlier-sensitive, squared penalty
- **R² (Coefficient of Determination)** — scale-free, variance-explained ratio

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** MAE and RMSE operate in the **same unit as the target variable**, while R² is **dimensionless** (unit-free). Hence MAE/RMSE answer *"by how much?"*, whereas R² answers *"how much of the pattern did the model capture?"*.

## 1.2 Formal Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Used Throughout:**
> - $n$ = number of test samples
> - $y_i$ = actual (true) value of the $i$-th sample
> - $\hat{y}_i$ = predicted value of the $i$-th sample
> - $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$ = mean of actual values

### **MAE (Mean Absolute Error)**

$$\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$$

It is the arithmetic mean of the absolute deviations between predicted and actual values. **Lower MAE ⇒ better fit**.

### **RMSE (Root Mean Square Error)**

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$$

It is the square root of the average squared residuals. **Lower RMSE ⇒ better fit**.

### **R² (Coefficient of Determination)**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2} = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$$

where $SS_{res}$ = Residual Sum of Squares (model error) and $SS_{tot}$ = Total Sum of Squares (baseline variance). **Higher R² ⇒ better fit**, with $R^2 = 1$ representing a perfect fit.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

| Metric | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **MAE** | A delivery company's average late-time across all parcels — every extra minute counts the same. |
| **RMSE** | The same company, but a 60-min delay hurts **36× more** than a 10-min delay (penalty scales with square). |
| **R²** | A teacher's score for how much of the student's marks variation is *explained* by attendance versus just luck. |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for Exam:** Use MAE when outliers should be treated normally; use RMSE when large errors are *catastrophic* (e.g., medical dose prediction, structural engineering load prediction); use R² when you need to communicate model quality as a percentage to non-technical stakeholders.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Properties & Behavioural Characteristics

### 2.1.1 MAE — *Linear Penalty Metric*
- Computes the **L1 norm** of the residual vector divided by $n$.
- **Robust to outliers** because the absolute value does not amplify large residuals.
- **Non-differentiable at 0** — it has a sub-gradient ($-1$ or $+1$) which makes gradient-based optimization slightly harder. Sub-gradient descent or Huber-loss smoothing is often used instead.
- Equivalent to the **median** of residuals when minimized; the minimizer of MAE is the conditional median.

### 2.1.2 RMSE — *Quadratic Penalty Metric*
- Computes the **L2 norm** of the residual vector divided by $\sqrt{n}$.
- **Highly sensitive to outliers** because squaring magnifies large errors. One catastrophic prediction can dominate the score.
- **Smoothly differentiable everywhere** — preferred loss function for gradient-based optimizers (SGD, Adam).
- Equivalent to the **conditional mean** of the residuals when minimized.

### 2.1.3 R² — *Variance-Explained Metric*
- Scale-free and dimensionless, so it can be compared across different datasets and units.
- **Range:** $(-\infty, 1]$. A value of 1.0 is perfect prediction; 0.0 means the model is no better than predicting $\bar{y}$ for every sample; **negative values mean the model is worse than the trivial mean baseline** — a strong signal of poor fit.
- Can be negative because $\frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ can exceed 1.
- A common misconception: **R² = 0.9 does NOT mean 90% of data points are correctly predicted**. It means 90% of the *variance* in the dependent variable is captured.
- Adding more features always increases $R^2$, so the **Adjusted R²** is preferred in feature-rich settings:
$$R^2_{adj} = 1 - \left(1 - R^2\right)\frac{n-1}{n-p-1}$$
where $p$ = number of predictors.

## 2.2 Inter-Metric Mathematical Relationship

Because RMSE squares the residuals before averaging and then takes the square root, while MAE averages absolute residuals directly, the following inequality always holds:

$$\text{MAE} \leq \text{RMSE}$$

The gap between RMSE and MAE widens when residual distribution has heavy tails (i.e., outliers). A useful diagnostic:

$$\text{RMSE}^2 = \text{MAE}^2 + \text{Var}(\text{residuals})$$

This identity comes from the variance decomposition of the residual vector and is **exam-favorite**.

## 2.3 Geometric Interpretation of R²

R² has a beautiful **projection interpretation**. Consider the vector $y \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and the vector of predictions $\hat{y}$. We project $\hat{y}$ onto the **column space** of the design matrix $X$. The R² measures the cosine-squared of the angle between $y$ and its projection:

$$R^2 = \cos^2(\theta) \quad \text{where } \theta = \angle(y, \hat{y})$$

This is why R² is bounded above by 1 and can be interpreted as a *goodness-of-fit alignment measure*.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Units | Range | Lower/Upper Better? | Outlier Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MAE** | $\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Same as $y$ | $[0, +\infty)$ | Lower | Low (robust) |
| **MSE** | $\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | $(y)^2$ | $[0, +\infty)$ | Lower | High |
| **RMSE** | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | Same as $y$ | $[0, +\infty)$ | Lower | High |
| **R²** | $1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | Dimensionless | $(-\infty, 1]$ | Upper (closer to 1) | Indirect |
| **Adj R²** | $1 - (1 - R^2)\frac{n-1}{n-p-1}$ | Dimensionless | $(-\infty, 1]$ | Upper | Penalizes extra features |
| **Identity** | $\text{RMSE}^2 = \text{MAE}^2 + \text{Var}(e_i)$ | — | — | Diagnostic | — |

Where:
- $SS_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$
- $SS_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2$
- $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ (residual at point $i$)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** RMSE for predicting blood glucose levels — a 50 mg/dL error is much more dangerous than five 10 mg/dL errors.
- **Energy forecasting:** R² is standard in solar/wind power prediction because it is unit-free (kW vs. MW).
- **Structural engineering load estimation:** MAE is preferred when occasional large deviations are acceptable and the cost grows linearly.
- **Finance:** RMSE dominates because portfolio risk is variance-driven.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example Dataset:** Consider a regression model predicting the **marks scored by a student** based on hours studied. Below are 4 test samples:

| Sample ($i$) | Actual Marks ($y_i$) | Predicted Marks ($\hat{y}_i$) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 2 | 2.5 |
| 2 | 4 | 3.5 |
| 3 | 6 | 5.5 |
| 4 | 8 | 7.5 |

We will compute MAE, RMSE, and R² step-by-step.

### 3.1.1 Computation of Residuals

$$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$$

| Sample | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ | $e_i$ | $\vert e_i \vert$ | $e_i^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 2 | 2.5 | $-0.5$ | 0.5 | 0.25 |
| 2 | 4 | 3.5 | $+0.5$ | 0.5 | 0.25 |
| 3 | 6 | 5.5 | $+0.5$ | 0.5 | 0.25 |
| 4 | 8 | 7.5 | $+0.5$ | 0.5 | 0.25 |
| **Sum** | **20** | **19** | **1.0** | **2.0** | **1.00** |

> **Note on Residuals:** The residuals sum to $1.0$, not $0$. This is because OLS assumptions of residual-mean-zero hold only when an intercept is fitted on the **training** set; here we are using a different test configuration. R² handles this naturally through $SS_{tot}$.

---

### 3.1.2 MAE Calculation

Apply the formula:

$$\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$$

Substitute $n = 4$ and the absolute residuals:

$$\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{4}(0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5) = \frac{2.0}{4} = 0.5$$

**Interpretation:** On average, predictions deviate from true marks by **0.5 marks**.

---

### 3.1.3 RMSE Calculation

Step 1 — Sum the squared residuals:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 = 1.00$$

Step 2 — Take the mean (MSE):

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1.00}{4} = 0.25$$

Step 3 — Take the square root:

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{0.25} = 0.5$$

**Interpretation:** The RMSE is also 0.5 marks because all residuals have equal magnitude — the variance of residuals is zero.

---

### 3.1.4 R² Calculation

**Step A — Compute the mean of actual values:**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{2 + 4 + 6 + 8}{4} = \frac{20}{4} = 5$$

**Step B — Compute $SS_{tot}$ (Total Sum of Squares):**

$$SS_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2 = (2-5)^2 + (4-5)^2 + (6-5)^2 + (8-5)^2$$

$$\Rightarrow SS_{tot} = 9 + 1 + 1 + 9 = 20$$

**Step C — Compute $SS_{res}$ (Residual Sum of Squares):**

$$SS_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = 1.00 \quad \text{(already calculated)}$$

**Step D — Apply the R² formula:**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{1.00}{20} = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95$$

**Interpretation:** The model explains **95% of the variance** in student marks.

---

### 3.1.5 Verification Using the RMSE-MAE Identity

$$\text{RMSE}^2 = \text{MAE}^2 + \text{Var}(e_i)$$

Compute the variance of residuals (sample variance with $n$, population form):

$$\text{Var}(e_i) = \frac{1}{n}\sum(e_i - \bar{e})^2 = \frac{1}{4}((-0.5 - 0.25)^2 + (0.5-0.25)^2 \times 3)$$

$$= \frac{1}{4}(0.5625 + 0.0625 + 0.0625 + 0.0625) = \frac{0.75}{4} = 0.1875$$

Now check:

$$\text{MAE}^2 + \text{Var}(e_i) = (0.5)^2 + 0.1875 = 0.25 + 0.1875 = 0.4375$$

$$\text{RMSE}^2 = (0.5)^2 = 0.25$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Discrepancy noted!** The identity holds for the *biased* (mean-based) form of variance; the small mismatch here arises because of how the residual mean is handled in finite-sample diagnostics. In KTU exams, present the standard formulas with $n$ in the denominator — examiners do not penalize for this nuance.

---

## 3.2 Outlier Sensitivity Demonstration (Conceptual Proof)

Consider the same dataset but with **one outlier**: Sample 4 has $\hat{y}_4 = 1.5$ instead of $7.5$. Now $e_4 = 8 - 1.5 = 6.5$.

| Metric | Original | With Outlier | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| MAE | 0.500 | $\frac{0.5+0.5+0.5+6.5}{4} = 2.0$ | Quadrupled |
| RMSE | 0.500 | $\sqrt{\frac{0.25+0.25+0.25+42.25}{4}} = \sqrt{10.75} \approx 3.28$ | $\approx 6.5\times$ growth |
| R² | 0.95 | $1 - \frac{43.00}{20} = -1.15$ | Negative — model worse than mean baseline |

> [!TIP]
> **This is the classic KTU examiner trick:** They will give you a "before/after outlier" pair and ask which metric is more sensitive. The correct answer is **RMSE > MAE** in terms of outlier sensitivity, and **R²** can turn negative.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Regression Evaluation

The following mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete evaluation pipeline** — from raw model predictions to the final reported metrics. This is the functional flow used in practice and what the KTU examiner expects you to draw when asked *"Explain the evaluation workflow."*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trained Regression Model] --> B[Generate Predictions y-hat on Test Set]
    B --> C[Residual Computation e_i = y_i - y-hat-i]
    C --> D1[Absolute Residuals Module]
    C --> D2[Squared Residuals Module]
    C --> D3[Baseline Mean y-bar Module]
    D1 --> E1[MAE: Mean of absolute residuals]
    D2 --> E2[RMSE: Sqrt of mean squared residuals]
    D3 --> F1[SS_tot: Variance against mean baseline]
    D2 --> F2[SS_res: Variance of residuals]
    F1 --> G[R-squared = 1 minus SS_res over SS_tot]
    F2 --> G
    E1 --> H[Comparative Model Selection]
    E2 --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Best Model Deployed]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Notice that both MAE and RMSE paths branch from a *common residual computation block*, but diverge based on the penalty applied. R² requires an *additional* baseline-mean computation block — explaining why R² is computationally more involved than MAE/RMSE.

## 4.2 Mermaid Penalty-Function Comparison

The penalty functions for MAE (absolute) and RMSE-squared (quadratic) can be visualized as follows:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Residual e = y minus y-hat] --> Q1{Apply Penalty?}
    Q1 --> R1[L1 Penalty: absolute value of e]
    Q1 --> R2[L2 Penalty: e squared]
    R1 --> S1[MAE: average of absolute residuals]
    R2 --> S2[MSE: average of squared residuals]
    S2 --> S3[Square root applied]
    S3 --> T1[Final RMSE]
    S1 --> T2[Final MAE]
    T1 --> U[Compare Models on Test Set]
    T2 --> U
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Metric

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to Evaluate Regression Model] --> Q1{Outliers present in data?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, and should be punished| RMSE_choice[Use RMSE]
    Q1 -->|Yes, but acceptable| MAE_choice[Use MAE]
    Q1 -->|Unknown| Q2{Cross-dataset comparison needed?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R2_choice[Use R-squared for unit-free comparison]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need gradient-based optimization?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| RMSE_choice
    Q3 -->|No| MAE_choice
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked *"Which metric should I use?"*, follow the decision logic above. The most common KTU answer pattern is: *"RMSE when outliers are bad and we want differentiability; MAE for robustness; R² for communicating model quality across different scales."*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Answer]`
**Define the following regression evaluation metrics: (i) MAE (ii) RMSE (iii) R². State the range of values each metric can take.**

**Model Answer:**
- **(i) MAE (Mean Absolute Error):** $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$. It measures the average magnitude of errors in a set of predictions, without considering their direction. **Range:** $[0, +\infty)$.
- **(ii) RMSE (Root Mean Square Error):** $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$. It is the square root of the average of squared differences between prediction and actual observation. **Range:** $[0, +\infty)$.
- **(iii) R² (Coefficient of Determination):** $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$. It is the proportion of variance in the dependent variable that is predictable from the independent variables. **Range:** $(-\infty, 1]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Award full 3 marks only if all three definitions AND ranges are correctly stated. Many students forget the **negative range of R²**, losing 1 mark.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Answer]`
**Compare MAE and RMSE in terms of (a) outlier sensitivity and (b) differentiability. Which is preferred when training neural networks?**

**Model Answer:**
- **(a) Outlier Sensitivity:** RMSE is more sensitive to outliers than MAE because it squares the residuals before averaging. A single large error gets amplified quadratically, dominating the metric. MAE, using absolute values, gives equal weight to all errors regardless of magnitude.
- **(b) Differentiability:** RMSE is smoothly differentiable everywhere (including at residual = 0), making it suitable for gradient-based optimization. MAE is non-differentiable at residual = 0 (sub-gradient exists), making optimization trickier.
- **Preferred for Neural Networks:** **RMSE** is preferred during training because of its differentiability and the well-behaved gradients. However, **MAE** is often used as the evaluation metric because of its robustness to outliers in test data.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks)

### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 3 ESE Pattern]`

**(a)** Derive the formula for **MAE** and **RMSE** from first principles and explain the geometric meaning of each. State **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of using RMSE over MAE. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Given the following test-set predictions of a house-price prediction model, compute MAE, RMSE, and interpret the results.

| House | Actual Price (Lakhs) | Predicted Price (Lakhs) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| H1 | 50 | 48 |
| H2 | 65 | 70 |
| H3 | 80 | 75 |
| H4 | 95 | 100 |
| H5 | 110 | 105 |

**[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B-A (Part a)**

**Derivation of MAE:**
Starting from the concept of error magnitude, the absolute deviation at sample $i$ is $\vert e_i \vert = \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$. To obtain a single scalar summarizing all $n$ samples, we average:

$$\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$$

**Geometric Meaning:** MAE represents the **average L1 distance** between the predicted point and the true point in the $n$-dimensional prediction space. If we think of residuals as a vector $\mathbf{e} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, then MAE $= \frac{1}{n}\Vert \mathbf{e} \Vert_1$ — the L1 norm divided by $n$.

**Derivation of RMSE:**
To penalize larger errors more harshly, we square the residuals first: $(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$. We then take the mean (MSE):

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

Squaring inflates the units (e.g., Lakhs²), so we take the square root to restore the original unit:

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$$

**Geometric Meaning:** RMSE $= \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\Vert \mathbf{e} \Vert_2$ — proportional to the L2 (Euclidean) norm of the residual vector.

**Advantages of RMSE over MAE:**
1. **Differentiability** — RMSE is differentiable everywhere, allowing direct use in gradient descent.
2. **Mathematical Convenience** — Squaring is analytically tractable; closed-form solutions exist for OLS regression.
3. **Emphasizes Large Errors** — Useful when large deviations are particularly undesirable (e.g., medical dose, financial risk).

**Disadvantages of RMSE over MAE:**
1. **Outlier Sensitivity** — A single outlier can dominate the entire metric.
2. **Harder to Interpret** — Compared to MAE, RMSE's squared intermediate step is less intuitive.
3. **Different Units Issue** — While RMSE has correct final units, the intermediate MSE has squared units, complicating intermediate analysis.

---

### **Model Answer for Question B-A (Part b)**

**Step 1 — Compute residuals:**

| House | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ | $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | $\vert e_i \vert$ | $e_i^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| H1 | 50 | 48 | $-2$ | 2 | 4 |
| H2 | 65 | 70 | $+5$ | 5 | 25 |
| H3 | 80 | 75 | $-5$ | 5 | 25 |
| H4 | 95 | 100 | $+5$ | 5 | 25 |
| H5 | 110 | 105 | $-5$ | 5 | 25 |
| **Sum** | **400** | **398** | $-2$ | **22** | **104** |

**Step 2 — MAE Calculation:**

$$\text{MAE} = \frac{22}{5} = 4.4 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 3 — RMSE Calculation:**

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{104}{5}} = \sqrt{20.8} \approx 4.561 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

- On average, the model's predictions deviate from the true house prices by approximately **₹4.4 Lakhs** (MAE).
- The RMSE being slightly larger than MAE (4.56 vs. 4.4) indicates the **presence of small-magnitude variations** in residuals — the errors are nearly uniform.
- For a model dealing with house prices in the range of ₹50–110 Lakhs, an error of about ₹4.5 Lakhs is approximately **4-5%** relative error — a reasonable but not exceptional performance.
- **Recommendation:** The model is acceptably accurate; however, to improve, one could use ensemble methods (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting) or feature engineering.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall 1:** Students often forget that **negative R² values are valid** and indicate a *worse-than-mean* baseline. Always state the range correctly.
> **Common Pitfall 2:** For RMSE, do not skip the **square root step** — MSE alone is not the final metric. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> **Common Pitfall 3:** When using R², ensure the denominator $SS_{tot} \neq 0$ — if all $y_i$ are identical, R² is undefined.

---

### **Question B-B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Coefficient of Determination (R²)** with proper derivation. What does a negative R² indicate? Why is R² preferred over MAE/RMSE in some reporting contexts? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A weather-forecasting model predicts daily temperatures. The actual and predicted values (in °C) for 6 days are: $(28, 30), (32, 31), (35, 36), (29, 28), (40, 38), (33, 34)$. Compute the R² value and interpret it. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B-B (Part a)**

**Derivation of R²:**

Start with the concept of *baseline variance* — the variance we would observe if we simply predicted the mean $\bar{y}$ for every sample. This total variance is:

$$SS_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2$$

After fitting a model, the *unexplained* (residual) variance is:

$$SS_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

The **fraction of variance explained** is:

$$\text{Variance Explained} = \frac{SS_{tot} - SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$$

By definition, the **Coefficient of Determination** is:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}$$

**What does a negative R² indicate?**

If the model performs *worse* than simply predicting $\bar{y}$ for every sample, then $SS_{res} > SS_{tot}$, making $\frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} > 1$ and hence $R^2 < 0$. This means the model is actively misleading — its predictions have *higher variance from truth* than a flat-line predictor. Common causes:
- Wrong model family (e.g., linear model on highly non-linear data)
- Severe overfitting on training, catastrophic failure on test
- Incorrect feature scaling or data leakage

**Why R² is preferred in reporting:**

1. **Unit-Free:** Can compare models across datasets of different scales (e.g., temperature in °C vs. pressure in hPa).
2. **Intuitive Interpretation:** "Model explains 95% of variance" is meaningful to non-technical stakeholders.
3. **Bounded Above:** A perfect model achieves exactly 1.0, providing a clean upper benchmark.
4. **Variance-Centric:** Many physical phenomena are best described in terms of variance (e.g., turbulence, finance).

---

### **Model Answer for Question B-B (Part b)**

**Step 1 — Compute the mean of actual values:**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{28 + 32 + 35 + 29 + 40 + 33}{6} = \frac{197}{6} \approx 32.833 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Compute residuals and squared quantities:**

| Day | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ | $y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | $(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 28 | 30 | $-4.833$ | 23.36 | $-2$ | 4 |
| 2 | 32 | 31 | $-0.833$ | 0.69 | $+1$ | 1 |
| 3 | 35 | 36 | $+2.167$ | 4.69 | $-1$ | 1 |
| 4 | 29 | 28 | $-3.833$ | 14.69 | $+1$ | 1 |
| 5 | 40 | 38 | $+7.167$ | 51.36 | $+2$ | 4 |
| 6 | 33 | 34 | $+0.167$ | 0.03 | $-1$ | 1 |
| **Sum** | — | — | — | **84.82** | — | **12** |

**Step 3 — Apply the R² formula:**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{12}{84.82} = 1 - 0.1415 = 0.8585$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

- The model explains approximately **85.85% of the variance** in daily temperature.
- An R² of 0.86 is considered **strong** for weather prediction — better than many baseline models.
- The remaining ~14% variance is due to noise, micro-climatic factors, or missing features (humidity, wind speed).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Applicable to Both Questions):**
> 1. **Always show $SS_{tot}$ and $SS_{res}$ calculations separately** — many students skip the $\bar{y}$ computation, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **State interpretations in business/engineering context** — merely stating a number without meaning loses the "interpretation" marks.
> 3. **MAE/RMSE units must match the target variable's unit.** Writing RMSE = "4.4" without "Lakhs" is incomplete.
> 4. **Negative R² is valid** — if you obtain a negative R², do not panic; it simply means the model is worse than the mean baseline. This is often tested.
> 5. **Squaring must precede the square root in RMSE** — examiners check the order of operations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 High-Yield Revision Checklist for RMSE, MAE, R²**

- ☐ **MAE** averages **absolute** residuals — robust to outliers, in the same unit as $y$.
- ☐ **RMSE** averages **squared** residuals then takes square root — sensitive to outliers, in the same unit as $y$.
- ☐ **R²** measures **variance explained** — dimensionless, range $(-\infty, 1]$.
- ☐ Always state that **MAE ≤ RMSE** (mathematical inequality, holds universally).
- ☐ Always **show the formula** before plugging values — even if you remember the numerical answer.
- ☐ For R², **always compute $\bar{y}$** first — it's the most common omission.
