- ☐ A **negative R²** is valid and indicates the model performs worse than the mean predictor.
- ☐ RMSE is **differentiable everywhere**; MAE has a **sub-gradient** at 0 — important for optimization choice.
- ☐ R² = 0.95 does **NOT** mean 95% of points are predicted correctly — it means 95% of *variance* is captured.
- ☐ For multi-feature regression, use **Adjusted R²** instead of plain R² to penalize unnecessary features.
- ☐ When the dataset has **outliers**, prefer **MAE** for reporting (RMSE can be misleading).
- ☐ **Identity to memorize:** $\text{RMSE}^2 = \text{MAE}^2 + \text{Var}(e)$ — frequently asked in viva.
- ☐ **Units discipline:** MAE/RMSE carry the unit of $y$; R² is unit-free.
- ☐ Always **interpret the metric** in domain context (e.g., "₹4.4 Lakhs error on houses worth ₹50–110 Lakhs").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification Algorithms: Logistic regression, decision trees, and k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST785 - ALGORITHMS FOR DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification Algorithms: Logistic regression, decision trees, and k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Classification?

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification** is a **supervised learning** paradigm in data science where the objective is to predict a discrete, categorical output variable $y \in \{0, 1, 2, ..., K-1\}$ (where $K$ is the number of classes) given a feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$. Formally, we seek a hypothesis function $h: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ that maps input space to a finite set of class labels.

**Binary Classification:** When $K = 2$ (e.g., Spam/Not Spam, Fraud/Legit).
**Multiclass Classification:** When $K \geq 3$ (e.g., Digit recognition 0-9, Iris species).

The three flagship algorithms studied in this module represent three fundamentally different **inductive biases**:

| Algorithm | Inductive Bias | Decision Boundary Type |
|---|---|---|
| Logistic Regression | Linear | Linear (hyperplane) |
| Decision Tree | Axis-aligned splits | Piecewise rectangular |
| k-Nearest Neighbors | Local instance-based | Arbitrary (Voronoi cells) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Three Doctors

Imagine you walk into a hospital with symptoms (fever, cough, fatigue). Three doctors diagnose you differently:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 🩺 **Doctor Logistic (Logistic Regression):** Draws a straight line through a chart of past patients. New patients above the line = "Sick", below = "Healthy". He assumes **one straight line can separate the world**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 🌳 **Doctor Tree (Decision Tree):** Asks a series of yes/no questions: "Fever > 101°F?" → If yes, "Cough present?" → If yes, "Duration > 3 days?" → Final verdict. He builds a **flowchart of rules**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 🏘️ **Doctor Neighbour (k-NN):** Looks at the 5 patients most similar to you (same age, symptoms, history) and lets them vote. If 4 out of 5 had flu, you have flu. He **memorizes the hospital and votes locally**.

---

## 1.3 The Supervised Learning Setup

All three algorithms operate on a labeled training dataset:

$$\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, y^{(1)}), (\mathbf{x}^{(2)}, y^{(2)}), \ldots, (\mathbf{x}^{(m)}, y^{(m)})\}$$

where $\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is a feature vector and $y^{(i)} \in \{0, 1, \ldots, K-1\}$ is the class label.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The module emphasizes that classification algorithms differ in their **assumptions about the data distribution**, **computational complexity during training vs. inference**, and **interpretability**. KTU examiners frequently test the trade-offs between parametric (Logistic) and non-parametric (k-NN) models.

---

## 1.4 Why These Three? KTU Syllabus Justification

These algorithms are foundational because they span the **entire taxonomy of learning paradigms**:

1. **Logistic Regression** → Probabilistic, parametric, differentiable (gradient-based optimization)
2. **Decision Trees** → Non-probabilistic, non-parametric, greedy heuristic (information gain)
3. **k-NN** → Instance-based, lazy learning, no explicit training phase

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examinable Distinction:** Logistic Regression and Decision Trees both have a *training phase* (they "learn" parameters or tree structure). k-NN has **no training phase** — it is a *lazy learner* that defers all computation to prediction time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logistic Regression

### 2.1.1 The Core Idea

Logistic Regression models the **probability** that a given input belongs to class 1, using the **logistic (sigmoid) function**:

$$P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sigma(\mathbf{z}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\mathbf{z}}}$$

where $\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \ldots + \theta_n x_n$.

### 2.1.2 The Sigmoid Function — Why It?

The sigmoid function $\sigma: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow (0, 1)$ has three crucial properties:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Bounded output:** $\sigma(z) \in (0, 1)$ → naturally interpreted as probability
> 2. **Monotonically increasing:** preserves the ordering of linear scores
> 3. **Differentiable everywhere:** enables gradient descent optimization

**Step-by-step intuition:**
- Start with linear regression: $z = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}$ produces a real-valued score
- For classification, we need a score in $[0, 1]$ that represents probability
- $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ squashes any real number into $(0,1)$
- At $z=0$: $\sigma(0) = 0.5$ (decision boundary)
- For large $z$: $\sigma(z) \to 1$
- For large negative $z$: $\sigma(z) \to 0$

### 2.1.3 The Decision Rule

After computing $\hat{p} = \sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x})$, classify as:

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \hat{p} \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{if } \hat{p} < 0.5 \end{cases}$$

The **decision boundary** is the locus where $\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x} = 0$ — a **hyperplane** in feature space.

### 2.1.4 Cost Function: Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss)

We **cannot** use Mean Squared Error because the sigmoid makes the cost function non-convex. Instead, the **log-loss** is used:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{p}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) \right]$$

**Why log loss?** It arises from the principle of **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**: if each sample is independently Bernoulli-distributed, maximizing the likelihood is equivalent to minimizing the negative log-likelihood, which yields the formula above.

### 2.1.5 Gradient Descent Update Rule

Taking the partial derivative of $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ with respect to each $\theta_j$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

Update rule:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate**.

### 2.1.6 Multiclass Extension: Softmax Regression

For $K > 2$ classes, generalize the sigmoid to the **softmax function**:

$$P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\Theta}) = \frac{e^{\boldsymbol{\theta}_k^T \mathbf{x}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{\boldsymbol{\theta}_j^T \mathbf{x}}}$$

This ensures the probabilities sum to 1 across all $K$ classes.

---

## 2.2 Decision Trees

### 2.2.1 The Core Idea

A decision tree recursively partitions the feature space using **axis-aligned splits** (each split is along a single feature dimension), creating a tree of decision rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology:** Each internal node represents a test on a feature (e.g., "Is $x_2 < 0.7$?"). Each branch represents the outcome of that test. Each leaf node represents a class label. The path from root to leaf is a **classification rule**.

### 2.2.2 The CART Algorithm (Classification and Regression Trees)

The most widely taught algorithm in KTU. It uses a **greedy, top-down recursive partitioning** approach:

1. Start at the root with all training data
2. For each candidate split (feature $j$, threshold $s$ that partitions into $x_j \leq s$ and $x_j > s$):
   - Compute the impurity of the resulting child nodes
3. Choose the split that produces the **greatest reduction in impurity**
4. Recurse on each child node
5. Stop when a stopping criterion is met (max depth, min samples, pure node)

### 2.2.3 Impurity Measures

For a node $m$ containing $N_m$ samples with class proportions $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_K$ (where $p_k$ is the fraction of class $k$ samples in node $m$):

**Entropy Impurity:**

$$H(N_m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k \log_2(p_k)$$

- $H = 0$ when the node is **pure** (all one class)
- $H = \log_2 K$ when classes are uniformly distributed (maximum impurity)

**Gini Impurity** (used by default in scikit-learn's CART):

$$G(N_m) = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k^2$$

- $G = 0$ when pure
- $G = 1 - \frac{1}{K}$ when uniformly distributed

### 2.2.4 Information Gain

The quality of a candidate split is measured by the **reduction in impurity**:

$$\Delta = I(\text{parent}) - \sum_{\text{children}} \frac{N_{\text{child}}}{N_{\text{parent}}} \cdot I(\text{child})$$

where $I$ is the impurity measure (Entropy or Gini). We choose the split that **maximizes** $\Delta$.

### 2.2.5 Prediction at a Leaf

For classification, the leaf predicts the **majority class** of the training samples in that leaf:

$$\hat{y}_{\text{leaf}} = \arg\max_{k} \left( \sum_{i \in \text{leaf}} \mathbb{1}[y^{(i)} = k] \right)$$

### 2.2.6 Overfitting Control: Pre-pruning and Post-pruning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-pruning (early stopping):** Set constraints *before* tree building: `max_depth`, `min_samples_split`, `min_samples_leaf`, `max_features`. These halt growth early.
>
> **Post-pruning (cost-complexity pruning):** Build the full tree, then *prune back* subtrees that do not improve validation performance. Uses the cost-complexity measure: $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$ where $R(T)$ is the misclassification rate and $\vert T \vert$ is the number of leaves.

---

## 2.3 k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)

### 2.3.1 The Core Idea

k-NN is a **non-parametric, instance-based** learning algorithm. It makes no assumptions about the underlying data distribution and **stores the entire training set**. Predictions are made by:

1. Compute the distance from the query point $\mathbf{x}_q$ to **every** training point
2. Identify the $k$ nearest training points
3. For classification, take a **majority vote** of their labels

### 2.3.2 Distance Metrics

The choice of distance metric is critical. For $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}' \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$:

**Euclidean Distance (L2 norm):**

$$d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n} (x_j - x_j')^2} = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}' \Vert_2$$

**Manhattan Distance (L1 norm):**

$$d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_j - x_j' \vert = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}' \Vert_1$$

**Minkowski Distance (generalized):**

$$d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_j - x_j' \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$$

### 2.3.3 Majority Vote (Classification)

For a query $\mathbf{x}_q$, let $\mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}_q)$ be the set of its $k$ nearest neighbors. The predicted class is:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}} \sum_{\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in \mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}_q)} \mathbb{1}[y^{(i)} = k]$$

### 2.3.4 The Curse of Dimensionality

> [!WARNING]
> As the number of features $n$ grows, the volume of feature space grows exponentially. All training points become roughly equidistant from any query. This means k-NN's notion of "nearness" becomes meaningless in high dimensions. **Dimensionality reduction (PCA) or feature selection is essential before applying k-NN to high-dimensional data.**

### 2.3.5 Choosing $k$ and the Bias-Variance Trade-off

- **Small $k$ (e.g., $k=1$):** Low bias, high variance → sensitive to noise, overfits
- **Large $k$:** High bias, low variance → smoother decision boundary, may underfit
- **Optimal $k$:** Determined via **cross-validation** (typically odd values to avoid ties)

### 2.3.6 Computational Complexity

| Operation | Complexity |
|---|---|
| Training | $O(1)$ (lazy learner — just stores data) |
| Prediction (brute force) | $O(m \cdot n + m \log m)$ per query |
| Prediction (KD-Tree) | $O(\log m)$ average, $O(m)$ worst case |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for this module. Memorize these formulas and their boundary conditions.

| # | Algorithm | Formula / Concept | Boundary / Special Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | $\sigma(0) = 0.5$; output range $(0,1)$ |
| 2 | Log Loss | $J = -\frac{1}{m}\sum[y\log\hat{p} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{p})]$ | Convex; minimized at $\hat{p}=y$ |
| 3 | Gradient | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum(\hat{p}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$ | Same form as linear regression! |
| 4 | Softmax | $P(y=k) = \frac{e^{\boldsymbol{\theta}_k^T \mathbf{x}}}{\sum_j e^{\boldsymbol{\theta}_j^T \mathbf{x}}}$ | $\sum_{k=1}^{K} P(y=k) = 1$ |
| 5 | Entropy | $H = -\sum_k p_k \log_2 p_k$ | $H = 0$ if pure; max $= \log_2 K$ |
| 6 | Gini | $G = 1 - \sum_k p_k^2$ | $G = 0$ if pure; max $= 1 - 1/K$ |
| 7 | Info Gain | $\Delta = I(\text{parent}) - \sum \frac{N_c}{N_p} I(c)$ | Always $\Delta \geq 0$ for any split |
| 8 | Euclidean | $d = \sqrt{\sum_j (x_j - x_j')^2}$ | Most common; sensitive to scale |
| 9 | Manhattan | $d = \sum_j \vert x_j - x_j' \vert$ | Robust to outliers; L1 norm |
| 10 | k-NN Vote | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k} \mathbb{1}[y^{(i)}=k]$ | Use **odd** $k$ for binary tasks |
| 11 | Bayes Optimal | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k P(y=k \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Theoretical upper bound for accuracy |
| 12 | Odds Ratio | $\frac{p}{1-p} = e^{\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}}$ | Linear in features (log-odds) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Logistic Regression:** Credit scoring, medical diagnosis, click-through rate prediction (where probability calibration matters)
> - **Decision Trees:** Medical diagnosis protocols, loan approval systems, fault diagnosis (where human interpretability is critical)
> - **k-NN:** Recommender systems, anomaly detection, image retrieval, handwriting recognition (when decision boundary is highly irregular)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sigmoid Function from Log-Odds

**Step 1: Define the odds of class 1**

For a binary outcome, the **odds** of the positive class are defined as:

$$\text{odds} = \frac{p}{1 - p} = \frac{P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x})}{P(y=0 \mid \mathbf{x})}$$

where $p$ is shorthand for $P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x})$.

**Step 2: Assert that log-odds are linear in features**

This is the **key modeling assumption** of logistic regression. We assert:

$$\log\left( \frac{p}{1 - p} \right) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this assumption?** It guarantees $p \in (0, 1)$ automatically (since odds is always positive), it is monotonically increasing in $\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}$, and the log transform ensures numerical stability when $p \to 0$ or $p \to 1$.

**Step 3: Solve for $p$**

Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{p}{1 - p} = e^{\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}}$$

Multiply both sides by $(1-p)$:

$$p = (1 - p) \cdot e^{\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}}$$

Expand the right-hand side:

$$p = e^{\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}} - p \cdot e^{\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}}$$

Collect $p$ terms on the left:

$$p + p \cdot e^{\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}} = e^{\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}}$$

Factor out $p$:

$$p \left(1 + e^{\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}}\right) = e^{\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}}$$

Isolate $p$:

$$p = \frac{e^{\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}}}{1 + e^{\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}}}$$

**Step 4: Convert to standard sigmoid form**

Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{-\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}}$:

$$p = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}}}$$

This is the **sigmoid function** $\sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x})$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Log-Loss Cost Function (MLE)

**Step 1: Model assumptions**

Assume each training example is **independently drawn** from a Bernoulli distribution:

$$P(y^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \hat{p}^{(i) y^{(i)}} \cdot (1 - \hat{p}^{(i)})^{1 - y^{(i)}}$$

where $\hat{p}^{(i)} = \sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)})$.

**Step 2: Form the likelihood**

The likelihood of the entire dataset is the product of individual probabilities (due to independence):

$$L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(y^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \hat{p}^{(i) y^{(i)}} (1 - \hat{p}^{(i)})^{1 - y^{(i)}}$$

**Step 3: Take the log-likelihood**

Since $\log$ is monotonically increasing, maximizing $L$ is equivalent to maximizing $\log L$:

$$\log L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{p}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log (1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) \right]$$

**Step 4: Form the cost function**

We convert to a minimization problem by negating and averaging (so that gradient descent minimizes):

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{m} \log L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{p}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log (1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) \right]$$

This is the **Binary Cross-Entropy / Log Loss**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Gradient Update Rule

**Step 1: Compute the derivative of the sigmoid**

A beautiful property of the sigmoid:

$$\frac{d\sigma(z)}{dz} = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$$

Derivation:

$$\frac{d}{dz}\left(\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}\right) = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1+e^{-z})^2} = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1+e^{-z}} = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$$

**Step 2: Apply the chain rule to a single training example**

For sample $i$, the loss is:

$$\ell^{(i)} = -\left[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{p}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) \right]$$

Differentiate with respect to $\theta_j$:

$$\frac{\partial \ell^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} = -\left[ y^{(i)} \cdot \frac{1}{\hat{p}^{(i)}} - (1 - y^{(i)}) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}} \right] \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{p}^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the sigmoid derivative**

Since $\frac{\partial \hat{p}^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} = \hat{p}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$:

$$\frac{\partial \ell^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} = -\left[ \frac{y^{(i)}}{\hat{p}^{(i)}} - \frac{1 - y^{(i)}}{1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}} \right] \cdot \hat{p}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the bracket**

$$-\left[ \frac{y^{(i)}(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) - (1 - y^{(i)})\hat{p}^{(i)}}{\hat{p}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)})} \right] \cdot \hat{p}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

The denominator cancels with $\hat{p}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)})$ in the multiplication:

$$= -\left[ y^{(i)}(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) - (1 - y^{(i)})\hat{p}^{(i)} \right] x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 5: Expand the bracket**

$$= -\left[ y^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\hat{p}^{(i)} - \hat{p}^{(i)} + y^{(i)}\hat{p}^{(i)} \right] x_j^{(i)}$$

$$= -\left[ y^{(i)} - \hat{p}^{(i)} \right] x_j^{(i)} = \left( \hat{p}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 6: Average over all training examples**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{p}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

This has the **exact same form as the linear regression gradient** — a satisfying and elegant result! $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Logistic Regression by Hand

**Problem:** Given training data with $\theta = [0.3, 0.5]^T$, $\mathbf{x} = [1, 2]^T$, $y = 1$. Compute one gradient descent step with $\alpha = 0.1$.

**Step 1: Compute linear combination**

$$z = \theta_0 x_0 + \theta_1 x_1 = 0.3 \times 1 + 0.5 \times 2 = 0.3 + 1.0 = 1.3$$

(assuming $x_0 = 1$ for the bias term).

**Step 2: Apply sigmoid**

$$\hat{p} = \sigma(1.3) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-1.3}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.2725} = \frac{1}{1.2725} \approx 0.7858$$

**Step 3: Compute error**

$$\hat{p} - y = 0.7858 - 1 = -0.2142$$

**Step 4: Compute gradients**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{1}(-0.2142)(1) = -0.2142$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{1}(-0.2142)(2) = -0.4284$$

**Step 5: Update parameters**

$$\theta_0^{\text{new}} = 0.3 - 0.1 \times (-0.2142) = 0.3 + 0.02142 = 0.32142$$

$$\theta_1^{\text{new}} = 0.5 - 0.1 \times (-0.4284) = 0.5 + 0.04284 = 0.54284$$

**Step 6: Verify improvement**

The error reduced slightly (became less negative), indicating the parameters moved in the correct direction. The probability of the positive class for this sample will now be higher.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Entropy and Information Gain

**Problem:** A parent node has 10 samples: 6 of class A, 4 of class B. A candidate split produces:
- Left child: 5 samples (5 A, 0 B) — pure!
- Right child: 5 samples (1 A, 4 B)

Compute entropy at parent, children, and the information gain.

**Step 1: Parent entropy**

$$H(\text{parent}) = -\frac{6}{10}\log_2\frac{6}{10} - \frac{4}{10}\log_2\frac{4}{10}$$

$$= -0.6 \times (-0.7370) - 0.4 \times (-1.3219) = 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2: Left child entropy (pure)**

$$H(\text{left}) = -\frac{5}{5}\log_2\frac{5}{5} - \frac{0}{5}\log_2\frac{0}{5}$$

By convention, $0 \log 0 = 0$:

$$H(\text{left}) = 0 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3: Right child entropy**

$$H(\text{right}) = -\frac{1}{5}\log_2\frac{1}{5} - \frac{4}{5}\log_2\frac{4}{5}$$

$$= -0.2 \times (-2.3219) - 0.8 \times (-0.3219) = 0.4644 + 0.2575 = 0.7219 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4: Weighted children entropy**

$$H(\text{children}) = \frac{5}{10}(0) + \frac{5}{10}(0.7219) = 0 + 0.3610 = 0.3610 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5: Information gain**

$$\Delta = H(\text{parent}) - H(\text{children}) = 0.9710 - 0.3610 = 0.6100 \text{ bits}$$

This is a **good split** — it reduced impurity by ~63%.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example: k-NN Classification

**Problem:** Query point $\mathbf{x}_q = (2, 3)$. Training points:
- $A_1 = (1, 2)$, class 0
- $A_2 = (2, 4)$, class 1
- $A_3 = (3, 5)$, class 1
- $A_4 = (4, 4)$, class 0
- $A_5 = (5, 6)$, class 1

Classify using $k=3$ with Euclidean distance.

**Step 1: Compute distances**

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, A_1) = \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (3-2)^2} = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, A_2) = \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (3-4)^2} = \sqrt{0+1} = 1.000$$

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, A_3) = \sqrt{(2-3)^2 + (3-5)^2} = \sqrt{1+4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$$

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, A_4) = \sqrt{(2-4)^2 + (3-4)^2} = \sqrt{4+1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$$

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, A_5) = \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (3-6)^2} = \sqrt{9+9} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.243$$

**Step 2: Identify 3 nearest neighbors**

| Rank | Point | Distance | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_2$ | 1.000 | 1 |
| 2 | $A_1$ | 1.414 | 0 |
| 3 | $A_3$ (or $A_4$) | 2.236 | 1 |

**Step 3: Majority vote**

Vote count: Class 0 → 1 vote ($A_1$), Class 1 → 2 votes ($A_2, A_3$).

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max (\text{Class 0: 1, Class 1: 2}) = 1$$

**Step 4: Final prediction**

The query point $\mathbf{x}_q = (2, 3)$ is classified as **Class 1**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.7 Comparative Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is a **favourite KTU exam question** — students must compare these algorithms on standard axes.

| Criterion | Logistic Regression | Decision Tree | k-NN |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Parametric | Non-parametric | Non-parametric |
| **Training** | Iterative (gradient descent) | Greedy recursive split | None (lazy) |
| **Inference Speed** | $O(n)$ (fast) | $O(\text{depth})$ (fast) | $O(mn)$ (slow) |
| **Memory** | $O(n)$ | $O(\text{nodes})$ | $O(mn)$ (stores all) |
| **Interpretability** | Medium (coefficients) | **High** (rules) | **Low** (memorized) |
| **Probabilistic Output** | **Yes** (calibrated) | No (leaf proportions) | No (vote fractions) |
| **Decision Boundary** | Linear (hyperplane) | Axis-aligned rectangles | Arbitrary (Voronoi) |
| **Feature Scaling Needed?** | **Yes** | No | **Yes** |
| **Handles Non-linearity** | Poor (needs features) | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Curse of Dimensionality** | Robust | Moderate | **Severe** |
| **Multiclass Native** | Needs extension (softmax) | **Yes** | **Yes** (natural) |
| **Sensitive to Outliers** | Moderate | Low (unless deep) | **High** |
| **Online Learning** | Yes (SGD) | No (retrain) | **Yes** (just add) |

## 4.1 The Classification Algorithm Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
```

## 4.2 Logistic Regression Functional Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Features X] --> B[Linear Combination]
    B --> C["z = theta transpose x"]
    C --> D[Sigmoid Function]
    D --> E[Probability Score 0 to 1]
    E --> F{Threshold 0.5}
    F -->|Greater Equal 0.5| G[Predict Class 1]
    F -->|Less 0.5| H[Predict Class 0]
    G --> I[Compare to True Label y]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute Log Loss]
    J --> K[Gradient Descent Update]
    K --> B
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree Construction Flow (CART)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start Root Node All Data] --> B[For Each Feature]
    B --> C[For Each Threshold]
    C --> D[Split Data into Left and Right]
    D --> E[Compute Impurity Left]
    D --> F[Compute Impurity Right]
    E --> G[Compute Weighted Children Impurity]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute Information Gain]
    H --> I{Split With Maximum Gain}
    I --> J[Create Child Nodes]
    J --> K{Stopping Criterion Met}
    K -->|No| L[Recurse on Child Node]
    L --> B
    K -->|Yes| M[Assign Leaf Label Majority Class]
```


# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define logistic regression. Why is the sigmoid function used in logistic regression instead of a linear function?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Logistic regression is a **supervised learning classification algorithm** used to predict the probability of a binary or categorical outcome. Unlike linear regression which predicts continuous values, logistic regression models $P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ using a linear combination of features passed through a non-linear activation.

**Why sigmoid (1.5 marks):** The sigmoid function $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ is used because:
1. It maps any real-valued input to the range $(0, 1)$, which is a valid probability range
2. It is **monotonically increasing**, preserving the ordering of the linear scores
3. It is **differentiable everywhere**, enabling gradient-based optimization
4. It is naturally derived from the assumption that **log-odds are linear in features**: $\log(p/(1-p)) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T\mathbf{x}$

A linear function $\boldsymbol{\theta}^T\mathbf{x}$ would output values outside $[0,1]$, making it invalid as a probability. Sigmoid elegantly resolves this.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Compare parametric and non-parametric models. Give one example of each from the algorithms studied.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Parametric | Non-Parametric |
|---|---|---|
| **Assumption** | Fixed functional form | No fixed form; grows with data |
| **Parameters** | Fixed number | Grows with training set size |
| **Training** | Iterative optimization | None or greedy partitioning |
| **Data Need** | Less data (efficient) | More data needed |
| **Example** | **Logistic Regression** (parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$) | **Decision Tree / k-NN** |

**Logistic Regression (1 mark):** Parametric — has a fixed set of weights $\theta_0, \theta_1, \ldots, \theta_n$ learned from data. Number of parameters does not grow with $m$.

**Decision Tree (1 mark):** Non-parametric — tree structure (number of nodes, depth) grows with the data. No fixed parameter vector is learned.

**k-NN (bonus):** Also non-parametric — it stores the entire training set, so "complexity" grows with $m$.

---

## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts typically split as (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. The internal choice means you answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the Logistic Regression algorithm in detail. Derive the cost function (log loss) using the principle of maximum likelihood estimation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a binary classification problem, the following training data is given:

| $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $y$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | 1.5 | 0 |
| 1.0 | 2.0 | 0 |
| 1.5 | 2.5 | 1 |
| 2.0 | 3.0 | 1 |

Initialize $\theta_0 = 0.1$, $\theta_1 = 0.2$, $\theta_2 = 0.1$. Perform **one iteration** of gradient descent with learning rate $\alpha = 0.1$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Hypothesis representation (2 marks)**

Logistic regression models the probability of class 1 as:

$$\hat{p} = h_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}}}$$

The decision rule is $\hat{y} = 1$ if $\hat{p} \geq 0.5$, else $\hat{y} = 0$.

**Step 2: Likelihood formulation (2 marks)**

Under the assumption of independent Bernoulli trials, the likelihood is:

$$L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \hat{p}^{(i) y^{(i)}} (1 - \hat{p}^{(i)})^{1 - y^{(i)}}$$

**Step 3: Log-likelihood (1.5 marks)**

$$\log L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{p}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log (1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) \right]$$

**Step 4: Cost function (1.5 marks)**

Negate and average to form the minimization objective:

$$\boxed{J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{p}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log (1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) \right]}$$

This is the **binary cross-entropy / log loss**. It is convex in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, ensuring gradient descent converges to the global minimum.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Augment data with bias column** $\theta = [0.1, 0.2, 0.1]^T$

| $x_0$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $y$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.5 | 1.5 | 0 |
| 1 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 0 |
| 1 | 1.5 | 2.5 | 1 |
| 1 | 2.0 | 3.0 | 1 |

**Step 2: Compute predictions $\hat{p}^{(i)}$ for each sample**

Sample 1: $z^{(1)} = 0.1(1) + 0.2(0.5) + 0.1(1.5) = 0.1 + 0.1 + 0.15 = 0.35$
$\hat{p}^{(1)} = \sigma(0.35) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.35}} = \frac{1}{1.419} = 0.7046$

Sample 2: $z^{(2)} = 0.1(1) + 0.2(1.0) + 0.1(2.0) = 0.1 + 0.2 + 0.2 = 0.5$
$\hat{p}^{(2)} = \sigma(0.5) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.5}} = \frac{1}{1.6065} = 0.6225$

Sample 3: $z^{(3)} = 0.1(1) + 0.2(1.5) + 0.1(2.5) = 0.1 + 0.3 + 0.25 = 0.65$
$\hat{p}^{(3)} = \sigma(0.65) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.65}} = \frac{1}{1.522} = 0.6570$

Sample 4: $z^{(4)} = 0.1(1) + 0.2(2.0) + 0.1(3.0) = 0.1 + 0.4 + 0.3 = 0.8$
$\hat{p}^{(4)} = \sigma(0.8) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.8}} = \frac{1}{2.2255} = 0.4493$

**Step 3: Compute errors $\hat{p}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}$**

- $\delta^{(1)} = 0.7046 - 0 = 0.7046$
- $\delta^{(2)} = 0.6225 - 0 = 0.6225$
- $\delta^{(3)} = 0.6570 - 1 = -0.3430$
- $\delta^{(4)} = 0.4493 - 1 = -0.5507$

**Step 4: Compute gradients for each parameter**

For $\theta_0$ (using $x_0 = 1$):

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{4}(0.7046 + 0.6225 - 0.3430 - 0.5507) = \frac{0.4334}{4} = 0.1084$$

For $\theta_1$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{4}\sum \delta^{(i)} x_1^{(i)}$$

$$= \frac{1}{4}\left[(0.7046)(0.5) + (0.6225)(1.0) + (-0.3430)(1.5) + (-0.5507)(2.0)\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{4}\left[0.3523 + 0.6225 - 0.5145 - 1.1014\right] = \frac{-0.6411}{4} = -0.1603$$

For $\theta_2$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_2} = \frac{1}{4}\sum \delta^{(i)} x_2^{(i)}$$

$$= \frac{1}{4}\left[(0.7046)(1.5) + (0.6225)(2.0) + (-0.3430)(2.5) + (-0.5507)(3.0)\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{4}\left[1.0569 + 1.2450 - 0.8575 - 1.6521\right] = \frac{-0.2077}{4} = -0.0519$$

**Step 5: Update parameters**

$$\theta_0^{\text{new}} = 0.1 - 0.1(0.1084) = 0.1 - 0.01084 = 0.08916$$

$$\theta_1^{\text{new}} = 0.2 - 0.1(-0.1603) = 0.2 + 0.01603 = 0.21603$$

$$\theta_2^{\text{new}} = 0.1 - 0.1(-0.0519) = 0.1 + 0.00519 = 0.10519$$

**Final updated parameters:** $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{new}} = [0.08916, 0.21603, 0.10519]^T$. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the ID3 / CART algorithm for building a decision tree. Discuss the role of entropy, Gini impurity, and information gain with a small numerical example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the k-Nearest Neighbors algorithm for classification. What is the **curse of dimensionality**? How does the choice of $k$ affect the bias-variance trade-off? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: CART algorithm overview (2.5 marks)**

The CART algorithm builds a binary decision tree using a **greedy, top-down recursive partitioning** strategy:

1. Start at the root node containing all training data
2. At each node, evaluate **all possible splits** (feature $j$, threshold $s$)
3. Choose the split that **maximizes information gain** (or equivalently, minimizes weighted child impurity)
4. Recurse on each child node
5. Stop when a stopping criterion is met (max depth, min samples, pure node)

**Step 2: Impurity measures (2 marks)**

**Entropy** measures the information disorder at a node:

$$H = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k \log_2 p_k$$

- $H = 0$ when the node is **pure** (one class)
- $H = \log_2 K$ at maximum impurity (uniform distribution)

**Gini impurity** measures the probability of misclassification if labels were randomly assigned per class probability:

$$G = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k^2$$

- $G = 0$ when pure
- Gini is **computationally faster** (no log) and is the default in scikit-learn

**Step 3: Information gain (1.5 marks)**

$$\Delta = I(\text{parent}) - \sum_{c \in \text{children}} \frac{N_c}{N_p} I(c)$$

**Step 4: Numerical example (1 mark)**

A node has 8 samples (5 class 0, 3 class 1). Entropy = $-\frac{5}{8}\log_2\frac{5}{8} - \frac{3}{8}\log_2\frac{3}{8} = 0.9544$ bits. Gini = $1 - (0.625^2 + 0.375^2) = 0.4688$.

A candidate split creates: Left (4, 4) → $H = 1$ bit; Right (4, 1) → $H = 0.7219$ bits.
Weighted child entropy = $\frac{4}{8}(1) + \frac{4}{8}(0.7219) = 0.5 + 0.3610 = 0.8610$ bits.
Information gain = $0.9544 - 0.8610 = 0.0934$ bits. (Modest split — not very informative.)

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: k-NN algorithm (2.5 marks)**

k-NN is a **non-parametric, lazy learning** algorithm that makes no assumptions about the data distribution:

**Training phase:** Store all training examples $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{m}$ (no computation).

**Inference phase (per query $\mathbf{x}_q$):**
1. Compute distance $d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}^{(i)})$ for all $i = 1, \ldots, m$
2. Sort distances in ascending order
3. Select the $k$ smallest distances → set $\mathcal{N}_k$
4. Predict via **majority vote**:
$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c} \sum_{\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in \mathcal{N}_k} \mathbb{1}[y^{(i)} = c]$$

**Step 2: Curse of dimensionality (2.5 marks)**

As the number of features $n$ increases, the **volume of feature space grows exponentially**. This causes three problems for k-NN:

1. **Distance concentration:** In high dimensions, the ratio of the nearest to the farthest neighbor approaches 1 — all points become roughly equidistant. "Nearness" loses meaning.

2. **Empty space paradox:** Most of the high-dimensional space is empty, so the $k$ nearest neighbors may actually be far from the query point.

3. **Sample sparsity:** To maintain the same local density, the required training set size grows exponentially with $n$.

**Mitigation:** Apply **dimensionality reduction** (PCA, t-SNE) or **feature selection** before k-NN. Use weighted k-NN or local dimensionality reduction.

**Step 3: Bias-variance and choice of $k$ (2 marks)**

| Choice of $k$ | Bias | Variance | Decision Boundary | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $k = 1$ | **Low** | **High** | Jagged, overfits | Sensitive to noise |
| $k = m$ | **High** | **Low** | Always majority class | Severe underfit |
| **Moderate $k$** | Balanced | Balanced | Smooth, generalized | Optimal |

- **Small $k$** (e.g., $k=1$): Every training point is its own predictor. The boundary wraps tightly around individual points — overfitting.
- **Large $k$** (e.g., $k=20$): The boundary becomes very smooth — underfitting.
- **Optimal $k$** is found via **k-fold cross-validation** on a validation set, typically choosing **odd** values for binary tasks to avoid tie votes.

**Other practical considerations (bonus):** Always **standardize features** before k-NN (since distance is sensitive to scale). Use **weighted voting** (weight by $1/d$) to reduce influence of distant neighbors. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing log-loss with MSE** — log-loss is mandatory for logistic regression because the cost function must be convex in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. MSE with sigmoid creates a non-convex surface with local minima.
> 2. **Forgetting the bias term $x_0 = 1$** in logistic regression calculations — losing 1-2 marks per numerical.
> 3. **Mixing up entropy and Gini** — both are impurity measures, but they have different formulas and properties. State which one you are using.
> 4. **Forgetting the weighting factor** $\frac{N_c}{N_p}$ in information gain — students often write the unweighted sum, which is wrong.
> 5. **Claiming k-NN has a training phase** — k-NN is a **lazy learner**; training is $O(1)$ and all work happens at inference.
> 6. **Not addressing feature scaling** — both k-NN and logistic regression are **distance/scale-sensitive** and require standardization. Decision trees do not.
> 7. **Forgetting to mention cross-validation** for hyperparameter selection — KTU examiners expect this as a complete answer.
> 8. **Confusing $\arg\max$ with $\max$** in the k-NN majority vote — the predicted class is the label that maximizes the count, not the count itself.
> 9. **In multiclass softmax**, forgetting that probabilities **must sum to 1** — this is a defining property.
> 10. **Not justifying the use of odd $k$** in binary k-NN — odd $k$ prevents tie votes which would otherwise require an arbitrary tie-breaking rule.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Classification Algorithms**

### A. Logistic Regression
- ✅ Models **probability** $P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x})$ using the **sigmoid function** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$
- ✅ Derives from the assumption that **log-odds** $\log(p/(1-p))$ are **linear in features**
- ✅ Cost function is **binary cross-entropy / log loss** (NOT MSE)
- ✅ Gradient has the **same form as linear regression**: $\frac{1}{m}\sum(\hat{p}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$
- ✅ Decision boundary is a **linear hyperplane** in feature space
- ✅ Extend to multiclass using **softmax regression**
- ✅ Coefficients are interpretable as **log-odds ratios**
- ✅ Requires **feature scaling** (gradient descent is scale-sensitive)
- ✅ Pros: probabilistic, fast inference, interpretable. Cons: linear boundary only

### B. Decision Trees
- ✅ **Greedy, top-down recursive partitioning** (CART algorithm)
- ✅ Splits are **axis-aligned** (along single feature dimensions)
- ✅ Splits chosen to **maximize information gain** (reduce impurity)
- ✅ Two impurity measures: **Entropy** (information-theoretic) and **Gini** (computational default)
- ✅ **Pure node** has $H = 0$ or $G = 0$
- ✅ **Leaf prediction** is the majority class of training samples in that leaf
- ✅ **Overfitting control:** Pre-pruning (`max_depth`, `min_samples_leaf`) or **post-pruning** (cost-complexity)
- ✅ **Highly interpretable** — can be visualized as flowcharts
- ✅ **No feature scaling required**
- ✅ Pros: interpretable, handles non-linearity, mixed data types. Cons: unstable (high variance), greedy (not globally optimal)

### C. k-Nearest Neighbors
- ✅ **Lazy learner** — no training phase, stores entire dataset
- ✅ Prediction via **majority vote** of the $k$ nearest training points
- ✅ Distance metrics: **Euclidean (L2)**, **Manhattan (L1)**, or **Minkowski (generalized)**
- ✅ **Curse of dimensionality:** severely impacts k-NN in high dimensions
- ✅ **Bias-variance trade-off** controlled by $k$: small $k$ = high variance, large $k$ = high bias
- ✅ Choose **odd $k$** for binary tasks to avoid ties
- ✅ Use **cross-validation** to find optimal $k$
- ✅ **CRITICAL: requires feature scaling** (distance is scale-sensitive)
- ✅ Pros: simple, no training, naturally multiclass, complex boundaries. Cons: slow inference, memory-intensive, curse of dimensionality

### D. Cross-Algorithm Comparison
- ✅ Logistic Regression: **parametric, probabilistic, linear boundary**
- ✅ Decision Tree: **non-parametric, greedy, axis-aligned boundary, interpretable**
- ✅ k-NN: **non-parametric, lazy, instance-based, arbitrary boundary**
- ✅ Common evaluation metrics: **accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC-ROC, log-loss**
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark questions, always present a **comparative table** covering: type, training complexity, inference complexity, interpretability, decision boundary type, and sensitivity to scaling/outliers

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks "compare and contrast", always provide a **tabular comparison** AND a **paragraph explanation** covering practical use cases. KTU examiners award 2-3 marks for clarity of organization alone.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST785 - ALGORITHMS FOR DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Performance Metrics?

In the context of **supervised machine learning**, **performance metrics** are quantitative measures used to evaluate how well a classification or prediction model performs on a given dataset. They provide an objective, mathematical basis for comparing different models, tuning hyperparameters, and assessing generalization to unseen data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Aligned with PECST785 Syllabus):**
> *Performance metrics are standardized mathematical functions that map the predicted outputs of a classification algorithm against the true ground-truth labels to produce a scalar score representing the quality of the model. For binary classification, the foundational structure is the **Confusion Matrix**, from which all elementary metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score) are derived, while **ROC-AUC** provides a threshold-independent assessment.*

## 1.2 The Confusion Matrix — The Foundation

Every binary classifier produces four possible outcomes for each test instance:

| Symbol | Meaning | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| **TP** | True Positive | Model predicts **Positive**, and actual class is **Positive** |
| **TN** | True Negative | Model predicts **Negative**, and actual class is **Negative** |
| **FP** | False Positive (Type I Error) | Model predicts **Positive**, but actual class is **Negative** |
| **FN** | False Negative (Type II Error) | Model predicts **Negative**, but actual class is **Positive** |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The COVID Testing Booth:**
> Imagine a rapid antigen testing kit deployed at an airport.
> - **TP (True Positive):** The test says "infected" and the person really is infected — *great catch*.
> - **TN (True Negative):** The test says "healthy" and the person is healthy — *peace of mind*.
> - **FP (False Positive):** The test says "infected" but the person is healthy — *unnecessary panic and quarantine*.
> - **FN (False Negative):** The test says "healthy" but the person is infected — *the most dangerous case, an undetected spreader*.
>
> Different stakeholders care about different errors. The **airline** cares about FP (boarding disruptions), while the **hospital** cares about FN (missed patients). Performance metrics encode these trade-offs mathematically.

## 1.4 Role in the KTU 2024 Syllabus

Within Module 3, these metrics form the **evaluation backbone** of every classification algorithm studied. A model is only as good as the lens through which it is judged, and these metrics supply that lens.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Metric

### A. Accuracy
- **Definition:** The proportion of total correct predictions (both positives and negatives) to the total number of predictions made.
- **When to use:** Balanced datasets where both classes are roughly equally represented.
- **Limitation:** Highly misleading on **imbalanced datasets** (e.g., 99% negative, 1% positive — a dumb classifier predicting "always negative" gets 99% accuracy).

### B. Precision (Positive Predictive Value)
- **Definition:** Of all instances the model *predicted* as positive, what fraction were *actually* positive.
- **When to use:** When the cost of a **False Positive** is high (e.g., spam detection, recommending a defective product, fraud flagging).
- **Intuition:** "When the model raises an alarm, how often is it right?"

### C. Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate)
- **Definition:** Of all *actual* positive instances, what fraction did the model *successfully retrieve*.
- **When to use:** When the cost of a **False Negative** is high (e.g., cancer diagnosis, terrorist detection, defect detection in aviation).
- **Intuition:** "Of all the real positives out there, how many did the model catch?"

### D. F1-Score (Harmonic Mean of Precision and Recall)
- **Definition:** A single scalar that balances Precision and Recall using the **harmonic mean** (not the arithmetic mean).
- **Why harmonic mean?** It penalizes extreme values — a model with Precision = 1.0 and Recall = 0.01 gets an F1 near 0, not 0.5.
- **Range:** [0, 1]; 1 = perfect, 0 = worst.

### E. Specificity (True Negative Rate)
- **Definition:** Of all actual negatives, what fraction was correctly identified.
- **Relation:** $\text{Specificity} = 1 - \text{FPR}$

### F. ROC Curve (Receiver Operating Characteristic)
- **Definition:** A 2D plot of **TPR (Recall)** on the y-axis against **FPR (1 − Specificity)** on the x-axis, generated by sweeping the classification threshold from 0 to 1.
- **Origin:** Developed during **World War II for radar signal detection**; adopted by ML in the 1980s.

### G. AUC (Area Under the ROC Curve)
- **Definition:** A single number summarizing the entire ROC curve.
- **Range:** [0, 1]
- **Interpretation:** The probability that a randomly chosen positive instance is **ranked higher** by the model than a randomly chosen negative instance.
- **AUC = 0.5** → random guessing; **AUC = 1.0** → perfect classifier.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Range | Ideal Value |
|--------|---------|-------|-------------|
| **Accuracy** | $\dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}$ | [0, 1] | 1 |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | [0, 1] | 1 |
| **Recall (TPR / Sensitivity)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | [0, 1] | 1 |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | [0, 1] | 1 |
| **FPR (Fall-out)** | $\dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = 1 - \text{Specificity}$ | [0, 1] | 0 |
| **F1-Score** | $\dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | [0, 1] | 1 |
| **F-beta Score** | $\dfrac{(1+\beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{\beta^2 P + R}$ | [0, 1] | 1 |
| **AUC (Trapezoidal)** | $\int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}) \, d(\text{FPR})$ | [0, 1] | 1 |
| **Log-Loss** | $-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log(p_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-p_i)\right]$ | $[0, \infty)$ | 0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Exams:**
> The table above avoids the vertical pipe symbol `|` to prevent markdown table breakage. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX when the absolute value is required.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnostics:** Recall-optimized models for cancer screening minimize missed cases.
- **Cybersecurity:** Precision-optimized intrusion detection prevents analyst fatigue from false alarms.
- **Recommendation Systems:** F1-score balances both — we want relevant items (precision) without missing good ones (recall).
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** AUC used in pedestrian detection — the threshold can be tuned post-deployment depending on environment.
- **Credit Risk Modeling:** Banks use AUC to compare models in production before regulatory approval.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of F1-Score from the Harmonic Mean

The **harmonic mean** of two positive numbers $a$ and $b$ is defined as:

$$
H(a, b) = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $a = P$ (Precision) and $b = R$ (Recall):

$$
H(P, R) = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}
$$

**Step 2:** Find a common denominator for the fraction inside:

$$
H(P, R) = \frac{2}{\frac{R + P}{P \cdot R}}
$$

**Step 3:** Invert and simplify:

$$
H(P, R) = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

This is the **F1-Score**. ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why harmonic mean and not arithmetic mean?**
> Suppose $P = 1.0$ and $R = 0.0$. Arithmetic mean $= 0.5$ (deceptively mediocre).
> F1 = $\frac{2 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.0}{1.0 + 0.0} = 0$ (correctly reflects model failure).
> The harmonic mean is dominated by the **smaller** value, enforcing that *both* must be high.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

A spam detection model is tested on **200 emails**. The resulting confusion matrix is:

|  | **Predicted Spam** | **Predicted Ham** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Spam** | 40 (TP) | 10 (FN) |
| **Actual Ham** | 20 (FP) | 130 (TN) |

**Step 1 — Extract the four counts:**

$$
TP = 40, \quad FN = 10, \quad FP = 20, \quad TN = 130
$$

**Step 2 — Verify totals:**

$$
\text{Total} = TP + FN + FP + TN = 40 + 10 + 20 + 130 = 200 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Accuracy:**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{\text{Total}} = \frac{40 + 130}{200} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.85
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Precision:**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{40}{40 + 20} = \frac{40}{60} = 0.6667
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Recall:**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{40}{40 + 10} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.80
$$

**Step 6 — Compute F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.6667 \cdot 0.80}{0.6667 + 0.80}
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{1.0667}{1.4667} \approx 0.7273
$$

**Step 7 — Compute Specificity and FPR:**

$$
\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{130}{130 + 20} = \frac{130}{150} = 0.8667
$$

$$
\text{FPR} = 1 - \text{Specificity} = 1 - 0.8667 = 0.1333
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Multi-Model Comparison

Consider three candidate models $M_1$, $M_2$, $M_3$ evaluated on the same dataset:

| Model | Accuracy | Precision | Recall | F1-Score | AUC | Verdict |
|-------|----------|-----------|--------|----------|-----|---------|
| $M_1$ | 0.85 | 0.6667 | 0.80 | 0.7273 | 0.91 | Balanced, decent F1 |
| $M_2$ | 0.82 | 0.95 | 0.50 | 0.6552 | 0.88 | High precision, low recall |
| $M_3$ | 0.88 | 0.60 | 0.95 | 0.7368 | 0.93 | High recall, lower precision |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:**
> - $M_3$ has the **best AUC** (0.93) and **best F1** (0.7368) — typically the best choice.
> - $M_2$ might be preferred in scenarios where FP is **catastrophic** (e.g., blocking legitimate emails).
> - $M_1$ is the safest compromise when both error types matter equally.

## 3.4 Derivation of the F-beta Generalization

The general F-beta score weights recall $\beta$ times more than precision:

$$
F_\beta = \frac{(1 + \beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}
$$

**Step 1:** When $\beta = 1$, $\beta^2 = 1$:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R} \quad \text{(balanced)}
$$

**Step 2:** When $\beta = 2$, recall is weighted $4\times$ more:

$$
F_2 = \frac{5 P R}{4P + R}
$$

**Step 3:** When $\beta = 0.5$, precision is weighted $4\times$ more:

$$
F_{0.5} = \frac{1.25 P R}{0.25 P + R}
$$

This is critical in **medical AI** and **safety-critical systems** where missing a positive case is far worse than raising a false alarm.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Confusion Matrix — Conceptual Block Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Test Instance] --> B{Actual Class Label Known?}
    B --> C[Positive Class]
    B --> D[Negative Class]
    C --> E{Model Prediction}
    D --> F{Model Prediction}
    E --> G[Predicted Positive]
    E --> H[Predicted Negative]
    F --> I[Predicted Positive]
    F --> J[Predicted Negative]
    G --> K[TP Block: True Positive]
    H --> L[FN Block: False Negative]
    I --> M[FP Block: False Positive]
    J --> N[TN Block: True Negative]
    K --> O[Confusion Matrix Output]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Metric Computation Engine]
    P --> Q1[Accuracy]
    P --> Q2[Precision]
    P --> Q3[Recall]
    P --> Q4[F1 Score]
    P --> Q5[Specificity]
```

## 4.2 ROC-AUC Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Trained Classifier] --> B[Score Output per Instance]
    B --> C[Threshold Sweep: 0.0 to 1.0]
    C --> D1[Threshold 0.0: All Predicted Positive]
    C --> D2[Threshold 0.25]
    C --> D3[Threshold 0.5]
    C --> D4[Threshold 0.75]
    C --> D5[Threshold 1.0: All Predicted Negative]
    D1 --> E[Compute TPR and FPR Pair]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[ROC Curve: Plot TPR vs FPR]
    F --> G[Apply Trapezoidal Rule]
    G --> H[AUC Scalar Value]
    H --> I[Model Comparison and Selection]
```

## 4.3 Metric Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Choosing a Performance Metric] --> Q1{Is the dataset balanced?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| A[Use Accuracy]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Which error is costlier?}
    Q2 -->|False Positive| P[Optimize Precision]
    Q2 -->|False Negative| R[Optimize Recall]
    Q2 -->|Both equally| F1[Use F1 Score]
    P --> Q3{Need threshold-independent view?}
    R --> Q3
    F1 --> Q3
    Q3 -->|Yes| ROC[Plot ROC Curve and Compute AUC]
    Q3 -->|No| DEC[Fix threshold and use scalar metric]
    A --> STOP[Final Model Selection]
    ROC --> STOP
    DEC --> STOP
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, PECST785]**
**Define Precision and Recall. Why are they preferred over Accuracy in imbalanced classification problems?**

**Model Answer:**

**Precision** is the fraction of correctly predicted positive observations out of all predicted positives:

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

**Recall** is the fraction of correctly predicted positive observations out of all actual positives:

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Why preferred over Accuracy for imbalanced data:**
In an imbalanced dataset (e.g., 990 negatives and 10 positives), a trivial classifier predicting "always negative" achieves 99% accuracy but 0% recall — failing completely to identify the minority class. Precision and Recall directly interrogate the model's behavior on the positive (minority) class, exposing this failure that Accuracy conceals. *(3 marks: 1 for each definition + 1 for justification.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, PECST785]**
**What is the ROC-AUC curve? What does an AUC of 0.5 signify versus an AUC of 1.0?**

**Model Answer:**

The **ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve** is a 2D plot of **True Positive Rate (TPR)** on the y-axis against **False Positive Rate (FPR)** on the x-axis, generated by varying the classification decision threshold from 0 to 1.

The **AUC (Area Under the Curve)** is the scalar area under this curve, ranging from 0 to 1.

- **AUC = 0.5** signifies that the model performs **no better than random guessing** — the ROC curve is the diagonal $y = x$.
- **AUC = 1.0** signifies a **perfect classifier** that perfectly separates the two classes — the ROC curve passes through (0,1).

AUC has a probabilistic interpretation: it equals the probability that a randomly selected positive instance is ranked higher in score than a randomly selected negative instance. *(3 marks.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Pattern – Module 3]**

**(a)** Consider a binary classifier tested on 150 instances with the following results: **TP = 50, FN = 10, FP = 20, TN = 70**. Compute the following metrics and explain the trade-off: **(7 marks)**
   - (i) Accuracy
   - (ii) Precision
   - (iii) Recall
   - (iv) F1-Score

**(b)** Explain the **ROC curve** and **AUC** in detail. Sketch the ROC curve for a perfect classifier, a random classifier, and a realistic classifier. Comment on the relationship between AUC and model ranking quality. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Metric Computation

**Step 1:** Extract counts: $TP = 50$, $FN = 10$, $FP = 20$, $TN = 70$.

**Step 2 (i) — Accuracy:**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN} = \frac{50 + 70}{50 + 20 + 70 + 10} = \frac{120}{150} = 0.80
$$

**Step 2 (ii) — Precision:**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{50}{50 + 20} = \frac{50}{70} = 0.7143
$$

**Step 2 (iii) — Recall:**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} = 0.8333
$$

**Step 2 (iv) — F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.7143 \cdot 0.8333}{0.7143 + 0.8333} = \frac{1.1904}{1.5476} \approx 0.7692
$$

**Trade-off Comment:** Precision (0.7143) and Recall (0.8333) are not identical. The model catches most positives (high recall) but at the cost of some false alarms (moderate precision). The F1-score of 0.7692 balances both, indicating a reasonably well-rounded classifier.

#### Part (b) — ROC and AUC

The **ROC curve** plots TPR (Recall) on the y-axis against FPR (1 − Specificity) on the x-axis as the classification threshold varies from 0 to 1.

| Classifier Type | ROC Curve Behavior | AUC |
|-----------------|--------------------|-----|
| Perfect | Passes through (0, 1) — top-left corner | 1.0 |
| Random | Diagonal line $y = x$ | 0.5 |
| Realistic | Curve bowing toward top-left | 0.7–0.95 |

**Relationship with Ranking Quality:** AUC is mathematically equivalent to the **Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney U statistic**, which measures the probability that a randomly chosen positive instance is ranked higher than a randomly chosen negative instance by the classifier. A high AUC means the model assigns systematically higher scores to positives, enabling effective threshold tuning post-deployment without retraining.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Pattern – Module 3 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the **F1-score** formula from the definition of the harmonic mean. Explain why the harmonic mean is used instead of the arithmetic mean with a numerical counter-example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bank's loan default model produces the following on a 500-customer test set: **TP = 80, FN = 40, FP = 30, TN = 350**. Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Specificity, and the False Positive Rate. Comment on which metric the bank should prioritize and why. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — F1-Score Derivation

The harmonic mean of $P$ and $R$ is:

$$
H = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}} = \frac{2PR}{P + R} = F_1
$$

**Counter-example:**
- Model A: $P = 0.9$, $R = 0.1$.
   - Arithmetic mean = 0.5.
   - F1-score = $\frac{2 \cdot 0.9 \cdot 0.1}{0.9 + 0.1} = \frac{0.18}{1.0} = 0.18$.
- Model B: $P = 0.5$, $R = 0.5$.
   - Arithmetic mean = 0.5.
   - F1-score = $\frac{2 \cdot 0.25}{1.0} = 0.5$.

Both models have the same arithmetic mean (0.5), but Model B is **far superior** because both metrics are simultaneously high. The F1-score correctly reflects this, while the arithmetic mean does not. The harmonic mean is **dominated by the smaller of the two values**, enforcing that *both* Precision and Recall must be high.

#### Part (b) — Bank Loan Default Computation

Given: $TP = 80$, $FN = 40$, $FP = 30$, $TN = 350$, Total = 500.

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{80 + 350}{500} = \frac{430}{500} = 0.860
$$

$$
P = \frac{80}{80 + 30} = \frac{80}{110} = 0.7273
$$

$$
R = \frac{80}{80 + 40} = \frac{80}{120} = 0.6667
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.7273 \cdot 0.6667}{0.7273 + 0.6667} = \frac{0.9697}{1.3940} \approx 0.6957
$$

$$
\text{Specificity} = \frac{350}{350 + 30} = \frac{350}{380} = 0.9211
$$

$$
\text{FPR} = 1 - 0.9211 = 0.0789
$$

**Bank's Priority — Recall:** A **False Negative** (approving a loan to a defaulter) causes direct financial loss. A **False Positive** (rejecting a good customer) loses a potential customer but does not cause a default. Hence, the bank should prioritize **Recall** and re-tune the threshold to improve it, accepting a modest drop in precision as a justified trade-off.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing Precision and Recall formulas** — memorize the denominator: Precision uses $TP + FP$ (predicted positive), Recall uses $TP + FN$ (actual positive).
> 2. **Forgetting to compute FPR** for ROC-AUC questions — the x-axis is FPR, not 1 − Accuracy.
> 3. **Reporting F1-score without units** — state explicitly that it is dimensionless and bounded in [0, 1].
> 4. **Ignoring class imbalance in accuracy interpretation** — a 99% accuracy on imbalanced data is **not** a good model; always cross-check with Recall on the minority class.
> 5. **Using `|` (pipe) inside markdown tables** — always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid table corruption in the answer script (especially in digital submissions).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Confusion Matrix** is the foundation — all scalar metrics are derived from its four cells: TP, FP, TN, FN.
- **Accuracy** = $(TP+TN)/\text{Total}$ — use only on balanced data.
- **Precision** = $TP/(TP+FP)$ — answer the question: *"When the model says positive, how often is it right?"*
- **Recall** = $TP/(TP+FN)$ — answer the question: *"Of all actual positives, how many did the model catch?"*
- **F1-Score** = $\frac{2PR}{P+R}$ — the harmonic mean, penalizes extreme imbalance between P and R.
- **F-beta** generalizes F1 — use $\beta > 1$ to weight recall more, $\beta < 1$ to weight precision more.
- **Specificity** = $TN/(TN+FP)$, and **FPR** = $1 - \text{Specificity}$.
- **ROC Curve** = TPR vs FPR plot, threshold-independent view of classifier behavior.
- **AUC** = scalar area under ROC, equivalent to the probability that a positive ranks above a negative.
- **AUC = 0.5** → random; **AUC = 1.0** → perfect; **AUC < 0.5** → worse than random (invert predictions).
- **Threshold tuning** can shift the precision-recall trade-off without retraining.
- **For imbalanced data**, prefer Precision, Recall, F1, and AUC over Accuracy.
- **For multi-class problems**, use **macro-averaging** (treat all classes equally) or **micro-averaging** (weight by frequency) — a common KTU follow-up question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algorithmic Optimization Techniques: Gradient descent and its variants (stochastic, mini-batch)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST785 - ALGORITHMS FOR DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Algorithmic Optimization Techniques: Gradient descent and its variants (stochastic, mini-batch)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimization in Data Science** is the process of finding the set of model parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ that minimize (or maximize) a pre-defined objective function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$, also known as the **cost function**, **loss function**, or **empirical risk**.

**Gradient Descent (GD)** is a first-order, iterative optimization algorithm used to find the minimum of a differentiable function. The algorithm updates the parameters in the direction of the steepest *negative* gradient of the cost function, scaled by a step-size hyperparameter called the **learning rate**, denoted $\alpha$.

For a model with parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and objective function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$, the canonical update rule is:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} - \alpha \cdot \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$$

where $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$ is the gradient vector evaluated at the current parameter values, and $t$ is the iteration index.

## 1.2 The Three Canonical Variants (KTU 2024 Module-3 Focus)

| Variant | Data Used per Update | Update Frequency | Cost per Iteration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch Gradient Descent (BGD)** | Entire training set $m$ | Once per epoch | High |
| **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** | Single sample $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ | $m$ times per epoch | Low |
| **Mini-Batch Gradient Descent (MBGD)** | A subset (batch) of size $b$, where $1 < b < m$ | $m/b$ times per epoch | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Epoch**: One complete pass through the entire training dataset. **Batch size $b$**: Number of samples processed before the model parameters are updated.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Blindfolded Mountain Hiker

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine you are a hiker stranded on a misty mountain at night.** You cannot see the valley floor, but you can feel the slope of the ground beneath your feet.
> - **The Mountain Surface** represents the cost function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$. Valleys = low loss; peaks = high loss.
> - **Your Feet** measure the local slope, which is the gradient $\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$.
> - **The Learning Rate $\alpha$** is your *stride length*. Too small and you take forever; too large and you overshoot the valley.
> - **Batch GD**: You measure the slope by walking across the *entire mountain* before taking one step. Accurate but slow.
> - **Stochastic GD**: You measure the slope from *one single footprint* at a time. Noisy, zig-zagging, but very fast per step.
> - **Mini-Batch GD**: You measure the slope from a *small cluster of footprints* (e.g., 32 or 64). A perfect balance — fast and reasonably accurate.

## 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Questions on optimization algorithms are **mandatory** in Module-3 of PECST785.
> - Examiners frequently test the **mathematical update rule**, **convergence behaviour**, **trade-offs between variants**, and the **role of the learning rate**.
> - Numerical problems on iterative convergence (computing parameter values across iterations) appear repeatedly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Optimization Problem

In supervised learning, given a training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{m}$, we seek parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ that minimize the **empirical risk**:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}\bigl(f(x^{(i)}; \boldsymbol{\theta}),\ y^{(i)}\bigr)$$

where $\mathcal{L}(\cdot,\cdot)$ is a per-sample loss function (e.g., squared error, cross-entropy) and $f(x^{(i)}; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ is the model's prediction.

## 2.2 First-Order Optimality Condition

A necessary condition for $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\star}$ to be a local minimum is that the gradient vanishes:

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\star}) = \boldsymbol{0}$$

Gradient descent is a numerical method that drives the gradient towards this stationary point by iteratively stepping downhill.

## 2.3 Derivation Intuition (Why Subtract the Gradient?)

For a small perturbation $\Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$, the first-order Taylor expansion gives:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta} + \Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}) \approx J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) + \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta})^{\top} \Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$$

To *decrease* $J$, we require $\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta})^{\top} \Delta\boldsymbol{\theta} < 0$. The most negative value is achieved when $\Delta\boldsymbol{\theta} = -\alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ for any $\alpha > 0$. This justifies the update rule.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **must-memorize** formula bank for any KTU Module-3 question on gradient descent. Learn it cold.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Generic GD Update** | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} - \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$ | $\alpha$: learning rate |
| 2 | **Batch GD Gradient** (MSE loss) | $\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{2}{m} X^{\top}(X\boldsymbol{\theta} - \boldsymbol{y})$ | $X$: design matrix, $\boldsymbol{y}$: target vector |
| 3 | **SGD Gradient** (single sample $i$) | $\nabla J^{(i)}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = 2\bigl(f(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\bigr)\, x^{(i)}$ | Uses *one* sample at a time |
| 4 | **Mini-Batch GD Gradient** | $\nabla J_{\mathcal{B}}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{2}{b} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}} \bigl(f(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\bigr)\, x^{(i)}$ | $\mathcal{B}$: mini-batch of size $b$ |
| 5 | **Number of updates per epoch** | $N_{\text{updates}} = \lceil m / b \rceil$ | $m$: total samples, $b$: batch size |
| 6 | **Convergence criterion** | $\vert \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}) \vert < \epsilon$ for some tolerance $\epsilon$ | Stop when gradient is tiny |
| 7 | **Convergence rate (convex)** | $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}) - J(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\star}) \leq \mathcal{O}(1/t)$ | Sub-linear for GD on convex |
| 8 | **Variance of SGD estimator** | $\text{Var}[\nabla J^{(i)}] \approx \sigma^{2}$ where $\sigma^{2}$ shrinks as $b$ grows | Larger batch $\Rightarrow$ lower variance |
| 9 | **SGD noise scale** | $\text{Var}[\nabla J_{\mathcal{B}}] \approx \sigma^{2} / b$ | Mini-batch averages reduce noise |
| 10 | **Polyak step-size** | $\alpha_{t} = \frac{J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}) - J(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\star})}{\Vert \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}) \Vert^{2}}$ | Optimal $\alpha$ for convex quadratic |
| 11 | **Bias-Variance Trade-off** | BGD = low variance, high per-step cost; SGD = high variance, low per-step cost | Drives the choice of variant |
| 12 | **Learning rate schedule** | $\alpha_{t} = \alpha_{0} / (1 + \text{decay} \cdot t)$ | Decays over time for convergence |

## 2.5 Engineering & Real-World Utility

- **Training Deep Neural Networks**: Mini-batch GD with batch sizes 32–512 is the de-facto standard in frameworks like TensorFlow and PyTorch.
- **Large-Scale Linear Regression**: BGD is preferred when $m$ is small (e.g., $m < 10{,}000$).
- **Online Learning**: SGD naturally handles streaming data since each new sample triggers an update.
- **Computer Vision & NLP**: Mini-batch GD enables GPU parallelism because matrix operations on $b$ samples are highly optimized.
- **Reinforcement Learning**: Policy-gradient methods rely on SGD-like updates with high-variance reward signals.

## 2.6 The Role of the Learning Rate $\alpha$

> [!WARNING]
> **Convergence pitfalls** — Examiners love this!
> - If $\alpha$ is **too small**: convergence is painfully slow; the algorithm may appear "stuck".
> - If $\alpha$ is **too large**: the iterates *diverge* or oscillate wildly.
> - If $\alpha$ is **just right (Goldilocks zone)**: convergence to a minimum.
> - For non-convex problems (deep learning), $\alpha$ controls whether we land in a *good local minimum* or a *sharp bad one*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Rigorous Derivation of the GD Update Rule from Taylor Expansion

We begin with a differentiable cost function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ and an arbitrary small step $\Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$. The first-order multivariate Taylor expansion around the current point $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}$ is:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} + \Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}) = J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}) + \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})^{\top} \Delta\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathcal{O}(\Vert \Delta\boldsymbol{\theta} \Vert^{2})$$

We want to choose $\Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$ such that $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} + \Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}) < J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$. Ignoring the higher-order term (valid for small $\Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$), this reduces to:

$$\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})^{\top} \Delta\boldsymbol{\theta} < 0$$

By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, the inner product is minimized when $\Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is anti-parallel to $\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$ and scaled by a positive constant $\alpha$:

$$\Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\star} = -\alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}), \quad \alpha > 0$$

Substituting $\Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\star}$ yields:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} + \Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\star} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} - \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$$

This completes the derivation. The parameter $\alpha$ is the learning rate, controlling the step magnitude.

## 3.2 Gradient for the Mean-Squared-Error (MSE) Loss

For linear regression $f(x) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top} x$, the per-sample squared loss is:

$$\mathcal{L}^{(i)}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top} x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\bigr)^{2}$$

The gradient with respect to $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ for sample $i$ is:

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L}^{(i)}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \bigl(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top} x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\bigr) x^{(i)}$$

Aggregating across all $m$ samples:

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \bigl(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top} x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\bigr) x^{(i)}$$

In matrix form with $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$:

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{m} X^{\top}(X\boldsymbol{\theta} - \boldsymbol{y})$$

The Batch GD update becomes:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} X^{\top}(X\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} - \boldsymbol{y})$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — All Three Variants

Let us minimize the simple convex function:

$$f(x) = x^{2} - 4x + 5$$

The true minimum is at $x^{\star} = 2$, with $f(x^{\star}) = 1$. The gradient is $\nabla f(x) = 2x - 4$. Set learning rate $\alpha = 0.1$ and initial point $x_{0} = 0$.

### Iteration-by-iteration trace (deterministic gradient descent):

$$x_{1} = x_{0} - 0.1 \cdot (2 \cdot 0 - 4) = 0 - 0.1 \cdot (-4) = 0.4$$

$$x_{2} = 0.4 - 0.1 \cdot (2 \cdot 0.4 - 4) = 0.4 - 0.1 \cdot (-3.2) = 0.72$$

$$x_{3} = 0.72 - 0.1 \cdot (2 \cdot 0.72 - 4) = 0.72 - 0.1 \cdot (-2.56) = 0.976$$

$$x_{4} = 0.976 - 0.1 \cdot (2 \cdot 0.976 - 4) = 0.976 - 0.1 \cdot (-2.048) = 1.1808$$

$$x_{5} = 1.1808 - 0.1 \cdot (2 \cdot 1.1808 - 4) = 1.1808 - 0.1 \cdot (-1.6384) = 1.34464$$

The iterates are converging toward $x^{\star} = 2$ at a sub-linear rate, demonstrating the convergence behaviour of gradient descent on a convex quadratic.

### Demonstration of variance difference:

For a noisy objective $\tilde{f}(x) = f(x) + \epsilon$ where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$, the noisy gradient is:

$$\nabla \tilde{f}(x) = 2x - 4 + \epsilon'$$

- **SGD** uses $\nabla \tilde{f}^{(i)}(x)$ for one sample — the noise term $\epsilon'$ is large per step.
- **Mini-batch** averages $b$ such gradients: $\frac{1}{b} \sum_{i=1}^{b} \nabla \tilde{f}^{(i)}(x)$, whose variance is $\sigma^{2}/b$, a factor of $b$ smaller.

This is the analytical foundation for the empirical observation that **mini-batch GD has lower-variance gradient estimates than pure SGD**, while still being computationally cheaper than full-batch GD.

## 3.4 Convergence Analysis on Strongly Convex Functions

For an $\mu$-strongly convex and $L$-smooth function, gradient descent with fixed step size $\alpha \leq 1/L$ achieves:

$$\Vert \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} - \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\star} \Vert^{2} \leq \left(1 - \alpha \mu\right)^{t} \Vert \boldsymbol{\theta}_{0} - \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\star} \Vert^{2}$$

This is a **linear convergence rate** (geometric decay). The optimal constant step size is $\alpha^{\star} = 1/L$, giving the contraction factor $(1 - \mu/L)$. The ratio $\kappa = L/\mu$ is the **condition number** — ill-conditioned problems ($\kappa \gg 1$) converge slowly.

## 3.5 Summary Matrix — Comparing the Three Variants

> [!NOTE]
> This is the single most important analytical comparison to internalize for KTU 2024 answers.

| Property | Batch GD | Stochastic GD | Mini-Batch GD |
|---|---|---|---|
| Samples per update | $m$ (full set) | $1$ | $b$ where $1 < b < m$ |
| Gradient estimate | Exact, deterministic | Noisy, unbiased | Less noisy, unbiased |
| Variance of update | $0$ | $\sigma^{2}$ | $\sigma^{2}/b$ |
| Updates per epoch | $1$ | $m$ | $m/b$ |
| Computational cost per update | $\mathcal{O}(mn)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(bn)$ |
| Memory footprint | Full $X$ in RAM | One sample | Mini-batch in RAM |
| Convergence path | Smooth, direct | Noisy, zig-zag | Smooth-ish |
| Suitable dataset size | Small ($m < 10^{4}$) | Streaming / huge | Medium to huge |
| Hardware efficiency | Poor | Suboptimal | Excellent (GPU-friendly) |
| Risk of local minima | Low (deterministic) | Higher (noise can escape) | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Optimization Algorithm Selection Flow

The following decision-flow diagram maps out the practical engineering choice between the three gradient descent variants based on dataset size and hardware constraints.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Choose Optimization Algorithm] --> nodeB{Dataset Size m}
    nodeB -- Small m less than 10k --> nodeC{Batch Loading Feasible in RAM}
    nodeB -- Large m more than 10k --> nodeD{Hardware GPU Available}
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeE[Use Batch Gradient Descent]
    nodeC -- No --> nodeF[Use Mini-Batch GD with b equals 32]
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeG[Use Mini-Batch GD with b equals 64 to 512]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeH[Use SGD or Mini-Batch with b equals 8 to 32]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Convergence Smooth and Deterministic]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Convergence Fast on GPU]
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeK[Convergence Noisy but Memory Efficient]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Stop Check Gradient Norm less than epsilon]
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM[Output Optimal Parameters theta star]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Mini-Batch GD Iteration

This schematic depicts one full inner iteration of mini-batch gradient descent, showing the data flow between the dataset, the gradient computation, and the parameter update module.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeDA[Training Dataset X y of size m] --> nodeSH[Shuffle and Partition into Batches of size b]
    nodeSH --> nodeMB[Sample Mini-Batch B sub i]
    nodeMB --> nodeFWD[Forward Pass: Compute Predictions f of x theta]
    nodeFWD --> nodeLOSS[Compute Loss J sub B of theta]
    nodeLOSS --> nodeBACK[Backward Pass: Compute Gradient grad J sub B]
    nodeBACK --> nodeUPD[Parameter Update: theta new equals theta minus alpha grad J sub B]
    nodeUPD --> nodeCHK{Convergence Check grad norm less than epsilon}
    nodeCHK -- No --> nodeSH
```
