```mermaid
    nodeCHK -- Yes --> nodeOUT[Output Optimal theta star]
```

## 4.3 Convergence Trajectory Comparison

The following state-transition diagram conceptually illustrates how the three variants traverse the loss landscape from the same starting point $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{0}$ to the optimum $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\star}$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS0[Start theta 0]
    nodeS0 --> nodeB1[BGD: Direct Smooth Path]
    nodeS0 --> nodeS1[SGD: Highly Noisy Zig-Zag]
    nodeS0 --> nodeM1[MBGD: Mild Oscillation]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2[theta 1 theta 2 theta 3 Direct Steps]
    nodeS1 --> nodeS2[theta 1 prime theta 2 prime Many Noisy Steps]
    nodeM1 --> nodeM2[theta 1 double prime theta 2 double prime Few Balanced Steps]
    nodeB2 --> nodeFIN[Converge to theta star]
    nodeS2 --> nodeFIN
    nodeM2 --> nodeFIN
```

## 4.4 Convergence Path Geometry (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeOPT[Global Minimum at theta star] --> nodeCVX[Convex Bowl Loss Landscape]
    nodeCVX --> nodePBGD[BGD Path: Smooth Descent]
    nodeCVX --> nodePSGD[SGD Path: Random Walk Around Valley Axis]
    nodeCVX --> nodePMBGD[MBGD Path: Smoothed Random Walk]
    nodePBGD --> nodeEPI[Epilogue: All Reach Optimum]
    nodePSGD --> nodeEPI
    nodePMBGD --> nodeEPI
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Batch Gradient Descent, Stochastic Gradient Descent, and Mini-Batch Gradient Descent. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Variant | One Advantage | One Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch GD** | Stable, deterministic convergence | Slow per update; memory-intensive |
| **Stochastic GD** | Very fast per update; enables online learning | Highly noisy convergence path |
| **Mini-Batch GD** | Balances speed and stability; GPU-friendly | Introduces the batch-size hyperparameter |

A correct table plus one-line distinction per row earns full marks.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the learning rate $\alpha$ in gradient descent. What happens if $\alpha$ is set too large or too small?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The learning rate $\alpha$ controls the step size in the parameter space at each iteration: $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} - \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$.
- **Too small $\alpha$**: convergence is *very slow*; the algorithm may appear to stall and may get trapped in plateaus.
- **Too large $\alpha$**: updates overshoot the minimum; the loss may *diverge* or oscillate indefinitely.
- **Ideal $\alpha$**: ensures steady decrease of $J$ toward a minimum. Often tuned via grid search or learning-rate schedules (decay, warmup).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**(a)** Derive the gradient descent update rule starting from the first-order Taylor expansion of a differentiable objective function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the function $J(x) = x^{2} - 6x + 13$, perform **five iterations** of gradient descent starting from $x_{0} = 0$ with learning rate $\alpha = 0.2$. Tabulate $x_{t}$, $\nabla J(x_{t})$, and $J(x_{t})$ at each step. Comment on convergence. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Set up the Taylor expansion.**
For a small perturbation $\Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$, the first-order multivariate Taylor expansion of $J$ around $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}$ is:
$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} + \Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}) \approx J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}) + \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})^{\top} \Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$$

**Step 2 — State assumptions.**
- $J$ is differentiable (so the gradient exists).
- $\Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is sufficiently small that higher-order terms $\mathcal{O}(\Vert \Delta\boldsymbol{\theta} \Vert^{2})$ are negligible.

**Step 3 — Formulate the descent condition.**
We want $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1}) < J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$, i.e., $\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})^{\top} \Delta\boldsymbol{\theta} < 0$.

**Step 4 — Apply Cauchy–Schwarz.**
The inner product is minimized (most negative) when $\Delta\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is anti-parallel to $\nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$:
$$\Delta\boldsymbol{\theta} = -\alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}), \quad \alpha > 0$$

**Step 5 — Write the update rule.**
$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} - \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$$

**Conclusion**: The update steps the parameters in the direction of steepest descent, scaled by $\alpha$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

$J(x) = x^{2} - 6x + 13$, so $\nabla J(x) = 2x - 6$ and $x^{\star} = 3$, $J(x^{\star}) = 4$.

| Iteration $t$ | $x_{t}$ | $\nabla J(x_{t}) = 2x_{t} - 6$ | $J(x_{t})$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0000 | $-6.0000$ | 13.0000 |
| 1 | 1.2000 | $-3.6000$ | 7.8400 |
| 2 | 1.9200 | $-2.1600$ | 5.6704 |
| 3 | 2.3520 | $-1.2960$ | 4.7738 |
| 4 | 2.6112 | $-0.7776$ | 4.4477 |
| 5 | 2.7667 | $-0.4666$ | 4.3143 |

**Sample step at $t=0$**:
$$x_{1} = x_{0} - \alpha \nabla J(x_{0}) = 0 - 0.2 \cdot (-6) = 1.2$$

**Sample step at $t=1$**:
$$x_{2} = 1.2 - 0.2 \cdot (2 \cdot 1.2 - 6) = 1.2 - 0.2 \cdot (-3.6) = 1.92$$

**Comment**: The iterates are converging toward $x^{\star} = 3$ and $J(x_{t}) \to 4$, but at a sub-linear (slowing) rate. With $\alpha = 0.2$ and curvature $L = 2$, we have $\alpha L = 0.4 < 1$, ensuring convergence.

---

### Question B-B. (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) algorithm. Derive the update rule for a linear regression model with mean-squared-error loss using SGD. Discuss why SGD has higher variance than Batch GD and how mini-batches reduce this variance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A linear regression model $f(x) = \theta x$ is trained on $m = 4$ data points $\{(1, 2), (2, 4), (3, 5), (4, 4)\}$ using **batch gradient descent** with learning rate $\alpha = 0.01$ and initial parameter $\theta_{0} = 0$. Compute $\theta_{1}$ and $\theta_{2}$ explicitly, showing all working. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Algorithm**: In SGD, we use one training sample $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ per update:
$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} - \alpha \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L}^{(i)}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$$

**Derivation for Linear Regression MSE**:
- Hypothesis: $f(x^{(i)}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top} x^{(i)}$
- Loss for sample $i$: $\mathcal{L}^{(i)} = \frac{1}{2}(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top} x^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^{2}$
- Gradient w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta}$: $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathcal{L}^{(i)} = (\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top} x^{(i)} - y^{(i)})\, x^{(i)}$

Hence the SGD update is:
$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} - \alpha \bigl(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}^{\top} x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\bigr) x^{(i)}$$

**Variance discussion**:
- Batch GD uses $\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} \nabla \mathcal{L}^{(i)}$, whose variance vanishes as $m \to \infty$ (law of large numbers).
- SGD uses a single-sample gradient, which is a *noisy unbiased estimator*: $\mathbb{E}[\nabla \mathcal{L}^{(i)}] = \nabla J$, but $\text{Var}[\nabla \mathcal{L}^{(i)}] = \sigma^{2} > 0$.
- Mini-batch GD averages $b$ samples: variance becomes $\sigma^{2}/b$, a factor of $b$ smaller than pure SGD.

**Conclusion**: Mini-batch GD strikes the balance — it retains SGD's cheap per-update cost while reducing gradient noise via averaging.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Setup**: $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{4} (\theta x^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^{2}$
$$\nabla J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{4} (\theta x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x^{(i)}$$

**Compute $\theta_{1}$** at $\theta_{0} = 0$:
- Sample 1: $(\theta_{0} \cdot 1 - 2) \cdot 1 = (0 - 2) \cdot 1 = -2$
- Sample 2: $(\theta_{0} \cdot 2 - 4) \cdot 2 = (0 - 4) \cdot 2 = -8$
- Sample 3: $(\theta_{0} \cdot 3 - 5) \cdot 3 = (0 - 5) \cdot 3 = -15$
- Sample 4: $(\theta_{0} \cdot 4 - 4) \cdot 4 = (0 - 4) \cdot 4 = -16$
- Sum: $-2 - 8 - 15 - 16 = -41$
- Mean: $\nabla J(\theta_{0}) = -41/4 = -10.25$

$$\theta_{1} = \theta_{0} - \alpha \nabla J(\theta_{0}) = 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-10.25) = 0.1025$$

**Compute $\theta_{2}$** at $\theta_{1} = 0.1025$:
- Sample 1: $(0.1025 \cdot 1 - 2) \cdot 1 = -1.8975$
- Sample 2: $(0.1025 \cdot 2 - 4) \cdot 2 = (-3.795) \cdot 2 = -7.59$
- Sample 3: $(0.1025 \cdot 3 - 5) \cdot 3 = (-4.6925) \cdot 3 = -14.0775$
- Sample 4: $(0.1025 \cdot 4 - 4) \cdot 4 = (-3.59) \cdot 4 = -14.36$
- Sum: $-1.8975 - 7.59 - 14.0775 - 14.36 = -37.925$
- Mean: $\nabla J(\theta_{1}) = -37.925/4 = -9.48125$

$$\theta_{2} = \theta_{1} - \alpha \nabla J(\theta_{1}) = 0.1025 - 0.01 \cdot (-9.48125) = 0.1025 + 0.0948125 = 0.1973125$$

**Result**: $\theta_{1} = 0.1025$, $\theta_{2} \approx 0.1973$. The parameter is gradually increasing toward the optimal $\theta^{\star} \approx 1$ (which would minimize the MSE on this data).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the sign**: Forgetting the minus sign in $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} - \alpha \nabla J$. A *plus* sign moves you *uphill*, away from the minimum.
> 2. **Mixing up Batch vs SGD gradients**: The SGD gradient is computed on **one sample**; the Batch GD gradient is averaged across **all $m$ samples**. Students often average $b$ samples and still call it SGD — that is mini-batch GD!
> 3. **Ignoring the $1/m$ factor**: Forgetting to divide by $m$ in the Batch GD update leads to an inflated gradient and divergence. (7-mark questions will deduct 2 marks for this.)
> 4. **Not stating the learning-rate schedule**: When answering about convergence, mentioning a fixed $\alpha$ alone is incomplete; state that a *decaying* $\alpha_{t}$ is required for guaranteed SGD convergence (Robbins–Monro conditions: $\sum \alpha_{t} = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha_{t}^{2} < \infty$).
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps**: Examiners award marks per logical step. Skipping from "Taylor expansion" directly to "update rule" forfeits 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Wrong convergence criterion**: Writing "$J$ becomes small" instead of "$\Vert \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}) \Vert < \epsilon$" is a precision error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as your last-minute revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

- [x] **Definition**: Gradient descent is a first-order iterative optimizer that steps parameters in the direction of the *negative* gradient, scaled by learning rate $\alpha$.
- [x] **Update Rule**: $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{t} - \alpha \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$.
- [x] **Batch GD**: Uses all $m$ samples per update; one update per epoch; smooth path; high per-update cost.
- [x] **SGD**: Uses 1 sample per update; $m$ updates per epoch; noisy path; enables online learning.
- [x] **Mini-Batch GD**: Uses $b$ samples ($1 < b < m$); $m/b$ updates per epoch; variance $\sigma^{2}/b$; GPU-friendly.
- [x] **Learning rate $\alpha$**: Controls step size; too small = slow; too large = divergence; tuned via schedules.
- [x] **Convergence**: Batch GD gives $\mathcal{O}(1/t)$ on convex functions; linear rate on strongly convex; SGD gives $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{t})$.
- [x] **Variance**: $\text{Var}[\nabla J_{\text{SGD}}] = \sigma^{2}$, $\text{Var}[\nabla J_{\text{MBGD}}] = \sigma^{2}/b$.
- [x] **MSE gradient for linear regression**: $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J = \frac{1}{m} X^{\top}(X\boldsymbol{\theta} - \boldsymbol{y})$.
- [x] **Stopping criterion**: $\Vert \nabla J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}) \Vert < \epsilon$ or $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t})$ plateau.
- [x] **Why mini-batch GD dominates modern ML**: Vectorization on GPUs, lower variance than pure SGD, lower cost than full-batch GD.
- [x] **Robbins–Monro conditions** for SGD convergence: $\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \alpha_{t} = \infty$ and $\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \alpha_{t}^{2} < \infty$.
- [x] **Engineering trade-off**: BGD = accuracy, SGD = speed, MBGD = sweet spot.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hyperparameter tuning: grid search, random search, Bayesian optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST785 - ALGORITHMS FOR DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hyperparameter tuning: grid search, random search, Bayesian optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hyperparameter Tuning** is the process of systematically searching the space of external configuration variables of a machine learning model — values that are *not* learned from the training data but must be set *prior* to learning — to identify the configuration that optimises a pre-defined objective (e.g., validation accuracy, F1-score, RMSE).

The two distinct variable families in any data-science algorithm are:

| Term | Definition | Set By | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Parameters** | Internal coefficients learned by fitting the model to data. | The algorithm during training. | Weights $w_i$, bias $b$, tree split thresholds (learned). |
| **Hyperparameters** | External configuration variables governing the learning process itself. | The practitioner *before* training. | Learning rate $\eta$, regularisation $\lambda$, tree depth $d$, number of estimators $K$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** Hyperparameters cannot be directly optimised by gradient descent on the training set (doing so causes overfitting). Hence a separate *search strategy* over a *validation metric* is mandatory.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Stovetop Cooking Experiment"

Imagine you are a chef who has to bake a cake using an oven you have never used before.

- The **recipe ingredients and quantities** (flour = 200 g, sugar = 100 g) are your *parameters* — they are fixed by the recipe and you don't search for them.
- The **oven settings** (temperature = 180 °C, baking time = 30 min, fan mode = ON) are your *hyperparameters* — you must choose them *before* baking, and a bad choice ruins the cake even if the recipe is perfect.
- Every time you change a setting and re-bake, you get to **taste (validate)** the result.
- The three tuning strategies are simply three different *budget-conscious strategies* to discover the best (temperature, time, fan-mode) triple:
  1. **Grid search** — try *every* combination on a fixed list.
  2. **Random search** — try *random* combinations within ranges.
  3. **Bayesian optimisation** — use *past tasting notes* to choose the next, most promising setting to try.

### 1.3 The Three Strategies at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> - **Grid Search (GS):** Exhaustive enumeration over a discrete Cartesian product of hyperparameter values.
> - **Random Search (RS):** Stochastic sampling of configurations from predefined marginal distributions.
> - **Bayesian Optimisation (BO):** Sequential, model-based search that builds a probabilistic surrogate of the objective and uses an acquisition function to balance *exploration* vs *exploitation*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Grid Search — Operational Logic

Grid search partitions each hyperparameter's range into a discrete grid and evaluates **every** Cartesian product.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. For each hyperparameter $h_i$ ($i = 1, \dots, d$), define a finite set $H_i = \{h_i^{(1)}, h_i^{(2)}, \dots, h_i^{(k_i)}\}$.
2. Form the Cartesian product:
   $$\mathcal{G} \;=\; H_1 \times H_2 \times \dots \times H_d$$
3. For every candidate $\mathbf{h} \in \mathcal{G}$:
   - Train model $M$ on training set $D_{\text{train}}$.
   - Score on validation set $D_{\text{val}}$: $s(\mathbf{h}) \;=\; \text{score}(M_{\mathbf{h}}, D_{\text{val}})$.
4. Return $\mathbf{h}^{\star} \;=\; \underset{\mathbf{h}\,\in\,\mathcal{G}}{\arg\max}\; s(\mathbf{h})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Grid Search fails in high dimensions:** If each $H_i$ has $k$ values and there are $d$ hyperparameters, the number of evaluations is $k^d$ — exponential. With $k=10$ and $d=9$, this is **1 billion fits**.

### 2.2 Random Search — Operational Logic

Random search samples configurations independently from predefined marginal distributions $p_i(h_i)$ (commonly uniform or log-uniform).

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Specify the maximum budget $N$ (number of iterations).
2. For $t = 1, 2, \dots, N$:
   - Draw $\mathbf{h}^{(t)} \sim p_1(h_1)\,p_2(h_2)\dots p_d(h_d)$.
   - Train and validate; record $s^{(t)} = s(\mathbf{h}^{(t)})$.
3. Return the best configuration observed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical insight (Bergstra & Bengio, 2012):** When only a small subset of hyperparameters strongly influences performance, random search explores a *distinct* value for each important hyperparameter on every trial, whereas grid search wastes trials on unimportant axes. The expected best validation score of random search converges to the optimum at a rate of $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{\log N / N})$ per axis in the low-effective-dimensionality regime.

### 2.3 Bayesian Optimisation — Operational Logic

Bayesian optimisation is a **sequential, model-based, global optimisation** technique tailored for *expensive*, *black-box* objectives (e.g., validation score after a full model fit).

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Initial design.** Evaluate $n_0$ initial configurations (often via Latin hypercube or uniform random) to build an initial dataset $\mathcal{D}_0 = \{(\mathbf{h}^{(i)}, s^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{n_0}$.
2. **Fit a surrogate probabilistic model** $p(s \mid \mathbf{h}, \mathcal{D}_{t-1})$. The most common choice is a **Gaussian Process (GP)**:
   $$s(\mathbf{h}) \;\sim\; \mathcal{GP}\!\left(m(\mathbf{h}),\, k(\mathbf{h}, \mathbf{h}')\right)$$
   where $m(\cdot)$ is the mean function and $k(\cdot, \cdot)$ is the kernel (e.g., Matern-$\tfrac{5}{2}$, RBF).
3. **Posterior at a candidate $\mathbf{h}$.** Given observations $\mathcal{D}_{t-1}$, the GP yields a Normal posterior:
   $$p(s(\mathbf{h}) \mid \mathcal{D}_{t-1}) \;=\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_t(\mathbf{h}),\, \sigma_t^2(\mathbf{h})\right)$$
4. **Optimise an acquisition function** $\alpha_t(\mathbf{h})$ to choose the next point:
   $$\mathbf{h}^{(t)} \;=\; \underset{\mathbf{h}\,\in\,\mathcal{H}}{\arg\max}\; \alpha_t(\mathbf{h})$$
5. **Evaluate the true objective** at $\mathbf{h}^{(t)}$: train and validate to obtain $s^{(t)}$.
6. **Augment data:** $\mathcal{D}_t = \mathcal{D}_{t-1} \cup \{(\mathbf{h}^{(t)}, s^{(t)})\}$. Repeat from step 2 until budget $T$ exhausted.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Meaning / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Total grid evaluations | $\vert\mathcal{G}\vert \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{d} \vert H_i \vert$ | Computational cost of GS — explodes with $d$. |
| RS trials in budget $N$ | $N$ | Linear cost; parallelisable trivially. |
| GP posterior mean | $\mu_t(\mathbf{h}) \;=\; \mathbf{k}^\top K^{-1} \mathbf{s}$ | Predicted validation score. |
| GP posterior variance | $\sigma_t^2(\mathbf{h}) \;=\; k(\mathbf{h}, \mathbf{h}) - \mathbf{k}^\top K^{-1} \mathbf{k}$ | Uncertainty in prediction. |
| Expected Improvement (EI) | $\mathrm{EI}_t(\mathbf{h}) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\max(s(\mathbf{h}) - s^{\star}_{\text{best}},\, 0)\right]$ | Default acquisition; closed-form under GP. |
| EI closed form (normal posterior) | $\mathrm{EI}_t(\mathbf{h}) \;=\; (\mu_t(\mathbf{h}) - s^{\star}_{\text{best}})\,\Phi(Z) + \sigma_t(\mathbf{h})\,\phi(Z)$, with $Z = \frac{\mu_t(\mathbf{h}) - s^{\star}_{\text{best}}}{\sigma_t(\mathbf{h})}$ | Practical evaluation rule. |
| Probability of Improvement | $\mathrm{PI}_t(\mathbf{h}) \;=\; \Phi(Z)$ | Simpler alternative to EI. |
| UCB acquisition | $\mathrm{UCB}_t(\mathbf{h}) \;=\; \mu_t(\mathbf{h}) + \kappa\,\sigma_t(\mathbf{h})$ | Explicit exploration–exploitation trade-off via $\kappa$. |
| Convergence rate (low-dim RS) | $\mathcal{O}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{\log N}{N}}\right)$ | Per-axis suboptimality gap. |
| BO total cost | $n_0 + T$ true evaluations | Each evaluation = one full model fit. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** BO is the de-facto standard in **AutoML pipelines** (e.g., Google Vizier, Optuna's TPE, scikit-learn's `BayesSearchCV`) for tuning deep networks, large ensemble models, and any pipeline where a single model-fit costs minutes-to-hours. Grid/Random search remains dominant for cheap models (linear, small trees).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Grid Search Total Cost and Combinatorial Blow-up

**Statement.** The total number of model fits required by grid search is the cardinality of the Cartesian product of the per-hyperparameter value sets.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } H_i & = \{h_i^{(1)}, h_i^{(2)}, \dots, h_i^{(k_i)}\}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, d. \\
\mathcal{G} & = H_1 \times H_2 \times \dots \times H_d \\
& = \big\{(h_1^{(j_1)}, h_2^{(j_2)}, \dots, h_d^{(j_d)}) : j_i \in \{1, \dots, k_i\}\big\}. \\
\text{Then } \vert\mathcal{G}\vert & = \prod_{i=1}^{d} k_i \quad \text{(axiom of cardinality of Cartesian product).}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of each step:**

- We define a *finite* value set for each of the $d$ hyperparameters.
- The grid $\mathcal{G}$ is the set of all $d$-tuples formed by picking one value from each $H_i$.
- The product rule $\vert A \times B \vert = \vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert$ generalises inductively, yielding $\prod_i k_i$.

**Engineering implication:** Even a modest $k_i = 5$ for $d = 6$ yields $\vert\mathcal{G}\vert = 5^6 = 15{,}625$ fits.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expected Best-Value Comparison of Random vs Grid Search

**Statement.** Under the assumption that the objective $s(\mathbf{h})$ depends *only* on a low-dimensional subset of effective hyperparameters (the *effective dimensionality* $d_e \le d$), random search finds a near-optimal configuration in fewer trials than grid search.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Define a budget of } N \text{ trials.} \\
\text{Grid search with } k^d & = N \text{ gives } k = N^{1/d} \text{ distinct values per axis.} \\
\text{Number of distinct values on an important axis for GS} & = N^{1/d}. \\
\text{Number of distinct values on an important axis for RS} & = N \;\;(\text{independent samples per trial}). \\
\text{Ratio of coverage} & = \frac{N}{N^{1/d}} = N^{1 - 1/d} \;\to\; \infty \text{ as } N \to \infty.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of each step:**

- Grid search must divide its $N$ total fits uniformly across $d$ axes, hence only $N^{1/d}$ values per axis.
- Random search samples each axis independently; *every* trial potentially introduces a *new* value on the important axis.
- The coverage ratio grows unboundedly with $N$, proving the asymptotic superiority of random search in the low-effective-dimensionality regime.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Expected Improvement under a Gaussian Posterior

**Statement.** Given $p(s(\mathbf{h}) \mid \mathcal{D}_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t(\mathbf{h}), \sigma_t^2(\mathbf{h}))$, derive the closed-form Expected Improvement.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{EI}_t(\mathbf{h}) & = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\max(s(\mathbf{h}) - s^{\star}_{\text{best}},\, 0)\right] \\
& = \int_{s^{\star}_{\text{best}}}^{\infty} (u - s^{\star}_{\text{best}})\, \mathcal{N}(u;\, \mu_t, \sigma_t^2)\, du \\
\text{Substitute } u & = \mu_t + \sigma_t z,\; du = \sigma_t dz, \\
& = \int_{z^{\star}}^{\infty} (\mu_t + \sigma_t z - s^{\star}_{\text{best}})\, \sigma_t \phi(z)\, dz \quad \text{where } z^{\star} = \frac{s^{\star}_{\text{best}} - \mu_t}{\sigma_t} \\
& = \sigma_t \int_{z^{\star}}^{\infty} (z - z^{\star})\, \phi(z)\, dz \\
& = \sigma_t \left[\phi(z^{\star}) - z^{\star}\,(1 - \Phi(z^{\star}))\right].
\end{aligned}
$$

Rewriting with $Z = -z^{\star} = \dfrac{\mu_t - s^{\star}_{\text{best}}}{\sigma_t}$:

$$
\boxed{\;\mathrm{EI}_t(\mathbf{h}) \;=\; (\mu_t(\mathbf{h}) - s^{\star}_{\text{best}})\, \Phi(Z) \;+\; \sigma_t(\mathbf{h})\, \phi(Z)\;}
$$

**Explanation of each step:**

- The integrand is $(u - s^{\star}_{\text{best}})$ over the region where the improvement is positive.
- We standardise to a standard Normal $z = (u-\mu_t)/\sigma_t$, where $\phi$ and $\Phi$ are the standard Normal PDF and CDF respectively.
- The integral $\int_{z^{\star}}^\infty (z - z^{\star})\phi(z)dz = \phi(z^{\star}) - z^{\star}(1-\Phi(z^{\star}))$ follows from integration by parts and the identity $\phi'(z) = -z\phi(z)$.
- The final closed form is a sum of (a) the expected *signed* improvement $\mu_t - s^{\star}_{\text{best}}$ weighted by $\Phi(Z)$ and (b) the *exploration* term $\sigma_t \phi(Z)$, which is large in regions of high uncertainty.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Grid Search | Random Search | Bayesian Optimisation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Search type | Exhaustive / deterministic | Stochastic | Sequential / model-based |
| Cost (fits) | $\prod_i \vert H_i \vert$ | $N$ | $n_0 + T$ |
| Scales to high $d$? | Poor (exponential) | Good | Excellent |
| Uses past evaluations? | No | No | Yes (via surrogate) |
| Parallelisable? | Trivially (embarrassingly parallel) | Trivially | Hard (sequential by design) |
| Handles conditional spaces? | Difficult | Moderate | Easy (via TPE / input warping) |
| Best use case | Low $d$, cheap fits, exhaustive guarantee | High $d$, cheap fits, time-limited | Expensive fits, $T$ small (< 100) |
| Optimality guarantee | Within $\mathcal{G}$ (no guarantee outside) | Probabilistic | Probabilistic (with regret bounds) |
| Implementation complexity | Lowest | Low | High (GP, kernels, acquisition) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Grid Search — Exhaustive Enumeration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start]) --> defA[Define grid H1 x H2 x ... x Hd]
    defA --> setA[Set best score = -infinity, best config = null]
    setA --> pickA[Pick next config h from grid G]
    pickA --> trainA[Train model M with h on Dtrain]
    trainA --> valA[Validate on Dval, compute score s h]
    valA --> checkA{s greater than best score?}
    checkA -- Yes --> updA[Update best score and best config]
    checkA -- No --> nxtA[Keep current best]
    updA --> nxtA
    nxtA --> moreA{All grid configs evaluated?}
    moreA -- No --> pickA
    moreA -- Yes --> outA([Return best config h star])
    style startA fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A66C00,color:#000
    style outA fill:#A0E0A0,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Random Search — Stochastic Sampling Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start]) --> specB[Specify budget N and marginals p1...pd]
    specB --> initB[Set best score = -infinity]
    initB --> sampB[Sample h from p1 x p2 x ... x pd]
    sampB --> fitB[Train and validate, get s h]
    fitB --> updB{Better than best?}
    updB -- Yes --> saveB[Update best]
    updB -- No --> skipB[Skip]
    saveB --> skipB
    skipB --> cntB{t equals N?}
    cntB -- No --> sampB
    cntB -- Yes --> retB([Return best h observed])
    style startB fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A66C00,color:#000
    style retB fill:#A0E0A0,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Bayesian Optimisation — Closed-Loop Sequential Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startC([Start]) --> initC[Initial design: n0 points via LHS or random]
    initC --> dataC[Dataset D0 with n0 observations]
    dataC --> gpC[Fit GP surrogate: posterior mean mu and variance sigma^2]
    gpC --> acqC[Compute acquisition alpha on candidate grid]
    acqC --> argC[Choose h next = argmax alpha h]
    argC --> trueC[True evaluation: train and validate, get s h next]
    trueC --> augC[Augment D: D t = D t-1 union observation]
    augC --> chkC{Budget T reached?}
    chkC -- No --> gpC
    chkC -- Yes --> endC([Return best h observed])
    style startC fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A66C00,color:#000
    style endC fill:#A0E0A0,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    style gpC fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    style acqC fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Comparative Strategy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GS[Grid Search]
        g1[Discrete mesh] --> g2[Exhaustive eval]
        g2 --> g3[No memory]
    end
    subgraph RS[Random Search]
        r1[Marginal sampling] --> r2[Independent trials]
        r2 --> r3[No memory]
    end
    subgraph BO[Bayesian Optimisation]
        b1[Surrogate GP] --> b2[Acquisition alpha]
        b2 --> b3[Next point]
        b3 --> b1
    end
    style GS fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#E65100
    style RS fill:#E6F4FF,stroke:#0277BD
    style BO fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** GS and RS are *one-shot* strategies with no feedback. BO is a *closed loop* where the surrogate is re-fit at every iteration, making the *next* query an informed decision based on *all* prior observations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Differentiate between *model parameters* and *hyperparameters*. Give two examples of each in the context of training a decision tree and a logistic regression model.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Model Parameters | Hyperparameters |
|---|---|---|
| Learned from data? | Yes (during training) | No (set before training) |
| Adjusted by | Optimisation algorithm (e.g., SGD) | Practitioner / search procedure |
| Logistic regression examples | Weight vector $\mathbf{w}$, bias $b$ | Regularisation strength $\lambda$, learning rate $\eta$ |
| Decision tree examples | Split thresholds at each node, leaf values | Maximum depth $d$, minimum samples per leaf, splitting criterion |

Award 1 mark each for: clear distinction, two correct examples per category. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
State any **two advantages** of Random Search over Grid Search when the number of hyperparameters is large and only a few of them significantly affect the model's performance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Better coverage of important hyperparameters:** Random search samples values independently on each axis, so it can test $N$ distinct values on a critical hyperparameter with $N$ trials, whereas grid search with $k^d = N$ only tests $N^{1/d}$ distinct values per axis — an exponential loss. **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Avoids combinatorial blow-up:** Total cost grows linearly $N$ rather than exponentially $k^d$, making it tractable for high-dimensional search spaces. **(1 mark)**
3. *(Bonus)* Trivial parallelisation and freedom to choose non-uniform (e.g., log-uniform) marginals. **(0.5 mark bonus)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In Module 3 questions, students frequently (a) confuse *parameters* with *hyperparameters* in definitions, (b) forget to mention the *role of the surrogate model* in BO, and (c) skip the *closed-form derivation* of Expected Improvement, which is a high-value 3–4 mark component. Always (i) state the *cost formula*, (ii) draw the *flow diagram*, and (iii) derive the *acquisition function* explicitly.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **Grid Search** and **Random Search** algorithms for hyperparameter tuning. Compare their **computational cost** in terms of the number of hyperparameters $d$ and grid resolution $k$, and discuss the conditions under which Random Search is provably superior.

**(b)** [7 marks] Describe the **Bayesian Optimisation** procedure in detail. Explain the role of the **Gaussian Process surrogate**, derive the closed-form expression for the **Expected Improvement (EI)** acquisition function, and state the *exploration–exploitation trade-off* explicitly.

---

**Solution to A(a) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Grid Search (2 marks):**
- For $d$ hyperparameters, define value sets $H_1, H_2, \dots, H_d$ with $\vert H_i \vert = k$.
- Form Cartesian product $\mathcal{G} = H_1 \times H_2 \times \dots \times H_d$.
- Evaluate every $\mathbf{h} \in \mathcal{G}$: total cost $= k^d$.
- Return $\mathbf{h}^{\star} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{h}\in\mathcal{G}} s(\mathbf{h})$.

**Step 2 — Random Search (2 marks):**
- Sample $\mathbf{h}^{(t)} \sim p_1(h_1) \cdots p_d(h_d)$ for $t = 1, \dots, N$.
- Track best observed $s(\mathbf{h}^{(t)})$.
- Return $\arg\max_{t} s(\mathbf{h}^{(t)})$. Cost $= N$.

**Step 3 — Cost comparison (2 marks):**
- GS cost $k^d$ vs RS cost $N$. For fixed budget $N = k^d$, GS gives $k = N^{1/d}$ values per axis.
- RS gives $N$ distinct values per axis.
- Ratio $= N^{1 - 1/d}$.

**Step 4 — Provable superiority condition (1 mark):**
- When *effective dimensionality* $d_e \ll d$ (only a few hyperparameters matter), random search asymptotically dominates grid search in coverage.

---

**Solution to A(b) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — BO framework (2 marks):**
- Treat validation score $s(\mathbf{h})$ as a *black-box* expensive function.
- Maintain dataset $\mathcal{D}_t = \{(\mathbf{h}^{(i)}, s^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{t}$.
- Fit GP: prior $s(\mathbf{h}) \sim \mathcal{GP}(m(\mathbf{h}), k(\mathbf{h}, \mathbf{h}'))$, posterior $p(s(\mathbf{h}) \mid \mathcal{D}_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t(\mathbf{h}), \sigma_t^2(\mathbf{h}))$.

**Step 2 — Acquisition (1 mark):**
- Choose $\mathbf{h}^{(t+1)} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{h}} \alpha_t(\mathbf{h})$, e.g., EI.

**Step 3 — EI derivation (3 marks):**
The expected improvement is

$$\mathrm{EI}_t(\mathbf{h}) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\max(s(\mathbf{h}) - s^{\star}_{\text{best}},\, 0)\right].$$

Under the GP posterior $s(\mathbf{h}) \mid \mathcal{D}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \sigma_t^2)$, substituting $u = \mu_t + \sigma_t z$ and integrating yields

$$\mathrm{EI}_t(\mathbf{h}) \;=\; (\mu_t(\mathbf{h}) - s^{\star}_{\text{best}})\, \Phi(Z) \;+\; \sigma_t(\mathbf{h})\, \phi(Z), \quad Z = \frac{\mu_t(\mathbf{h}) - s^{\star}_{\text{best}}}{\sigma_t(\mathbf{h})}.$$

**Step 4 — Exploration–exploitation (1 mark):**
- The first term $(\mu_t - s^{\star}_{\text{best}})\Phi(Z)$ **exploits** regions of high predicted score.
- The second term $\sigma_t \phi(Z)$ **explores** regions of high uncertainty.
- Together they constitute the canonical trade-off in BO.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Internal Choice*

**(a)** [7 marks] With the help of a **flow diagram**, describe the **Bayesian Optimisation** procedure for hyperparameter tuning. Clearly state the role of (i) the *surrogate model*, (ii) the *acquisition function*, and (iii) the *prior dataset*.

**(b)** [7 marks] Consider the surrogate posterior $s(\mathbf{h}) \mid \mathcal{D}_{t-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t(\mathbf{h}), \sigma_t^2(\mathbf{h}))$ at a candidate point $\mathbf{h}$. Derive the **Expected Improvement (EI)** acquisition function in closed form. Explain how the EI formula balances *exploration* and *exploitation*, and discuss what happens when $\sigma_t(\mathbf{h}) \to 0$ and when $\sigma_t(\mathbf{h}) \to \infty$.

---

**Solution to B(a) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Flow (2 marks):** Diagram follows Section 4.3 — Start $\to$ Initial design $\to$ GP fit $\to$ Acquisition optimisation $\to$ True evaluation $\to$ Augment $\to$ loop or terminate.

**Step 2 — Surrogate model (2 marks):**
- A *cheap-to-evaluate probabilistic model* (usually a Gaussian Process) that approximates the true, expensive validation score $s(\mathbf{h})$.
- Provides both a *prediction* $\mu_t(\mathbf{h})$ and *uncertainty* $\sigma_t(\mathbf{h})$ at any untried $\mathbf{h}$.
- Enables principled reasoning about the unknown objective.

**Step 3 — Acquisition function (1.5 marks):**
- A *utility function* $\alpha_t(\mathbf{h})$ that uses the surrogate's posterior to score every candidate.
- Trade-off criterion between *exploitation* (high $\mu_t$) and *exploration* (high $\sigma_t$).
- Example: EI, UCB, PI.

**Step 4 — Prior dataset (1.5 marks):**
- $\mathcal{D}_0 = \{(\mathbf{h}^{(i)}, s^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{n_0}$ from initial design (Latin hypercube, Sobol, or uniform random).
- Used to condition the GP and obtain the *first* posterior.
- Size $n_0$ is typically $5$–$20$ depending on $d$.

---

**Solution to B(b) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):**
$$
\mathrm{EI}_t(\mathbf{h}) = \int_{s^{\star}}^{\infty} (u - s^{\star})\, \frac{1}{\sigma_t\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(u-\mu_t)^2}{2\sigma_t^2}\right) du.
$$

**Step 2 — Substitution (2 marks):**
Let $z = (u - \mu_t)/\sigma_t$, $du = \sigma_t dz$, $z^{\star} = (s^{\star} - \mu_t)/\sigma_t$:

$$\mathrm{EI}_t = \sigma_t \int_{z^{\star}}^{\infty} (z - z^{\star})\, \phi(z)\, dz.$$

**Step 3 — Integration by parts (2 marks):**
Using $\int \phi(z)\,dz = \Phi(z)$ and $\phi'(z) = -z\phi(z)$:

$$\mathrm{EI}_t = \sigma_t \left[\phi(z^{\star}) - z^{\star}(1 - \Phi(z^{\star}))\right].$$

Substituting $Z = -z^{\star} = (\mu_t - s^{\star})/\sigma_t$:

$$\boxed{\;\mathrm{EI}_t(\mathbf{h}) = (\mu_t - s^{\star})\,\Phi(Z) + \sigma_t\,\phi(Z).\;}$$

**Step 4 — Limiting cases (2 marks):**

| Limit | Behaviour | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma_t \to 0$ | $\mathrm{EI} \to (\mu_t - s^{\star})\,\mathbb{1}[\mu_t > s^{\star}]$ | Pure *exploitation*: trust the prediction; ignore the point if it is no better than the best. |
| $\sigma_t \to \infty$ | $\mathrm{EI} \to \sigma_t\,\phi(0) = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{\sqrt{2\pi}}$ | Pure *exploration*: pick the most uncertain point regardless of $\mu_t$. |

**Step 5 — Trade-off summary (extra credit, 0.5 mark):**
- Finite $\sigma_t$ yields the standard BO compromise: maximise $\mu_t$ *and* high-uncertainty regions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Hyperparameter Tuning):**
> - **Parameter vs Hyperparameter:** Parameters are *learned*; hyperparameters are *set* by the practitioner before training. (1 mark definition question almost every exam.)
> - **Grid Search:** Exhaustive, $\vert\mathcal{G}\vert = \prod_i \vert H_i \vert$ fits; deterministic; no feedback between trials.
> - **Random Search:** Stochastic, $N$ fits; independent sampling per axis; asymptotically dominates GS in *low-effective-dimensionality* regimes.
> - **Bayesian Optimisation:** Sequential, model-based, with a **GP surrogate** and an **acquisition function** (EI / UCB / PI). Total cost $n_0 + T$.
> - **EI closed form:** $\mathrm{EI} = (\mu - s^{\star})\Phi(Z) + \sigma\,\phi(Z)$ with $Z = (\mu - s^{\star})/\sigma$ — *derivable in exam* using integration by parts.
> - **Exploration term:** $\sigma\,\phi(Z)$ — large where model is uncertain.
> - **Exploitation term:** $(\mu - s^{\star})\,\Phi(Z)$ — large where predicted score beats the current best.
> - **Parallelisation:** GS and RS are embarrassingly parallel; BO is inherently sequential (parallel variants exist but are advanced).
> - **Best practice rule of thumb (exam favourite):** Use **Grid Search** for $d \le 3$ and cheap fits; use **Random Search** for higher $d$ or as a strong baseline; use **Bayesian Optimisation** when each fit costs minutes-to-hours and the total budget $T$ is small (< 100).
> - **Remember the constant:** There is **no universal constant**; all tuning algorithms use the practitioner-chosen budget $N$ (or $T$, or $k$).

Evaluate the models using appropriate performance metrics and fine-tune them for better accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3 — Project 3: Predictive Modeling and Evaluation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Predictive Modeling** is the process of using historical data, statistical algorithms, and machine learning techniques to predict future or unknown outcomes. In the context of this KTU project, it involves two distinct paradigms:

1. **Regression** — predicting a continuous numerical target (e.g., the exact sale price of a house in USD).
2. **Classification** — predicting a discrete categorical label (e.g., categorising a house as *Low-priced*, *Mid-priced*, or *High-priced*).

The **House Prices: Advanced Regression Techniques** dataset (popularly sourced from Kaggle, originally a Ames, Iowa housing dataset compiled by Dean De Cock) contains **1460 training observations** and **80 explanatory variables** describing residential homes. It serves as the canonical benchmark for end-to-end supervised learning pipelines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This project maps directly to **Course Outcome CO3** — *Apply predictive modeling algorithms to real-world datasets and evaluate them using standard performance metrics.* It also touches **CO4** (model tuning/optimization).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **real-estate appraiser** in Ames, Iowa, with no prior experience. A colleague hands you a notebook of past sales — every line contains details like *number of bedrooms, square footage, neighborhood, year built, garage size*. Over time, your brain subconsciously forms rules:

- *"Houses in StoneBrook with 3 bedrooms sell for around ₹25 lakh."*
- *"An extra garage adds roughly ₹3 lakh."*

**Predictive modeling formalises exactly this intuition.** Instead of the human brain, we use mathematical functions (Linear Regression, Decision Trees, etc.) to extract these patterns automatically. The dataset acts as the *experience*, and the algorithm acts as the *apprentice appraiser*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both Regression AND Classification on the same dataset?**
> Because house prices can be approached from two angles:
> - *How much will it sell for?* → Regression (continuous price).
> - *Is it a cheap, mid-range, or luxury house?* → Classification (categorical bucket).
> This dual approach tests the student's versatility across the **supervised learning** spectrum.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded for KTU Valuation Emphasis)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Feature (X)** | Independent input variable (e.g., `GrLivArea`). |
| **Target (y)** | Dependent variable to predict (e.g., `SalePrice`). |
| **Train/Test Split** | Partitioning data, typically **70:30** or **80:20**. |
| **Hypothesis Function** | The mapping $h_\theta(X) \rightarrow \hat{y}$. |
| **Loss Function** | Quantifies prediction error. |
| **Overfitting** | Model memorises training data, fails on unseen data. |
| **Underfitting** | Model is too simple to capture patterns. |
| **Hyperparameter Tuning** | Adjusting knobs (e.g., polynomial degree, tree depth) via GridSearchCV/RandomizedSearchCV. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Regression

### 2.1.1 Simple Linear Regression (SLR)

The hypothesis is a straight line in feature space:

$$h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$$

where $\theta_0$ is the **intercept (bias)** and $\theta_1$ is the **slope (weight)**.

**Cost Function — Mean Squared Error (MSE):**

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is a convention that cancels out the exponent during differentiation. The parameter $m$ denotes the number of training samples.

### 2.1.2 Multiple Linear Regression (MLR)

For the House Prices dataset with $n$ features:

$$\hat{y} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \dots + \theta_n x_n$$

In **vectorised matrix form**, with $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (n+1)}$ (augmented with the bias column) and $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+1) \times 1}$:

$$\hat{y} = X \theta$$

**Closed-form Normal Equation (OLS):**

$$\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$$

This provides an exact analytical solution but is computationally expensive for very large $n$ due to the matrix inversion.

### 2.1.3 Polynomial Regression

To capture non-linear relationships (e.g., price vs. square footage is rarely a straight line), we introduce polynomial features:

$$\hat{y} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x + \theta_2 x^2 + \theta_3 x^3 + \dots + \theta_d x^d$$

This is achieved by augmenting the feature matrix with powers of $x$ up to degree $d$:

$$X_{\text{poly}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x^{(1)} & (x^{(1)})^2 & \dots & (x^{(1)})^d \\ 1 & x^{(2)} & (x^{(2)})^2 & \dots & (x^{(2)})^d \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & x^{(m)} & (x^{(m)})^2 & \dots & (x^{(m)})^d \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Polynomial degree pitfall:** As $d$ increases, the model fits training data better but generalises worse. KTU examiners frequently test the bias-variance tradeoff concept.

### 2.1.4 Gradient Descent Optimisation

When $n$ is large, the Normal Equation is impractical. **Gradient Descent** iteratively updates parameters:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$$

For MSE, the partial derivative becomes:

$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

Here, $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** — a critical hyperparameter.

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundation of Classification

For the KTU project, house prices are binned into categories (e.g., Low ≤ ₹15L, Mid ₹15L–₹30L, High > ₹30L). The following algorithms are typically applied:

### 2.2.1 Logistic Regression (despite the name, a classifier)

Applies the **Sigmoid function** to a linear combination of inputs:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad z = \theta^T X$$

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(\theta^T X) = P(y=1 \mid X; \theta)$$

**Cost Function — Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss):**

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]$$

### 2.2.2 K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)

A non-parametric, instance-based learner. For a new point $x_q$, find the $K$ closest training samples and use majority vote (classification) or mean (regression):

$$\hat{y} = \text{mode}\left( y^{(i)} \mid x^{(i)} \in N_K(x_q) \right)$$

Distance is typically measured by **Euclidean distance**:

$$d(x, x') = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \left( x_j - x'_j \right)^2}$$

### 2.2.3 Decision Tree Classifier

Recursively partitions the feature space using feature-threshold splits chosen to maximise **information gain** or minimise **Gini impurity**:

$$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c^2$$

$$\text{Entropy}(S) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2(p_c)$$

## 2.3 Performance Metrics — Formula Sheet

### 2.3.1 Regression Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **MSE** (Mean Squared Error) | $\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(y^{(i)}-\hat{y}^{(i)})^2$ | Penalises large errors; differentiable |
| **RMSE** (Root MSE) | $\sqrt{MSE}$ | Same units as target; KTU favourite |
| **MAE** (Mean Absolute Error) | $\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\vert y^{(i)}-\hat{y}^{(i)} \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| **R²** (Coefficient of Determination) | $1 - \frac{\sum (y^{(i)}-\hat{y}^{(i)})^2}{\sum (y^{(i)}-\bar{y})^2}$ | Variance explained (0–1) |
| **Adjusted R²** | $1 - \frac{(1-R^2)(m-1)}{m-n-1}$ | Penalises extra features |

### 2.3.2 Classification Metrics (from Confusion Matrix)

Let **TP** = True Positives, **FP** = False Positives, **FN** = False Negatives, **TN** = True Negatives.

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Overall correctness |
| **Precision** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions |
| **Recall (Sensitivity, TPR)** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives |
| **F1-Score** | $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | True negative rate |
| **ROC-AUC** | $\int_0^1 TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$ | Threshold-independent quality |

### 2.3.3 Cross-Validation

To combat overfitting, **K-Fold CV** splits training data into $K$ folds and rotates the validation set:

$$CV_{(K)} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{Score}_k$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** The examiner expects you to mention that **RMSE** is preferred over **MAE** for house-price problems because large pricing errors are economically very costly (penalty grows quadratically with MSE).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This methodology is the backbone of:
- **Banking**: Credit scoring (loan default classification).
- **Insurance**: Claim severity (regression) and fraud detection (classification).
- **Retail**: Demand forecasting and customer churn.
- **Healthcare**: Disease diagnosis and length-of-stay prediction.
- **Civil Engineering**: Concrete strength prediction, structural load estimation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Normal Equation (OLS)

### Step 1 — Define the Loss

We want to minimise the **Sum of Squared Errors**:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \theta^T x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Matrix Form

Using $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ and $y \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$, the residuals vector is:

$$e = X\theta - y$$

The squared error becomes:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} (X\theta - y)^T (X\theta - y)$$

### Step 3 — Expand the Quadratic Form

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ \theta^T X^T X \theta - 2\theta^T X^T y + y^T y \right]$$

**Explanation:** Using the identity $(A^T B)^T = B^T A$, we have $(X\theta)^T y = \theta^T X^T y$, and the scalar is symmetric, so we double it.

### Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\theta$

Using standard matrix calculus identities:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}(\theta^T A \theta) = 2A\theta \quad \text{(when A is symmetric)}$$

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}(\theta^T b) = b$$

Applying these:

$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ 2 X^T X \theta - 2 X^T y \right] = \frac{1}{m} \left[ X^T X \theta - X^T y \right]$$

### Step 5 — Set Gradient to Zero (Stationary Point)

$$X^T X \theta - X^T y = 0$$

$$X^T X \theta = X^T y$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $\theta$ (assuming $X^T X$ is invertible)

$$\boxed{\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y}$$

This is the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** closed-form solution. It yields the unique $\theta$ that globally minimises the MSE on the training set.

## 3.2 Gradient Descent Update Rule Derivation

Starting from $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$:

### Step 1 — Partial Derivative

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

### Step 2 — Simultaneous Update Rule

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

### Step 3 — Convergence Criteria

Iteration stops when either:
- $\vert J^{(t)} - J^{(t-1)} \vert < \epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$), or
- maximum iterations reached, or
- $\Vert \nabla J(\theta) \Vert_2 < \tau$ (gradient norm threshold).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Polynomial Feature Generation

Given a single feature vector $x = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_m]^T$ and target polynomial degree $d=2$:

### Step 1 — Construct Polynomial Feature Matrix

$$X_{\text{poly}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_1 & x_1^2 \\ 1 & x_2 & x_2^2 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ 1 & x_m & x_m^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Fit Linear Regression on $X_{\text{poly}}$

Treating the polynomial terms as *new independent features*, the **same OLS formula** applies:

$$\theta = (X_{\text{poly}}^T X_{\text{poly}})^{-1} X_{\text{poly}}^T y$$

### Step 3 — Resulting Hypothesis

$$\hat{y}^{(i)} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} + \theta_2 (x^{(i)})^2$$

## 3.4 Confusion Matrix Computation — Worked Example

Suppose 100 houses are tested and a binary classifier (Luxury vs Non-Luxury) yields:

| | Predicted Luxury | Predicted Non-Luxury |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Luxury** | TP = 20 | FN = 5 |
| **Actual Non-Luxury** | FP = 10 | TN = 65 |

### Step 1 — Accuracy

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} = \frac{20+65}{100} = 0.85$$

### Step 2 — Precision

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{20}{30} = 0.667$$

### Step 3 — Recall

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{20}{25} = 0.80$$

### Step 4 — F1-Score

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P+R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.667 \times 0.80}{0.667+0.80} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.533}{1.467} = 0.727$$

## 3.5 Feature Scaling — Why It Matters for House Prices

Features like `GrLivArea` (range 300–6000 sq ft) and `OverallQual` (range 1–10) have vastly different scales, causing gradient descent to oscillate. Apply **Standardisation**:

$$x'_j = \frac{x_j - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}$$

This centres each feature at mean $0$ with unit variance $\sigma = 1$, ensuring all features contribute equitably to the loss landscape.

## 3.6 R² Score Derivation

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{\text{res}}}{SS_{\text{tot}}}$$

where:

$$SS_{\text{res}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$$

$$SS_{\text{tot}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} (y^{(i)} - \bar{y})^2$$

**Interpretation:**
- $R^2 = 1$ → perfect predictions.
- $R^2 = 0$ → model predicts only the mean.
- $R^2 < 0$ → model is worse than the baseline mean predictor.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Predictive Modeling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Data: Kaggle House Prices CSV"] --> B["Exploratory Data Analysis"]
    B --> C["Handle Missing Values: Imputation or Drop"]
    C --> D["Feature Engineering: Encode Categoricals"]
    D --> E["Feature Scaling: StandardScaler"]
    E --> F["Train Test Split: 80 percent 20 percent"]
    F --> G1["Linear Regression Model"]
    F --> G2["Polynomial Regression Model"]
    F --> G3["Logistic Regression Classifier"]
    F --> G4["Decision Tree Classifier"]
    F --> G5["KNN Classifier"]
    G1 --> H["Cross Validation: K Fold K equals 5"]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    H --> I["Hyperparameter Tuning: GridSearchCV"]
    I --> J["Final Model Selection"]
    J --> K["Performance Metrics: RMSE and F1"]
    K --> L["Test Set Evaluation"]

```

## 4.2 Regression vs Classification Workflow

```mermaid

    D --> D3["Decision Tree Classifier"]

    F --> G
```

## 4.3 Bias-Variance Tradeoff Visualisation (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Model Complexity Increases"] --> B["High Bias Region: Underfitting"]
    A --> C["Sweet Spot: Balanced Generalization"]
    A --> D["High Variance Region: Overfitting"]

    B --> B2["Test Error: HIGH"]
```

```mermaid
    F4 --> I4["Iteration 4: Validate on Fold 4, Train on Rest"]

```
