# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`
**Q: Differentiate between regression and classification with one real-world example each from the House Prices dataset.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Regression** predicts a *continuous numerical value*. Example: Predicting the exact `SalePrice` (in USD) of a house using `GrLivArea` and `OverallQual`. (1.5 marks)
- **Classification** predicts a *discrete categorical label*. Example: Predicting whether a house falls into `Low`, `Mid`, or `High` price tier based on its features. (1.5 marks)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`
**Q: Why is RMSE preferred over MAE for evaluating house-price prediction models?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- RMSE squares the error before averaging, then takes the square root. (1 mark)
- This **penalises large errors more heavily** than small ones. (1 mark)
- For house pricing, an error of ₹10 lakh vs an error of ₹1 lakh is much more financially damaging; RMSE captures this asymmetry. MAE treats both linearly, under-representing catastrophic mispredictions. (1 mark)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### `Question A` `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Pattern]` (14 Marks)

**A. (a)** Derive the Normal Equation for linear regression using matrix calculus. Explain its computational limitations for the House Prices dataset. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Define the cost function (residual sum of squares) in matrix form. (1 mark)

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}(X\theta - y)^T(X\theta - y)$$

**Step 2:** Expand the quadratic form using transpose identities. (1 mark)

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}[\theta^T X^T X \theta - 2\theta^T X^T y + y^T y]$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $\theta$ and set to zero. (2 marks)

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m}(X^T X \theta - X^T y) = 0$$

$$X^T X \theta = X^T y \implies \theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$$

**Step 4:** Final equation and interpretation. (1 mark)

$$\boxed{\theta_{\text{OLS}} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y}$$

**Step 5:** Computational limitations for House Prices dataset. (2 marks)

- After one-hot encoding, the dataset has $\sim 300$ features. Inverting a $300 \times 300$ matrix has cost $O(n^3) \approx 2.7 \times 10^7$ operations — feasible but expensive.
- **Multicollinearity**: Many housing features (e.g., `GarageCars` and `GarageArea`) are highly correlated, making $X^T X$ near-singular and the inverse numerically unstable.
- **Solution**: Use gradient descent, ridge regression, or PCA.

---

**A. (b)** For a binary classifier evaluated on 200 houses, the confusion matrix is:

| | Predicted Luxury | Predicted Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Luxury** | 45 | 15 |
| **Actual Standard** | 25 | 115 |

Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

From the matrix:
- TP = 45, FN = 15, FP = 25, TN = 115
- Total = 200

**Step 1: Accuracy** (2 marks)

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{Total} = \frac{45+115}{200} = \frac{160}{200} = 0.80 \text{ (80%)}$$

**Step 2: Precision** (1.5 marks)

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{45}{70} = 0.6429$$

**Step 3: Recall** (1.5 marks)

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{45}{60} = 0.75$$

**Step 4: F1-Score** (2 marks)

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = \frac{2 \times 0.6429 \times 0.75}{0.6429 + 0.75} = \frac{0.9643}{1.3929} = 0.6923$$

**Final Answer:** Accuracy = **0.80**, Precision = **0.643**, Recall = **0.75**, F1 = **0.692**.

---

### `Question B` `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Pattern]` (14 Marks)

**B. (a)** Explain the concept of polynomial regression. Show how a degree-3 polynomial fit is constructed from a single feature vector $x$, and derive its hypothesis function. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Definition. Polynomial regression is a form of regression that models the relationship between $x$ and $y$ as an $n$-th degree polynomial, while still being linear in the parameters $\theta$. (1 mark)

**Step 2:** Given a single feature $x \in \mathbb{R}^m$, we create a new feature matrix with powers up to degree $d=3$: (2 marks)

$$X_{\text{poly}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x^{(1)} & (x^{(1)})^2 & (x^{(1)})^3 \\ 1 & x^{(2)} & (x^{(2)})^2 & (x^{(2)})^3 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ 1 & x^{(m)} & (x^{(m)})^2 & (x^{(m)})^3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Hypothesis function. (2 marks)

$$h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x + \theta_2 x^2 + \theta_3 x^3$$

**Step 4:** Apply the same OLS closed-form solution to find $\theta$. (1 mark)

$$\theta = (X_{\text{poly}}^T X_{\text{poly}})^{-1} X_{\text{poly}}^T y$$

**Step 5:** For House Prices, applying polynomial features to `GrLivArea` (square footage) captures the typical *non-linear* price-vs-size curve — a luxury premium kicks in above 2500 sq ft. (1 mark)

---

**B. (b)** Discuss the bias-variance tradeoff in the context of polynomial degree selection for the House Prices dataset. Use 5-fold cross-validation scores to justify your answer. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Define Bias and Variance. (2 marks)

- **Bias**: Error from wrong assumptions (e.g., assuming a linear relationship when it is quadratic). High bias → **underfitting**.
- **Variance**: Error from sensitivity to small fluctuations in training data. High variance → **overfitting**.

**Step 2:** Relate to polynomial degree. (2 marks)

| Degree $d$ | Behaviour | Training RMSE | CV RMSE |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (linear) | Underfits | High | High |
| 3 (cubic) | Sweet spot | Low | Low |
| 10 (very high) | Overfits | Very low | Very high |

**Step 3:** Explanation of cross-validation role. (2 marks)

K-fold CV (with K=5) provides an *unbiased estimate* of generalisation error. As $d$ increases, training RMSE keeps decreasing (model fits training data perfectly), but CV RMSE first decreases then increases. The optimal $d$ is where CV RMSE is minimised.

**Step 4:** Mitigation techniques. (1 mark)

- **Regularisation** (Ridge/Lasso) penalises large coefficients.
- **Reduce feature count** via PCA or feature selection.
- **Gather more training data** to combat variance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callouts — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bias column** when writing the Normal Equation — always prepend a column of 1s.
> 2. **Confusing MSE and RMSE** — RMSE has the same units as the target; MSE does not. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing $\log$ instead of $\log_2$** in Entropy formulas — KTU expects natural logarithm variations to be explicitly justified.
> 4. **Reporting R² without Adjusted R²** when claiming a "best" model — if you added useless features, R² can still rise while the model worsens.
> 5. **Skipping the StandardScaler step** in KNN — KNN is distance-based and is crippled without scaling. Always mention it.
> 6. **Using accuracy on an imbalanced dataset** — if 95% of houses are "Standard", a trivial classifier gets 95% accuracy. Always pair with Precision/Recall/F1.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The House Prices dataset** has **1460 training rows** and **80 features** (after encoding, often 200+ columns). Always apply log-transform on `SalePrice` to handle right-skewness.
- **Linear Regression closed-form** is $\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$. It is exact but $O(n^3)$. Use gradient descent when $n$ is large or $X^T X$ is singular.
- **Polynomial regression** still uses OLS — only the feature matrix changes. Watch out for the **Runge phenomenon** at high degrees.
- **Logistic Regression** is a *classifier*, not a regressor. It outputs probabilities via the sigmoid $\sigma(z)$.
- **Evaluation rules of thumb**:
  - Regression → **RMSE**, **MAE**, **R²**, **Adjusted R²**.
  - Classification → **Accuracy**, **Precision**, **Recall**, **F1**, **ROC-AUC**.
  - Imbalanced classes → prefer **F1** over Accuracy.
- **Cross-validation** (typically K=5 or K=10) is the gold standard for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. Use `GridSearchCV` or `RandomizedSearchCV` from scikit-learn.
- **Feature scaling** (`StandardScaler`) is *mandatory* before KNN, Logistic Regression, and gradient-descent-based models. It is *not* required for tree-based models.
- **Bias-Variance tradeoff**: high-degree polynomials → low bias, high variance. Regularisation balances them.
- **Encoding**: Use one-hot encoding for nominal categoricals (e.g., `Neighborhood`); ordinal encoding preserves order for ordinal categoricals (e.g., `ExterQual`: `Ex > Gd > TA > Fa`).
- **Final pipeline order**: Imputation → Encoding → Scaling → Train/Test Split → Model → Cross-Validation → Hyperparameter Tuning → Test Evaluation.
- **KTU 2024 scheme** emphasises *understanding* over *coding memorisation*. Be ready to derive equations and interpret metrics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algorithms for Big Data and Scalability: Introduction to Big Data Algorithms - Overview of big data challenges and processing techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST785 - ALGORITHMS FOR DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Algorithms for Big Data and Scalability: Introduction to Big Data Algorithms - Overview of big data challenges and processing techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Big Data" — The Formal KTU Definition

**Big Data** refers to data sets whose **size (Volume)**, **input speed (Velocity)**, and **structural diversity (Variety)** exceed the ability of conventional relational databases and single-machine algorithms to capture, manage, and process them within an acceptable elapsed time. In the context of KTU's *Algorithms for Data Science* syllabus, Big Data is not just "a lot of data" — it is a regime where the input size $N$ is so large that classical $O(N \log N)$ or $O(N^2)$ algorithms running on a single commodity node become **infeasible** in time, memory, or both.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Approved Wording):**
> "Big Data is a collection of data that is massive in volume, yet growing exponentially with time, and consists of varied formats (structured, semi-structured, unstructured), which cannot be processed, stored, or analyzed using traditional data-processing systems."

## 1.2 The "5 V's" of Big Data — The Foundation Block

The 5 V's is the **single most-asked 3-mark question** in KTU ESE on this topic. Memorize it as a block.

| V | Dimension | Engineering Meaning | Typical Metric |
|---|-----------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Volume** | Size of data | Terabytes to Zettabytes (ZB) | $\geq 1\ \text{TB}$ threshold |
| **Velocity** | Speed of generation | Streams at GB/sec | $\geq 10^3$ records/sec |
| **Variety** | Data formats | Structured / semi / unstructured | $\geq 3$ schema types |
| **Veracity** | Data trustworthiness | Noise, bias, inconsistency | $\leq 95\%$ accuracy |
| **Value** | Business insight | Extracted intelligence | ROI per TB processed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** If the question says "List the characteristics of Big Data" (3 marks) — write all 5 V's with one-line definitions each. If it says "Explain", add a real example for each (e.g., Volume → Facebook generates 4 PB/day; Velocity → Stock ticker; Variety → Twitter JSON + images + videos).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Library Problem"

Imagine a single librarian (your laptop) trying to find a specific book in a library that has **doubled in size every hour** for a year.

- **Classical algorithm approach:** The librarian walks every aisle → impossible after Day 30.
- **Big Data approach:** Hire 10,000 librarians (a *cluster*), give each a stack of books (a *partition*), let them work in parallel, and use a master index to merge their answers.

That "cluster of workers + parallel work + index" idea is the **entire spirit of Big Data algorithms**. Everything else — Hadoop, Spark, MapReduce, sketching, streaming — is a clever engineering realization of this simple intuition.

## 1.4 Big Data Algorithms vs Classical Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction (Frequently Asked):**
>
> | Aspect | Classical Algorithm | Big Data Algorithm |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Data fits in RAM** | Yes | No (RAM-bound failure) |
> | **Number of passes** | Often multiple passes | **One pass / sub-linear** preferred |
> | **Exact result** | Yes | Often **approximate** (probabilistic) |
> | **Execution model** | Single-thread / single-node | **Parallel + distributed** |
> | **Memory model** | RAM, pointers | Streams, sketches, hash tables |
> | **Failure handling** | Assumes no failure | **Fault-tolerant** (re-execution) |

The move from classical to Big Data is a move from **deterministic exactness on small data** → **probabilistic/proximal answers on massive data, delivered fast**.

## 1.5 Why a Separate Course Module on This?

Modern data-science pipelines (recommendation, fraud detection, search ranking) deal with $N$ in the **billions to trillions**. A single scan at 1 GB/sec on 1 PB of data takes $\approx 11.5$ days. Sequential algorithms are mathematically provably infeasible — hence the need for:

1. **Algorithmic ingenuity** — sub-linear, streaming, sketching, sampling, hashing.
2. **Systems engineering** — MapReduce, Spark, distributed file systems, clusters.
3. **Theoretical frameworks** — CAP theorem, PAC learning, communication complexity.

This module gives you the **conceptual vocabulary** to understand all three pillars.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Big Data Challenges

A KTU 14-mark answer on "Big Data challenges" must be structured along these three pillars. Do **not** write a vague essay — examiners reward **structured, bulleted, technically accurate** answers.

### Pillar A — Data-Side Challenges
- **Volume:** Storage cost grows linearly with $N$ but I/O bandwidth grows sub-linearly → bottleneck.
- **Velocity:** Real-time ingestion with bounded memory (streaming constraint).
- **Variety:** Schema-less parsing (JSON, Parquet, Avro, ORC) → no fixed data type.
- **Veracity:** Noise, missing values, duplicates → algorithm must be robust.
- **Value:** Density of useful signal is often $< 10^{-6}$ → algorithmic "needle in haystack".

### Pillar B — Computational Challenges
- **Memory wall:** Data $\gg$ RAM → algorithm must operate on disk/stream.
- **I/O bottleneck:** Disk seek ≈ 10 ms, RAM access ≈ 100 ns → **$10^5$× difference**.
- **Communication cost:** Network bandwidth between nodes is the true bottleneck in a distributed system — **moving data is more expensive than computing on it**.
- **Stragglers:** One slow node can dominate job time in Hadoop/Spark → need speculative execution.

### Pillar C — Algorithmic Challenges
- **Sub-linearity:** Need $o(N)$ or $O(\log N)$ algorithms (e.g., Bloom filters, Count-Min sketch).
- **Approximation:** $(\epsilon, \delta)$-approximations are accepted.
- **One-pass / streaming:** Cannot re-read data.
- **Parallelizability:** Algorithm must decompose cleanly (associative, commutative).
- **Fault tolerance:** Re-execution and replication must be cheap.

## 2.2 Processing Techniques — The KTU Master Taxonomy

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-Examiner Favorite:** "Classify the Big Data processing techniques with examples." (14-mark question)

### Taxonomy Tree

```
Big Data Processing
├── 1. Batch Processing (latency: minutes–hours)
│     ├── MapReduce (Hadoop)
│     └── Apache Spark (in-memory batch)
├── 2. Stream Processing (latency: ms–seconds)
│     ├── Apache Flink
│     ├── Apache Kafka Streams
│     └── Spark Streaming (micro-batch)
├── 3. Graph Processing (latency: minutes)
│     ├── Google Pregel
│     └── Apache Giraph
├── 4. Interactive / Query Processing (latency: sub-second)
│     ├── Google Dremel / BigQuery
│     └── Apache Impala, Presto
└── 5. Hybrid / Lambda / Kappa Architecture
      ├── Lambda: batch layer + speed layer
      └── Kappa: stream-only with replayable log
```

### Detailed Comparison Table (Write This in the Exam)

| Technique | Latency | Data Size | Example Tool | Use Case |
|-----------|---------|-----------|--------------|----------|
| **Batch** | Hours | Petabytes | Hadoop MapReduce | Nightly log aggregation |
| **Micro-batch** | Seconds | Terabytes | Spark Streaming | Near-real-time dashboards |
| **True Stream** | Milliseconds | Gigabytes/sec | Flink, Kafka Streams | Fraud detection, IoT |
| **Graph** | Minutes | Web-scale graphs | Pregel, Giraph | PageRank, shortest path |
| **Interactive SQL** | < 1 sec | Petabytes | Presto, BigQuery | Ad-hoc analyst queries |

## 2.3 MapReduce — The Foundational Programming Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MapReduce is the single most-tested concept in Module 4.** Master its two functions.

**Definition:** MapReduce is a distributed programming model proposed by Dean & Ghemawat (Google, 2004) that automatically parallelizes the processing of massive data sets across a cluster by abstracting the programmer's view into two pure functions: **Map** and **Reduce**, with an implicit **Shuffle & Sort** phase in between.

**The two functions:**

- **Map:** $(k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list of } (k_2, v_2)$
- **Reduce:** $(k_2, \text{list of } v_2) \rightarrow \text{list of } (k_3, v_3)$

**Canonical Example — Word Count:**

```
Input: ["hello world", "hello bigdata", "world of bigdata"]

Map phase (per record):
("hello world", _)   → [("hello", 1), ("world", 1)]
("hello bigdata", _) → [("hello", 1), ("bigdata", 1)]
("world of bigdata", _) → [("world", 1), ("of", 1), ("bigdata", 1)]

Shuffle & Sort (group by key):
hello  → [1, 1]
world  → [1, 1]
bigdata→ [1, 1]
of     → [1]

Reduce phase (sum the list per key):
hello  → 2
world  → 2
bigdata→ 2
of     → 1
```

## 2.4 Scalability — Vertical vs Horizontal

**Scalability** is the property of a system to handle increasing load by adding resources.

- **Vertical scaling (scale-up):** Add more CPU/RAM/disk to **one** node. Limited by hardware ceiling. No code change.
- **Horizontal scaling (scale-out):** Add **more nodes** to a cluster. Theoretically unlimited. Requires distributed algorithms.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Distinction (3-mark question):** "Vertical scaling increases the capacity of a single machine; horizontal scaling increases the number of machines. Big Data prefers horizontal scaling because the data size outgrows the capacity of any single machine."

## 2.5 The CAP Theorem (Brewer's Theorem)

A distributed data store can simultaneously deliver **at most two** of the following three guarantees:

1. **C — Consistency:** Every read sees the most recent write.
2. **A — Availability:** Every request receives a (non-error) response.
3. **P — Partition tolerance:** The system continues to operate despite arbitrary network message loss between nodes.

$$\text{CAP} : \quad |\{C, A, P\}_{\text{chosen}}| \leq 2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Trap:** In real distributed systems, **P is non-negotiable** (network partitions *will* happen). So the real choice is **CP vs AP**. Do NOT write "you can have all three" in the exam.

## 2.6 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!CAUTION]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table.**

| # | Concept / Formula | Meaning | Where Used |
|---|------------------|---------|------------|
| 1 | $N \gg M_{\text{RAM}}$ | Data size $N$ exceeds available RAM | Streaming motivation |
| 2 | $T_{\text{seq}}(N) = \Theta(f(N))$ | Time on 1 node | Lower bound for sequential |
| 3 | $T_{\text{par}}(N, p) \approx \frac{T_{\text{seq}}(N)}{p} + T_{\text{comm}}$ | Parallel time with $p$ nodes | Amdahl's law territory |
| 4 | $S_p = \frac{T_{\text{seq}}}{T_{\text{par}}}$ | Speedup with $p$ processors | Scalability metric |
| 5 | $E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} \leq 1$ | Parallel efficiency | Ideal $E_p = 1$ |
| 6 | Amdahl: $S_p \leq \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$ | $f$ = serial fraction | Hard scalability ceiling |
| 7 | $M_{R} = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon entropy (bits) | Information content of data |
| 8 | $P_{\text{false-pos}} \approx \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^k$ | Bloom filter false-positive prob. | Membership testing |
| 9 | $\Pr[\vert \bar{X}_n - \mu \vert \geq \epsilon] \leq \frac{\sigma^2}{n\epsilon^2}$ | Chebyshev bound | Sampling theory |
| 10 | $h(k) = k \bmod m$ | Hash function for partitioning | Sharding/MapReduce shuffle |

## 2.7 Why These Techniques Matter in Industry

- **MapReduce** → still used at Google, Meta for batch log analytics.
- **Spark** → powers ML pipelines at Netflix, Uber, Amazon.
- **Streaming** → Stripe fraud detection, Twitter timeline ranking.
- **Bloom filters & Count-Min sketch** → every CDN (Akamai, Cloudflare), every database query planner.
- **CAP theorem** → architects choose DynamoDB (AP) vs HBase (CP) deliberately.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry tie-in fetches 1–2 grace marks** in KTU answers. Add one line: "In real-world systems such as Google's Bigtable, this trade-off is exploited by choosing an AP design with eventual consistency."
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section builds the **mathematical backbone** that supports the conceptual understanding. Every step is explicit — no "it is easy to see" shortcuts, as per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE mandate.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why $O(N)$ Sequential is Infeasible: The Memory Wall

**Goal:** Show that a single-pass $O(N)$ algorithm on 1 PB of data on a single node is *practically* infeasible.

### Step 1: Define the data size

$$N = 1\ \text{PB} = 10^{15}\ \text{bytes} = 10^{12}\ \text{KB}$$

### Step 2: Define typical sequential I/O bandwidth

A modern NVMe SSD sequential read ≈ $3.5\ \text{GB/s}$. A single HDD ≈ $150\ \text{MB/s}$.

$$B_{\text{ssd}} = 3.5 \times 10^9\ \text{bytes/s}, \qquad B_{\text{hdd}} = 1.5 \times 10^8\ \text{bytes/s}$$

### Step 3: Compute the lower bound on time for a single scan

The minimum time to read $N$ bytes once is:

$$T_{\min} = \frac{N}{B}$$

### Step 4: Evaluate for SSD and HDD

$$T_{\min}^{\text{ssd}} = \frac{10^{15}}{3.5 \times 10^9} \approx 2.857 \times 10^5\ \text{seconds} \approx 3.31\ \text{days}$$

$$T_{\min}^{\text{hdd}} = \frac{10^{15}}{1.5 \times 10^8} \approx 6.67 \times 10^6\ \text{seconds} \approx 77.1\ \text{days}$$

### Step 5: Conclusion

A single scan of 1 PB takes **3+ days on SSD** and **77+ days on HDD**. This **memory-wall argument** is the formal justification for distributed/parallel processing. A cluster of $p=100$ SSD nodes can finish the same scan in:

$$T_{\text{cluster}} \approx \frac{T_{\min}^{\text{ssd}}}{100} \approx 0.033\ \text{days} \approx 48\ \text{minutes}$$

(ignoring communication overhead).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Amdahl's Law for Maximum Speedup

**Goal:** Derive the maximum speedup $S_p$ achievable by parallelizing a fraction $(1 - f)$ of a workload across $p$ processors, where $f$ is the **serial fraction**.

### Step 1: Define the time on a single processor

Let $T_1$ be the time on one processor. Without loss of generality, set $T_1 = 1$.

$$T_1 = f \cdot 1 + (1 - f) \cdot 1 = 1$$

### Step 2: Define the time on $p$ processors

The serial part still takes time $f$. The parallel part takes time $\frac{1-f}{p}$:

$$T_p = f + \frac{1 - f}{p}$$

### Step 3: Apply the speedup definition

$$S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_p} = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$$

### Step 4: Take the limit as $p \to \infty$ (the scalability ceiling)

$$\lim_{p \to \infty} S_p = \frac{1}{f}$$

This is the famous **Amdahl ceiling**: even with infinite processors, speedup is bounded by $\frac{1}{f}$. If 5% of the code is serial ($f = 0.05$), the maximum possible speedup is $20\times$, no matter how many nodes you add.

### Step 5: Numerical example for KTU exam

Suppose $f = 0.10$ and $p = 16$:

$$S_{16} = \frac{1}{0.10 + \frac{0.90}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.05625} = \frac{1}{0.15625} = 6.4$$

$$\text{Efficiency} = \frac{S_{16}}{16} = \frac{6.4}{16} = 0.40 = 40\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Typical Question:** "A program spends 20% of its time in serial code. Find the maximum speedup achievable with 8 processors." → Use $f = 0.20$, $p = 8$ in the formula.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bloom Filter False Positive Probability

**Goal:** Derive the asymptotic false-positive probability of a Bloom filter with $m$ bits, $n$ inserted elements, and $k$ hash functions.

### Step 1: Probability a single bit is *not* set by one hash

After one hash, probability a bit remains 0:

$$p_0^{(1)} = 1 - \frac{1}{m}$$

### Step 2: After $k$ hashes for one element

$$p_0^{(1\ \text{elem})} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^k$$

### Step 3: After inserting $n$ elements

Assuming independence:

$$p_0^{(n)} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{kn} \approx e^{-kn/m}$$

### Step 4: Probability a bit is 1

$$p_1 = 1 - e^{-kn/m}$$

### Step 5: False positive probability

A query is a false positive if **all** $k$ hashed positions are 1 (independently):

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{FP}} = \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^k\,}$$

### Step 6: Optimal $k$

To minimize $P_{\text{FP}}$, set $k = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2$, giving:

$$P_{\text{FP}}^{*} \approx \left(0.6185\right)^{m/n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this in 7-mark sub-parts:** "Explain how a Bloom filter works in a distributed cache." Derivation above + a numerical example (e.g., $m/n = 10$ → $P_{\text{FP}} \approx 0.008$).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Streaming Median via Two Heaps (Conceptual)

**Goal:** Sketch the proof of why a streaming median algorithm using a max-heap (lower half) and a min-heap (upper half) achieves $O(\log N)$ per insertion and $O(1)$ per query.

**Invariant:** After $N$ insertions, $|H_{\max}| = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor$ and $|H_{\min}| = \lceil N/2 \rceil$ (sizes differ by at most 1).

**Per insertion:**

1. Insert into one heap (depending on value vs. median) — $O(\log N)$.
2. Rebalance to restore invariant — $O(\log N)$.

**Per median query:** Top of max-heap or average of two tops — $O(1)$.

**Time complexity:**

$$T_{\text{insert}}(N) = O(\log N), \qquad T_{\text{query}} = O(1)$$

**Space complexity:** $O(N)$ to store all elements (necessary for exact median).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Technique

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the KTU "decision matrix" — a high-yield 14-mark answer format.**

| Problem Type | Data Fits in RAM? | Need Exact Answer? | Recommended Technique | Time Complexity |
|--------------|-------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|----------------|
| Word count on 100 GB | No | Yes | MapReduce (Hadoop) | $O(N/p)$ parallel |
| Median of 1 TB stream | No | Approx. | $t$-digest or two-heap sketch | $O(\log N)$ per item |
| "Did user X visit URL Y?" | No | Approx. OK | Bloom filter | $O(k)$ per query |
| Top-100 hashtags in 1 PB | No | Approx. OK | Count-Min + heap | $O(1)$ per update |
| PageRank on web graph | No | Approx. OK | Pregel / Spark GraphX | $O(\text{iter} \cdot \lvert E \rvert / p)$ |
| SQL aggregate on warehouse | No | Yes | Presto / Spark SQL | $O(N/p)$ |
| Real-time fraud score | Streaming | No | Flink + ML model | ms latency |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — MapReduce Cost Model

**Problem:** A Hadoop cluster has 10 nodes. Each node can read input at $100\ \text{MB/s}$. Input size is $10\ \text{TB}$. Map phase runs at full bandwidth. Reduce phase has a 1:1 ratio with map output. Compute total time.

### Step 1: Total data per node

$$D_{\text{per node}} = \frac{10\ \text{TB}}{10} = 1\ \text{TB} = 10^6\ \text{MB}$$

### Step 2: Map time per node

$$T_{\text{map}} = \frac{D_{\text{per node}}}{B_{\text{node}}} = \frac{10^6}{100} = 10{,}000\ \text{seconds} \approx 2.78\ \text{hours}$$

### Step 3: Shuffle time (assumed equal to map output transfer)

$$T_{\text{shuffle}} \approx T_{\text{map}} = 2.78\ \text{hours}$$

### Step 4: Reduce time (assume reduce reads 1:1 of map output)

$$T_{\text{reduce}} = 2.78\ \text{hours}$$

### Step 5: Total MapReduce time

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{map}} + T_{\text{shuffle}} + T_{\text{reduce}} = 3 \times 2.78 = 8.34\ \text{hours}$$

> [!TIP]
> **This kind of "cost-model" calculation is a 7-mark favorite.** Memorize the pattern: $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{map}} + T_{\text{shuffle}} + T_{\text{reduce}}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

> All Mermaid diagrams below use **alphanumeric, letter-prefixed node IDs** and **clean uppercase labels** (no markdown bold, no HTML tables inside node text) to compile safely.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List and explain any three characteristics (V's) of Big Data.**

**Model Answer:**

The three primary characteristics of Big Data are:

1. **Volume:** Refers to the massive size of data, typically in the range of **terabytes to zettabytes**. Example: Facebook generates approximately 4 petabytes of data per day.

2. **Velocity:** Refers to the speed at which data is generated, captured, and processed. Example: Stock exchange tickers generate millions of transactions per second, requiring real-time processing.

3. **Variety:** Refers to the different formats of data — **structured** (relational tables), **semi-structured** (JSON, XML), and **unstructured** (images, videos, free text). Example: A single Twitter post contains text, hashtags, images, and metadata.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only 2 V's and miss one → loses 1 mark. Always cover all **5 V's** if the question is open-ended; if "any three" is specified, pick the strongest three with one-line examples.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between vertical scaling and horizontal scaling.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Vertical Scaling (Scale Up) | Horizontal Scaling (Scale Out) |
|-----------|------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Method** | Add more resources (CPU, RAM, disk) to a **single** node | Add **more nodes** to the cluster |
| **Limit** | Bounded by hardware ceiling of one machine | Theoretically unlimited |
| **Cost** | High-end hardware is expensive per unit | Commodity hardware is cheap per unit |
| **Downtime** | Usually requires downtime to upgrade | Online, nodes added without interruption |
| **Fault tolerance** | Single point of failure | Tolerates node failures (replication) |
| **Used by** | Traditional RDBMS (Oracle, MySQL on big server) | Big Data (Hadoop HDFS, Cassandra) |

Big Data systems prefer **horizontal scaling** because data growth outpaces any single machine's upgrade path.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Choice 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **MapReduce programming model** in detail. Describe the **Map** and **Reduce** functions with their input-output signatures, and illustrate with the **Word Count** example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **key challenges of Big Data** under three heads: data-side challenges, computational challenges, and algorithmic challenges. Briefly mention how each is addressed in modern systems. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Q.A(a): MapReduce

**Definition (2 marks):** MapReduce is a distributed programming model proposed by Dean & Ghemawat (2004) for processing massive data sets in parallel across a cluster. It abstracts computation into two user-defined functions — **Map** and **Reduce** — with an automatic **Shuffle & Sort** phase in between.

**Function signatures (2 marks):**

- **Map:** $\quad (k_1, v_1) \;\rightarrow\; \text{list of } (k_2, v_2)$
- **Reduce:** $\quad (k_2, \text{list of } v_2) \;\rightarrow\; \text{list of } (k_3, v_3)$

**Word Count illustration (3 marks):**

Input: 3 lines — `"hello world"`, `"hello bigdata"`, `"world of bigdata"`.

**Map phase** (emits a key-value pair per word):
- Line 1 → `(hello, 1), (world, 1)`
- Line 2 → `(hello, 1), (bigdata, 1)`
- Line 3 → `(world, 1), (of, 1), (bigdata, 1)`

**Shuffle & Sort phase** (groups by key):
- `hello → [1, 1]`
- `world → [1, 1]`
- `bigdata → [1, 1]`
- `of → [1]`

**Reduce phase** (sums the values per key):
- `hello → 2`, `world → 2`, `bigdata → 2`, `of → 1`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students forget to show the **Shuffle & Sort** phase — it is worth 1 mark by itself. Always include it explicitly.

---

### Model Solution — Q.A(b): Big Data Challenges

**Data-side challenges (2–3 marks):**
- **Volume:** Storage and I/O bottlenecks; addressed via **distributed file systems** (HDFS splits files into 128 MB blocks across nodes).
- **Velocity:** Real-time ingestion; addressed via **stream processing frameworks** (Flink, Kafka Streams).
- **Variety:** Mixed schemas; addressed via **schema-on-read** (Parquet, Avro).
- **Veracity:** Noisy data; addressed via **cleansing algorithms** and **data lineage tools**.

**Computational challenges (2–3 marks):**
- **Memory wall:** $N \gg$ RAM; addressed by streaming and sketching.
- **I/O bottleneck:** Sequential disk is $10^5\times$ slower than RAM; addressed by **locality-aware scheduling** in Hadoop.
- **Network bottleneck:** Inter-node communication dominates; addressed by **data-local computation** (move code to data, not vice versa).
- **Stragglers:** Slowest node delays the job; addressed by **speculative execution**.

**Algorithmic challenges (2 marks):**
- Need for **sub-linear, one-pass, approximate, parallelizable, fault-tolerant** algorithms.
- Examples: **Bloom filter, Count-Min sketch, HyperLogLog, MinHash, t-digest**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Choice 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **CAP theorem** with a neat diagram. Discuss the trade-off between **Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance** with one real-world example for each of the CP and AP systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Lambda and Kappa architectures** for Big Data processing. Compare them on at least four parameters. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Q.B(a): CAP Theorem

**Statement (2 marks):** Proposed by Eric Brewer (2000), the CAP theorem states that a distributed data store can simultaneously provide **at most two** of the following three guarantees:

- **Consistency (C):** Every read sees the most recent committed write.
- **Availability (A):** Every request receives a non-error response (no timeout).
- **Partition tolerance (P):** The system continues operating despite arbitrary network message loss between nodes.

**Diagrammatic representation (2 marks):** Draw a triangle with vertices C, A, P and shade the CA, CP, AP sides (3 sides = 3 possible pairs).

**CP systems (1.5 marks):** Examples — **HBase, MongoDB (default), Redis**. Chosen when data correctness is critical, e.g., **banking transactions** (cannot show stale balance).

**AP systems (1.5 marks):** Examples — **Amazon DynamoDB, Cassandra, CouchDB**. Chosen when uptime is critical, e.g., **shopping cart** on e-commerce sites (better to keep cart available than to lock it down for a sync).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "you cannot have all three" without explaining **why P is mandatory in practice**. State explicitly: "Since network partitions *will* occur in any real distributed system, P is non-negotiable, so the real choice is CP vs AP." This is worth 1 mark.

---

### Model Solution — Q.B(b): Lambda vs Kappa Architecture

**Lambda Architecture (3 marks):** Proposed by Nathan Marz (2011), it splits processing into two layers:
- **Batch layer:** Uses Hadoop/MapReduce for accurate, high-latency computation on full historical data.
- **Speed layer:** Uses stream processors (Storm, Spark Streaming) for low-latency, approximate results on recent data.
- **Serving layer:** Merges both layers to answer queries.

**Kappa Architecture (3 marks):** Proposed by Jay Kreps (2014), it eliminates the batch layer. All data flows through a **single stream-processing pipeline** (Flink, Kafka Streams). Historical recomputation is done by **replaying the log** (e.g., Kafka topics).

**Comparison (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Lambda | Kappa |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| **Layers** | Batch + Speed | Stream only |
| **Codebase** | Two (different APIs) | One (single stream processor) |
| **Recomputation** | Re-run batch on full data | Replay log through stream |
| **Latency** | Mixed (batch slow, speed fast) | Uniformly low |
| **Complexity** | Higher (merge logic needed) | Lower (simpler) |
| **Use case** | When batch accuracy is critical (analytics) | When events dominate (event sourcing, IoT) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Summary

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in Module 4 Answers:**
> 1. Forgetting the **Shuffle & Sort** phase in MapReduce diagrams (loses 1 mark).
> 2. Stating "CAP can have all three" (loses 1 mark).
> 3. Confusing **horizontal scaling** (more nodes) with **vertical scaling** (more RAM) (loses 1 mark).
> 4. Omitting the **optimal-$k$ formula** in Bloom filter answers (loses 1 mark).
> 5. Writing Amdahl's law as $S_p = p$ instead of $\frac{1}{f + (1-f)/p}$ (loses 1 mark).
> 6. Forgetting to mention **fault tolerance** in Hadoop/Spark (loses 1 mark).
> 7. Mixing up **batch vs stream** examples (e.g., saying "Hadoop is used for fraud detection" — wrong, use Flink/Kafka).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **5 V's of Big Data:** Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value — list with one-line definitions and a real example.
- ✅ **Big Data definition:** Data too big / fast / diverse for single-machine classical systems.
- ✅ **MapReduce = Map + Shuffle + Reduce.** Word Count is the canonical example.
- ✅ **Batch vs Stream:** Batch (MapReduce, Spark) has minute–hour latency; Stream (Flink, Kafka) has ms–s latency.
- ✅ **Vertical vs Horizontal scaling:** Vertical = scale-up (one machine); Horizontal = scale-out (many machines).
- ✅ **CAP theorem:** At most 2 of {C, A, P}. In practice, P is mandatory → choose CP (HBase) or AP (DynamoDB).
- ✅ **Amdahl's law:** $S_p = \frac{1}{f + (1-f)/p}$; ceiling $= 1/f$ as $p \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Bloom filter FP:** $P_{\text{FP}} = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k$; optimal $k = (m/n) \ln 2$.
- ✅ **Memory wall argument:** $T_{\min} = N / B$ → 1 PB single-node scan ≈ 3 days on SSD.
- ✅ **Big Data algorithms must be:** sub-linear, one-pass, parallel, approximate, fault-tolerant.
- ✅ **Lambda vs Kappa:** Lambda = batch + speed layer; Kappa = stream-only with log replay.
- ✅ **Always state that Big Data prefers horizontal scaling due to fault tolerance and the absence of a single hardware ceiling.**
- ✅ **Examples to memorize:** Hadoop = batch log aggregation; Spark = ML pipelines; Flink = fraud detection; Bloom filter = URL deduplication; HBase = CP storage; Cassandra = AP storage.

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Strategy (Night-Before Exam):**
> 1. Draw the **MapReduce pipeline** (Map → Shuffle → Reduce) with Word Count — 5 minutes.
> 2. Recite the **5 V's** with examples — 3 minutes.
> 3. Write down **Amdahl's law** and compute one numerical — 5 minutes.
> 4. Sketch **CAP triangle** and write one CP + one AP example — 3 minutes.
> 5. Skim the **decision matrix** (Section 3.5) to cement technique-vs-problem matching — 5 minutes.
> Total: ~20 minutes for a confident 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distributed computing frameworks: Hadoop, Spark
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST785 - ALGORITHMS FOR DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Distributed computing frameworks: Hadoop, Spark

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Apache Hadoop — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Apache Hadoop** is an open-source, Java-based **distributed computing framework** that enables the storage and processing of extremely large datasets (Big Data) across clusters of commodity computers using simple programming models. It is designed to scale up from a single server to thousands of machines, each offering local computation and storage.

Hadoop is built on **four core modules**:
- **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** — Distributed storage layer.
- **MapReduce** — Distributed batch processing engine.
- **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** — Cluster resource management and job scheduling.
- **Hadoop Common** — Shared utilities and libraries.

The official release baseline as of 2024 is **Hadoop 3.3.6**, supporting single-node and multi-node clusters. Hadoop guarantees **high availability (99.9% uptime)** and **fault tolerance** through data replication (default replication factor = **3**).

### 1.2 Apache Spark — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apache Spark** is a unified analytics engine for **large-scale distributed data processing**, designed for speed, ease of use, and sophisticated analytics. It extends the MapReduce model by supporting **in-memory computation**, enabling workloads up to **100x faster** than Hadoop MapReduce for iterative algorithms (e.g., Machine Learning).

Spark operates on the concept of **RDDs (Resilient Distributed Datasets)** and supports multiple workloads: batch, streaming (Structured Streaming), MLlib (Machine Learning), GraphX (Graph Processing), and Spark SQL.

The 2024 stable release is **Apache Spark 3.5.x**, with **PySpark** as the Python API widely used in industry.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Hadoop = A Massive Library with Photocopy Machines**

Imagine a library with millions of books that simply cannot fit in one room. Hadoop acts like:
- **HDFS** = Multiple library branches, each storing a portion of the books. Every important book is **photocopied 3 times** and stored in different branches (data replication).
- **MapReduce** = A librarian who first **maps** each book to a shelf based on a topic, then **reduces** the results by combining similar topics.
- **YARN** = The head librarian who assigns workers (resources) to specific tasks.

**Spark = A Genius Librarian with a Notepad**

Spark is the same library, but now the librarian has a **notepad (RAM)**. Instead of going back to the shelf every time to read a book, the librarian:
- Loads relevant pages into the **notepad (in-memory caching)**.
- Performs **iterative lookups, joins, and computations** in milliseconds.
- Only writes the final answer back to the shelf.

This explains why Spark is **~100x faster** for iterative ML tasks — it avoids the **disk I/O bottleneck** of Hadoop.

### 1.4 Why These Frameworks Matter for Data Science

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, **Algorithms for Data Science** focuses on the *algorithmic backbone* of Big Data. Hadoop and Spark are the **execution engines** where algorithms like K-Means, PageRank, and Logistic Regression are parallelized using **data parallelism** and **task parallelism** principles. Understanding them is essential for Module 4 (Parallel & Distributed Algorithms).

**Key Metrics at a Glance:**
- **Data Scale:** Hadoop handles **Petabytes (10¹⁵ bytes)**; Spark handles **Petabytes** in-memory.
- **Latency:** Hadoop MapReduce ≈ **minutes-to-hours**; Spark ≈ **seconds-to-minutes**.
- **Fault Tolerance:** Both use **lineage-based recovery** (Spark) or **replication-based recovery** (HDFS).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 HDFS — The Storage Backbone of Hadoop

HDFS follows the **Master-Slave (or NameNode-DataNode) architecture**.

**Core Components:**
1. **NameNode (Master):** Maintains metadata, file-to-block mapping, directory tree. Stores this in `fsimage` and edit logs.
2. **DataNode (Slaves):** Store actual data blocks. Default block size = **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x and later).
3. **Secondary NameNode:** Periodically merges `fsimage` with edit logs (does **NOT** provide hot standby).
4. **Checkpoints & Replication:** Each block is replicated across **multiple racks** for fault tolerance.

**Write Pipeline (3 replicas):**
- Step 1: Client requests block write permission from NameNode.
- Step 2: NameNode returns list of DataNodes (typically across **2 different racks**).
- Step 3: Data is pushed in a **pipeline** — DataNode 1 → DataNode 2 → DataNode 3.
- Step 4: Acknowledgements flow back to confirm write.

### 2.2 MapReduce — The Processing Engine

MapReduce is a **two-phase functional programming model**:

- **Map Phase:** Input is split, each mapper processes a `(key, value)` pair and emits intermediate `(k', v')` pairs.
- **Shuffle & Sort Phase:** Framework groups all values with the same key and sorts them.
- **Reduce Phase:** Each reducer aggregates values for a key and emits final output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MapReduce works for Big Data:** It moves **computation to data**, not data to computation. This is critical because data is huge, but the program is small — known as the **"code mobility"** principle.

### 2.3 Spark — The In-Memory Evolution

Spark's key innovations are:

1. **RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset):** Immutable, partitioned collection of records that can be operated on in parallel. Five properties: *partitions, dependencies, compute function, partitioner, locations*.
2. **Lazy Evaluation:** Transformations (e.g., `map`, `filter`) are not executed until an *action* (e.g., `count`, `collect`) is called. This allows the DAG scheduler to **optimize the execution plan**.
3. **DAG Scheduler:** Builds a **Directed Acyclic Graph** of stages and tasks. Stages are separated by *shuffle boundaries*.
4. **Lineage:** RDDs remember how they were derived. On failure, Spark recomputes **only the lost partitions** using lineage, not the entire job.
5. **Caching & Persistence:** RDDs can be marked with `persist()` or `cache()` to keep them in memory (StorageLevel: `MEMORY_ONLY`, `MEMORY_AND_DISK`, `DISK_ONLY`).

**Spark Cluster Architecture:**
- **Driver Program:** Runs the `main()` function and holds the `SparkContext`.
- **Cluster Manager:** Standalone, YARN, or Mesos — allocates resources.
- **Executors:** Worker processes that run tasks and store data in memory/disk.

### 2.4 Comparison: Hadoop vs Spark (KTU High-Yield)

| Feature | Apache Hadoop MapReduce | Apache Spark |
|---|---|---|
| **Processing Model** | Disk-based batch | In-memory batch + streaming |
| **Speed (relative)** | 1x (baseline) | Up to **100x** (in-memory), 10x on disk |
| **Ease of Use** | Verbose Java code, low-level | High-level APIs (Scala, Python, R, Java) |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Replication (3x) | Lineage (RDD recomputation) |
| **Real-time Processing** | Not natively supported | Supported via Structured Streaming |
| **Cost (RAM requirement)** | Lower RAM needed | High RAM (because of in-memory) |
| **Iterative Algorithms** | Slow (writes to disk each iteration) | Very fast (cached in RAM) |
| **Lineage Recovery** | Not applicable | Recomputes lost partitions via DAG |
| **Storage** | HDFS (write once, read many) | HDFS, S3, Cassandra, HBase, local FS |
| **Latency** | Minutes-to-hours | Sub-second to minutes |
| **Best Use Case** | Large batch ETL, archival | ML, iterative, streaming, interactive |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Replication Factor (RF)** | $\text{RF} = \frac{\text{Total Copies of Block}}{\text{Original Block}}$ | Default = 3 |
| **Block Size** | $\text{Block Size} \geq \text{File Size} \Rightarrow \text{Number of Blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Block Size}} \right\rceil$ | 128 MB default |
| **Storage Cost (with RF)** | $S_{\text{logical}} \times \text{RF} = S_{\text{physical}}$ | e.g., 1 PB logical $\rightarrow$ 3 PB physical |
| **Amdahl's Law (Speedup)** | $S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}}$ | $p$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = nodes |
| **Data Locality Ratio** | $\eta = \frac{\text{Tasks Executed on Local Node}}{\text{Total Tasks}}$ | $\eta \in [0, 1]$ |
| **Time Complexity (MapReduce WordCount)** | $O\!\left(\frac{\mid D \mid}{N} + k \log k\right)$ | $\mid D \mid$ = data size, $N$ = mappers, $k$ = unique keys |
| **Spark Stage Count** | $\text{Stages} = \text{Wide Transformations} + 1$ | Wide ops trigger new stage |
| **Task Count per Stage** | $\text{Tasks} = \text{Max Partitions across RDDs in Stage}$ | Parallelism unit |
| **RDD Partition Count (recommended)** | $\text{partitions} = 2 \text{ to } 4 \times \text{Number of CPU Cores}$ | Cluster-wide optimum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amdahl's Law is the most exam-relevant formula** for this module. If asked about the theoretical speedup of parallelizing an algorithm, use $S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \frac{p}{N}}$.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Hadoop:** Used by **Facebook (Meta)** to store over **300 PB** of data; by **Yahoo!** for search indexing; by **Netflix** for batch recommendation pipelines.
- **Spark:** Used by **Uber** for real-time fraud detection, **Pinterest** for content ranking, **IBM** for Watson's ML pipelines, and **Databricks** as a commercial offering.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Word Count using MapReduce

Consider a file with two lines:
```
"hello world hello"
"hadoop spark hello"
```

**Input Split (assume 2 mappers, line-by-line):**
- Mapper 1 receives: "hello world hello"
- Mapper 2 receives: "hadoop spark hello"

**Step 1: Map Function Execution**
Each input record is tokenized. For every word, emit `(word, 1)`.

*Mapper 1 output (intermediate):*

$$
\text{emit: } (\text{`hello'}, 1), \quad (\text{`world'}, 1), \quad (\text{`hello'}, 1)
$$

*Mapper 2 output (intermediate):*

$$
\text{emit: } (\text{`hadoop'}, 1), \quad (\text{`spark'}, 1), \quad (\text{`hello'}, 1)
$$

**Step 2: Shuffle and Sort Phase**
The framework groups all values for the same key across all mappers. Keys are sorted lexicographically.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{`hadoop'} &\rightarrow [1] \\
\text{`hello'}  &\rightarrow [1, 1, 1] \\
\text{`spark'}  &\rightarrow [1] \\
\text{`world'}  &\rightarrow [1]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Reduce Function Execution**
For each unique key, sum the list of 1s to get the count.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{`hadoop'} &\rightarrow 1 \\
\text{`hello'}  &\rightarrow 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 \\
\text{`spark'}  &\rightarrow 1 \\
\text{`world'}  &\rightarrow 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Final Output**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{hadoop} \;\; 1 \\
\text{hello} \;\;\;\; 3 \\
\text{spark} \;\;\; 1 \\
\text{world} \;\;\; 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **shuffle phase** is the most expensive step — it involves cross-node network transfer and disk I/O. This is precisely what Spark optimizes by avoiding unnecessary shuffles through **narrow transformations**.

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Storage Cost with Replication

**Given:**
- Logical data size $D_{\text{logical}} = 500 \text{ TB}$
- Default replication factor $\text{RF} = 3$
- Block size $B = 128 \text{ MB}$

**Step 1: Compute physical storage.**

$$
S_{\text{physical}} = D_{\text{logical}} \times \text{RF}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
S_{\text{physical}} = 500 \text{ TB} \times 3 = 1500 \text{ TB} = 1.5 \text{ PB}
$$

**Step 2: Compute number of blocks per file.**
For a single 1 GB file:

$$
N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{1 \text{ GB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1024 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 8 \right\rceil = 8 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3: Total physical blocks on cluster (3 replicas each).**

$$
N_{\text{physical\_blocks}} = N_{\text{blocks}} \times \text{RF} = 8 \times 3 = 24 \text{ blocks}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Amdahl's Law for Spark Parallelism

**Scenario:** A K-Means clustering algorithm in Data Science has a parallel portion $p = 0.95$ (i.e., 95% of the work can be parallelized). The remaining 5% (cluster centroid updates) is sequential. Find the speedup when using $N = 16$ worker nodes.

**Formula:**

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}}
$$

**Step-by-step substitution:**

$$
S(16) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \frac{0.95}{16}}
$$

Compute sequential term:

$$
(1 - p) = 0.05
$$

Compute parallel term:

$$
\frac{p}{N} = \frac{0.95}{16} = 0.059375
$$

Add them:

$$
0.05 + 0.059375 = 0.109375
$$

Final speedup:

$$
S(16) = \frac{1}{0.109375} \approx 9.14
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even with infinite nodes, the maximum speedup is bounded by $\frac{1}{1-p}$. For $p = 0.95$, the **theoretical maximum speedup = 20x**, regardless of cluster size. This is the **Amdahl's Law ceiling** — a frequent KTU question topic.

**Maximum theoretical speedup:**

$$
S_{\max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1 - p} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Hadoop vs Spark

| Scenario | Recommended Framework | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Linear batch ETL on petabytes | **Hadoop** | Lower cost, disk-based, fault-tolerant |
| Iterative ML (K-Means, PageRank) | **Spark** | In-memory caching of intermediate results |
| Real-time fraud detection | **Spark Streaming** | Sub-second latency, micro-batches |
| Archival of log files (write-once) | **Hadoop HDFS** | Optimized for sequential reads |
| Interactive SQL queries | **Spark SQL / Hive** | Catalyst optimizer benefits |
| Graph algorithms (BFS, shortest path) | **Spark GraphX** | In-memory iterative processing |
| Budget-constrained large storage | **Hadoop** | Commodity hardware, lower RAM needs |
