|-----------|--------------|-----------|
| **When** | Before / during job DAG construction | After map stage, before reduce stage |
| **Granularity** | Per-record (row-level) | Per-batch / per-block (file-level) |
| **Cost driver** | Hash function & key space | Network bandwidth & disk I/O |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic (same input → same partition) | Deterministic per stage, but data location varies |
| **Statefulness** | Stateless function of key | Requires intermediate state (spill files) |
| **Optimisation knobs** | Partitioner choice, salting, bucketing | `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`, compression codec, push-merge |
| **Failure impact** | Lost partition → recompute whole input split | Lost shuffle block → re-fetch from mapper |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Distributed Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceA[Source HDFS Block A] --> mapper1[Mapper Task 1]
    sourceB[Source HDFS Block B] --> mapper2[Mapper Task 2]
    sourceC[Source HDFS Block C] --> mapper3[Mapper Task 3]
    sourceD[Source HDFS Block D] --> mapper4[Mapper Task 4]

    mapper1 --> partFunc{Partitioner h key mod k}
    mapper2 --> partFunc
    mapper3 --> partFunc
    mapper4 --> partFunc

    partFunc --> buf1[Map Output Buffer 1]
    partFunc --> buf2[Map Output Buffer 2]
    partFunc --> buf3[Map Output Buffer 3]
    partFunc --> buf4[Map Output Buffer 4]

    buf1 --> spill1[Spill Sort Merge]
    buf2 --> spill2[Spill Sort Merge]
    buf3 --> spill3[Spill Sort Merge]
    buf4 --> spill4[Spill Sort Merge]

    spill1 -->|HTTP Fetch| reduce1[Reducer Task 1 key range 0 to 25]
    spill2 -->|HTTP Fetch| reduce1
    spill3 -->|HTTP Fetch| reduce2[Reducer Task 2 key range 26 to 50]
    spill4 -->|HTTP Fetch| reduce2

    reduce1 --> out1[Output Partition 0]
    reduce2 --> out2[Output Partition 1]
```

### 4.2 Consistent Hashing Ring

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ringH["Consistent Hash Ring 0 to 2 to the 32 minus 1"]
    nodeA["Node A virtual positions 3 7 12"]
    nodeB["Node B virtual positions 1 9 15"]
    nodeC["Node C virtual positions 5 11 14"]
    keyX["Key X hash 4"]
    keyY["Key Y hash 10"]
    keyZ["Key Z hash 2"]

    ringH --- nodeA
    ringH --- nodeB
    ringH --- nodeC

    keyX -->|"clockwise to 5"| nodeC
    keyY -->|"clockwise to 11"| nodeC
    keyZ -->|"clockwise to 3"| nodeA
```

### 4.3 Shuffle-Phase State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Map
    Map --> Buffering: serialize record
    Buffering --> Spilling: buffer threshold reached
    Spilling --> Merging: collect spills
    Merging --> MapOutputReady: final index written
    MapOutputReady --> Fetching: reducer requests block
    Fetching --> Sorting: merge sort all blocks
    Sorting --> Reducing: apply user function
    Reducing --> [*]: write output
```

### 4.4 Skew Detection & Mitigation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw[Raw Dataset] -->|profile keys| freq[Key Frequency Histogram]
    freq -->|compute CV| cv{CV greater than 0.3}
    cv -->|No| hash[Standard Hash Partition]
    cv -->|Yes| skewed[Skewed Keys Identified]
    skewed -->|isolate| hot[Hot Keys List]
    hot -->|replicate| repl[Replicate Hot Keys to All Reducers]
    repl -->|add salt| salted[Salted Salting Key Range 0 to B]
    salted -->|two-stage agg| partial[Partial Aggregate]
    partial -->|rejoin| final[Final Aggregate by True Key]
    hash --> done[Balanced Partitions]
    final --> done
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Differentiate between *data partitioning* and *data shuffling* in a distributed computing environment. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Data Partitioning (1 mark):** It is the *static* division of an input dataset into disjoint logical chunks (partitions/shards) and their placement on cluster nodes *before* processing begins. Example: HDFS dividing a 1 TB file into **128 MB** blocks and storing them across DataNodes with replication factor **3**.
- **Data Shuffling (1 mark):** It is the *dynamic* inter-node transfer of intermediate key-value records between map and reduce stages so that all values for a given key are co-located at the same reducer. Example: In a `reduceByKey` operation in Apache Spark, the shuffle stage re-distributes tuples according to `h(key) \bmod R`.
- **Key Difference (1 mark):** Partitioning decides *where data lives*; shuffling decides *how data moves* between computational stages.

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] What is *consistent hashing*? Mention one advantage over modular hashing.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Consistent hashing is a partitioning scheme where both keys and nodes are mapped onto the same hash ring (typically of size $2^{32}$); a key is assigned to the *first* node encountered when moving clockwise from the key's hash position. Each physical node is represented by $\sim 100$ virtual nodes to ensure balance.
- **Advantage (1 mark):** Adding or removing a node only requires re-mapping the keys that fall in the affected ring segment, i.e., $O(k/n)$ keys versus $O(k)$ in modular hashing — enabling **elastic scaling** without a "re-hashing storm".

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A** (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **shuffle phase** of a MapReduce / Spark job in detail, listing the four sub-steps and the configuration parameters that control buffer size and parallelism. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With a worked example, derive the expected load per partition and the **load imbalance ratio** when a uniform hash partitioner distributes $N = 10^6$ records into $k = 100$ partitions. What is the standard deviation? **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Shuffle Phase — Detailed Explanation [7 marks]**

The shuffle phase is the data-exchange mechanism that moves intermediate records from upstream *map* tasks to downstream *reduce* tasks, grouping all records sharing the same key on the same reducer. The four sub-steps are:

**Sub-step 1 — Map-side partitioning (1.5 marks).** Every mapper, upon producing a key-value pair $(k, v)$, applies a *partitioner* function $p$ to determine the target reducer:

$$\text{target\_reducer} = p(k) \bmod R$$

In Spark, this is governed by `spark.default.parallelism` and `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`; in MapReduce, by the `Partitioner<K, V>` subclass.

**Sub-step 2 — Serialization & buffering (1.5 marks).** The record is first serialized (Java/Kryo in Spark, Writable in Hadoop). It is then written into a circular in-memory buffer of size `spark.shuffle.file.buffer` (default **32 KB**) inside the map task. Once the buffer reaches 80% (`spark.shuffle.spill.numElementsForceSpillThreshold`), a spill is triggered.

**Sub-step 3 — Spill, sort & merge (2 marks).** When spilling, the buffer is sorted by partition id (and then by key) using an in-memory comparator. Each spill is written to local disk as a `mapOutput` segment. After all records are emitted, the spills are merged via an $N$-way merge into a single output file per map task, accompanied by an index file. This step uses the `SortShuffleWriter` in Spark and `MapTask` sort phase in Hadoop.

**Sub-step 4 — Reduce-side fetch, merge-sort & reduce (2 marks).** Each reducer contacts every mapper (via the `MapOutputTracker` in Spark or via HTTP in Hadoop) and fetches its assigned partition slice. The fetched blocks are merge-sorted. A `Combiner` may have already done partial aggregation on the map side (saving network bytes). The user-defined `reduce` function is then applied to each grouped key, and the output is written to the final storage (HDFS, S3, etc.).

**Key configuration parameters:** `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions = 200`, `spark.shuffle.file.buffer = 32k`, `spark.shuffle.compress = true`, `spark.shuffle.spill.compress = true`, `mapreduce.job.reduces = R`.

---

**(b) Worked Numerical Derivation [7 marks]**

**Given:** $N = 10^6$ records, $k = 100$ partitions, uniform hash function.

**Step 1 — Mean load per partition (2 marks).** Let $X_i \sim \text{Binomial}(N, 1/k)$. The expected number of records in partition $i$ is:

$$\mu = \mathbb{E}[X_i] = N \cdot \frac{1}{k} = \frac{10^6}{100} = 10{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Variance per partition (2 marks).**

$$\text{Var}(X_i) = N \cdot \frac{1}{k} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{k}\right) = 10^6 \cdot \frac{1}{100} \cdot \frac{99}{100} = 9{,}900$$

**Step 3 — Standard deviation (1.5 marks).**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{9{,}900} \approx 99.499 \approx 99.5$$

**Step 4 — Load Imbalance Ratio in worst case (1.5 marks).** By the 3-sigma rule for the maximum of $k$ i.i.d. variables:

$$\max(X_i) \approx \mu + 3\sigma \approx 10{,}000 + 3 \times 99.5 = 10{,}298.5$$

Therefore:

$$\text{LIR} = \frac{\max(X_i)}{\mu} = \frac{10{,}298.5}{10{,}000} \approx 1.030$$

**Conclusion:** Even with a perfectly uniform hash, a 1-million-row dataset spread across 100 buckets shows a worst-case deviation of only **3%** — a direct application of the central limit theorem to hash bucketing.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Compare **hash partitioning**, **range partitioning**, and **round-robin partitioning** with respect to: (i) load balance, (ii) range-query efficiency, (iii) join efficiency, and (iv) ease of elastic re-sizing. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** What is **data skew**? Explain **salting** as a mitigation technique with a suitable example. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table [7 marks — 1.75 marks per dimension]**

| Dimension | Hash Partitioning | Range Partitioning | Round-Robin |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------|-------------|
| **(i) Load Balance** | Excellent when key space is uniform; poor under skew (Zipfian). | Good if sort order matches volume; hotspots on clustered keys. | Perfect for uniform row sizes. |
| **(ii) Range Query** | Poor — full scan of all partitions. | **Excellent** — only partitions overlapping the range are scanned. | Poor — no key locality. |
| **(iii) Join Efficiency** | **Excellent** for equi-joins on the partitioning key (co-located). | Excellent if both tables are range-partitioned on the same key. | Poor — requires full shuffle of both sides. |
| **(iv) Elastic Re-sizing** | Requires re-hashing; use **consistent hashing** for $O(k/n)$ remap. | Requires range re-splitting; can be expensive and disruptive. | Trivial — just adjust the modulus. |

**Conclusion (extra credit):** BigTable/HBase → range; Cassandra → MurmurHash3 consistent; Spark default → hash; broadcast joins circumvent shuffle entirely.

---

**(b) Data Skew & Salting [7 marks]**

**Definition (2 marks):** Data skew is the non-uniform distribution of data across partitions such that one or a few partitions receive disproportionately large volumes, causing straggler tasks and degraded cluster utilization. It is typically quantified by $\text{CV} = \sigma/\mu > 0.3$ or $\text{LIR} > 2$.

**Salting — Concept (2.5 marks):** Salting is a skew-mitigation technique in which a random integer prefix (the "salt") is concatenated with the original key before partitioning. This artificially spreads a hot key's records across multiple reducers, where a *partial* aggregation is performed; the results are then re-aggregated by the *true* key in a second stage.

**Worked Example (2.5 marks):** Consider a `reduceByKey` on the key `city` for a sales dataset where `"Bangalore"` appears 50% of the time. Without salting, the reducer for `Bangalore` becomes a hotspot.

```
Original record:  (city="Bangalore", amount=100)
Salted record:    (city_salted="Bangalore_3", amount=100)   // salt ∈ {0..7}
```

**Step 1:** Apply a map-side transform that appends a random salt in $[0, 7)$:

$$\text{key}_{\text{salted}} = \text{key} \oplus \text{rand}(0, B-1)$$

**Step 2:** Perform partial aggregation: `(Bangalore_0..7) → sum`.

**Step 3:** Second shuffle strips the salt and re-aggregates: `Bangalore → total sum`.

Result: the hot key's load is split across **8** parallel reducers, restoring load balance while preserving correctness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing partitioning with replication.** Partitioning divides data; replication *copies* it. Mixing these in a 14-mark answer loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the difference between combiner and reducer.** A combiner runs on the *map side* (in-memory) and is optional; a reducer runs after the *full shuffle*. Worth 1 mark in part (a) of Q-A.
> 3. **Writing the formula $|x|$ inside a markdown table.** Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to keep the table renderer happy — this is a frequently-cited board complaint.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation step from binomial to CV in part (b).** The board expects $\text{Var}(X_i) = Np(1-p)$, then the ratio. Jumping straight to a number is **not** awarded full marks.
> 5. **Not labelling axes/legends in diagrams.** Mermaid flowcharts are accepted, but you must add short textual annotations in the surrounding prose.
> 6. **Treating salting as a universal fix.** Salting helps *aggregation*, not *joins on the salted key* — state the precondition explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4, Topic: Data Partitioning & Shuffling**

- **Partitioning strategies:** Round-robin, Hash, Range, Custom, Consistent Hashing — know the formula and the failure mode of each.
- **Hash partitioning formula:** $p = h(k) \bmod R$; default $h$ = MurmurHash3 in Spark/Cassandra.
- **Consistent hashing** ring size $= 2^{32}$; virtual nodes per physical node $\approx 100$–256.
- **Shuffle sub-steps:** Partition → Serialize/Buffer → Spill/Merge → Fetch/Sort/Reduce.
- **Spark defaults to remember:** `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions = 200`, `spark.shuffle.file.buffer = 32 KB`, Tungsten sort shuffle on by default in 2.0+.
- **Skew metrics:** $\text{LIR} = \max(L_i)/\bar{L}$ and $\text{CV} = \sigma/\mu$; thresholds $> 2$ and $> 0.3$ respectively.
- **Mitigations for skew:** Salting, custom partitioner, broadcast joins, Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) skew join optimisation.
- **Amdahl's bound:** $S(k) = 1/(s + p/k)$ — quantify why a 1000-node cluster does not speed up an inherently sequential workload.
- **Shuffle cost:** $C_{\text{shuffle}} \approx 2 \sum_{m,r} \vert D_{m,r}\vert$ — network is the bottleneck; compress and combine.
- **Combiner vs. Reducer:** Combiner is optional & runs locally on map output; reducer runs after full shuffle.
- **Push-based shuffle (Spark 3.x):** Merges shuffle blocks on the shuffle service → fewer intermediate files, lower latency.
- **Hash bucket variance:** $\text{Var}(X_i) = N p(1-p)$ with $p = 1/k$; $\text{CV} = \sqrt{(k-1)/N}$ — the bigger the data, the more uniform the hash.
- **Range vs. Hash tradeoff:** Range wins for analytical range scans; Hash wins for point lookups and equi-joins.
- **Default block sizes:** HDFS = 128 MB; Spark shuffle file buffer = 32 KB; replication factor = 3.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling data with memory constraints: external memory algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST785 - ALGORITHMS FOR DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Handling data with memory constraints: external memory algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **External Memory Algorithms (EMAs)** are algorithmic procedures specifically designed to process datasets that are **too large to fit entirely in the main Random Access Memory (RAM)** of a computer. They operate by explicitly managing the transfer of data between **fast but limited internal memory** and **slow but voluminous external storage devices** (such as HDDs, SSDs, or distributed file systems) using the **I/O Model** (also known as the **Aggarwal–Vitter Model**).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context of *Algorithms for Data Science*, external memory algorithms are the foundation of scalable data engineering — they power **database systems, MapReduce/Hadoop, Spark, and disk-based indexing structures** (like B-Trees and LSM-Trees) that handle terabyte- and petabyte-scale information.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** Standard RAM-model algorithms (e.g., QuickSort, Binary Search) analyze computational cost in terms of *CPU operations* (time complexity) only. EMAs, in contrast, account for the **I/O cost** — the dominant bottleneck in modern big-data systems.

---

### 1.2 The Memory Hierarchy (Why External Memory Matters)

Modern computing systems use a layered memory hierarchy. Each layer differs by an order of magnitude in **capacity**, **latency**, and **bandwidth**.

| Layer | Typical Capacity | Latency | Volatility |
|-------|------------------|---------|------------|
| CPU Registers | bytes | ~1 ns | Volatile |
| L1 / L2 / L3 Cache | KB – tens of MB | 1–10 ns | Volatile |
| **Main Memory (RAM)** | **8 – 256 GB** | **~100 ns** | Volatile |
| **SSD (Flash)** | **512 GB – 10 TB** | **~100 µs** | Non-Volatile |
| **HDD (Disk)** | **1 TB – 20 TB** | **~10 ms** | Non-Volatile |
| Network / Cloud | Petabytes | ms – seconds | Non-Volatile |

The performance gap between RAM and disk is roughly **5–6 orders of magnitude**. An algorithm that performs $10^6$ disk I/Os of 10 ms each wastes $10{,}000$ seconds (≈ 2.78 hours) — even if its CPU time is zero.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Librarian's Desk

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition: "The Researcher's Desk"**
> Imagine a researcher writing a literature survey. The **desk** represents *internal memory* (small, fast, and you can manipulate papers instantly). The **library** represents *external storage* (huge, slow, and every trip to fetch/return a book costs significant time). 
> - If the entire library could fit on the desk, no planning is needed — just read everything (this is the *in-memory* case).
> - If only a few books fit, the researcher must plan: **fetch books in bulk**, **work on them while they are on the desk**, and **write summaries back** strategically to minimize the number of trips.
> - This is exactly the principle behind external memory algorithms: **minimize the number of block transfers (trips)**, because each one is enormously expensive compared to in-desk processing.

> [!NOTE]
> **In Algorithmic Terms:**
> - The **desk** = RAM of size $M$
> - A **single book trip** (or shelf-fetch) = one **I/O operation** that transfers a contiguous **block of size $B$**
> - **Goal:** Minimize the number of I/O operations (often called the *I/O complexity*)

---

### 1.4 The I/O Model — Formal Setup

The **Aggarwal–Vitter I/O Model** (1988) abstracts away CPU details and focuses on I/O cost. It has only three parameters:

$$\text{Model Parameters} = \{N, \, M, \, B\}$$

where:
- $N$ = total number of data elements (problem size)
- $M$ = number of elements that fit in internal memory (RAM)
- $B$ = number of elements per **I/O block** (block transfer size)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Constraint:** $1 \le B \le M \le N$ (you cannot transfer more in one I/O than your memory can hold).

The cost of an algorithm is measured in the number of **block transfers** (reads + writes) between internal and external memory, ignoring the cost of in-memory computation.

---

### 1.5 KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights for Module 4

The KTU syllabus identifies the following sub-topics to be mastered:

1. The **memory hierarchy** and motivation for I/O-aware algorithms.
2. The **Aggarwal–Vitter I/O model** and its parameters $N, M, B$.
3. **External Sorting** (External Merge Sort) and its optimality: $\Theta\left(\frac{N}{B}\log_{M/B}\frac{N}{B}\right)$.
4. **External Searching** via B-Trees.
5. **External Hashing** (briefly).
6. **Buffering techniques** (parallel buffering, distribution sweeping).
7. **Cache-oblivious algorithms** (conceptual introduction).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an I/O Operation

When an algorithm accesses any element at address $x$, the system transfers the entire **block of size $B$** that contains $x$ from disk to memory. If the block is already present in a memory **buffer**, no I/O is needed — this is called a **cache hit**. Otherwise, an **I/O fault (cache miss)** occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Spatial Locality Principle:** Accessing consecutive memory addresses in the same block is essentially "free" after the first I/O. Hence, algorithms that process data **sequentially in block-sized chunks** vastly outperform algorithms with **random access patterns**.

---

### 2.2 Why Standard Sorting Algorithms Fail

Consider sorting $N$ elements using a standard in-memory algorithm (e.g., QuickSort) that we naïvely extend to disk by virtual memory:

- QuickSort accesses elements in a **random pattern** (due to partitioning).
- Each random access has a high probability of triggering an I/O.
- Total I/Os $\approx O\left(\frac{N}{B}\log_{M/B}\frac{N}{B}\right)$ in the best case, but in practice often $O(N \log N)$ in I/Os — **catastrophically slow**.

A more disciplined approach: **External Merge Sort**, which respects the $B$-block structure.

---

### 2.3 External Merge Sort — Operational Steps

External Merge Sort is a direct adaptation of standard merge sort to the I/O model. It proceeds in two conceptual phases.

#### **Phase 1: Run Formation (In-Memory Sorting)**

The input is divided into chunks of size $M$ (which fit in RAM).

1. Read in $M$ elements at a time (using $\lceil M/B \rceil$ I/Os per chunk).
2. Sort each chunk in-memory using a standard $O(M \log M)$ sort.
3. Write the sorted chunk back to disk as a **sorted run**.

**Number of runs produced:**

$$R = \left\lceil \frac{N}{M} \right\rceil$$

**I/O cost of Phase 1:**

$$\text{I/Os}_{\text{phase 1}} = 2 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{N}{B} \right\rceil$$

(The factor of 2 accounts for one read and one write per element.)

#### **Phase 2: Multi-Way Merging**

The $R$ sorted runs are merged using a **multi-way merge** powered by a min-heap of size $M/B$ (so the heap fits in memory and one block per run is buffered).

**Number of merge passes** required:

$$P = \left\lceil \log_{M/B} R \right\rceil = \left\lceil \log_{M/B} \frac{N}{M} \right\rceil$$

**I/O cost of Phase 2:**

$$\text{I/Os}_{\text{phase 2}} = 2 \cdot P \cdot \frac{N}{B} = 2 \cdot \frac{N}{B} \cdot \left\lceil \log_{M/B} \frac{N}{M} \right\rceil$$

**Total I/O Cost of External Merge Sort:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{I/Os}_{\text{EMS}} \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{N}{B} \cdot \left(1 + \left\lceil \log_{M/B} \frac{N}{M} \right\rceil \right)\;}$$

This matches the proven lower bound, making External Merge Sort **asymptotically optimal** in the I/O model.

---

### 2.4 B-Trees — External Searching

Searching in external memory requires an index structure that minimizes I/Os. The dominant structure is the **B-Tree** of order $m$:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **B-Tree Properties (Order $m$):**
> - Every node holds between $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ and $m$ keys (except the root: between $1$ and $m$).
> - Internal nodes have between $\lceil m/2 \rceil + 1$ and $m+1$ children.
> - A node of degree $m$ has size $O(mB)$ — tuned so it matches a disk block.
> - All leaves are at the same depth → **balanced**.
> - Search, insert, and delete each cost $O(\log_m N)$ I/Os.

The **I/O complexity of B-Tree search** is:

$$\text{I/Os}_{\text{BTree}} = O\!\left(\log_{B} N\right) = O\!\left(\frac{\log N}{\log B}\right)$$

because the branching factor is $\Theta(B)$, leading to a tree of height $O(\log_B N)$.

---

### 2.5 Buffering and Distribution Sweeping

When the merge phase of external sort is bottlenecked by I/O latency (not bandwidth), a **parallel buffer** technique can hide latency by reading from many runs concurrently.

**Distribution Sweeping** solves problems like *external 1D and 2D range searching*:

- The data is partitioned into $M/B$ buckets using a *distribution* pass.
- Each bucket is then solved recursively in memory.
- The total cost is:

$$\text{I/Os}_{\text{dist-sweep}} = O\!\left(\frac{N}{B} \log_{M/B} N\right)$$

---

### 2.6 Cache-Oblivious Algorithms

> [!NOTE]
> **Cache-Oblivious Algorithms** are designed to be optimal *without knowing $M$ or $B$*. They exploit the memory hierarchy automatically via recursive divide-and-conquer and **tall-cache assumptions** ($M \ge B^2$).

Example: The cache-oblivious **funnel sort** matches the optimal I/O bound of external merge sort without any tuning parameters — a major KTU exam point.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the most exam-critical formulas for Module 4. **Memorize these — they are worth guaranteed marks.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Variables | Typical Marks |
|---|---------|---------------------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | I/O Model Cost | Minimize **block transfers** | $N, M, B$ | 2 |
| 2 | Constraint | $1 \le B \le M \le N$ | — | 2 |
| 3 | Number of runs in EMS | $R = \lceil N/M \rceil$ | $N, M$ | 3 |
| 4 | Phase-1 I/Os of EMS | $2 \lceil N/B \rceil$ | $N, B$ | 2 |
| 5 | Number of merge passes | $P = \lceil \log_{M/B} R \rceil$ | $M, B, R$ | 4 |
| 6 | **Total EMS I/Os** | $2 \cdot (N/B)(1 + P)$ | All | 7 |
| 7 | **Optimal sorting I/Os (lower bound)** | $\Omega\!\left(\frac{N}{B}\log_{M/B}\frac{N}{B}\right)$ | All | 5 |
| 8 | B-Tree height | $O(\log_B N)$ | $B, N$ | 4 |
| 9 | B-Tree search I/Os | $O(\log_B N)$ | $B, N$ | 3 |
| 10 | Distribution sweep I/Os | $O\!\left(\frac{N}{B}\log_{M/B} N\right)$ | $N, M, B$ | 3 |
| 11 | Funnel sort I/Os (cache-oblivious) | $O\!\left(\frac{N}{B}\log_{M/B}\frac{N}{B}\right)$ | All | 3 |
| 12 | Buffer size per stream in merge | $M/(M/B) = B$ blocks | $M, B$ | 2 |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas directly determine the **throughput of database engines (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB), Hadoop's Terasort benchmark, and Spark's shuffle phase**. When a company reports "sorting 100 TB in 1 minute," these I/O equations are the underlying analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — I/O Cost of External Merge Sort

**Problem:** Compute the total number of I/Os required to externally sort $N = 100{,}000$ records using a machine with $M = 1{,}000$ records of internal memory and disk block size $B = 100$ records per I/O.

#### **Step 1 — Identify model parameters**

$$N = 100{,}000, \quad M = 1{,}000, \quad B = 100$$

The fundamental I/O-model constraint holds: $1 \le B \le M \le N$. ✔

#### **Step 2 — Determine the number of sorted runs**

Each pass reads $M$ records, sorts them in RAM, and writes a sorted run. Thus:

$$R = \left\lceil \frac{N}{M} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{100{,}000}{1{,}000} \right\rceil = 100$$

**Interpretation:** Phase 1 produces **100 sorted runs**, each of length $M = 1{,}000$ records.

#### **Step 3 — Determine the maximum merge fan-out**

The merge phase uses a min-heap of size $M/B$ because we keep one buffer block of size $B$ per input stream:

$$\text{Merge arity} = k = \frac{M}{B} = \frac{1{,}000}{100} = 10$$

**Interpretation:** Each merge step can combine at most **10 runs** into a single longer run, while still keeping all 10 input buffers plus the output buffer in RAM.

#### **Step 4 — Determine the number of merge passes**

We start with $R = 100$ runs and reduce by a factor of $k = 10$ each pass:

$$P = \left\lceil \log_{k} R \right\rceil = \left\lceil \log_{10} 100 \right\rceil = 2 \text{ passes}$$

**Pass 1:** $100 \to 10$ runs.
**Pass 2:** $10 \to 1$ run (fully sorted output). ✔

#### **Step 5 — Compute I/Os for Phase 1 (Run formation)**

Every record is read once and written once:

$$\text{I/Os}_{\text{phase 1}} = 2 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{N}{B} \right\rceil = 2 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{100{,}000}{100} \right\rceil = 2 \cdot 1{,}000 = 2{,}000 \text{ I/Os}$$

#### **Step 6 — Compute I/Os for Phase 2 (Merging)**

Each merge pass reads all $N$ records once and writes them once (to a new run):

$$\text{I/Os}_{\text{phase 2}} = 2 \cdot P \cdot \left\lceil \frac{N}{B} \right\rceil = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 1{,}000 = 4{,}000 \text{ I/Os}$$

#### **Step 7 — Total I/O cost**

$$\text{I/Os}_{\text{total}} = \text{I/Os}_{\text{phase 1}} + \text{I/Os}_{\text{phase 2}} = 2{,}000 + 4{,}000 = 6{,}000 \text{ I/Os}$$

#### **Step 8 — Compare with the asymptotic formula**

Using the symbolic formula:

$$\text{I/Os} = 2 \cdot \frac{N}{B} \cdot \left(1 + \left\lceil \log_{M/B} \frac{N}{M} \right\rceil \right)$$

$$= 2 \cdot \frac{100{,}000}{100} \cdot \left(1 + \left\lceil \log_{10} \frac{100{,}000}{1{,}000} \right\rceil \right)$$

$$= 2 \cdot 1{,}000 \cdot (1 + \lceil \log_{10} 100 \rceil) = 2{,}000 \cdot (1 + 2) = 6{,}000 \;\; \checkmark$$

Both methods agree. ✔

#### **Step 9 — Sanity check against optimal lower bound**

Theoretical lower bound for external sorting:

$$\Omega\!\left(\frac{N}{B}\log_{M/B}\frac{N}{B}\right) = \Omega\!\left(\frac{100{,}000}{100}\log_{10} \frac{100{,}000}{100}\right) = \Omega(1{,}000 \cdot 3) = \Omega(3{,}000)$$

Our result of 6,000 I/Os is within a small constant factor of the optimum, confirming that **External Merge Sort is asymptotically optimal**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — B-Tree Search Cost

**Problem:** A database uses a B-Tree of order $m = 100$ (each node holds up to 100 keys) to index a table of $N = 10^{10}$ records. Each node corresponds to one disk block. Estimate the worst-case number of I/Os to search for a key.

#### **Step 1 — Effective branching factor**

A B-Tree node of order $m$ has between $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ and $m$ children. Worst-case branching factor (most branching) is:

$$b = m = 100$$

For an asymptotic bound we use the minimum branching factor $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ in the worst case, but typical estimates use the maximum (i.e., the average case). We'll use $b = 100$ for the typical case.

#### **Step 2 — Tree height**

For a B-Tree of height $h$ (root at height 1), the maximum number of leaves is $b^h$. To contain at least $N$ records, we need:

$$b^h \ge N \quad\Longrightarrow\quad h \ge \log_b N = \log_{100} 10^{10}$$

#### **Step 3 — Evaluate**

$$\log_{100} 10^{10} = \frac{\log 10^{10}}{\log 100} = \frac{10}{2} = 5$$

#### **Step 4 — I/O cost**

Searching the tree requires reading one node per level, so:

$$\text{I/Os}_{\text{search}} = h = O(\log_B N) = O(\log_{100} 10^{10}) \approx 5 \text{ I/Os}$$

#### **Step 5 — Interpretation**

> [!TIP]
> Even with **10 billion records**, the B-Tree needs only **5 disk reads** to find any record. This is why databases like MySQL InnoDB can answer queries on billion-row tables in milliseconds — the I/O bound is logarithmic in the dataset size.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favourite Question Type)

| Algorithm | Operation | RAM-Model Cost | I/O-Model Cost | Optimal? |
|-----------|-----------|----------------|----------------|----------|
| QuickSort (in-memory) | Sort | $O(N \log N)$ | N/A | Yes (in RAM) |
| QuickSort naïvely paged | Sort | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N \log N)$ I/Os (random access) | **No** |
| **External Merge Sort** | Sort | — | $O\!\left(\frac{N}{B}\log_{M/B}\frac{N}{B}\right)$ | **Yes** ✔ |
| Funnel Sort (cache-oblivious) | Sort | — | $O\!\left(\frac{N}{B}\log_{M/B}\frac{N}{B}\right)$ | **Yes** ✔ |
| Binary Search | Search | $O(\log N)$ | $O(\log N)$ I/Os (random) | **No** |
| **B-Tree Search** | Search | $O(\log N)$ | $O(\log_B N)$ I/Os | **Yes** ✔ |
| Hash Table | Search | $O(1)$ avg | $O(1)$ I/Os avg (with caching) | Heuristically |
| Linear Scan (sequential) | Search | $O(N)$ | $O(N/B)$ I/Os | Yes (sequential) |

> **Key Takeaway:** Random-access patterns incur $O(\log N)$ or worse I/Os; **B-Tree's $O(\log_B N)$ is the canonical optimal** because $B$ enters the logarithm base.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Memory Hierarchy — Block Transfer Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **flow of data blocks** through the memory hierarchy, which is the fundamental abstraction used to define I/O cost.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU[CPU Registers] -->|1 ns latency| L1[L1 Cache 32 KB]
    L1 --> L2[L2 Cache 256 KB]
    L2 --> L3[L3 Cache 8 MB]
    L3 -->|100 ns latency| RAM[Main Memory RAM 16 GB - size M]
    RAM -->|100 us latency| SSD[Solid State Drive SSD 1 TB]
    SSD -->|10 ms latency| HDD[Hard Disk Drive HDD 10 TB - external]
    HDD -->|network I/O| CLOUD[Cloud Object Storage Petabytes]

    classDef fast fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef medium fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef slow fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef external fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000

    class CPU,L1,L2,L3 fast
    class RAM medium
    class SSD,HDD slow
    class CLOUD external
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each downward arrow represents a **block transfer (I/O)**. The cost increases by orders of magnitude at each step, which is why EMA design focuses on **minimizing downward transfers** and **maximizing upward transfers in batched blocks of size $B$**.

---

### 4.2 External Merge Sort — Phase 1 and Phase 2 Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input[Input File on Disk size N]
        I1[Unsorted Records]
    end

    subgraph Phase1[Phase 1: Run Formation - in RAM chunks of size M]
        R1[Read chunk of M records]
        S1[In-memory sort O M log M]
        W1[Write sorted run to disk]
        R1 --> S1 --> W1
    end

    subgraph Runs[Initial Sorted Runs count R = ceil N over M]
        Run1[Run 1]
        Run2[Run 2]
        Run3[Run 3]
        RunD[Run R]
    end

    subgraph Phase2[Phase 2: Multi-way Merge using M over B buffers]
        BUF[Buffer pool of M over B blocks]
        HP[Min Heap of size M over B]
        MERGE[Multi-way merge: pick smallest head]
        OUT[Write merged output chunk of size B]
        BUF --> HP --> MERGE --> OUT
    end

    subgraph Final[Final Sorted Output on Disk]
        F1[Single sorted run of size N]
    end

    I1 --> R1
    W1 --> Run1
    W1 --> Run2
    W1 --> Run3
    W1 --> RunD
    Run1 --> BUF
    Run2 --> BUF
    Run3 --> BUF
    RunD --> BUF
    OUT --> F1

    classDef phase fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000
    class R1,S1,W1,BUF,HP,MERGE,OUT proc
    class I1,Run1,Run2,Run3,RunD,F1 data
    class Phase1,Phase2 phase
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The input is split into $R$ sorted runs in **Phase 1**. **Phase 2** uses a buffer pool of $M/B$ blocks to perform a $k$-way merge, progressively halving the number of runs until a single sorted output remains.

---

### 4.3 B-Tree Indexing Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[Root Node keys: 50 - max 100 - block on disk]
    N1[Internal Node keys 10 to 30 - block 1]
    N2[Internal Node keys 60 to 80 - block 2]
    N3[Internal Node keys 90 to 120 - block 3]
    L1[Leaf block 1 keys 0 to 9]
    L2[Leaf block 2 keys 10 to 19]
    L3[Leaf block 3 keys 30 to 49]
    L4[Leaf block 4 keys 50 to 59]
    L5[Leaf block 5 keys 60 to 79]
    L6[Leaf block 6 keys 80 to 89]
    L7[Leaf block 7 keys 90 to 99]
    L8[Leaf block 8 keys 100 to 120]

    ROOT --> N1
    ROOT --> N2
    ROOT --> N3
    N1 --> L1
    N1 --> L2
    N1 --> L3
    N2 --> L4
    N2 --> L5
    N2 --> L6
    N3 --> L7
    N3 --> L8

    classDef root fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef internal fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    class ROOT root
    class N1,N2,N3 internal
    class L1,L2,L3,L4,L5,L6,L7,L8 leaf
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each level is one **disk I/O**. To find any key, the search descends at most $h$ levels, where $h = O(\log_B N)$. With $B = 100$ and $N = 10^{10}$, we have $h = 5$ I/Os — a tremendous saving over linear scan's $10^{10}/B = 10^8$ I/Os.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. Define the Aggarwal–Vitter I/O model. List its three parameters and state the constraint among them.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

The **Aggarwal–Vitter I/O model** is a computational model used to analyze algorithms that handle data too large to fit in main memory. It abstracts away CPU details and counts the **number of block transfers (I/Os)** between internal memory (RAM) and external storage (disk) as the primary cost metric.

The three parameters are:
- $N$ = total number of data elements
- $M$ = number of elements that fit in internal memory
- $B$ = number of elements per I/O block (block size)

**Constraint:** $\;1 \le B \le M \le N$ — you cannot transfer a block larger than memory, and memory cannot be larger than the dataset.

---

**Q2. Distinguish between a cache hit and a cache miss in the I/O model. Why is sequential access preferred over random access?** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

- A **cache hit** occurs when the block containing the requested data is already present in the internal memory buffer; no I/O is needed.
- A **cache miss** (also called an **I/O fault** or **page fault**) occurs when the block is not in memory; a new I/O is required to fetch it from external storage.

Sequential access is preferred because it exhibits **spatial locality** — accessing the next element in a block is essentially free after the first I/O. Random access (e.g., QuickSort's partitioning) has poor locality, since each access has a high probability of triggering a fresh I/O. The performance gap can be **3–5 orders of magnitude** in practice.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): External Merge Sort**

**(a) Describe the External Merge Sort algorithm. State the two phases and explain the role of the buffer pool and the min-heap.** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

External Merge Sort is an I/O-efficient sorting algorithm for data too large to fit in RAM. It proceeds in **two phases**:

**Phase 1 — Run Formation (In-Memory Sort):**
- The input file of $N$ records is read in chunks of $M$ records each (which fit entirely in RAM).
- Each chunk is sorted in-memory using a standard $O(M \log M)$ sort.
- The sorted chunk is written back to disk as a *sorted run*.
- Total runs produced: $R = \lceil N/M \rceil$.
- I/O cost of this phase: $2 \lceil N/B \rceil$ I/Os (one read + one write per record).

**Phase 2 — Multi-Way Merging:**
- The $R$ runs are merged into a single sorted output using a $k$-way merge, where $k = M/B$.
- A **buffer pool** allocates one block (size $B$) per input run, totaling $k \cdot B = M$ memory for input buffers, plus output buffer space.
- A **min-heap** of size $k$ holds the current head element of each run. To find the next smallest element (in $O(\log k)$ CPU time), the algorithm extracts the heap minimum, then refills that run's buffer block with the next $B$ elements from disk.
- The merged output is written in blocks of size $B$.
- $P = \lceil \log_k R \rceil$ merge passes are required.

**Total I/O cost:**
$$\text{I/Os} = 2 \cdot \frac{N}{B} \cdot (1 + P) = 2 \cdot \frac{N}{B} \cdot \left(1 + \left\lceil \log_{M/B} \frac{N}{M} \right\rceil \right)$$

The min-heap ensures that selecting the next output element is $O(\log M/B)$ — much smaller than the $O(\log R)$ of binary merging, justifying multi-way merge.

---

**(b) For $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ records, $M = 10{,}000$ records, and $B = 1{,}000$ records, compute the total number of I/Os required by External Merge Sort. Compare it with the lower bound.** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Number of runs:**
$$R = \left\lceil \frac{N}{M} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000} \right\rceil = 100$$

**Step 2 — Merge arity:**
$$k = \frac{M}{B} = \frac{10{,}000}{1{,}000} = 10$$

**Step 3 — Number of merge passes:**
$$P = \left\lceil \log_{k} R \right\rceil = \left\lceil \log_{10} 100 \right\rceil = 2$$

**Step 4 — I/Os in Phase 1 (run formation):**
$$\text{I/Os}_{\text{phase 1}} = 2 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{N}{B} \right\rceil = 2 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000} \right\rceil = 2 \cdot 1{,}000 = 2{,}000$$

**Step 5 — I/Os in Phase 2 (merging):**
$$\text{I/Os}_{\text{phase 2}} = 2 \cdot P \cdot \left\lceil \frac{N}{B} \right\rceil = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 1{,}000 = 4{,}000$$

**Step 6 — Total I/Os:**
$$\text{I/Os}_{\text{total}} = 2{,}000 + 4{,}000 = \boxed{6{,}000 \text{ I/Os}}$$

**Step 7 — Optimal lower bound:**
$$\Omega\!\left(\frac{N}{B}\log_{M/B}\frac{N}{B}\right) = \Omega\!\left(\frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000} \cdot \log_{10}\frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000}\right) = \Omega(1{,}000 \cdot 3) = \Omega(3{,}000)$$

**Comparison:** External Merge Sort achieves 6,000 I/Os, which is within a constant factor of the optimal lower bound 3,000. Hence, **External Merge Sort is asymptotically I/O-optimal**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): B-Trees and I/O Optimality**

**(a) Define a B-Tree of order $m$. State its key properties. How does the choice of $m$ relate to the disk block size $B$?** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

A **B-Tree of order $m$** is a self-balancing search tree designed for storage systems with slow external memory (e.g., disks). It is the dominant indexing structure in database systems.

**Key Properties:**

1. Every node contains at most $m$ keys and at most $m+1$ children.
2. Every internal node (except possibly the root) has at least $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ children.
3. The root has at least 2 children unless it is a leaf.
4. A node with $k$ keys has exactly $k+1$ children.
5. All leaves appear at the same depth — the tree is **perfectly balanced**.
6. Keys within a node are stored in **sorted order** to enable binary search.

**Relation to Block Size $B$:**

The order $m$ is chosen so that a single B-Tree node fits in **exactly one disk block of size $B$**. If each key-pointer pair occupies $K$ bytes and the disk block is $B$ bytes, then:

$$m \approx \frac{B}{K}$$

This choice ensures that **each node access requires exactly one I/O**, minimizing the I/O cost of any tree operation to be proportional to the tree height.

---

**(b) A database table has $N = 10^{12}$ rows. The B-Tree used for indexing has order $m = 200$, and each node fits in one disk block. Calculate the worst-case number of I/Os required for a search. Justify why this is I/O-optimal.** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Branching factor:**

The maximum (and typical) number of children per node is $b = m = 200$.

**Step 2 — Tree height:**

A B-Tree of height $h$ can have up to $b^h$ leaves. To accommodate $N = 10^{12}$ records, we need:

$$b^h \ge N \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 200^h \ge 10^{12}$$

Solving:
$$h = \left\lceil \log_{200} 10^{12} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{\log 10^{12}}{\log 200} \right\rceil$$

$$= \left\lceil \frac{12}{2.301} \right\rceil \approx \left\lceil 5.214 \right\rceil = 6$$

**Step 3 — Search I/Os:**

A search descends from root to leaf, accessing one node per level:
$$\text{I/Os}_{\text{search}} = h = 6 \text{ I/Os}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with linear scan:**

A linear scan would require:
$$\text{I/Os}_{\text{linear}} = \frac{N}{B} \approx \frac{10^{12}}{B} \gg 6$$

(For $B = 200$, that's $5 \times 10^9$ I/Os — 8 orders of magnitude worse!)

**Step 5 — I/O optimality justification:**

Any comparison-based search in external memory must distinguish among $N$ possibilities, and each I/O yields at most $B$ keys (and thus at most $B+1$ subtrees). This gives a lower bound of $\Omega(\log_B N)$ I/Os. The B-Tree matches this bound:
$$\text{I/Os}_{\text{search}} = O(\log_B N) = O(\log_{200} 10^{12}) \approx 6$$

Hence, the B-Tree is **I/O-optimal** for comparison-based external search.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 4)**
> 1. **Confusing the two $\log$ bases:** In the EMS cost formula, the base of the logarithm is $M/B$ (the merge arity), **not 2 or $B$**. Writing $\log N$ instead of $\log_{M/B} N$ will cost **2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in I/O counts:** Every I/O cost involving "reading and writing" has a multiplier of 2. Omitting it loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Ignoring the constraint $1 \le B \le M \le N$:** If a numerical problem gives $B > M$, the parameters are inconsistent — students often plug in blindly. Always verify the constraint first.
> 4. **Confusing cache-oblivious with cache-aware:** Cache-aware algorithms know $M$ and $B$ at design time (e.g., External Merge Sort). Cache-oblivious algorithms (e.g., Funnel Sort) **do not** — they achieve optimal I/O across all levels of the hierarchy simultaneously.
> 5. **B-Tree fan-out error:** The number of children is $b = m$ (max) or $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ (min). For asymptotic search cost, use $b = m$, giving $h = O(\log_m N)$.
> 6. **Not specifying "worst case" vs "amortized":** Insertions and deletions in B-Trees may require $O(\log_B N)$ *amortized* I/Os, but split/merge operations can spike. Be explicit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4**

- ✅ **I/O Model parameters** = $\{N, M, B\}$ with $1 \le B \le M \le N$. **Cost = block transfers.**
- ✅ **I/O cost dominates** CPU cost on big data — latencies range from 100 ns (RAM) to 10 ms (HDD).
- ✅ **Spatial locality** is the design principle; sequential block access is essentially free after the first I/O.
- ✅ **External Merge Sort** has two phases: (1) run formation — $2\lceil N/B \rceil$ I/Os, (2) multi-way merge — $2P \cdot \lceil N/B \rceil$ I/Os, where $P = \lceil \log_{M/B} (N/M) \rceil$.
- ✅ **Total EMS I/Os** $= 2 \cdot \frac{N}{B} \cdot (1 + \lceil \log_{M/B} \frac{N}{M} \rceil)$ — **asymptotically optimal**.
- ✅ **External sorting lower bound** = $\Omega\!\left(\frac{N}{B}\log_{M/B}\frac{N}{B}\right)$ — proved by counting the height of the decision tree restricted to I/Os.
- ✅ **Multi-way merge arity** = $M/B$. The min-heap has size $M/B$; the buffer pool has $M/B$ input blocks plus 1 output block.
- ✅ **B-Tree of order $m$** has branching factor $m$ and height $O(\log_m N)$. **Tune $m$ so that one node = one disk block.**
- ✅ **B-Tree search I/Os** = $O(\log_B N)$ — matches the lower bound, hence **I/O-optimal**.
- ✅ **B-Tree guarantees:** all leaves at same depth, between $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ and $m$ keys per node, root has 2+ children.
- ✅ **Distribution sweeping** achieves $O\!\left(\frac{N}{B}\log_{M/B} N\right)$ I/Os for geometric problems (range searching, line-segment intersection).
- ✅ **Cache-oblivious algorithms** (e.g., Funnel Sort) achieve the same optimal I/O bound *without knowing $M$ or $B$*, by using recursive divide-and-conquer and a **tall-cache assumption** ($M \ge B^2$).
- ✅ **Buffering** (parallel / double / read-ahead) hides I/O latency by overlapping I/O with CPU work.
- ✅ **Real-world systems** built on these ideas: **B-Tree** → InnoDB, PostgreSQL; **External Merge Sort** → Hadoop Terasort, Spark shuffle; **Cache-oblivious** → modern matrix-multiplication libraries; **Distribution sweep** → spatial database query engines.
- ✅ **Engineering heuristic:** if your algorithm makes a random I/O inside a loop, redesign it — even a single random I/O per iteration dominates CPU cost.

> **One-Line Mantra for KTU Exam:**
> *"Minimize the number of block transfers, not the number of CPU operations. Think in $\log_{M/B}$, not $\log_2$."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project 4: Scalable Data Processing with Spark - Implement scalable algorithms using Apache Spark to process large datasets efficiently using datasets like Kaggle - Google Analytics Customer Revenue Prediction. Tasks: Set up a Spark environment for large-scale data processing. Implement scalable algorithms for sorting, searching, and filtering the dataset. Analyze the performance of your algorithms on different dataset sizes and optimize for scalability.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST785 - ALGORITHMS FOR DATA SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Project 4: Scalable Data Processing with Spark - Implement scalable algorithms using Apache Spark to process large datasets efficiently using datasets like Kaggle - Google Analytics Customer Revenue Prediction. Tasks: Set up a Spark environment for large-scale data processing. Implement scalable algorithms for sorting, searching, and filtering the dataset. Analyze the performance of your algorithms on different dataset sizes and optimize for scalability. # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Apache Spark** is an *open-source, distributed, in-memory cluster-computing framework* designed for large-scale data processing. It provides high-level APIs in Java, Scala, Python (PySpark), and R, along with an optimized engine that supports general execution graphs (DAGs) for data processing. For the **Kaggle – Google Analytics Customer Revenue Prediction (GA-CRP)** dataset, the objective is to: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Project Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > *"Design and implement scalable algorithms using Apache Spark RDDs, DataFrames, and Spark SQL to perform sorting, searching, and filtering operations on the GA-CRP dataset, followed by rigorous performance benchmarking across varying dataset sizes to evaluate horizontal and vertical scalability."* ### Key Terminology for KTU Valuation | Term | Formal Definition | |------|-------------------| | **RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset)** | An immutable, distributed collection of objects that can be processed in parallel across a Spark cluster. | | **DataFrame** | A distributed collection of data organized into named columns (tabular structure with schema). | | **Driver Program** | The process that runs the `main()` function of the Spark application and creates the `SparkContext`. | | **Executor** | A distributed agent responsible for executing tasks on worker nodes. | | **Partition** | A logical chunk of a distributed dataset that resides on one executor. | | **Lazy Evaluation** | Transformations are not executed until an *action* is triggered. | | **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)** | The execution plan Spark builds to optimize the lineage of operations. | | **Shuffle** | The redistribution of data across partitions/partitions of executors. | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **🍕 Pizza Shop Analogy for Spark:** > Imagine a single chef (a single computer) trying to make **10,000 pizzas**. It will take hours. Now imagine hiring **100 chefs** and giving each chef a specific set of instructions and a portion of the orders — they cook **in parallel** and combine the finished pizzas at the end. That is exactly what **Spark** does with your data. > - The **chef manager** = **Driver Program** > - The **kitchen stations** = **Executors** > - The **pizza order batches** = **Partitions** > - The **cooking instructions** = **Transformations** > - The **"serve the customer"** step = **Action** For the **GA-CRP dataset**, each row is a "pizza order" (a user session on the Google Merchandise Store), and Spark lets us process millions of such sessions across multiple machines in parallel. ## 1.3 What is the GA-CRP Dataset? The **Google Analytics Customer Revenue Prediction** dataset (publicly available on Kaggle) contains: - **Training set:** ~903,653 rows - **Test set:** ~804,684 rows - **Features:** ~55 columns including traffic source, device type, geographical data, page views, hits, and the **target column `transactionRevenue`** (the natural log of revenue per customer session). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this dataset is relevant for KTU Project-4:** > It is **large**, **messy** (contains JSON fields like `device`, `geoNetwork`, `totals`, `trafficSource`), and **realistic** — perfect for demonstrating Spark's strengths in **JSON parsing**, **wide transformations**, and **performance benchmarking**. ## 1.4 Spark Execution Architecture — At a Glance Spark follows a **master–worker (driver–executor) architecture**: 1. The **Driver** launches the `SparkSession`. 2. The **Cluster Manager** (Standalone, YARN, or Mesos) allocates executors. 3. The **Driver** converts the user program into a **DAG of stages and tasks**. 4. **Executors** run tasks in parallel and return results to the driver. ## 1.5 The Three Tasks of Project-4 (KTU Mandatory) | Task | Spark Construct Used | Goal | |------|---------------------|------| | **T1: Environment Setup** | `SparkSession`, `pyspark` | Install Spark, configure cluster, load GA-CRP CSV/JSON. | | **T2: Scalable Algorithms** | RDD, DataFrame, Spark SQL | Implement sort, search, filter using `sort()`, `filter()`, `groupBy()`. | | **T3: Performance & Scalability Analysis** | `time()`, `Spark UI`, `repartition()` | Benchmark runtime vs. partitions/executors; tune `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`. | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Spark ### 2.1.1 The Spark Execution Model Spark's execution follows the principle: > **Transformations are lazy; Actions are eager.** The lineage (DAG) of transformations is materialized only when an **action** (e.g., `count()`, `collect()`, `show()`, `write`) is invoked. The DAG Scheduler then converts this lineage into **Stages** separated by **shuffle boundaries**, and each stage is split into **Tasks** (one per partition). ### 2.1.2 Narrow vs. Wide Transformations - **Narrow Transformations:** Parent RDD partitions map to exactly one child partition. *No shuffle required.* Examples: `map()`, `filter()`, `union()`. - **Wide Transformations:** Data from multiple parent partitions may be required for a single child partition. *Shuffle required.* Examples: `groupByKey()`, `reduceByKey()`, `join()`, `sort()`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wide transformations are expensive.** They involve **disk I/O**, **network I/O**, and **serialization** — the primary cost in scalable processing. ### 2.1.3 Lineage and Fault Tolerance Instead of replicating data (as Hadoop does), Spark recomputes lost partitions using the **lineage graph** of transformations. This makes it **RDDs Resilient**. ## 2.2 Deep Dive: Sorting in Spark ### 2.2.1 `sortBy()` on RDDs Signature: ```python rdd.sortBy(keyfunc, ascending=True, numPartitions=None) ``` Internally, Spark uses **Range Partitioner** to distribute sorted data evenly across partitions, then performs a **Timsort** within each partition, then a final merge. ### 2.2.2 `orderBy()` on DataFrames Translates to a `sort` operation at the SQL level. By default, it issues a **global sort** (all data on a single partition → causes shuffle). ```python df.orderBy("transactionRevenue", ascending=False) ``` ### 2.2.3 Cost of Sorting Sorting requires **shuffling all data**, so its complexity is bounded by: $$ T_{\text{sort}} = O\!\left(N \log N + N \cdot S_{\text{shuffle}}\right) $$ where $N$ is the number of records and $S_{\text{shuffle}}$ is the shuffle cost. ## 2.3 Deep Dive: Searching in Spark Searching in Spark is implemented via: - **`filter()` (Transformation)** — keeps records matching a predicate. - **`join()`** — lookup-style searching across two datasets. - **`isIn()` / `isin()`** — membership test. For binary search on sorted data, Spark offers **`zipWithIndex()`** + **partition-based lookup** in RDDs, or **Z-order / Bucket pruning** in Delta Lake (advanced). ## 2.4 Deep Dive: Filtering in Spark Filtering is a **narrow transformation**, hence cheap: $$ T_{\text{filter}} = O(N) \quad \text{(local scan per partition)} $$ The selectivity $s = \frac{|R_{\text{out}}|}{|R_{\text{in}}|}$ directly impacts the *effective data volume* for downstream operations. ## 2.5 Performance Metrics for Scalability ### 2.5.1 Speedup $$ S(p) = \frac{T(1)}{T(p)} $$ where $T(1)$ is the runtime on 1 core/node and $T(p)$ is the runtime on $p$ cores/nodes. ### 2.5.2 Efficiency $$ E(p) = \frac{S(p)}{p} = \frac{T(1)}{p \cdot T(p)} $$ ### 2.5.3 Scalability (Weak Scaling) A system is *weakly scalable* if: $$ T(p) \approx T(1) \quad \text{when dataset size grows linearly with } p $$ ### 2.5.4 Amdahl's Law (Upper Bound on Speedup) $$ S_{\text{max}}(p) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{p}} $$ where $f$ is the parallelizable fraction. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes | |---|---------|----------------------|----------------| | 1 | Speedup | $S(p) = T(1) / T(p)$ | dimensionless | | 2 | Efficiency | $E(p) = S(p) / p$ | $\in [0, 1]$ | | 3 | Amdahl's Law | $S_{\max}(p) = 1 / [(1-f) + f/p]$ | $f$ = parallel fraction | | 4 | Weak Scaling | $T(p) \approx T(1)$ as $N \propto p$ | ideal = constant | | 5 | Strong Scaling | $T(p)$ decreases as $p \uparrow$ for fixed $N$ | ideal = $1/p$ | | 6 | Sort Complexity | $O(N \log N + N \cdot S_{\text{shuffle}})$ | Big-O | | 7 | Filter Complexity | $O(N)$ | per partition | | 8 | Shuffle Cost | $C_{\text{shuffle}} = \sum_{i} \text{bytes}(P_i) \cdot \text{hops}$ | network I/O | | 9 | Partition Count | $P = \text{ceil}(\text{cluster\_cores} / 2)$ | rule of thumb | | 10 | Block Size (HDFS) | Default = 128 MB | tunable | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas underpin the design of every modern data pipeline at companies like **Netflix, Uber, and Airbnb**, where Spark jobs process **petabytes** daily. The same Speedup and Amdahl analyses are used to justify cluster-size decisions in cloud billing. ## 2.7 Spark Configuration Parameters (KTU High-Yield) | Parameter | Default | Recommended for GA-CRP | |-----------|---------|-------------------------| | `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` | 200 | 100–400 depending on cluster | | `spark.executor.memory` | 1g | 4g–8g | | `spark.driver.maxResultSize` | 1g | 2g–4g | | `spark.sql.adaptive.enabled` | false | **true** (for AQE) | | `spark.sql.broadcastTimeout` | 300s | 600s | | `spark.serializer` | Java | ** KryoSerializer** | --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Task 1 — Setting Up the Spark Environment (Exhaustive Walkthrough) ### Step 1: Install PySpark ```bash pip install pyspark==3.5.1 findspark ``` ### Step 2: Initialize SparkSession ```python import findspark findspark.init() from pyspark.sql import SparkSession spark = (SparkSession.builder .appName("GA-CRP-Scalable-Processing") .master("local[*]") # use all cores locally .config("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "50") .config("spark.executor.memory", "4g") .getOrCreate()) sc = spark.sparkContext print(f"Spark version: {spark.version}") print(f"Parallelism: {sc.defaultParallelism}") ``` ### Step 3: Load the GA-CRP Dataset ```python # Kaggle's train.csv uses flat columns; nested fields are JSON strings. df_raw = (spark.read .option("header", True) .option("inferSchema", True) .option("escape", "\"") .csv("train.csv")) print(f"Rows: {df_raw.count()}, Cols: {len(df_raw.columns)}") df_raw.printSchema() ``` ### Step 4: Parse Nested JSON Columns The GA-CRP dataset contains JSON-string columns: `device`, `geoNetwork`, `totals`, `trafficSource`. ```python from pyspark.sql.functions import from_json, col from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StringType, MapType json_schema = MapType(StringType(), StringType()) for c in ["device", "geoNetwork", "totals", "trafficSource"]: df_raw = df_raw.withColumn(c, from_json(col(c), json_schema)) ``` > [!NOTE] > Each `from_json` call is a **wide transformation** because Spark must compute it before downstream ops can resolve the nested fields. ## 3.2 Task 2 — Scalable Algorithms ### 3.2.1 Filter (Narrow Transformation) **Goal:** Keep only sessions with a non-zero `transactionRevenue`. ```python from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum as _sum, when # totals.transactionRevenue is nested after JSON parse df_filtered = (df_raw .filter(col("totals.transactionRevenue").isNotNull() & (col("totals.transactionRevenue") > 0.0))) print(f"Non-zero revenue rows: {df_filtered.count()}") ``` **Complexity Analysis:** $$ T_{\text{filter}} = O(N), \quad \text{no shuffle required} $$ ### 3.2.2 Sort (Wide Transformation) **Goal:** Top 20 highest-revenue sessions. ```python from pyspark.sql.functions import desc, cast df_sorted = (df_filtered .orderBy(col("totals.transactionRevenue").cast("double").desc()) .limit(20)) df_sorted.show(truncate=False) ``` **Step-by-Step Logical Plan Spark Builds:** 1. **Parse** JSON column `totals`. 2. **Filter** rows with revenue. 3. **Project** only required columns (predicate pushdown). 4. **Exchange** (shuffle) — `RangePartitioning` for global order. 5. **Sort** within each partition (Timsort). 6. **Limit(20)** — collects from all partitions and merges. **Complexity:** $$ T_{\text{sort}} = O(N \log N) + \text{shuffle cost} $$ ### 3.2.3 Search / Lookup via `join` **Goal:** Find all sessions from users in a list of high-value countries. ```python high_value_countries = ["United States", "United Kingdom", "Canada", "Japan"] # Strategy 1: filter (linear scan) df_us = df_raw.filter(col("geoNetwork.country").isin(high_value_countries)) # Strategy 2: broadcast join (more efficient for large filter lists) from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast, lit countries_df = spark.createDataFrame( [(c,) for c in high_value_countries], ["country"] ) df_joined = (df_raw .join(broadcast(countries_df), col("geoNetwork.country") == countries_df.country, "inner") .select(df_raw["*"])) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Broadcast join** sends the small DataFrame to every executor, avoiding the shuffle of the large table. This is the canonical Spark optimization for **star-schema-style searches**. ## 3.3 Task 3 — Performance Analysis & Scalability Optimization ### 3.3.1 Benchmarking Across Dataset Sizes ```python import time, random def benchmark(df, operation_name, op_func): start = time.time() result = op_func(df) count = result.count() if hasattr(result, "count") else len(result) end = time.time() print(f"{operation_name:20s} | rows={count:>10,d} | time={end-start:.3f}s") return end - start # Sub-sample 10%, 50%, 100% of the dataset for frac in [0.1, 0.5, 1.0]: sample = df_raw.sample(withReplacement=False, fraction=frac, seed=42) print(f"\n--- Sample fraction = {frac} ---") benchmark(sample, "Filter", lambda d: d.filter(col("totals.transactionRevenue") > 0)) benchmark(sample, "Sort", lambda d: d.orderBy("totals.transactionRevenue")) ``` ### 3.3.2 Impact of `repartition()` ```python # Default partitions print(f"Default partitions: {df_raw.rdd.getNumPartitions()}") # Repartition to 50 df_50 = df_raw.repartition(50) benchmark(df_50, "Filter@50", lambda d: d.filter(col("totals.transactionRevenue") > 0)) # Repartition to 200 df_200 = df_raw.repartition(200) benchmark(df_200, "Filter@200", lambda d: d.filter(col("totals.transactionRevenue") > 0)) ``` **Empirical Findings (typical):** $$ T_{\text{filter}}(P) \approx \frac{N}{P} \cdot t_{\text{per-record}} + C_{\text{overhead}} $$ - Too few partitions → under-utilized cluster. - Too many partitions → scheduler overhead dominates. ### 3.3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cost vs. Operation Type | Operation | Type | Shuffle? | Complexity | Spark Cost Driver | |-----------|------|----------|------------|-------------------| | `filter` | Narrow | No | $O(N)$ | CPU scan | | `map` | Narrow | No | $O(N)$ | CPU + serialization | | `orderBy` | Wide | Yes | $O(N \log N)$ | Shuffle + sort | | `groupBy` | Wide | Yes | $O(N \log P)$ | Hash aggregate + shuffle | | `join` (default) | Wide | Yes | $O(N + M)$ | Shuffle of both sides | | `join` (broadcast) | Narrow | No | $O(N)$ | Driver → executor broadcast | | `count` | Action | No (if no preceding shuffle) | $O(N)$ | Aggregation | | `collect` | Action | No | $O(N)$ | **Pulls all data to driver** ⚠️ | | `distinct` | Wide | Yes | $O(N \log N)$ | Shuffle + dedup | | `coalesce` | Narrow | No | $O(P)$ | Reduces partitions | ### 3.3.4 Derivation of Speedup (Amdahl's Law) Consider a Spark job with: - Parallelizable fraction $f = 0.95$ (e.g., filter + map) - Sequential fraction $(1-f) = 0.05$ (e.g., final `collect`) For $p = 8$ executors: $$ S_{\max}(8) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \frac{0.95}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.11875} = \frac{1}{0.16875} \approx 5.93 $$ This means **8 executors can give at most ~5.9× speedup** — a critical bound for the KTU project report. ### 3.3.5 Optimization Heuristics (KTU Board Favourite) 1. **Cache / Persist** intermediate DataFrames used multiple times. 2. **Broadcast** small lookup tables (≤ 10 MB). 3. **Partition by** high-cardinality join keys. 4. **Avoid `collect()`** on huge datasets; use `take(n)` or `toPandas().head()`. 5. **Enable Adaptive Query Execution (AQE)** for dynamic coalescing. 6. **Use columnar formats** (Parquet, ORC) over CSV. ```python df_raw.cache() # in-memory caching df_raw.persist() # explicit storage level df_raw.unpersist() # release ``` --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Spark Cluster Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CLIENT["CLIENT MACHINE"] APP["Spark Application

With a neat diagram, explain the cluster architecture of Spark mentioning the role of Driver, Cluster Manager, and Executors.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Apache Spark is an **open-source, distributed, in-memory cluster computing framework** used for large-scale data processing. Its cluster architecture consists of: 1. **Driver Program** — Runs the `main()` function, holds the `SparkContext`, builds the DAG, and coordinates executors. 2. **Cluster Manager** — Allocates resources (Standalone, YARN, Mesos, or Kubernetes). 3. **Executors** — Worker processes that execute tasks and store data in memory or disk. *Diagram: See Section 4.1 — High-Level Spark Cluster Architecture.* **Mark split:** Definition 1m, Driver 1m, Executors/Manager 1m. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]` **Differentiate between narrow and wide transformations in Spark. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Narrow Transformation | Wide Transformation | |--------|------------------------|---------------------| | Shuffle | Not required | Required | | Partition mapping | 1 parent → 1 child | Many parents → 1 child | | Example | `filter`, `map` | `groupByKey`, `orderBy` | | Cost | Low | High (disk + network) | **Mark split:** Definition 1m + example 1m + cost 1m. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **Q-A. `[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]` (a)** Explain the Spark execution model in detail, covering RDD lineage, lazy evaluation, DAG construction, and the role of the DAG Scheduler and Task Scheduler. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider the Google Analytics Customer Revenue Prediction (GA-CRP) dataset with $N = 9 \times 10^5$ rows. Suppose a Spark job has a parallelizable fraction $f = 0.92$ and the sequential overhead is due to the final `collect()` action. Using Amdahl's Law, compute the maximum speedup achievable with **(i)** 4 executors, **(ii)** 8 executors, and **(iii)** 16 executors. Comment on the scalability. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks:** The Spark execution model is built on the concept of **RDD lineage** and **lazy evaluation**: 1. **RDD Creation** — Data is loaded into an RDD/DataFrame (lazy, not materialized). 2. **Transformations** — `map`, `filter`, `groupBy` are recorded as a logical plan (DAG). 3. **Lazy Evaluation** — Spark does not compute the lineage until an *action* (`count`, `collect`, `write`) is called. 4. **DAG Construction** — When an action is called, the **DAG Scheduler** splits the DAG into **stages** at shuffle boundaries. 5. **Task Scheduling** — The **Task Scheduler** assigns each task (one per partition per stage) to executors. 6. **Execution** — Executors run tasks in parallel, shuffle data across stages, and return results. **Fault Tolerance:** Lost partitions are recomputed using lineage, not replicated. **Mark split:** RDD lineage 2m, lazy eval 2m, DAG 2m, fault tolerance 1m. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** Amdahl's Law: $$ S_{\max}(p) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{p}} $$ Given $f = 0.92$, $(1 - f) = 0.08$. **(i) For $p = 4$:** $$ S_{\max}(4) = \frac{1}{0.08 + \frac{0.92}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.08 + 0.23} = \frac{1}{0.31} \approx 3.23 $$ **(ii) For $p = 8$:** $$ S_{\max}(8) = \frac{1}{0.08 + \frac{0.92}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.08 + 0.115} = \frac{1}{0.195} \approx 5.13 $$ **(iii) For $p = 16$:** $$ S_{\max}(16) = \frac{1}{0.08 + \frac{0.92}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.08 + 0.0575} = \frac{1}{0.1375} \approx 7.27 $$ **Comment on Scalability:** As $p$ doubles from 4 → 8, speedup rises from 3.23 → 5.13 (gain ≈ 1.59×). Doubling again to 16 only adds ~2.14×. This is **sublinear scaling** caused by the **8% sequential overhead** (the `collect()` and driver-side aggregation). The asymptotic limit as $p \to \infty$ is: $$ \lim_{p \to \infty} S_{\max}(p) = \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5 $$ Therefore, beyond 16 executors, the marginal benefit diminishes rapidly. **Recommendation:** use 8–16 executors for the GA-CRP workload and minimize driver-side `collect()`. **Mark split:** Formula 1m, each sub-part 1m, comment 3m. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q-B. `[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]` (a)** Discuss the architecture of the Google Analytics Customer Revenue Prediction (GA-CRP) dataset. Identify at least three challenges in processing it using traditional single-node tools and explain how Spark overcomes each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write PySpark code to perform the following on the GA-CRP dataset: 1. Load `train.csv` and parse the nested JSON column `totals` to extract `transactionRevenue`. 2. Filter sessions with `transactionRevenue > 0`. 3. Find the **top 10 countries** by total revenue. 4. Benchmark the filter operation's runtime on samples of 10%, 50%, and 100% of the dataset and report observations on scalability. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **GA-CRP Architecture:** - **~903 K rows × 55 columns** in training set. - Nested JSON columns: `device`, `geoNetwork`, `totals`, `trafficSource`. - Target: `transactionRevenue` (log of revenue per session). - Mix of categorical, numerical, and JSON-string fields. **Challenges & Spark Solutions:** | # | Challenge | Spark Solution | |---|-----------|----------------| | 1 | **Scale** — single-node tools (Pandas) cannot hold the entire dataset in memory (~2 GB+ after JSON expansion). | Spark's **distributed in-memory RDDs/DataFrames** spread data across cluster nodes. | | 2 | **Nested JSON parsing** — requires custom code in Pandas; very slow. | Spark's built-in `from_json()` with schema and **Catalyst optimizer** speed up parsing via vectorization. | | 3 | **Wide transformations** (groupBy, sort) are OOM-prone on single machines. | Spark's **shuffle mechanism** + **partitioning** distributes work, and `persist()` reuses intermediate results. | | 4 | **No fault tolerance** in Pandas. | Spark's **lineage-based recomputation** recovers lost partitions. | **Mark split:** Architecture 2m, three challenges × 1.5m, Spark solutions 1.5m. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** ```python from pyspark.sql import SparkSession from pyspark.sql.functions import col, from_json, sum as _sum, desc from pyspark.sql.types import MapType, StringType, DoubleType import time spark = (SparkSession.builder .appName("GA-CRP-Project-4") .master("local[*]") .getOrCreate()) # 1. Load + parse df = spark.read.option("header", True).option("inferSchema", True).csv("train.csv") json_schema = MapType(StringType(), StringType()) df = df.withColumn("totals_parsed", from_json(col("totals"), json_schema)) # Extract transactionRevenue as double df = df.withColumn("revenue", col("tot_parsed.transactionRevenue").cast(DoubleType())) # 2. Filter non-zero revenue df_rev = df.filter(col("revenue") > 0) # 3. Top 10 countries by total revenue top10 = (df_rev .groupBy("geoNetwork.country") .agg(_sum("revenue").alias("total_revenue")) .orderBy(desc("total_revenue")) .limit(10)) top10.show(truncate=False) # 4. Benchmark filter for frac in [0.1, 0.5, 1.0]: sample = df.sample(withReplacement=False, fraction=frac, seed=42) t0 = time.time() cnt = sample.filter(col("revenue") > 0).count() t1 = time.time() print(f"Fraction={frac} | rows={cnt} | time={t1-t0:.3f}s") ``` **Expected Observations:** - Runtime scales **roughly linearly** with sample size (filter is $O(N)$). - With 100% data, runtime increases only ~10× compared to 10% (Amdahl efficiency > 0.9). - A **5–10× speedup** is achievable with proper partitioning. **Mark split:** Code correctness 3m, top-10 query 1.5m, benchmark loop 1.5m, observation 1m. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Valuation:** > > 1. **Confusing RDDs with DataFrames** — RDDs are *untyped* and lack schema optimization; DataFrames leverage Catalyst. Mention this explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting `action` triggers** — A common error is claiming `filter()` runs the job. It does NOT — only actions like `count()`, `show()`, `write` materialize results. > 3. **Skipping shuffle discussion** — Every wide transformation incurs shuffle. Examiners allocate 2–3 marks specifically for shuffle cost. > 4. **Wrong units in formulas** — Speedup is dimensionless; efficiency is in $[0, 1]$; time in seconds. > 5. **Using `collect()` on huge data** — causes `OutOfMemoryError` on the driver. Always recommend `take(n)`, `show()`, or `write`. > 6. **Ignoring `persist()`** — repeated use of a DataFrame without caching wastes re-computation cycles. > 7. **Applying Amdahl's Law incorrectly** — Students often forget the limit $1/(1-f)$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **🔑 High-Density Revision Checklist for Project-4:** > > - [x] **Spark = In-memory, distributed, lazy-evaluated cluster framework**. > - [x] **Driver → Cluster Manager → Executors** architecture. > - [x] **RDDs are immutable + lineage-based** for fault tolerance. > - [x] **DataFrames** are RDDs with schema + Catalyst optimization. > - [x] **Narrow** = no shuffle; **Wide** = shuffle required. > - [x] **Lazy evaluation** → only **actions** trigger execution. > - [x] **Sort** is wide: $O(N \log N + \text{shuffle})$. > - [x] **Filter** is narrow: $O(N)$, no shuffle. > - [x] **Search** via `filter` or **broadcast `join`**. > - [x] **Amdahl's Law**: $S_{\max}(p) = 1 / [(1-f) + f/p]$; max limit = $1/(1-f)$. > - [x] **Speedup** = $T(1) / T(p)$; **Efficiency** = $S(p) / p$. > - [x] **Optimization:** cache, broadcast, partition, AQE, columnar formats. > - [x] **GA-CRP:** ~903 K rows, JSON columns, target = `transactionRevenue`. > - [x] **Avoid:** `collect()` on full data; too many/few partitions; missing `persist()`. > - [x] **Always** analyze runtime with **multiple sample sizes** and **partition counts** for KTU lab report. > - [x] **Spark UI** (port 4040) is your friend — DAG visualization, stage timings, shuffle read/write. ---
